# Chapter 01 - There are no magic traps in that cave.

*The War of the Cult of the Dragon, Excerpt from the Great Sage Konstantinious the 12th*
The defeat of Tiamat at the Well of the World was a victory for the people of the North. The Cult of the Dragon had achieved what no one thought possible unity of all factions in the face of extinction. 
And yet, the unity was short-lived. 
The divisions that kept the Harpers, Zhentarim, Lord’s Alliance, Order of the Gauntlet, and Emerald Enclave from working together reared their ugly heads. Their distinct visions for the future were at odds with each other. The common ground with no extinction-level event to focus the mind evaporated as quickly as the portal summoning the Queen of Dragons.
As for the people of the North, rebuilding was the word of the day. Many towns had been destroyed or plundered to create the hoard to entice Tiamat. And the villagers were waiting to see how much of the recovered treasure would make it back to their towns. 
Not all was bad. The evil dragons had been soundly defeated and were no longer an existential threat to the realms. And the armies of the North were so battered and bruised that war seemed unlikely. And the Cult of the Dragon was destroyed. Their mad project of creating a new undead Dragon race, the dracolich, was over. 
In short, the realms were at peace. 
Rumors persisted, however, that there was some treasure that the Cult Leaders had hidden away and not handed to Tiamat. Some Cultists had a plan B, just in case their plots for Armaggedon failed or perhaps even succeeded. Or maybe it was just plain avarice. 
According to this sage, most of these maps were primarily cons. An opportunity to make some coin from some foolish adventurer, down-on-their-luck soldier, lost soul, or an unlucky fool. Some maps lead nowhere. Some towns would use the story of a map to deal with enemies encroaching on their territories. “Oh, those goblins were allied with the Dragon and have treasure!” was a common way to get the naïve to eliminate tribes that were in the wrong place at the wrong time. There was always treasure, but was it related to the Cult? Who knows.

## Orsik and Vardis

Orsik and Vardis had run out of garrisons, armies, city guards, and factions that wanted to hire them. If what they had heard at Secomber, Zelbross, Loudwater, Llorkh, and Parnast was true, their preferred employment choice was no longer an option. 
No one wanted to hire them.
And Parnast was, literally, the end of the road. And so when they heard that a treasure hunter, Veradaa Storr, had a map of Cult gold, they saw a new employment opportunity. Veradaa would need someone to help her guard her treasure, and she would be willing to pay for it.
But there was a deeper reason, the two of them had a treasure map, and perhaps Veradaa would be someone who could reveal a clue about that map. 
But they just had to get to her first before anyone else got the job.  
And that’s how they ended up in a clearing along the Black Road staring at her dead body. 
## Thistle Wendrod

“Being a fairy is hard work,” thought Thistle. “And it’s tough to work when you are looking for someone.” 
And sometimes, it was hard to stay motivated, but he had to find her. And he had three things going for him; the first was his unshakeable conviction that he would find her. Two, he had the stories his uncle had told him about the world. And three, he had a burning desire to get vengeance on the Cult that had put him in prison for a long time. Fairy’s hate enclosed spaces. 
The story of Cult treasure always piqued his interest. After all, what was more valuable than his friend? 
Most of the stories had gone nowhere, but he knew a guy, who happened to be a Harper, that had told him that Veradaa was the real deal. 
And that’s how he ended up near Parnast staring at the very odd pair of a dwarf and an elf, looking like they had seen better days, standing over a dead body. 
## Unla Key

“Luck is what other people call my intuition,” was how he liked to think about the world. He had this peculiar talent to be in the right place at the right time, most of the time. And hearing about Veradaa’s map was a stroke of luck. 
He knew this was the real thing. After all, weren’t his instincts always right? 
And so when he heard a rumor about Cult gold, and those around him dismissed it, he quickly set out to find this treasure hunter and offer his services. 
When he arrived at the appointed location, he saw her dead body. And he was shocked that there were three other people there. 

“Would they all come to blows?” he thought.
He was relieved when the other three introduced themselves and said they had come to work for Veradaa as an assistant. 
“They were all obviously trying to scam the poor woman,” he thought. 
After the introductions were over, Unla Key joined the others to look through her belongings. 
It was then that the one called Vardis said, “our travels were uneventful, but there were rumors of Giant activity – rune-covered boulders, huge footprints, and missing livestock.
Unla Key dismissed this as just talk, “Giants this far south is unheard of.” 
## Thistle Wendrod 

“Found it!” he shouts. And presents a set of notes to the group. 

***The First Cache. ****The first treasure cache was hidden by the Cult of the Dragon in an abandoned cave at the base at of the southernmost mountain in the Greypeak range. While it was thought to be the smallest of the caches, it is also not thought to be protected by any traps or magic. The caves and tunnels of the complex, according to my sources, are not very structurally sound. Goblins are known to inhabit the area.
****The Second Cache. ****My sources confirmed that one cache was buried beneath a unique structure: a pyramid of stone resting on a flat field of stone in the middle section of the Greypeaks, directly between the two highest mountains of that section of the range. The area was once rent with volcanic activity, making the flat stone field difficult to navigate. My sources could not tell me who or what built the boulder pyramid.
****The Third Cache. ****The source I questioned about this cache said that it was carefully hidden behind a secret entrance in the wall of a green-sided mountain. There is only one of those in the Greypeak range, according to my research. The source also said that the cultist who hid the treasure was an expert in magical curses.
****The Fourth Cache. ****This cache was secured in an abandoned dwarven outpost used for treating ore that was being prepared for use in weapon-smithing. My sources implied that the place had been overrun by something terrible and immortal, causing the dwarves to leave it centuries ago. Subterranean volcanic activity has been reported in the area regularly over the years.
****The Fifth Cache. ****At the top of the tallest summit of the Greypeaks, one contact told me that there is an abandoned shrine once revered by giants. Dragons used the area as a perch since then, and a cache is going to be hidden there. Someone also told me that occasionally, when the wind is blowing in just the right direction, a pealing of loud bell can be heard from there.*
All at once, debate starts. Thistle notices that the dwarf and the elf talk hushed tones about a map. The one known as Unla Key seems lost in his head. As for Thistle, he tries to remember any story his uncle told him but can’t.
Thistle tries to get people to listen to him. “The group is still storming,” he reflects, “not yet norming or performing.”
Eventually, consensus emerges, and Thistle happily heads towards the first cache because everyone agrees it must be the safest because there are no magical traps. 
## Sergeant Gronk

“This mission has turned into a disaster. We came with two giants, one died in a cave-in, and the other is a tiny little creature, and to top it all off, half our soldiers are little toys.” 
He shouts to the other goblins, “all right, we need to guard this cave and not let anything pass until whatever magical curses we triggered end.” 
Just then, he hears a group heading towards the cave entrance debating about whether they should enter and how they should enter. He smiles; he’s got them where he wants them. Setting up his troops to take advantage of partial cover, he waits for the fools to enter. 
The cavern he picked to make his last stand isn’t too bad of a defensive position. There’s a tunnel to the south he could flee into if things get really ugly, and there’s a lot of cover to hide behind. As he sits and waits, the dust in the air tickles his nose, “can’t cough, he thinks.” He then looks at the Giant recently buried in a cave-in on the east tunnel that produced all of the dust. 
At first, the battle goes his way; two of the invaders are dead. The pompous dwarf is slaughtered. And the flying creature is put down. And then disaster, a magic-user casts thunderwave, killing two of his goblins. 
“Damn it,” he thinks, “will that giant ever recover?”
And that’s his last thoughts before he dies. 
## Unla Key

Unla Key did not die. “I am knocked down, but I get up again,” he thinks. 
And now he’s looking at the Fairy, who is flitting away down the southern hallway when he hears scurrying and a high-pitched squeaking echo.
He expects to see rats but instead sees a swarm of goblin-sized mice charging at him. Their tiny scimitars and shields seem almost comical at first until he realizes how many of them there are.
Just then, the lights go out as they swarm all over him. 
As the goblins in the swarm are killed, they grow back to normal size, and pretty soon, the entire area in front of the southern cave is almost impassable with dead goblin bodies. 
And that clues Unla Key and the group that whatever is cursing these creatures is not permanent, and it might be wise for them to advance. 
## Orsik and Vardis

The two anti-heroes follow the Fairy from a safe distance, “never be too close to the front or the rear” is their motto. 
And they see over Thistle’s head another cavern with lanterns. Their eyes are drawn to a small creature dressed in ratty furs wielding a club standing in front of a stone chest that is even with his eyes. The thing is grumbling and fuming about something. 
As Thistle enters the cave proper, a yappy growl alerts the creature. That’s when Orsik and Vardis recognize what it is, a tiny giant. They try hard not to laugh.
The tiny Giant screams, “Slayermighty the Giant will tear you apart and feed you to Blooddrinker! I’ll be ruling all of Giant-kind soon!”
But the Giant cannot lay a hand on the group, and Orsik smiles when Slayermight dies. He turns to Vardis and says, “Didn’t even get a chance to swing its club!” 
At that point, they both notice that the fearsome bear, Blooddrinker, turns on his belly expecting to be rubbed. 
## Thistle Wendrod 

As Vardis and Orsik open the chest and look for the treasure, Thistle plays with the tiny bear. And just then, it starts to convulse. Thanks to some quick thinking, he saves the creature. The bear quickly bonds to him. Blooddrinker is the size of a cat. And is adorably ferocious. 
As Thistle plays with his new friend, he hears Orsik groan, “Another giant came here before us and left a note - 

*“Stupid runt-giants of the hills,
*
*You are too slow and dim-witted to challenge us. The treasure is ours. You are probably feeling small now, aren’t you? If you think you are going to prevail, you are mistaken. By the way, prevail means win, idiots.*
*
Clan True-Nimbus of the Clouds”*

Thistle is disappointed but is pleased to hear that it’s not a complete loss. Slayermighty had two vials of alchemist fire, a potion of healing, and a necklace of 20 copper charms. 

# Chapter 02 - Going off script
# Grrrk
We sat in silence, basking in our role as parts of this great pyramid. And we enjoyed the quiet and our serene existence.
One of the younger rocks, Prrrt was complaining about its feet. Why I remember when I was a young rock, how I had rock mites that tore into me, but I didn’t complain, I didn’t fidget. A Galeb Duhr of my generation knows how to do their job. 
As we sat there in the great pyramid, we noticed a fairy, a dwarf, a gnome and an elf. And we noticed how they fought the badgers that hid in the rocks. 
And then the fairy flew tickled my nose with its wings, and we talked. I told it about what I had seen – giants and kobolds in great numbers. 
Prrrt, of course, whined about his mites. But the fairy was happy to help, and Prrrt told them what the dead giant had said.
And they left to the top, I was sure that they would be dead, but Prrrt felt otherwise. He was young and optimistic. And he was right! They didn’t get zapped like the giant. I smiled! And then I heard them climb down into the pyramid. And then they disappeared. 
Enough excitement for this century, I think.
## The group

Vardis reads the Valdorra’s note - **. ***The source I questioned about this cache said that it was carefully hidden behind a secret entrance in the wall of a green-sided mountain. There is only one of those in the Greypeak range, according to my research. The source also said that the cultist who hid the treasure was an expert in magical curses*.
“So guys, why would we want to go get shrunk like this cute bear? What’s the thought process?” 
Thistle sighs, “he’s so cuute!” 
“Why are we going to this place?” asks Orsik.
“Because treasure!” pipes up Thistle. 
Unla Key muses, “what if we went off script? Why should we let this dead treasure hunter set our fate? What if we did something different?”
“Yeah, like get something that fights magical curses,” agrees Vardis
The group stares at its treasure to see whether they have enough money. Their kitty is almost bare: a ledger of the cult of the dragon, 100 gold coins, a necklace of copper charms and a scroll of comprehend language. 
Orsik pulls a map, and says, “Llorkh. It’s ruled by a bandit king. We had a bit of trouble of finding employment with the guards, but they didn’t kick us out. Parnast is the other town nearby, but it’s smaller.”
Thistle Wendrod whispers, “according to my uncle, Llorkh was at the western end of the black road that the Zhentarim controlled through the desert. It used to be the last outpost of that group.” 
Vardis adds, “I recall that treasure hunters met at the local tavern exchanging tall tales, and tribesmen from the great desert would show up with strange artifacts to sell.”
Orsik turns to the gnome, “where to?” 
Unla Key rolls a dice, and announces, “Llorkh.” 
After a short rest, the group heads out, and Grrrk, Prrrt shout out, “nice seeing you!” 
Vardis shouts back, “Nice to see you! Thanks for the treasure!” 
Prrrt shouts back, “Wasn’t ours! And thank you so much for dealing with the beetles!” 
Then the gruff voice of Grrrk is heard, “alright back to your positions.” And with that the Galeb Duhr are invisible again.
Before the group heads out, they look around and in unison shout, “that way!” 
The day is hot and sunny for the spring in the Greypeak mountains, but the whole atmosphere just feels so pleasant.
Until they sniff a pretty nasty wiff. Unla Key pauses, wondering what the smell is, but Vardis and Orsik plow on. “It’s a stench, why do we care?” 
Vardis and Orsik have been in smellier places, but the tender noses of Unla Key and Thistle are not comfortable with such a stench and start puking. Now it turns out that fairy’s when they vomit, release a staggering amount of vomit. In fact, the physics of how so much liquid escapes their body is damn near incomprehensible. 
As the two ruffians drag their fellow travelers out, four bandits surround the group. 
“Hand me your treasure or die!” 
“Good news there is none!” shouts Orsik. 
The bandits are unconvinced, “surely you and your friends must have something? You look like the type to be looking for treasure of the Cult of the Dragon that is hidden in these hills!” 
Unla Key turns to Orsik, “I don’t want to hand over anything.” 
Orsik agrees. Channeling every officer, sergeant that had ever screamed at him, in disgust he says, “back off! We don’t have any treasure! You’re easy to slaughter. Go away!” 
Unla Key whispers to him, “and hand us your treasure!” 
Orsik growls back softly, “don’t push it!” 
The bandit leader seeing this turn of events whispers to his friends, and shouts, “never let it be said that old-one-eye doesn’t have a soft spot for down on their luck mercenaries!” The bandits then melt away into the rocks. 
A few hours more of scrambling through rocks in the foothills of the Greyspeak mountains and the group ends up near the road to Llorkh. 
The sun is setting, and they breakout camp. Vardis and Orsik sleep as far way from everyone else while still be close enough to respond to an attack. In their minds, their not sure whether respond is flee, join in or defend.  Unla Key sleeps in his own corner.  Thistle cuddles his new pet bear friend and falls asleep feeling warm and loved. 
## Eden “Gleeful” Gleeson
“Oh, my goodness, I forgot a present for my beloved!” Eden has been traveling for several days to attend to his wedding, and he just remembered that he forgot to buy a wedding gift. Ever since he left Llorkh he despaired of finding any gift.
As he mumbles about his fate on the road east, he sees ahead a fairy, an elf, a dwarf and gnome. 
“Hello!”
“Not interested” and “goodbye” they shout back to him.
He is desperate and shouts back, “No, you don’t understand, I am going to my wedding and forgot to get my bride a wedding gift! Surely you must have something for sale?” 
Vardis shouts, “a copper necklace for 500?”
“No price is too high for my bride, but **that** is too high” 
“A magnifying glass with no glass?” asks Orsik.
“Perhaps something shiny, goldy?” 
“A polished gold piece we will sell for two?” Unla Key proposes. 
“A giant silver nose ring?” asks Vardis. 
“That will do just fine! I will turn it into a necklace!”
“What were you thinking?” 
And before he knows it, Vardis and Eden Gleeson are haggling. 
“200 GP!” 
“200? I would not be able to pay for my wedding”
“Sell one of your servants!” 
“They are in my employee; they are not my slaves! How dare you suggest I am part of the slave trade!” 
“150 seems fair,” shouts Vardis.
“You drive a hard bargain!” 
At that point his manservant Cisco steps forward. Cisco has an eyepatch and a small teddy bear that he cradles in his arms. “Eddie, you can’t make these impulsive purchases! You just ate into the whole desert budget!” 
# 
The group

Later on,in the evening a few miles out of Llorkh, four wolves emerge from the trees on either side of the road and attack our adventurers. Unla Key is killed. Orsik kills a wolf. Thistle waits for another wolf to run out of the woods, and misses.  That wolf clamps down it’s teeth on Orsik and bites air. And then things take a turn for the worse, when Vardis is brought to death’s door by another wolf. Orsik stares at Unla Key and Vardis and decides to revive the mage. 
Orsik turns to Unla Key, “don’t make me regret my choice to heal you!” 
Unla Key casts a thunder wave and the wolves are blasted away. 
Unfortunately, the third wolf brings Thistle to death's door. 
Orsik kills a wolf. They are now down to 2 combatants on either side. 
The wolf over Vardis body, seeing that the battle is too evenly balanced, takes Vardis’ bow and runs into the forest. The other wolf over Thistle’s body takes his arrows and runs.
Orsik starts to scream, “Fucking wolves! We killed two of theirs, they killed two of ours. This was an embarrassing encounter. We lost a healing potion. We got no treasure! AND WE LOST A BOW!” 
The group takes a short rest under the trees where the wolves had staged their ambush. 
Feeling somewhat refreshed, the group heads out again and arrives towards sunset at Llorkh. 
Llorkh is a small town dominated by a keep on a hill. It has a single main road, and a few offshoots to either side. 
## The Guards of the Iron Circle -  Captain Jolanth

“Harumph, newcomers!” he mumbles as he sees an elf, a dwarf, a fairy and a gnome trudge up to town. 
Jolanth has been in Llorkh and managed to survive the Cult of the Dragon, and the decline in Zhentarim traffic after certain unfortunate events. He’s thought about leaving, but the pay is good, and he likes the weather. 
He stands in the middle of the road – “who goes there!” 
The gnome shouts back at him, “we are we!” 
“This one is funny,” he thinks.  “We are the Guards of the Iron Circle, and we protect this town. Those who disrupt the peace, are sent to the keep!” he shouts and points to the castle. “We managed to survive that nasty business with the Dragons, and we will not tolerate any disruption to the peace!” 
Orsik cuts him off, “we were almost killed off by a pack of wolves. You don’t have to worry about us.” 
Jolanth stares at Orsik and thinks, “doesn’t the fool know I know he is too weak to present a challenge to the guard! I don’t often get to lord it over the passers-by!” 
“This is a safe town, and I want to keep it that way!” 
Orsik smiles, “so that means if we are in danger, you will protect us? Is that what I heard?”
Jolanth is stunned, this was unexpected. He is flustered. “Well, if the mission to protect the town happens to require saving you,” 
But Vardis cuts him off, “free protection! I like it!” 
Jolanth stares in shock as the four walk past him. 

## The group 
“So, let’s see,” says Orsik. “This town, if I recall, has a tavern, a store and a temple.”
Vardis mumbles, “I can go to that one.” 
“You know what, let’s go to the tavern.” 
As they enter the only tavern in town, they notice that the bartender is a ghost.
It sees them and shouts, “hello!”
Vardis stares, “you can talk?”
“Yes, why?”
“Your condition?”
At that point Vardis notices that the tavern is full of local patrons who tense up with that question. 
“So, what will you have?” 
“What do you have?” 
“Ale, roasted turkey, a puff pastry and a pine nut salad!” 
“Is the pine nut salad vegan?”
“Yes!”
“Then I’ll have one of those.” 
Vardis stares at the ghost thinking how it is that the ghost cares about money. And that everyone is behaving as if this is totally normal. 
The rest order some food and drink and sit down. While waiting for it to be delivered, the group takes in the scene in the tavern. It's full of Guards of the Iron Circle. 
Vardis turns to Orsik, “you know they would protect us!” 
Orsik smiles, “hey ghost, how did you get to be ghost?” 
The bartender looks at him, “I am not a ghost.” 
Meanwhile everyone else in the bar starts to find a way to leave or brace themselves for what will come next. 
Orsik presses on, “No, you really ARE a ghost!” 
The ghostly bartender transforms from a kindly barkeep into a monstrous creature that terrifies Orsik. A few moments later he has aged 30 years. 
Orsik expects to see the guards come to his defense, but they have already fled the establishment. 
When he finally comes out, Diego Malfoy, a guard screams, “are you mad? We never tell him that he’s a ghost!” 
“Well, you could have told me when we came into town.” 
“Most people aren’t that stupid.” Diego pauses, “wait what is your name?” 
Orsik realizes that his reputation has preceded him. 
“No, I am not.” 
“You are Orsik! And that one is Vardis! You wanted a job, and you couldn’t even show up sober on your first day!” 
“Look, we’re nobodies.” 
Vardis interrupts the discussion, and asks, “where can we find a scroll of dispel magic?” 
“Oh, at the local store, The Lionel Coster outpost.” 
Just then a man appears, almost out of nowhere. He’s wearing boots, breeches, a sturdy belt with a steel buckle, a shirt and over that a brown cloak with a hood. 
“I saw your courage there and have an offer of employment.” 
Orsik rolls his eyes, “you saw a bunch of fools poke a ghost.” 
“Well, I don’t have a lot to people to pick from. And the clock is ticking.” The man sighs, “Do you want the job? It pays.”
Vardis smiles, “why didn’t you start with that?” 

# Chapter 03 - The middle person

## Fiora Bendaega

“So, are you willing to take the job?” 
The fairy called Thistle stares at him, “would this have anything to do with the cult of the dragon?” 
“Would it help if I said yes?” 
Thistle grabs the note, and while he’s reading it, the middle person explains that he’s but a middle person looking for adventurers for honest tradespeople working the black road. 
Seeing an opening, the middle person tries empathy, “The cult of the dragon was a terrible tragedy. I noticed you were a victim. It was terrible for business.” 
That didn’t quite go according to plan as the fairy glares, “not exactly helping me. I want revenge.” 
The middle person sighs, “I’m a middle person. I connect people who need people with people who need jobs. You need jobs, and I need employees. And what happens is people pay me to give me these letters of introduction for quests that need to be completed. And I look for adventures which might fit the bill.” 
The dwarf cocks his head, “so how do I get your job? That looks like a pretty sweet gig; I hand out letters and get paid. How much does your job pay? Where do I sign up? I could take your job, and you can take the letter.” 
The middle person is shocked, “that is not how it works!” 
Seeing the dwarf approaching threateningly, Fiora signals to a guard, who advances, growling, “are these guys giving you problems?” 
Vardis mumbles, “just having a conversation.” 
Fiora smiles and whispers, “Diego is so fond of me. Diego, come here to meet these fine folks!” 
Fiora knows they will say yes, and when Orsik asks, “how long do they have?” the deal is closed. 
“Oh, the merchant will not be there for three weeks, and it’s a two-week journey. You have a month, I would say.” 
Although she does notice how Vardis seems dubious, “we are the best people for the job? I mean, you do notice that we poked the ghost?” 
She then notices Orsik, who sees Diego reaching for his sword, “heya, Diego.” 
Fiora winks to Diego, who backs down, “well then, if you ever need another job, you can find me at Nonna’s place. I don’t think you are ever going to be welcome here again.” 
## The group 2nd day of the first tenday of Taraskh

“Time to go to unload some of this stuff,” Thistle squeaks. 
*The town of Llorkh has a Lionel Coster franchise. The Lionel Coster trading house has storefronts everywhere, and even in the most obscure parts of the realm, the Lionel Coster is the place where you can find anything. They specialize in small towns and villages where their unparalleled inventory management allows them to sell goods that would typically be unavailable anywhere. Some claim that the trading firm is a front for the Harpers. Others argue that their destruction of small shopkeepers proves that they are a Zhentarim. Still, others claim they are a front of a cult, with some suggesting Mammon’s demonic power. Finally, others argue that it’s just a trading house committed to making money, but only the foolishly naïve believe that. *
The group walks into the franchise and is greeted by the immaculately dressed person, even in the most casual of clothes. He could wear a peasants tunic and wool pantaloons to a king’s ball and be the well-dressed person there. He’s got that clean, pressed, precise look that, if it wasn’t for the fact that magic is available and you could do illusions at the drop of a hat, would almost be unbelievable. But this guy achieves this look through careful and meticulous preparation.
His hair is perfectly combed. He’s got a beard, but it’s like perfectly cut. Even his teeth are freakishly white. 
Speaking with a gruff husky voice, he declares, “my name is Leo, and I am the proud owner of this franchise. I want you to know that I don’t do credit for first-time buyers.” 
Vardis sees an opening and asks, “so if we buy something, then we aren’t first-time buyers?” 
Leo glares at him. Vardis notices and walks out, “Diego wants to talk to me.” 
Leo then turns to Thistle, “what can I do for you?” 
“We are looking for a scroll of dispel magic.”
“Well, I have one of those, and it will be 300 gold.” 
Thistle looks at it and notices something about the scroll, “look at this, the tube has scuff marks, and the scroll is creased?” 
Leo is aghast and appalled. He is particularly proud of his store and that he has been nominated for the “Lionel Coster best presentation award!” “If the committee were to find out,” he thinks, “they would remove me from consideration.”
He turns to Thistle, “this is disgraceful. All my merchandise is perfectly kept. I can not sell this for full price; you can have it for 250 gold!” 
A few moments later, after buying a new bow and some new arrows, Thistle meets the group outside. 
“Where to next?” he asks. 
“There’s a temple. Let’s go check it out.” 
The group finds an old, abandoned temple to Bane. 
*Bane is vilified in many legends. Those who favor him have committed dark deeds in his name throughout history, but most people don’t worship Bane out of malice. Bane represents ambition and control, and those who have the former but lack the latter pray to him to give them strength. It is said that Bane favors those who exhibit drive and courage and that he aids those who seek to become conquerors, carving kingdoms from the wilderness and bringing order to the lawless.*
*At many times and in many places in Faerûn, the faithful of Bane have been seen as saviors for their efforts in slaughtering raiders, throwing down corrupt rulers, or saving armies on the brink of defeat. But in just as many other places, the worship of Bane had created or supported cruel dictatorships, aided mercantile monopolies, or brought about the practice of slavery where before it didn’t exist.*

The temple was built by the Zhentarim, who have since abandoned Llorkh. The two shrines are to two kinder Gods, Chauntea, The Great Mother or Grain Goddess, and Tempus the Foehammer, the Lord of Battles. 
*Chauntea’s faith is one of nurturing and growth. Agricultural aphorisms and farming parables dot her teachings. Growing and reaping, the eternal cycle, is a common theme in the faith. Destruction for its own sake, or leveling without rebuilding, is anathema to her.*

*Tempus’s favor might be randomly distributed, but over the centuries, his priests have made an effort to spread and enforce a standard code of warfare — to make war a thing of rules, respect for reputations, and professional behavior. This code, called Tempus’s Honor, has the purpose of making conflicts brief, decisive, and as safe as possible for those not directly involved. The rules in the code include the following: arm anyone who needs a weapon; disparage no foe; acquit oneself with bravery; train all for battle, and don’t engage in feuds. Those who poison wells, taint fields, kill noncombatants, or engage in torture in the name of war are all considered sinners.*
The shrines are not attended by any regular priests. 
Vardis sighs in relief, “won’t be banned here.” 
Orsik turns to the group, “Hey, there’s the tower of Green Flame?” 
Thistle and Unla Kee stare at him.
“Yeah, when we came through here, we thought it might be worth checking it out, but we did that thing, and then we had to leave.” 
More staring.
“Well, it’s rumored to be a residence of a mighty wizard that was destroyed during the Spellplague. Locals avoid it, fearing the arcane traps and wards still in place. One of the local kids told me that a dare is to try and go to it and touch the tower.” 
Thistle then argues, “not at night?” 
Vardis and Orsik nod. Orsik then remembers Old Keller and decides to go talk to him. 
Vardis warns Orsik, “I don’t think he likes us.” 
Orsik scoffs and says, “we dwarves stick together.” 
Arriving at the stonemason’s hut, Orsik shouts, “My kinsman!” 
“Don’t take a step closer,” shouts Keller. 
“Hey, I thought we dwarves stick together?” 
“We do until they do that thing in the dwarf’s place of work, disgracing all dwarves in the process.” 
“What thing?” 
“The thing in the keep!” 
Orsik groans, “I really need to do something, so that isn’t an issue.” He turns to Old Keller, “what can I do to redeem myself?”
“Stop doing that thing! And when you are less likely to be goblin food, come back to me, and I can tell you what you can do to redeem yourself in my eyes.” 
## Nonnie’s Place

Nonnie is a short ruddy-cheeked woman. She runs her small inn with its kitchen and common room for the few visitors to Llorkh that don’t guest with the Iron Circle. 
She is proud of her vegetarian-only menu and insists that everyone call her “Aunt Nonnie.” 
When the group enters her inn, she beckons them to eat. 
Vardis mumbles, “nice to be treated like a human being.” 
One of the clients lifts her head up from the table and says, “she treats everyone like a human being. Even refugees like me.” 
The woman notices that they are still listening and says, “I come from a town to the west, called Greenest. It was destroyed by the Cult of the Dragon. I fled east, looking to be as far away from the Dragons as I could. Now I am hearing rumors of giants nearby, so I am going back west to see if my town is safe and rebuild.” 
After the woman finishes her tale, Nonnie takes their order of four cheese pies. She heads off to the kitchen, whistling a whimsical tone. She comes back 20 minutes later with four warmed-up pies cooked to perfection, tasty beyond belief, and quite filling. 
After dinner, she asks, “you’ll need a place to stay. It will cost you. It’s not the fanciest rooms.”
As they head to bed, she thinks, “what a nice group of people.” 
## The Wizard Tower 3nd day of the 1st tenday of Taraskh

The entity in the tower sees the group advance upon its building. 
It starts talking to itself. 
“Oh dear, someone wishes to come to the tower. I suppose the usual wards will do.” 
It notices that half of the group is still advancing. 
“Well, how awkward, the dwarf and the elf are still approaching. And they have found the handle. And they are about to try it.” 
The elf touches the handle, and the entity is pleased to see that the dwarf and elf are hurt but not dead. 
It says to no one in particular, “They are still alive; that should be a good enough warning for now.” 
As the group walks away, it realizes with sorrow, “the door can now be opened.”  
## The group 3rd day of the 1st tenday of Taraskh 

The two-day hike to the third treasure trove that Veradda Store had pointed was remarkably uneventful, with only a druid collecting herbs interrupting the monotony. 
Her notes lead them a narrow path halfway up a mountain in the northern Greypeaks. And there they stood, looking down over a cliff to their right, with the sheer mountain to their left. 
“No way in,” shouted Orsik. 
“Must be a way in,” shouted Vardis
Thistle and Vardis work together to find a pair of secret doors hidden in the rocks. 
Vardis marches into the one to the south, Thistle follows. The door opens into an unlit corridor of unworked stone. Thistle flitters ahead until the natural tunnel splits in two. He waves to Vardis to come forward, “be careful of the pit trap.” 
Orsik shouts, “what about the other way?”
“Another trap!” shouts Thistle, then really gets annoyed at the dungeon makers -  “Really, they have no imagination. The same pit trap, everywhere. Like what do they expect invaders to do? OOOPS, I FELL INTO A TRAP! CAN’T POSSIBLY IMAGINE ANOTHER ONE!”
Thistle lands next to the trap and observes that the group can safely walk across the weight-triggered trap as long as he flies across. 
“Right-hand rule,” announces Thistle as he leads the group. The tunnels eventually end at a large chamber covered with ice. Thistle looks into and notices two large humanoid figures. “Frost giants!” he exclaims.  

The group heads over and notices that each giant clutches a bloody great ax in its hands, and each seems to have died from slashing wounds created by large bladed weapons. 
Vardis notices a diamond atop a three-foot-tall pedestal on the far side of the room. As he approaches the diamond, his mind sees that it gleams with an inner light that is definitely supernatural. That does not deter him, however. 
He shouts, “Unla Kee use the scroll we bought.” Unla Kee carefully walks to the bed, making sure to not slip on the ice. 
And just then, the diamond magically rises from the pedestal. Beams of dazzling green light erupt, and everyone and everything is bathed in a sickly green glow. 
Vardis shouts urgently, “NOW, Unla Kee.” 
A magical hand streaks from Unla Kee and slaps the diamond to no effect. 
The spell had no effect. 
Or maybe it did, but not the desired effect?” thinks the mage as he sees three masses of translucent green energy. These masses pulse in rhythm with the light of the diamond. He realizes that they are all connected by energy. The masses move towards the group, lashing out with barb-tipped tentacles. Unla Kee shouts, “every time they lash out, the synchronized pulsing of the creatures and the diamond grows brighter!” 

Vardis shouts, “destroy the diamond.” 
Thistle retorts, “that’s a pretty expensive diamond; how about not.” 
The two gang up on one of the creatures and bring it to near death, and when the killing blow should have killed the creature, the gem glows. 
Unla Kee thinks and shouts, “we have to destroy the diamond.” 
Thistle screams, “would a blanket work?” 
“No!” Unla Kee uses his magic missiles. He spreads them around, one on the diamond and one on the other creatures. The diamond is barely damaged. 
The creatures strike back. Pretty soon, only Orsik and Unla Kee are alive. But that is enough to destroy the diamond. With the diamond destroyed, the creatures are finally killed. 
As the last creature dissipates, they see the diamond dust swirl, creating the form of a green-tinted spirit that speaks in a provocative whisper, “Your companions want to kill you and take your possessions! Kill them before they kill you!” 
The group shrugs, pick up the treasure left behind by the giants,, and collects the diamond dust. 

# Chapter 04 - Clan Licehair

## The Group 3rd day of the 1st tenday of Taraskh

“Good berries!” proclaims Thistle. “I have good berries every morning for everyone!” 
Vardis, Unla, and Orsik take their share and thank the ranger. 
After several days of hiking through the mountains in an uncommonly warm spring, the group arrives at an old abandoned dwarven outpost. The gentle weather seems a reasonable recompense by the gods for the almost destruction of the world that Tiamat and their negligence nearly triggered. 
On the 5th day of the 1st tenday of Taraskh, Veradda Stoor’s notes bring the group to a hallway that leads inwards. 
Thistle observes that the dust and cobwebs have been disturbed by many footprints, “too many to tell what has gone through here,” he shouts. 
Orsik stares at the stonework that is centuries old and mutters, “my kinsmen built this.” 
Vardis mumbles, “as if your kinsmen want to have anything to do with you any more than mine with me.” 
Orsik turns pink. He’s embarrassed.
Vardis stares, “well?” 
Orsik turns pink, “I’d rather not talk about it.”
At this point, everyone stares at him.
“Fine, it’s clan Licehair.” 
The group erupts in laughter. Vardis slaps Orsik on the back and laughs loudest.
Orsik gruffly shouts, “Clan Licehair is a proud clan that secured the massive gem hoard of a black dragon. Of course, I am not a member because I don’t have lice in my hair.”
Vardis, who had tried to contain his laughing, laughs louder. When he recovers, he turns to Orsik, “well, there may be a gem hiding there.” 
With that, the group heads into the hallway. As they turn the corner, they enter a room that contains four small creatures, “kobolds” whispers Thistle. 
The kobolds are behind a small barricade blocking the passageway to the south. 
The middle of the room has a small stone building with no apparent entrances, although it does have holes. 
Before they can react, a kobold sees them and shouts, “begone foul creatures!” 
## Agra

In the stone building are two kobolds. Their red scales are scared with burn marks. And their clothes look like they are stinky even by the standards of their kind. 
“That damned fool will fight those heavily armed adventurers,” Agra mutters to Gen, standing right next to him. 
Agra notices that Gen is about to do something, clamps his hand over his mouth, and shouts, “Let’s sit tight and wait.” 
Gen nods. And is silent.
Agra hears Nug argue with Thistle over whether the fairy is or is not a filthy creature. 
“Good, lure them into the trap,” Agra thinks. 
But then, to his horror, the trap failed. The Kobolds that were hidden move so loudly that they could wake up the undead. 
The sound of stones from the hidden kobolds falling on the adventuring group brings him a smile. “Filthy interlopers!” 
But then he starts hearing other sounds, the telltale sounds of a sleep spell, and then the dull sound of dead bodies dropping to the ground.
Gen turns to him, “what will we ever do?” 
Agra looks at him, “surrender and pray for the best?” He grabs something that looks white and steps outside, “we surrender!” 
The group stares at Agra, and he wonders why they won’t talk to him. He then notices a halfling chewing some berries. 
“We are trying to surrender here,” shouts Agra. 
Thistle remembers how the Kobolds were the foot soldiers of the Cult of the Dragon’s foot soldiers and shouts, “did you work for the Cult? Did you praise Tiamat?” 
Agra stares, “no, it can’t be!” and then turns pale. The fairy has the telltale marks of one who was tortured by the Cult of the dragon. “look, it was an accident, right? I was under orders. I didn’t mean any of it!”
“Where is my girlfriend?” shouts the enraged fairy. 
Agra is terrified, “with the defeat of the cult and so many dragons, I simply do not know.” He almost looks disgusted with himself, “we have been serving giant masters now.” 
The fairy flirts up in the most ridiculous poses and says in a voice that cracks Agra up, “where is my girlfriend?” Agra sees the gesture and assumes that Thistle has forgiven him for everything. 

So he makes an offer, “do you need bootlickers?” 
The elf shouts, “do you want to be our slaves?” 
Agra is offended, “no, employees. We are very fluid in our loyalties.” 
Vardis sighs, “we don’t have enough money.” 
The kobold sighs. Then it says, “I suppose this is when I offer you information?” 
Seeing the pointy ends, he begins. “With the defeat of the dragons, our tribes have been reduced to working for Giants. A fire giant hired us after our Red Dragon left.” 
The elf interrupts him, “How many giant types are there in general?”
“I don’t know, but others were hired by hill and cloud giants. There seems to be some kind of trouble brewing between them. They seem to be competing for something or other. I am not quite sure. It is odd because they don’t normally do that.” 
Thistle interrupts, “my girlfriend?” 
“Well, after the Red Dragon, the boss was in a bit of a situation. So he made a deal with a cloud giant. When the cloud giants arrived in their castle, we ran for the hills. The fire giants sent us here to find the treasure of the cultists. They said it was because this was too small of an area for them. But Gun heard that the place was cursed. So we hid in the stone building. When these buffoons,” and he looks at the dead kobolds and corrects himself, “dead buffoons, entered the room to the south, creatures emerged and attacked. When the creatures died, they exploded. Our leader Nug said there was a treasure in the corner of the room to the south, a glowing magical fire symbol, and a river of lava.”  
Thistle growls at him, “where is my girlfriend?” 
“We don’t know,” he cowers. 
Orsik steps in, “got it, cloud giants, girlfriend. Go on!” 
“Well, we don’t have anything else to say. And Thistle, now that we are friends, you wouldn’t mind if we just left?” 
The halfling that didn’t die says, “kobolds make good shields?” 
Agra is paralyzed with fear, and then he hears Gen speak, “perhaps if we offered some treasure?” 
Agra breathes when the dwarf takes the treasure and shouts, “go and no looting.” 
As they leave, Vardis shouts, “Stop.” He then turns to his friend, “should we be worried about them telling the Fire Giants about us?” 
Agra coughs, “if I may, the Fire Giants expect us to show up with some treasure, or not at all. They made it clear that failure to show up with treasure would result in a, how shall we say, negative review of our performance that would result in our termination.” Agra picks up some dirt, squishes it in his hand, and swallows. 
## Orsik
“Let them go,” shouts Orsik.
Vardis looks at him.
Orsik is tired of being looked down upon by everyone. That thing he does, he’s decided to stop doing it. “I’m going in.”
Vardis stares, “are you okay?” 
“Yes, I am done with being the guy everyone hates. The fact that Old Keller didn’t even want to talk to me got to me. It’s like I am not even good enough to be a member of Clan Licehair. LICEHAIR!” 
Orsik moves through the barricade into the southern room. 
The area in front of him is full of smoke, making it hard to see. He hears the comforting sound of crackling large fires, and deeper in the room, he sees large fires. In the middle of the room, he notices a brighter glow that splits the room in half, giving the impression of a river. 
He walks in. And just then, he hears and sees a large burst of fire from a prominent symbol etched into the wall in the southeastern corner of the room. 
The smell of dead burned kobolds is rank. 
Orsik pauses and thinks and says, “hey Thistle, why don’t you go on ahead.”
## The Group

Vardis smiles.
Thistle looks around and processes the scene. He approaches one of the fires and notices that he burns if he gets within 10 feet. So he carefully plots a route around the hot spots until he reaches the fire symbol. 
Once there, he begins to examine the symbol and realizes that disabling it will be necessary if they are to take the silver. 
He’s unable to make heads or tales of what god it represents. He stares some more and tries to remember what his uncle had said.
“Right got it,” he thinks. He starts to damage the symbol by firing arrows at it. 
Orsik, Vardis and Unla watch.
The symbol belches fire, and a magmin emerges from the fire approaching Thistle. 
Thistle rushes to the corner to be as far as possible, but in so doing, the magmin hiding in the lava come out to play. 
He’s surrounded. 
Orsik turns to Vardis, who turns to Unla, and they silently agree to help Thistle. 
Unla Kee uses his thunderwave to push them away from Thistle, and they explode harmlessly. Orsik tosses his hand-ax at one and kills the last one. Unfortunately, in all the excitement, Thistle had a dying magmin explode in his face and another leave lava that he had to brush off to avoid burning to death. 
Vardis helps by healing Thistle.
The fairy once again starts firing his arrows on the symbol.
A few arrows later, he shouts, “okay, I now need to cast a spell.” 
Orsik shouts, “What?” 
“Yeah, there are a few more steps.”
“How many?”
“Well, after I cast the spell, I need to figure out how to sever the connection to the god.”
“How are you going to do that?”
“I’ll figure it out.” 
Unfortunately, he doesn’t in time, and another magmin emerges from the lava. And the battle begins again. 
Thistle ignores the creature and focuses on the altar. “C’mon, what did uncle Yorn say about these gods?” 
Thistle dodges the arms of the creature, and while doing so, it finally makes sense, “got it!” 
And with that, the symbol becomes inert. 
Meanwhile, Orsik attacks the Magmin and kills it. Unfortunately, Thistle was next to the creature, and its exploding entrails burned him. 
Thistle shrugs. 
And with that, the smoke in the room clears. 
Orsik realizes that this room was used as a forge before whatever evil artifact took over. 
The group searches the room, grabs some silver, and extracts several magmin shells prized by spell casters. 
As they leave, Orsik turns to Vardis, “you know, I have heard legends of heroes who stayed in one place, killing hundreds of summoned monsters until they had become powerful beyond measure.”
Vardis yawns, “that’s a legend. Most of the time, the summoning creatures get annoyed, and before you know it, something really awful gets summoned that kills the hero.” 
Orsik scratches his beard and sighs.  

# Chapter 05 - Thoughts and Prayers
## Orsik 6th day of the 1st tenday of Taraksh 

The climb to the highest peak in the Greypeak mountain range is tedious. The weather is a little bit warm, and the sky is a brilliant blue with but trace clouds racing across the sky. 
Orsik stares at the quiet sky and shouts, “there is nothing to excite the nerves!” 
Everyone stares at him. 
“Whaaat?” 
Then Vardis points in the distance, a griffon. 
Orsik stares at the griffon.  
Vardis stares at him. 
Onla Kee grouses, “you do realize that this is your fault? You could have been quiet, but you taunted fate!” 
Orsik turns to the group, “What does he have against us?” 
And then realizes it is not personal as they are a meal to the creature. 
He then dumps his food in a pile, urges everyone else to do the same, and hide. 

At first, there is confusion, “some or all,” they ask. Orsik looks up and realizes that they are four meat sacks, and they must look pretty tasty. There needs to be another bigger meat sack in front of them. So he shouts, “all,” and pretty soon, there is a substantial pile of food that the griffon is drawn to. 
The creature lands in front of the pile. Orsik observes it pecks at the jerky first, then the dried fruit. After it’s eaten most of the food, it purrs contentedly. Then the griffon rubs its belly and flies off. 
Unla Kee turns to him and asks, “do we have to worry about flying poop? Is their poop like a bird’s?” Orsik rolls his eyes but not before Thistle says, “great question. My uncle never saw a griffon poop.” 
## The group  

A few hours later, the group reaches the final push to the peak. The air has gotten thin and cold, and as they pass through a layer of cloud. They emerge into the sunlight. The scene before them is magnificent, an endless sea of white as they stare down at the clouds.
Unla Kee notices some writing on the wall. He casts comprehend languages and notices that they form mantras. Most extol the virtues of Knowledge, Magic, Intelligence, Meditation, and Understanding. 
He sighs, “worthless religious claptrap.” 
Meanwhile, the rest of the group reaches the top of the sloping path. A large brass bell and a cave entrance are at the end of the trail.
As they stand there, four giant owls swoop in to attack. The first owl flies 30’ and attacks Orsik, the dwarf tries to strike as it flies off, but the owl is too tricky. “Ranged weapons,” he shouts.
Orsik hurls two hand-axes, and they fall down into the cloud cover, “useless!” 
Another Owl flies in from the south, strafes Thistle, and misses him. 
The wind howls and the sounds of owls hooting are eerie. 
“Let’s go inside,” shouts Vardis.
“I at least want to try using my bow!” The arrow flies and hits the owl, a severe blow but not a mortal one. 
“I have a plan,” shouts Vardis, who steps as close to the edge as he feels is safe. He pulls out the symbol of Corellon Larethian, a silver circlet, summons his god’s power, and a blast of light burns everywhere. 
One owl hoots one last time and falls to its death.
Another owl seeing the danger that Vardis presents, swoops by to attack, but Vardis dodges it at the last second. The claws of the bird uselessly clutch air. 
The Magic-User stares at the flight patterns of the owls. He realizes that they are flying predictably, probably something they were trained to do. He summons his divination skill to twist the fates in his favor, and two of the birds fly closer than they should. He casts sleep on them. 
Vardis and Orsik cheer as they see two owls fall into the abyss. 
Thistle hits with his bow one more time. Vardis’ new bow is too new, and he misses. 
Vardis, Thistle, and Orsik huddle together, ready to hit the bird if it comes close.
The bird sees Unla Kee 20 feet away and attacks the gnome. The gnome is surprised, and the owl almost deals with a killing blow. 
Unla Kee bellows, “I SURVIVED AN ATTACK! I AM INVINCIBLE!” 
The bird is skewered dead with a magic missile. 
Vardis asks, “I thought owls were friendly!” 
Thistle responds, “well, I noticed that they flew in a particular pattern, suggesting there is some kind of trainer.” 
Unla Kee, meanwhile, is popping good berries. 
Orsik is fed up, charges into the open passageway, and bounces off a magical forcefield. Vardis shouts, “the bell?” 
Unla Kee steps forward and reads a riddle etched into the bell: 

*What doubles when you share it, is unseen but gives power, and is useless unless practiced?*
Everyone has an opinion.
“Music!” observes Thistle. 
“Magic!” shouts Vardis from a safe distance. 
Unla Kee notes, “Friendship? I mean, it’s useless if not practiced. If you don’t practice it, you have no friends.” 
Orsik grouses, “why would giants have a riddle about friendship?”
Unla Kee then remembers the mantras, and Thistle agrees that “Understanding” is the word they want to use.
He turns to Unla Kee and says, “well, I am a little bit tougher than you. I’ll do it.” Unla Kee nods. Thistle waves his hand, “you might want to stand a little further away and not quite as close to the ledge.” 
The bell is rung, the word said, and the force field collapses.
Orsik runs in. 
## Thistle

The tough fairy rushes in with Orsik. The size of the place is enormous. The entrance is big enough for even the largest of giants. 
Walking a short distance, the entrance opens into a massive cavern with 50’ tall ceilings. He stares at it and notices that it has collapsed in a few places, littering the floor with debris and large rocks. 
Orsik nods that the place is safe. 
Once he understands it is safe to enter, he notices four huge braziers made of copper that decorate the room. They appear useable, so he flies up to see if they can be lit, but they are empty with some residue coal and insense. 
The sight of a long stone slab 15-feet high catches his attention from where he is. There is a tattered cloth that decorates the top. 

The decorations on the cloth help bring into focus what he was missing, that the walls were also decorated in similar patterns, but much of the wall paints are ruined.
Orsik and Unla Kee rush to the front while he hangs back near the exit near one of the braziers. 
Orsik looks at him, Unla Kee says, “I take risks.” 
## Unla Kee 

“And I was lucky!” he thinks. All the braziers magically light up. And then, in the south of the room, where Vardis and Thistle were hanging back, two creatures that look like living icicles appear. They shriek and gibber in pain and frustration. Their sharp claws clack. 
The one from the southeastern brazier runs behind Vardis and breathes a blast of icy frost. Thistle is also caught up in the cold wind but manages to hide behind some rocks. 
Vardis is ready to strike when he notices the brazier’s incredible heat that burns his skin. “I need to get away from this.” But he stands his ground and strikes a blow that the creature dodges at the last minute. 

Thistle takes a step to get a better angle on the creature attacking Vardis and misses. 
Unla Kee thinks, “I ventured, and I gained. The world will not end today!” Then casts toll the dead on one of the creatures. It is not over. 
Orsik finishes it off, and it explodes on everyone but Unla Kee. “Lucky again,” he thinks. 
The remaining creature casts fog cloud, making everyone effectively blind. 
Unla Kee sees a flash of magic power streak through the cloud, a shriek from the creature, and a satisfied Vardis standing. 

Unla Kee watches as Orsik climbs. He shouts, “well, there are some gold plates and some dead cultists a few minutes later.” 
“Are they worth a lot?” 
“Yes, Unla Kee, but it would be bad if angered a god. I think there’s the same writing as before.” 
“Would it be less sacrilegious if we melted the gold first?” 
“No!” shouts Vardis. 
“I’ll take them anyways.” 
Meanwhile, Thistle loots the bodies of the cultists and shouts fairy curses.
As the group walks out, they hear the booming voice of a storm giant who speaks with an open-air pipe. 
Thistle whispers to Orsik, “I think he can kill us without breaking a sweat.” 
The giant smiles and says, “I assume you’re not here simply to loot this ancient holy site. You can trust me on what I’m about to say because I have never spoken a truer statement in my life. Annam, the all-father is in no mood to be trifled.” The storm giant chuckles, but you can see a great sadness in his eyes. I am Kraddock. I was told that this shrine was desecrated several days ago, and I’ve come to see what state this place was left in. What brings you here?”
Kraddock then stares at the halfling, trying to hide the large gold plates behind his back. 
Shamelessly Unla Kee declares, “we stopped those bandits from stealing the gold plates.” Kraddock’s face becomes sterner. Meanwhile, the halfling notices that his friends are backing away from him. He tries a different tack, “we’re treasure hunters, and we got this map, and we didn’t think it belonged to anyone.” 
Unla Kee stares at the giant and thinks, “I could twist the fates just enough to make him fear me. Or not. And then it would be a very brief battle. I’m not sure I would call it a battle. But if I don’t take risks, the world may end!” 
“Kraddock, look, we just got here today. This is what it looked like when we got here.” 
“I’m sure that you were just trying to ensure that the gold plates were not stolen.” 
“These gold plates?” 
“Yes, these sacred gold plates.” 
Unla Kee smiles, “why didn’t you say so!” And hands them back to the storm giant. 
“I wasn’t going to kill you. I was just going to invoke a curse of the All-Father.” He takes the plates and carefully shines them. 
## Kraddock 

The storm giant stares at the little people with sadness because he knows that the All-Father has made decisions. And those decisions will affect them. He doesn’t agree with those decisions, but he serves the All-Father.
“I have bad tidings for you. I am here because the Dragon Queen Tiamat failed in her plots to conquer this world. But in failing, she inspired the giants, and they are at war with you. All of the creatures of the earth, air, and sea will probably be caught up in the fracas in some way or another. The age of dragons is over.”
He notices the small fairy asking about the cult. He smiles. He knows why this poor creature suffered. “The Cult of the Dragon was pursuing a spell that Tiamat told them would let her enter the world. But she failed. Now the age of the dragons is over, and our age has begun.” 
He looks at them and feels a great sadness, “You want to tell your friends and loved ones to steer clear of locations where giants live or even where giants were rumored to live.”
The dwarf asks, “where is that?” 
“Great question, this part of the world that you little people live in.” 
“Not cool!” 
“We’re big. You’re little.” 
“You should think about your language. It can cause harm.” 
Thistle shouts, “you need to understand how your communication is being received instead of how you think it is intended.” 
“You are so right. And King Hekaton has talked about having a newer inclusive way of talking about all people. To not otherwise your kind. His great wife who disappeared had much to say.” He coughs. “I worry that the age of tolerance is over as well. But where should we go? As far away as the ancient kingdom of Ostoria. The kingdom of Ostoria stretches from the Spine of the world to the great desert to the east, to the sword coast to the west, and then to the kingdom of Amn in the south.” 
“The entire North?” 
“Yes, there was a giant kingdom here. And over the centuries, the under 16 feet crowd.” He looks at them expectantly.
Everyone nods their heads that this is better. 
“Invaded from the south and drove the giants to the north and to isolated pockets of giant power.”
Vardis observes, “so now they see this as a time to reclaim what is rightfully theirs and may end up terrorizing the entire sword coast! What will you do, Kraddock?”
“I am just a shaman. Clean it up, just trying to make sure that this temple is safe. I’ve got to, “ he looks at the tattered drapes, “I’ve got to fix the drapes. There’s the roof over there that needs patching. The braziers haven’t had charcoal in years, and then there are the frescoes. And plus, I have to polish the alters, get rid of the dead body.” 
“The complete destruction of the sword coast is at risk, and as long as your shrine is fine,” screams Vardis. 
“I must pray to the All-Father to understand what he wants. I offer you my thoughts and prayers and will ask him to protect you from what must come.” 
“So basically nothing.” 
“Yes.” 
He hands Vardis some potions as they leave, “perhaps more than just thoughts and prayers.” 
Unla Kee, the thief, asks, “If you came with us, that would help even more.” 
“There is such a thing as pushing your luck?” 
As they leave, Kraddock sighs, “perhaps the All-Father will learn that this war will bring no glory to anyone.” 
## Group 8th day of the 1st tenday of Taraksh

After a long rest, the group heads back to Llorkh. The weather has turned to the worse and is cold. And they have no food. 
They quickly explain their circumstances and then buy some of his game. 
The rabbit is gutted by Thistle. Apparently, Vardis and Orsik spent too much time writing subversive poetry and never learned that life skill. It is one of the best rabbits the group has ever eaten. 
The next day there is snowfall. And at first, they attempt to walk through the snow, but soon it’s too cold, and they are too tired. Thistle starts a fire, and the group waits it out.
Vardis says, “let’s double time to Llorkh.”
As they can almost taste Nonnie’s food, a giant centipede erupts. 
Orsik shouts, “Seriously, this single giant centipede pops out in front of us in this massive field.” 
A few seconds later, it is dead. The group arrives at Nonnies, Aunt Nonnie, as she likes to be called. She is as friendly and welcoming as always.
“What’s wrong with her Vardis?” asks Orsik. 
“I don’t know. She’s probably affiliated with an evil cult.” 
After they get their room and eat their vegetarian entrees, Aunt Nonnie says, “There have been so many reports of kobolds coming around. And those guards seem unwilling to do very much but stay in their tower.”
“I’d do that,” mumbles Orsik.
“No, you wouldn’t,” she stares at him, “because of that THING you did.” 

# Chapter 06 - The tower
## Group 9th day of the 1st Tenday of Taraskh, Llorkh

Orsik wakes up and is curiously refreshed, “time to see what we can do here.”
Vardis groans, “seriously, can’t we just sleep more.”
The two leave their room, enter Nonnie’s rustic main dining hall, and sit with Thistle and Unla. 
“I would like to buy better armor,” Thistle declares between bites of food.
“I suppose we could sell our treasure at the local Lionel Coster,” mutters Orsik.
And it’s settled; that’s where they go.
Leo, as always, is spotless. Immaculately dressed, facial hair cut to perfection, his skin smooth as marble. They assumed Leo used magic of some kind, but as he would say, “this is all 100% natural, Leo.” 
Unla Kee stares, “to spend so much time on one’s appearance.” 
Orsik acts, “do you have a magnifying glass that could fit this?” 
Leo stares at it, “I could have such a thing in a few days. It will cost extra as it is not part of our standard inventory.”
Unla Kee snorts, “dd bay?” 
Leo is aghast, “we don’t work with THAT firm, sir! We have our inventory and exchange system that doesn’t have a grotesque surcharge!” 
Finally, he turns to Orsik, who is waiting to find out the fee, “well, 5 gold should cover the cost of shipping, etc.” 
Thistle is anxious, “I would like some studded leather armor.” 
The dwarf stares at him, “We got enough money, but why don’t we sell everything to Leo. Leo, we have some stuff you buying?” 
Leo looks at it, “yes, 10% discount.” 
With the group flush with cash, Leo stares at the floor and the dirt that the heroes dragged in. He looks at the door and then the doormat and notices that the doormat is spotless, and his floor is not. 
“My goodness, what a mess you dragged into my store.” Leo runs out and comes back with cleaning implements muttering, “what if an inspector came in?” 
With the store spotless, Leo notices that the group has some serious coin and begins to talk up his store, “I always carry a minimal inventory in a small town, such as this one. We do not have quite the range of products and services that Aurora Realms Stores have. They are famous for their magical inventory and boutiques of rare and exotic items. We try to offer lower price goods for the common sentient.”
Unla Kee reminds the group about rations, and Leo offers some. 
Finally, Thistle asks about magic. Leo ponders, “I do have a very rare item, which I think,” he pauses as he looks at their clothes and that they are counting coins, “out of your price range. But I think there might be something more in your price changes. There’s a potion of healing and a potion of climbing and a scroll with detect magic.” 
Unla Kee shrugs.
## The tower 9th day of the 1st Tenday of Taraskh, Llorkh
“Hmm, they have come again.”
It notices how the one called Vardis has used blessing to help his friends deflect the permanent antipathy field that surrounds the tower. 
“Surely, the mage and cleric will not continue without their warriors.” 
The cleric shouts, “Alright, Unla Kee, Let’s go check this place out. This is our shot.” The cleric then says, “this is what you get for swearing off that thing you used to do.”
The tower sighs, “oh, that thing.” 
The tower wonders if the mage and the cleric will continue or abandon their quest. It then turns to the scene in front of it. 
The cleric marches up to the dwarf and attempts to grapple the dwarf and bring him to the tower. The dwarf escapes and stars, cursing Vardis. At the same time, Unla Kee tries to grapple Thistle and fails. Thistle is more subdued in his reactions. 
The tower sighs, “I suppose I will have to see what can be done.” 
When the two adventures open the door, it says, “ah, this will do nicely.” 
## Unla Kee 9th day of the 1st Tenday of Taraskh, some interdimensional location 

Vardis turns to Unla Kee, “I’m game, let’s go do this.” 
Unla Kee, consumed with fear if he stops taking risks, agrees. 
The cleric observes, “huh, this isn’t the entrance to a tower. It’s a stairwell into a dungeon.” 
Unla Kee is increasingly unsure of the wisdom of exploring a dungeon without a fighter. 
A plaque is at the bottom of the stairs, “Acerak’s Construction Company.” 
Vardis stares and shouts, “It’s a generative dungeon!” 
The halfling stares, “what?”
“The dungeon generates every time you enter.”
“What?” 
Vardis shrugs at Unla Kee, who is very confused. 
Vardis turns to him and says, “lead on!” 
The mage is surprised but goes forward. The stairways lead to a T intersection, and from there, Unla Kee heads south. Then there is another intersection, and the halfling walks south again. Unla Kee follows the passageway until it ends in an archway that he enters. 
The place is very curious. It’s a large hexagonal room with a rope that ascends to a balcony. As he looks around, he notices a corroded key in the room’s southwest corner. 
“Should I pick up the key?” 
“Yes, mage. And be quiet while I prepare a ritual of detect magic.” 
A cranky bugbear arrives as the spell completes, and no magic is detected. 
Unla Kee stares at it. 
“What are you doing here?”
Vardis interjects before Unla Kee can say anything, “We’re not really sure.” 
“Did you go to some tower, open a door and enter?” 
“Yes, we did. Should we not have? I mean, I plead ignorance.” 
“The rules are you have the find a way to get out. It’s not clear what the way to get out is.” He stops and sniffs, “and there is not a lot of food growing over here. I smell rations.” 
“Really,” groans Vardis.
“Yes. Or I could eat the two of you.” 
As the bugbear munches down their food, Vardis again steps forward, “How do you get out of here anyway?” The bugbear ignores him. “Since we’re feeding you, we could have a friendly conversation.” 
The creature looks up, “It’s pretty easy. One group believes you have to find the secret passage out of here. There’s another group that believes there is no escape. And that all that happens is you eventually die of hunger and turn into a zombie.” Unla Kee looks pale but is relieved that he is with a  cleric. “And there’s a couple of those around here.”
The bugbear scratches its jaw, “And then there’s a third group that believes if you die, whatever traps you in here kicks you out as a sort of reminder to not mess around with a wizard’s tower.” 
To Unla Kee’s question of what he believes, he answers, “there are some people who go crazy and kill themselves and hope that the right thing happens. Some people give up and turn into zombies. I decided I’m going to tough it out and find the secret door.”
Vardis punches Unla Kee, and the halfling agrees, “Do you want to pair up with us? Because we could use the help. We could all look together.” The bugbear stares, “my mom always used to say that two walking meat bags is better than rations. I’ll come with you.” 
Now in a peculiar alliance, the bugbear draws the dungeon’s map. He explains that the dungeon has a tribe of goblins, a few ogre zombies, and himself. The goblins don’t believe that escape is possible and are just trying to stay alive. The zombies can be avoided if you move fast enough. To whether anyone has escaped, he smiles, “those goblins I killed escaped. They are no longer trapped in this infernal prison.” 
With the introductions completed, Vardis leads the group west. 
## Vardis 9th day of the 1st Tenday of Taraskh, some interdimensional location
The room is large. As his eyes scan, he notices a remarkably good wooden door to the north. A wall covered in tapestries of vile acts is right in front of him. Vardis thinks, “Nope, not what Orsik did.” Vardis’ eyes are drawn to the floor, where several candles are scattered on the floor along with spirals of white stone. 
Vardis walks up to the tapestry, looks behind it, and shouts, “the secret door!” He then turns smugly to the bugbear, “how long have you been looking for it?”
“A really long time.” 
“This is your lucky day! You found me, and I found the door!” The cleric opens the door, and an ice dart bounces off his armor. “Traps, there are traps.” 
The passageway behind the doorway leads to a door. Vardis realizing that the bugbear is the new Orsik, shouts, “Open the door!” 
It’s a mimic. 
The bugbear slashes at the creature, screaming, “you will not get in the way of my escape!” 
Vardis first rubs his silver circle and then casts a guiding bolt. A green flowing dart flies from his fingers and critically wounds the creature. “First the door, and now the bolt, I am on fire!” 
At this point, the bugbear notices that his morning star is stuck. He shouts, “that’s my morning star!” As the bugbear struggles, the mimic strikes him. 
Vardis turns to Unla Kee, “good thing we spellcasters are here, not the fighters.” Unla Kee agrees, but his cantrip is ineffective. 
Vardis thinks, “if our spells were effective, that is.” 
Vardis watches the grappled bugbear miss with its javelin. He is not much better with his last spell. Vardis thinks, “maybe I should have not been so quick to say I am on fire.” 
Unla Kee smiles, “sleep.” 
And the creature falls asleep. 
The bugbear smashes the creature with its morning star, and then Vardis kills it.
Inside the room, a pile of treasure lies in the center of the room, and a note says, “congratulations for making it this far!” 
The group picks up the treasure, goes through the northern door, and notices a staircase. Vardis, done with interdimensional prisons, says, “let’s get out of here.” 
A voice, quite unexpectedly, enters his head, “if you use the key, you forfeit the gold.” 
Unla Kee is confused, but Vardis is not. “The thing that runs this tower wants us to stick around. It’s tempting us with gold. Let’s get out of here.”
They shout, “come on, this is your chance.” 
But the bugbear is not swayed, “more treasure,” it growls and heads back into the dungeon. 
Vardis and Unla Kee are teleported in Llorkh, next to Orsik and Thistle. Vardis looks around and notices that it is late afternoon.
“To Aunt Nonnie’s?” 
On their way, they encounter Diego, the para-amour of Fiora. He is curious to know if they have entered the tower. Unla Kee manipulates fates and convinces him that not only did they escape the building, but it was essential that Diego and the Guard investigate the tower. 
As he leaves, Vardis explains the secret of the entrance. 
Aunt Nonnie is surprised to see them and asks if they plan to go back, but Vardis explains that this was a one-time thing. She looks at him, disappointed. 
His friend Orsik snickers and shouts, “are you talking smack about us?” Vardis is annoyed and sighs as he thinks, “at least it’s not the thing.” 
Aunt Nonnie is offended and increases the prices. Vardis glares at Orsik, who apologizes. 
## 1/2 Taraskh on the road to Anuaroch, some distance from Llorkh
Our heroes stumble on a group of cultists of the Dragon that are down on their luck, covered in dirt, with robes tattered, and are trying to be inconspicuous.
Thistle exacts a modest amount of revenge in killing several. He keeps one to ask questions. This cultist is obviously starving and desperate to stay alive. All of the arrogance of the Cult of the Dragon is gone. 
Thistle learns that his nemesis Adimond Kroskas abandoned the Cult just before the defeat at the Well of the Worlds. Adimond, apparently, left to work with a Giant. Thistle is surprised because the Giants loathe the Dragons and vice versa. Adimond always struck him as a true believer, but perhaps he was an opportunist. 
The cultist has no knowledge of where Thistle’s drake is, but it is in the desert if it is anywhere. 
Finally, to the question of what happened to his beloved, the cultist has terrible news. Apparently, the enslaved people either died from mistreatment or were eaten alive by a Dragon as a sacrifice to bring Tiamat into the world. The cultist tries to reassure Thistle that it would have been best if she were eaten alive because, as he puts it, “there can be no greater honor!”. 
Thistle disagrees and kills him. 
## 1/3 Taraskh on the road to Anuaroch, some distance from Llorkh

A group of kobolds attempts to ambush our heroes and is defeated.
The journey to the Oasis of Vuerthyl in Anauroch to meet Azan the caravaneer continues.

# Chapter 07  - Vuerthyl
## On the road 1/4 of Taraskh
The road to Parnast is not well-traveled. Once controlled by the Zhentarim, the Cult of the Dragon took over in the events leading up to the Battle at the Well of the Worlds but then abandoned the route after Tiamat’s entry into the North was blocked. 
The Zhentarim attempted to reassert control over the Black Road through great desert Anauroch, but their hold remains tenuous. 
Our heroes were crossing the Dawn pass, the easiest route through the Greypeak Mountains on the Grayvale Run road, when they stumbled on a massive pair of black bears that saw them as a tasty snack. 
# 
Thistle
To be fair, Thistle didn’t entirely stumble on them. He spotted them and had time to cast “Speak with Animals” so he could ask, “Hello! How goes it in bear land?” 
“HUNGRY!” they both shout. 
Meanwhile, Thistle’s pet bear hugs him, saying, “Mommy!” 
The fairy thinks and then casts a goodberry spell and drops the berries in two separate piles near them. 
The bears rush up, swallow the five berries each, sit down, patting their full bellies, and purr contentedly. 
Fed and happy, Thistle asks them questions about dangers on the road east. The bears can only say that significant threats are coming from behind them. When the ranger tries to ask what, they keep saying danger. 
He knows danger is ahead, and he is determined to find it. 
For the next few hours, Thistle scans the horizon, looking for danger. As the road winds downwards to the hilly dry country, the weather changes, from cold and moist, to cold and dry. Vegetation gets sparser, and the dirt is dry; Thistle recognizes this as the rain shadow of the Greypeak mountains. 
And then, out of the dusty hills, two scorpions emerge, racing towards him and his friends, co-travelers, some guys he picked up and can’t shake off, allies in his holy crusade to find the love of his life and get vengeance, he’s still undecided.
Orsik shouts to him, “we could run?”
“But then they would get the next travelers.” 
“When did we start caring about the next travelers?” 
Thistle grunts and fires an arrow at the lead scorpion. The scorpion charges straight into Vardis, who flanks the creature and lands a hard blow on the monster. Orsik brings it to near-death letting Unla Kee kill it with a magic missile. 
The second scorpion charges at Orsik and misses both with its claws and stinger. Vardis seeing his friend in danger flanks the creature and smashes it again with his sword. 
The scorpion surrounded by Unla Kee, Orsik, and Vardis lashes out at each of them. Orsik is grappled, and Vardis is struck, poisoned, and almost dead forever. All that time drinking booze with Orsik taught his endocrine system to digest poisons of various kinds more efficiently, so he could survive what should have killed him. 
Orsik screams, “VARDIS!” 
Thistle sees what happened and flies a little higher to be out of range of the stinger. 
With Vardis down, the creature grabs the grappled Orsik and flees to the hills. But the beast is killed when Orsik smashes its skull and exults in his vengeance. 
“Let’s track them to their nest!” 
“Really?” 
The trail leads towards the desert, not towards Parnast, and Thistle says, “another day.” 
## Welcome to Parnast 1/7 of Taraskh

The town or village or hamlet of Parnast existed as the last settlement before the Black Road. Control was a source of power for the Zhentarim and later the Cult. But now, it lies abandoned. The village has a few dozen houses and a small square, with buildings that include a tavern, a stable, and a shrine. A small group of locals stares at the adventurers distrustfully. 
Vardis examples the temple and mumbles, “Shrine of Axes!” 
Orsik notes the absence of guards, sighs, and then smiles when he sees the tavern’s name, “The Golden Tankard!” 
But the sealed doors of what look like stables draw everyone’s attention. 
Orsik knocks on the door, and a dangerous roar comes from inside. 
Vardis dismisses the sound, “you knocked over something,” and prepares to enter.
Thistle shouts, “It’s a wyvern!” 
From the temple, an elderly elven woman approaches – “ are you planning to deal with the wyvern?” The group shrugs. “Oh, well, if you’re not planning to deal with it, would you mind not going in there. After they were defeated, you see, the Cult of the dragon left this Wyvern in the, uh, in the stables.” 
The ranger perks up. Vengeance against the Cult is possible! He asks, “Oh, so this is a minion of the cults of the dragon. And you are looking for someone to take care of it? 

“Oh, no, no. We’re looking for you to stop knocking on the door. The problem is that the last person to go in there ended up getting injured and moaning and dying and it was really awkward as we had to listen to them begging for help and healing. The Wyvern took days to kill him.” 
Thistle is now intrigued and asks about the situation, wondering if it would be possible to kill the creature from a distance when the elf explains that the Wyvern is chained to the ground. She laughs at him and explains that the chain is something Wyvern can easily break. And the only reason the Wyvern hasn’t broken the chain is that the villagers keep feeding it.
