She opens a slit into the stables, and everyone takes a turn looking into a wide-open area with dead carcasses picked clean. 
Thistle explains that they could kill it from afar. The elf is aghast and explains that that would put the entire village at risk because it might escape. 
“If you want to go kill it, you must go into the stables; that way, the Wyvern will think of them as food.”
She then realizes that this group is really determined to kill the Wyvern and switches gear, “We like the Wyvern. And it simplifies prison sentences. The threat of the Wyvern is good enough to keep the peace here.” 
The group stares at the creature and decides that this is a monster for another day. 
The elf looks at this strange group and asks, “if it’s work you are looking for, then I have a job. A merchant Azam is stuck in Vuerthyl, bringing back a religious artifact that we sent into the desert during the war. The middle person, Fiora, said she would find us some mercenaries.” 
Thistle glares at her; he knows she is keeping information from him.
“I want to know about the cult.”
“And I want my idol.” 
“I want to know about Adimond Kruskas.” 
“And I want my idol, and what information I may have is only to be exchanged when I have my idol.” 
They glare at each other, and then Thistle backs off grumbling, “she isn’t going to talk until we get her idol.” 
She walks off, and Thistle glares while Orsik moves to the center of the town. He notices the empty shrine and a wheelwright shop to go along with the stables and tavern. 
He takes in the town and gets the distinct impression that this town is used to having a faction controlling the village and the locals profiting from them. They are neutral to all conquerors, as long as they can go about their business and trade. 
The elf stares at the group once more and then sighs and marches up, “the cult of the dragon brought a big flying castle over here to move a lot of treasure.” 
Orsik stares – “that’s the castle that crashlanded near Waterdeep that convinced everyone that there was some deeper, more sinister plot and led to the alliance to defeat the Cult at the Well of the Worlds.” 
“I need a drink,” he says and marches to The Golden Tankard. Outside he notices a yellow tankard over the door. He smirks, steps inside, and sees the gloom, “false advertising,” he thinks. 
And then he notices the quiet as all conversation stops and all eyes turn in his direction. 
The silence is broken by a tall, heavyset man with enormous muttonchop sideburns steps forward and asks, “What can I get you, travelers?”
“We’re on our way to pick up on an idol for your local cleric.” 
“Chandra Stol? She’s been looking for fools to get her idol for weeks. Azam won’t come because of dangers.” 
He pauses, “I have already spoken to you for two minutes, and you haven’t bought anything.” 
“What’s on tap?” 
“A copper for ale and a silver piece for the mead.”
Vardis turns to Orsik, “we could buy the place. A nice turnkey operation?” 
The barkeep’s till a bit fuller puts him in a good mood, and he explains that business has been slow as very few folks have come from the east. He remarks, “many more going east.” 
Orsik and Vardis show him the treasure map, and he sighs and says, “yes, yes! I know that place.”
They get excited. “Yeah, that’s gotta be the silver marches or the spine of the world.” He then thinks more, “or maybe the spine of the world, that’s where the biggest mountains are.” Orsik knows where this is going, “the Icespires are a good mountain range! Or maybe it’s the Nethermountains!”
“So basically somewhere in the North?” 
“Yes!” 
Orsik groans, “here’s a gold piece for your information.”
The keeper’s eyes light up. As he picks up the gold, he says, “well, for that kind of generosity, I’ll give my finest sausage and cabbage. And never let it be said that Ragnar Redtooth isn’t grateful to those who do him a kindness!” 
“Kindness when paid,” snarks Vardis.
“I’m good with that. I’m transactional,” says Orsik. 
They both nod. 
Of course, when Ragnar charges them 5 gold for a dingy, smelly room and another five for the use of sheets, their enthusiasm for the new friend ends. 
“You’re treating us like marks!” 
“When a poor man sees a fool throwing coins around, he’s a fool if he doesn’t try to get some more.”
The next day our heroes head out to Vuerthyl on the Black Road.
Arriving at Vuerthyl 2/1 of Taraskh

The journey east is peculiarly safe and eerily quiet. The trip through the desert is hot and miserable. Massive dunes roll across the landscape in every direction, and an even bigger sky looms overhead.  
Finally, up ahead is the oasis of Vuerthyl, and around it, a motley collection of sun-bleached tents. Looking around, Orsik, who has a nose for these things, finds the tent that passes for a tavern. 
A pair of tieflings, unaffected by the heat, approach him warily as he enters. 
As his eyes adjust to the dim interior, he feels the relief of no more sun or wind, even if it is still hot. 
He looks around and takes in the sounds and sights. A stringed instrument fills the air, and people are hunched over food and conversation.
Just then, a Dragonborn waves to Orsik and guides him to a private table. Orsik asks about Azam, and the waiter nods and says, “he will be here shortly.” 
The Dragonborn waiter delivers water and dates, olive, and bread. 
And then, soon enough, a lean half-elven man with a long, craggy face and dark skin and hair that shows his Rashemi ancestry sits down. 
Vardis notices his impressive mustache.
Unla Kee notices a golden-scaled pseudodragon that lays draped around his shoulders. 
It stirs, blinks with milky irises, and sniffs the air. The creature gives the man a nod and then shifts its wings and falls back to sleep.
The man gestures at the pseudodragon, “Hsing, my patron is most pleased you have come. We have a business to discuss, and hopefully, you are ready for a long journey. I am Azam, and we would like you to deliver a statue to Parnast.” 
Thistle is curious, “what kind of statue?” 
He sighs, “the statue for the Shrine of Axes was made in the Anauroch desert by artisans here. It’s not small, and it’s not light. I don’t know if it’s magical, but I don’t really care. I’ve been paid to transport it securely, and that is what I plan to do.” 
Orsik wonders if he can see it. Azam gives Hsing, the golden scaled pseudodragon, a questioning glance, and the little dragon nods his head in a regal gesture of assent. Azam takes the characters to a worn wagon where a stout-looking half-orc woman carefully wraps the statue in canvas for its journey. 
Azam explains that the statue is of a complicated piece of woodwork by an artisan who has exiled himself to the Anauroch desert. “Never understood why the need to make it this big,” he sighs. “It’s five feet tall, four feet wide, and very heavy.” 
Vardis examines the statue and realizes it is the Triune Queen, the threefold deity Angharradh. Examining the base, he finds an inscription 

*“Unity and diversity bring strength. Be ever vigilant against She Who Was Banished and work together in defending the lands of the Fair Folk from those who would work evil. Celebrate the One and the Three for their collective purpose and individual expressions of life. Through the melding of widely different skills and interests, creativity, life, and artistry are nurtured, and new ideas are discovered.” *
“This is the wisdom of Angharradh,” says Vardis as he feels a calming presence. 
Thistle snarks, “fair folk? I presume it means the fey.” 
“Elves.” 
Vardis looks at him, “Well, the elves are part fey.” 
“The better part.” 
Vardis rolls his eyes, and everyone else stares at Thistle, who feels like he said something awkward. 
“Fine.” 
Azam turns to the group, “My patron Hsing has more work for you if you complete the mission.”
Thistle laughs, “if we are dead, I guess then no more missions?”
“No, if you don’t bring the statue. Although Hsing has been known to forgive.” 
Hsing opens his eyes, “when there is a seed, it must be let to grow before it is killed.” 
Azam nods, “as my patron so wisely said.” 
At this point, Thistle wants answers, “have you seen a Drake named Thule?” 
Azam is taken aback at this, but Hsing smiles and says, “where there is the yellow sky, there is the drake you are looking for.” 

# Chapter 08 - Encounters on the Black Road
## Azam’s Journal 2/1 of Taraskh
The travel to Parnast is finally on its way. I had four wagons with three laden with goods. One carried the food, another the water and a shipment of medicinal herbs, another wagon had a shipment of weapons, and another wagon had the statue of Angharradgh. 
Travel in the desert is slow, hot and full of blistering sun. There are no clouds, no promise of water anywhere. 
It is crucial that we travel well before sunrise, leveraging the light of the moon that makes the dunes look like mountains of diamonds. 
We walk until sunrise where there is a gorgeous spill of pink and gold across the horizon. 
From sunrise until noon, we look for shade. 
At noon we must rest from the baking sun pounding on our skulls like a forge.
When the sun is setting we head out until it is time to eat. 
And then sleep. 
Such is the daily routine of the traveler.
The middleperson’s retainers are a curious bunch. A fairy who seems bent on revenge. Then there is the curious pair of the dwarf Orsik and the elf Vardis. Orsik, Hsing tells me, has done things that have made him unemployable. Vardis’ fellow clerics will not allow him in their temples. And then there is Unla Kee who is convinced that the fate of the world sits on his willingness to take risks. 
Hsing tells me that these are the heroes that he is looking for. 
I shrug. 
They arrived in Vuethel stinking of sweat, covered in sand, sunburned and exhausted. They looked every bit the raggedy adventurer who is down on their luck. I had hoped one of the factions might have sent an agent, but instead we get these independents. 
But last night, the faith Hsing had in them might be justified.
The Thistle, the fairy, proposed that he sleep during the day, and guard during the night. And his decision was wise indeed. 
I had gone to sleep leaving him and his friends to guard the caravan. The nighttime is bitterly cold and dry. Camel spiders, sand scorpions, snakes and large eared hopping mice come out at night to casually murder each other. Sometimes these creatures get into the tents. 
I was awakened by the shouts of the fairy, and the sounds of goblins charging. When I opened my eyes, I could see the moonlight glinting off the steel of the goblins’ weapons. 
A pack of goblins attacked us. They were after our goods and our water. But the one known as Thistle saw them, and before they were able to steal anything, two goblins were dead, and their boss and his foul steed. 
The other two fled from the battle. Thistle argued that they should pursue them to the ends of the earth, but the one known as Vardis objected. The rest of the night passed quietly and our journey continued. 
## Report from Fzoul Slovis 
Report on the state of the Black Road from 3/1 of Taraskh
Increasing reports had come in that the Goblin, Hobgoblin, and Bugbears of the area have been stirring up trouble along the Black Road up to the Greypeaks. 
On our journey from Parnast we saw a toll being set up on the edge of the desert. It is manned by hobgoblins that are decently armed and armored. They were too many for us to fight. I enclose their location and recommend that we deal with them. Our goal was to collect information, not clear the road of threats and so we paid the toll of 15 GP per camel. An expense report has been filed. 
We encountered a caravan near Vuerthyl that had been attacked by Goblins. These goblins lacked the sophistication of the attacks of the earlier groups. 
Note: I have said this many times, but this is further proof that the Zhentarim leadership must take a firmer stand on the Goblin threat to our trade on the black road. Things have gotten worse, and the threat cannot be ignored forever. We will not be taken seriously if Goblins threaten traders. I believe the time to take a more systematic approach of extermination of the Goblin menace must be considered. The piece meal attempts have emboldened them. 
We did find a potential new recruit, Orsik. He is a dwarf who has been accused of doing many things that makes him unemployable as a guard. The Zhentarim may find him useful. I intend to recruit him at the earliest opportunity. 

# Chapter 09 - Ending a civil war
## Wallace 
“What the hell! Bugbears?” 
Wallace stares at the Bugbears on the cliffs firing javelins at the group below. There is no cover. The only way to get to the Bugbears is to climb the twenty or so feet to the top of the cliff. And while you are climbing, you can’t fight back, and it’s hard to defend yourself. 
Meanwhile, everyone cannot react to the ambush, and as he sees blood spurt, he wonders how he got here. 
![image]()

*Orsik had said that we had to hike to find treasure and glory. Apparently, some hobgoblins were going to investigate some magic-users tower in the Marsh of Chelimber. My dad thought it would be something Orsik and the gang would find valuable. I’m not sure he thought I would be going with them, but hey, I gotta make my own mark in the world. And Dad really understood how to survive. We are survivors. *
*As we walked out, the golden pseudo-dragon that belonged to Assam showed up. I think Vardis explained that the pseudo-dragon doesn’t belong to Assam. Assam belongs to the pseudo-dragon. This ancient creature, whose name is Hsing, said that he would like us to investigate the tower. *
*Dad was pleased. Dad figures that the defeat of the Cult of the Dragon and the chaos in the Zhentarim will encourage the Lord’s Alliance to expand and take control of the black road. He wants to make sure they do and wants them to claim this last outpost before Anauroch. I’m sure Dad will tell the Lord’s Alliance that Dad had already sent us on this quest! Or maybe, the Lord’s Alliance already told him to send us out, and Dad was pretending that he heard about it. And – really – how would he hear about it. *
*Orsik explained that this creature Hsing had been giving them jobs for a while, and since we had already planned to visit the tower, saying yes was very easy. For all this talk about how he’s going to be this kick-ass Zhentarim, he didn’t negotiate for any money. *
*
And so we hiked down to the gap between the Graypeak Mountains and the Forgotten Forest. Of course, Orsik never bothered to ask what I knew about the area. The opening is called Ambush Gap, and it’s a prized piece of real estate for gangs. The Zhentarim aren’t stupid. Taking the Black Road through Parnast is costly. And Ambush Gap would allow caravans to avoid paying the fees. The Zhentarim, ever since the War of the World, haven’t been able to reassert their control over the Black Road. So many caravans are trying other routes, and this is one so damn obvious. *
*But Orsik sees me as a young boy who knows nothing. You don’t survive in Parnast without knowing things, people, and what’s going on. *
*And I figured Orsik wasn’t a dumbass. But here we are, javelins from Bugbears, more or less where I knew they would hit us.*
Wallace watched as the party engaged the Bugbears and was happy that the Bugbears weren’t very lucky that day. And worse didn’t count on a flying ranger. 
Wallaces was underwhelmed with Orisk, who through his sword at the bugbears. 

But before long, the battle was over. The looting left them with very little money. 
*What the hell? These guys should have had more money. They are working for someone. I could talk, but Orsik looks like he’s going to get killed, and the rest look like they will also get killed. No need to speak; I just need to survive and find Orsik’s Zhentarim contact to offer the treasure’s location. *
## Feetor
Feetor, the mud mephit, hides in one of the many clumps of trees with hanging vines that stand together throughout the marsh. 
“Which side do I join? The half-ogres are powerful but have left. And the grabby water monster is powerful and will never leave. I just avoided getting killed in an ambush by halfsies today. I need a new plan.” 
Feetor hears a noise that breaks him from his reverie. “Wait, someone is fighting the grabby water monster. I need to go see it. It doesn’t sound like the Half-ogres.” 
He pauses a few seconds, “yep, definitely not them.” 
From his vantage point, he sees a water-filled channel that splits around a small muddy island strewn with a half-dozen dead hobgoblins with their weapons drawn, almost all sprawled halfway in the fetid water of the marsh. 
“Ah yes, the intruders. The Ogreshippers seem confident that the intruders will rid them of the grabby water monster.”
Soon he can hear screaming and shouting that blocks out the usual hum of bugs and insects. “Ah yes, it will be invisible and kill them all.” 
He realizes he needs to get closer as the bright light creates a haze that limits ground visibility to 100 feet. 
When he gets within visual range, he notices a weird glow surrounds the creature, making it visible to its enemies.
“They have the power to unhide the grabby water monster?”
The glowing outline of the monstrous creature allows Feetor to follow the battle carefully. No matter what the monster does, it cannot hide, and if it cannot hide, it can be defeated. 
“A new power! I must join this new power!” 
He steps out of his hiding place and sits on a vine-covered branch of a broad tree, clapping his flabby hands together eagerly and flinging stinky droplets of mud.
He thinks,  “I need an angle with these folks. I think the dumb swamp creature should do….” 
“You killed the evil grabby water monster that has kept me prisoner, all these years.”
“That’s good! You’re tough! The grabby water monster ain’t gonna stay dead—it reforms out of the marsh after a coupl’a day—butt here’s not many who can best it. Most’a we mud-folks serve whoever’s the toughest around; some reckon it’s the grabby water monster, and some think it’s Mama Manyknuckles, but my vote is that’s you lot. You’re best of all!”
He then pauses, “Oh, I forgot my manners in th’excitement! I’m Feetor.”
Feetor stands up, puffs out its chest, and gives an awkward pose. 
Feetor smiles, “it works!” 
At first, Feetor is worried he decided to work with some pretty unintelligent folks. The Dwarf asks him what killed the hobgoblins as if it wasn’t obvious. 
But then the Fairy asks the right questions, “where is the lair of the grabby water monster?” 
Sadly Feetor doesn’t know and suspects it doesn’t have one.
“Probably thinks the whole swamp is its lair. The monster is immortal and ancient; if destroyed, it always reforms in another part of the marsh within a few days. It mostly ignores us mud mephits that “serve” it.”
Feetor notices how the human whispers to the Dwarf, “there’s a reason we don’t announce that we are looting bodies before we loot them, Orsik.” 
## Orsik
Orsik picks through the bodies and finds a map, “Hey, here’s a map to the wizard’s tower.” 
As Orsik and Vardis stare at the map and compare it to that lost map of theirs, the mud mephit coughs. 
“You should ask me about Mama Manyknuckles. You have to defeat her, and then you are the King of the swamp, and I am your loyal follower!” 
Orsik thinks, “more followers.” 
“Can you leave the swamp? You’re bound to it?”
The creature nods its head. 
And then, unprompted, starts talking about Mama Manyknuckles. 
“She is the particularly ugly leader of a band of half-ogres. She doesn’t have nice smooth skin,” and he points to his big muddy belly, “like me. They live somewhere deeper in the marsh. I don’t know the way because I avoid her and her half-ogres as much as possible. They mock and attack me. They call me fat and muddy. Although I don’t understand why they think mud is an insult, I am appropriately sized.” 
Seeing Orsik unconvinced, he continues, “There is a big civil war between the mephits. Some support Mama Manyknucles, and some the grabby water monster. So you kill Mama Manyknuckles, and when you stay and keep killing the grabby water monster, or you leave. Then there is peace as everyone supports the grabby water monster.” 
Vardis asks, “where is the grabby water monster?” 
“It’s gone for a few days, so you are now the most powerful of all the creatures!” 
Vardis taps Orsik, “Three days as the King. Not so bad, eh?” 
Orsik smiles and shouts, “fall in.”
## Thistle

Thistle stares at the map and, thanks to the information that the mephit gives him, can quickly find Mama Manyknuckles’ hideout. 
As the group is walking, he stops. 
“Tsk, tsk.” 
Everyone stares at him.
“The map was wrong. Clearly, the island is this way. You can tell from the water flow and the bugbears. Anyone who followed this map was liable to get lost for hours.” 
Within three hours, they arrive at the very large kidney-shaped island. There are two different encampments, one to the North and the South. 
Feetor shouts, “there is where Mama Manyknuckles rules!”
Thistle watches as Orsik tries to probe Feetor’s suggestions on how to attack, but Feetor is very evasive. 
Finally, Orsik asks, “You being an expert on mud mephits,” 
“You tokenizing me? Not every mud mephit knows everything about every other mud mephit.” Feetor states indignantly. 
Thistle barely restrains a laugh.
“I was more trying to get the other Mud Mephits to align to us!” 
Thistle notices that Feetor has a big smile, “Yes! A token of your power! And I will say that all Mud Mephits must follow me as your number one Mud Mephit!” 
Before Thistle can intervene, Orsik and Vardis confer and Feetor a gold coin. And the mud mephit disappears into the swamp, which burbles and bubbles for a few minutes. 
“They agreed to wait and see who lives and who dies!” 
The group decides to wait until the sun sets before they attack. 
Thistle, who lost his drake to the water weird, conjures him back. 
While they wait for night to fall, Thistle decides to fly up and scout around. As they are near the island’s southern end, he first scouts the southern camp. The south end of the island contains four large, crudely built huts. One of the huts is sturdier than the others and appears to be cobbled together from overlapping pieces of rafts and flat-bottom swamp boats. A fire pit stands at the northwest side of the camp with a heap of logs next to it. Around the fire pit are a pair of ogres. 
Two of the half-ogres in the southern camp notice him as he is flying. The creatures start cursing, “damn bug!” Thistle realizes that they have him confused for some kind of swamp creature, zigs and zags, and doesn’t engage with them after they throw rocks and dirt in his general direction. 
The half-ogres laugh and then go back to doing whatever they are doing. 
Thistle continues to the middle of the island, where between the Razorvine lies a sinkhole. A wide stone foundation frames this sinkhole with a roughly square shape. The edges of the foundation are badly weathered but appear crenelated, like the walls of a battlement, making the stone frame seem less like a foundation and more like the top of a large keep or tower. 
“Found it,” he thinks. He knows this is the wizard tower. Looking a little bit closer, he notices that the floor of this sinkhole is ancient but well-laid flagstone covered with a thin sheen of swamp water. The northwest corner of the sinkhole contains a badly weathered statue. 
As he investigates further, Thistle notices something unexpected, “A large table with filthy furs and a bed?” Someone must live here, he muses. Then he sees two wide ramps made of logs and dirt that lead into the sinkhole. 
“Someone lives here,” he thinks. 
Unla Kee 
*Should have attacked during the day, bigger risk, bigger reward. I saw portents and signs that indicated that daylight was a better choice. *
As the group quietly moves towards the island from their hideout, he notices burbling. “The mephits are all talking and taking bets on who will be the marsh’s new king.” 
Feetor, who has figured out Unla Kee, approaches the Halfling, “you can get into the action if you want to, but you need to have special mud.” 
Unla Kee is crestfallen until Feetor says, “I am happy to front you some.” 

# Chapter 10 - The SEER

## Group

Our heroes quietly advance on the half-ogres sitting around the fire with cat-like tread, catching them unawares. 
Vardis opens with a guiding bolt that streaks through the night sky and plunges into the half-ogres body. It is visibly hurt. 
Unla Kee prepares his spell and thinks, “so the Freetor let me bet we would kill the half-ogres. The way I see it, if I win, I get money. If I am dead,  it doesn’t matter.”
“For the 50!” he mutters.     
And he charges up to the ogres and casts Rimes Binding Ice on the pair. Unla Kee manipulates the fates just a little, and the first is killed. The second is too groggy from being woken up to react in time. The ice wounds him and traps him. He is in shock and immobile. 

Orsik throws a handaxe. And Vardis kills the remaining ogre with a single arrow. 
“That’s how it’s done, Wallace.” 
Wallace, who had been surprised, smiles sheepishly. He then asks – “He’s good. I’m impressed. Do you guys hang out a lot? You went to school together?” 
The dwarf is annoyed, “He’s my best friend.” 
“Your best friend?” says an obviously hurt Wallace. 
“I met you like one week ago.” 
Orsik stares at Wallace. Wallace stares at Orsik in awe. 
Meanwhile, Thistle decides to explore the Razervine while the rest of the crew searches the corpses and the huts around the fire. 
Thistle flies into the bushes and into a swarm of poisonous snakes on the branches. The snakes drop on his body, biting him with their fangs and injecting their poison. Thistle barely escapes with his life.
He remembers what Unla Kee said – “I think we should stick together.”
And his flippant response, “I am a scout. I scout.” 
He beats a hasty retreat and thinks, “I scouted!” 
Meanwhile, Orsik, Unla Kee, Vardis, and Wallace search the bodies. 
In one of the huts is a dwarf. Orsik stares, “my kinsman.” Now it turns out that the word kinsman among the dwarves is only used among members of the same clan. But Orsik is a modern dwarf and rejects the clannish exclusionary behavior of his kind. And takes a more pan-dwarven view of the world, that every dwarf is a kinsman. Or it might be that Orsik’s relationship with Vardis and his actions got him kicked out from his clan, and no other self-respecting clan wants him.
Orsik shows the golden beard clips he removed from the dead to Thistle, “look what we found. What did you find?” 
“Snakes.” And then he proceeds to munch some goodberries. 
At this point, the group pauses and discusses what to do next. Straight to the north is the sinkhole, which is probably where  Mama Manyknuckles lives. To the northwest is a bunch of Razervine with poisonous snakes. To the northeast is also Razervine, a beach that will allow the group to reach the northern camp. 
The group stealthily arrived at the northern island near an open wooden pavilion stained with gore. They see six large ramshackle wooden hunts huts past the gazebo, surrounded by a massive fire pit.  
Orsik mutters, “perfect.”
Thistle notices that the camp could accommodate 12 giants, but all he can see are 2 half-ogres. Thistle tries to see if he can use his tracking skills to determine how many creatures are there but fails. The tracks are unclear. 
“Let’s advance quietly.” 
Wallace is not paying attention as the rest of the group surprises the two creatures. 
Thistle strikes with his bow and sends Thuul up ahead.  
Orsik is too far for his handaxes and dashes.
Vardis’s strike is accurate, and one of the two half-ogre dies.  
The other one is in shock and slow to react but screams, “Mama Mia!” 
Wallace misses and thinks, “I gotta step up my game.” 
Orsik hurls his handaxes. Vardis strikes again. 
Unla Kee strikes with a magic missile. Thuul, who has reached the half-ogre, smashes him at Thistle’s command. 
And the creature dies. 
## Mama Manyknuckles
*Those knuckleheads. *
She quickly signals to her two guards to quietly approach the north ramp. 
*They have no idea what they are about to get into.*
She whispers to her mud mephits, but she doesn’t hear them respond. 
“Damn annoying creatures!” 
She overhears them talking about taking a breather, “maybe they will go away,” she hopes. 
But then she hears them trying to surprise her by flanking her from the south. 
She whispers to her half-ogres, “quickly get into position for an ambush.” 
The ambush is a partial success. The invaders are surprised and they are frightened. But their will is too strong, and Mama Mia can only kill one with her death gaze. 
Mama Mia, or Mama Manyknuckles, is a hag, and she reveals herself as such. 
Orsik is killed when he doesn’t look away in time, and Mama Mia smiles her devilishly grin. But that doesn’t last long. As the rest of the group overwhelms her ogres and her beloved Belg. 
She tries to flee, but this time there is no escape. Her last thoughts are, “Damn you, Bad Fruul. Ever since my gang signed up with you, I knew this was inevitable.” 
# Unla Kee
*I gambled and won, and the world continued for another day. *
He notices Feetor, “New king of the swamp!” 
Feetor hands him his winnings. 
“Where’s my gold?”
Orsik looks at Unla – “Where’s the gold?” 
Feetor is confused, “this is special mud! It makes you look prettier and heals you from bad wounds!” 
“We have altered the deal, Mephit,” says Vardis in a deep raspy voice. “Give us the gold!” 
Unla thinks it has come time to teach the Mephits who is boss. And as he is about to attack, he notices that the swamp is bubbling. 
He overhears Thistle shouting, “we are not fighting these Mud Mephits.” 
Feetor trying to defuse the situation gives Unla a hug and says, “you are an honorary Mud Mephit!” 
The dwarf snickers, “Congratulations, we are now part of the tribe.” 
“And because you are a tribe member, here’s a stone you can use to ask for our help!” 
The dwarf whispers to Wallace, “Good lesson. Never give your money to a mud mephit.” He pauses, “but you knew that?” 
When Wallace explains to Unla Kee that he should never give money to a mud mephit, Unla decides he’s had enough. 
“I’ll make a new deal. How about we bet this mud that I can kill you in a duel?”
Feetor is impressed and frightened, “I will get your money, big boss! But can you say I am #2?” 
Unla sighs, “sure.” 
Feetor shouts, “Today is a great day! The civil war has ended. We are now all followers of Unla Kee!” 
But something odd happens. The Mud Mephits are not convinced that Unla Kee is the strongest. And pretty soon, there is bubbling and squabbling, and the civil war erupts again as mud mephits argue over whether that Orsik is the strongest because Wallace is already following him. Others say that Vardis is the clear leader because of his grace. Some are fans of Thistle and see his command of the Drake as proof of his leadership skills. 

Regardless the battle continues, and soon, the place is quiet again.
Feetor shrugs, “oh well.” 
## Orsik and Wallace
Orsik and Wallace explore the sinkhole and realize it is the roof of a wizard’s tower. On it, they find a statue. On Mama Mia, they find a curious stone pendant with a jade inlay of Stein, the stone rune. 
Exhausted, they take a rest. In the morning, they are awakened by the whistling noise announcing the arrival of a golden pseudodragon with milky-white eyes. 
“Greetings and congratulations.
“That again,” mumbles Orsik. 
Wallace turns to Orsik, “can I ask a question?” 
“Sure.” 
“What if I ask the wrong one?”
“I trust you know about theses
 situation.” 
Hsing whistles, “I just teleported here. I am the emissary of the SEER. You’re supposed to be paying attention to me.” 
Orsik stares at the creature, “it’s always about you.”
“Yes. But enough about me and my relationship with the Great SEER!” 
“The SEER believes you have found a site long sought, the tower of the Wizard of the Crag. She wishes to encourage you to explore the tower. She is interested in the repository of Lore he is rumored to have had. If you retrieve these manuscripts on ancient forms of magic, we shall pay you handsomely.”
Orsik stares at the blind creature, “Killing it would be awesome! We could get some serious cred with the Zhentarim.” 
Wallace likes the idea but urges caution, “The SEER is the top Spymaster of the Lord’s Alliance. And if she can find us here, she can find us anywhere. Not sure we will survive to collect the reward.” 
And with that, Orsik lets Thistle question Hsing, and they learn that the Wizard was a master of at least the elemental powers of water and earth and that he worked with all of the elements. 
They also learned that he was a devotee of both Mystara and Oghma. 
And last, they learn what the SEER hopes to get from this tower. Apparently, she needs any kind of help to deal with Bad Fruul, whose emergence as a local power thwarted the Zhentarim. 
And with that, he turns to them and says – “I can’t do everything for you. I am behind on my naps as is. This is the place, your job to enter. Coming out with just one miserable spellbook is not acceptable. Use the pendant that you found. I hear SEER calling me,” and he apparently teleports away.

# Chapter 11 - Into the Tower

The group stands on the island in what was Mama Mia’s bedroom. 
Looking around, they figure out from the crenelated battlements that this must have been the roof of the Wizard’s tower. A tower that has sunk into the swamp.
Taking a closer look at Mama Mia’s bed, they realize it’s a stone table. And across the stone table in the northeast corner is a statue. 
Wallace walks up to the statue. He looks to Orsik for guidance. Orsik waves his hand, and the young lad starts to clean it.
Pretty soon, it’s obviously a gargoyle, and this time, just a statue. 
The young lad from Parnast discovers that it has an arm that can be rotated.
Unla, who always feels lucky, steps forward and moves the arm.
The table tries to move. Thistle examines the stone and realizes Mama Mia’s pendant can be used as a key. 
Unla pulls again, and the table opens, revealing a staircase going down. 
Wallace is excited, “My first tower,” and runs down. 
The stairs end at a landing with a poem and symbols 
*Be not faithless
Magic and Knowledge divine
Not in power or sphere presented
But in names Ascending*
And the symbols are 
Thistle and Unla quickly determine that they must be put in order and that the symbols represent various gods of the realms. The human hand, finger pointing upward, is Azuth. The single-lit candle is Deneir. And Oghma is a simple blank scroll. 
Unfortunately, Vardis doesn’t recognize the star of Mystara, a god of wizards. Neither does Unla Kee, but then again, he has a dim view of the gods as he knows that he is why the world continues. 
Regardless, the failure to recognize Mystara causes an old trap to spring, and a boulder collides with our heroes in front of the wall. 
After gathering themselves up, Thistle takes on the task of guessing again because he can fly away from a boulder. 
But, as they say in the Fey, he picked a dandelion, and the door opened. 
And with that, the doors open, revealing a room 30 feet deep and 25 - feet wide. Archways to the left and right provide a view into the rooms beyond. Immediately facing the entrance, a prismatic-hued will-o’-wisp hovers over a short pillar. Atop the pillar rests an offering bowl, and behind it, a fresco depicting a blue, seven-pointed star. The walls are covered in murals depicting a woman performing feats of magical prowess. 
At this point, the group figures out that this must be Mystara. The others give some sideways glances at Vardis and Unla, but they disregard them. 
Vardis approaches the offering bowl, sees a tourmaline, and doesn’t steal it. Unla leaves it, explaining he’s a gambler, not a thief. 
The group heads through the north passageway. As they walk, they take notice of their surroundings. The areas are well lit with globes of spun glass that glow with bright, magical light. The floors are stone, bear a thick layer of dust, and seem to have formed mounds. The air they breathe is chilly and, oddly, stale and dry for a castle in a swamp. 
Thistle notices that there are no insects or spiders, “weird.” 
The archway north leads to a pillared hall bathed in a dancing cascading rainbow light. 
Thistle examines the arches and then looks up and concludes this place was once open to the elements, but earth and mud have filled the openings. 
He sighs. And then takes in the peculiar light sources. They are walls of scintillating light anchored by pillars in the center of the room. 
And they block his view of the entire room. He’s about to go down the hall when he notices a pair of altars. 
Vardis, Thistle, Unla Kee, and Orsik examine the first altar made of jasper with an offering bowl that resembles a polished geode. 
Vardis reads an engraving, “Grumbar’s oath unchanging and true. Earth remains strong, protecting all.” 
The Fey remembers an old story from his uncle and says, “He’s the boss of elementals. His symbol represents stability and resistance to change and is the guardian of secret treasures. He is said to be fond of offerings of sardonyx and jasper, and often statues of him are made of those with rubies as the eyes.” 
Everyone stares at Thistle.
“My uncle traveled a lot and told me many stories.” 

Thistle shouts, “okay, let’s put some dirt on the altar!” 
Vardis suggests they use their mud mephit. 
And Orsik turns to Wallace, “back away. They are planning to make an offering of dirt. We need to be out of the blast radius.” 
As Thistle drops the dirt in the geode, he mutters, “it is a god of the earth.” 
And suddenly, he feels a blessing course through his bones that makes him resistant to being hurt by the earth. 
Thistle explains that to his colleagues. Orsik hits the ground and thinks, “I guess it’s only the people making an offering.” 
Unla seeing what happened, steps forward to the next altar. It resembles a wave crashing and appears made from quartz crystal with varying shades of green and blue with flecks of white resembling crashing surf. A deep valley between the waves serves as the altar’s offering bowl. 
He looks for an engraving and finds one – 
*Istishia’s triumph to confirm
Water rushes out to cover all*
Without hesitation, he drops some water and knows that water can not harm him. 
Vardis walks past the shimmering walls to the next altar, determined to get the blessing. The altar is shaped like a 4-foot-high tornado; the eye of the funnel cloud serves as the offering bowl. Streaks of lightning that are carved into the stone clouds are engraved with the words:

*Akadi’s healing kiss once more to feel
Air bursts free in no one’s thrall*
The cleric turns to Thistle, who once again remembers his lessons about the Gods of Faerun. 
“Istishia *(Is-TISH-ee-ah) *is more abstract than some of the better-known deities of water, such as Umberlee, Eldath, and Valkur. He represents water, but not any specific formation or body of water. He furnishes a crucial resource but apparently cares not how it is used. He prizes offerings of water-hued fine fabrics that ripple like waves, gemstones that echo the color of water, fine inks or dyes, or books or papers on which are written unsolvable mathematical problems.”
Prompted by Thistle, Vardis burns incense and feels a surge of power. He is blessed too. 

Orsik looks at him and smirks, “isn’t your willingness to offer incense to other gods why you were exiled?” 
“Pragmatic.” 
Orsik pushes everyone aside and mumbles, “I gotta get me some resistance.”
He steps in front of the altar and examines it. The altar is a 4-foot high flame made of crystals of varying shades of red and yellow. A brazier before the altar serves as the altar’s offering bowl. Engraved are the words:

*Kossuth’s renewing purification performed
Fire erupts, dancing death for all*
Vardis wanting to help his friend offers a lit candle. 
Thistle flies out of the room, muttering, “I don’t want to be here for the explosion.” 

But the candle and torch they burn seem to have no effect.
Unla stares at the inscription and says, “maybe I should set myself on fire and put myself in the offering.” 
“Sounds risky,” says Vardis. 
Unla considers it for a moment and decides against it. 
After burning some rope, casting produce flame also fails to produce an effect. Orsik is tempted to smash the altar but reconsiders.
Seeing nothing else to be done, Thistle leads the group through a door to the south. It is a guest room. As he takes in the sights,  he notices the walls are paneled in wood. The room has three large piles of rocks with statues scattered about. The center of the room has a layer of fine gravel. There is a door in the wall opposite. The eyes of the figures open, and a sound like grinding rocks comes forth from the open mouth of the largest statue.
 Unla casts comprehend languages and can translate the grinding words – “What is your business here?” 
Orsik realizes that they can’t speak, so he makes a hand gesture. 
The gargoyle smiles, “Yes, I agree with you. His arrogance bound us to protect his tower.” 
Orsik tries another hand gesture, and the gargoyle stares back. 
On the other hand, Wallace is excited to go through his first secret door and marches into one in the southeast part of the room. 
It’s not an actual secret door, as the floor has a clear sign of wear from the door and the creatures going to and from it. 
The secret door leads to another secret door. 
“Leave it for later,” shouts Orsik.
Orsik then turns to the door going south and tries to open it. 
When he fails, he gestures to the gargoyle, saying, “well, just go through the wall.” 
Orsik stares and realizes that the gargoyle did not need a key to this door. 
Thistle can find and remove a trap, but the door is too tightly locked to be opened without the tools.
Everyone stares at Orsik, who mutters, “I am offended that you think I would have thieves’ tools.” 
So the group heads back the other way through the hall with the shimmering walls and past the shrine of Mystara. 
Thistle once again is in the lead. As he passes the archway, he sees a fresco depicting a human hand, index finger pointed up. A bearded, old human male statue stands on the southern wall, holding a topaz-capped staff lovingly in both hands.
Orsik recognizes him as Azuth and is not entirely happy with this god’s unwillingness to part with his blessings. 
He notices a staff one and half times Azuth’s height topped by a sizeable cabochon-cut topaz.
Vardis shouts, “I strayed from my God’s teachings, but I am not looting.” 
Thistle once again takes the lead into the next room. The walls of what the Fey assumes is a shrine are covered in all types of scripts and glyphs, some from languages long dead. The statue is of a balding man clad in voluminous robes, covered in glyphs, standing beside a desk bearing a quill and a beautiful ink pot. His arms cradle several scrolls.
“Deneir,” shouts Thistle.
“Your uncle?” 
“No, I recognized the symbol from earlier.” 
Vardis once again announces, “no looting of places of worship.” 
And as everyone leaves the room, a distinct sound of something is heard behind them.
Vardis turns and sees a scroll tube. He opens it, and it is a scroll of tongues. 
“I’ll take that,” says Unla. 
Just then, Orsik and Wallace enter the next room. The walls of this room show various ways of preserving knowledge, including carving stone tablets, preparing paper, and binding books. Also shown are people of different races and genders creating knowledge by experimentation or spreading knowledge.

Orsik mumbles, “book people.” He scans for a statue but finds a desk with a blank, open tome, an inkstand with a quill, and a large diamond paperweight.
“Great, just when it’s my turn, I don’t get a statue but a desk. Who needs a desk?” Orsik thinks. 
As he is about to take the diamond paperweight, a voice is heard – “All should surrender knowledge so it may be shared.”
And then the quill rises up next to the book. 
Vardis, who is outside, shouts, “They want us to write some knowledge in the book.” 
Wallace writes – “My name is Wallace.” 
Orsik can’t resist and writes – “Dwarves are awesome.” Although he does wonder if that constitutes knowledge as it is a well-known and established fact. 
Vardis writes – “berries are good.” 
Thistle writes – “spring’s first flower!” 
Unla steps forward and writes for some time. When queried, he says, “I know how the world is going to end.” 
Everyone tries to read his writing, but it is written in a cipher only known to Unla Kee. The cipher is called “Bad Hand Writing.” 
A voice suddenly shouts, “The gift of knowledge freely given is blessed. And yes, Vardis, I do enjoy a good berry. And Unla Kee, that’s not how the world ends.” 
Orsik snickers, “You just destroyed this guy’s whole raison d’etre!” 
“I didn’t say it was all wrong. Unla just got some details wrong. On the,” and the voice whispers something to Unla Kee alone. 
Vardis picks up the scroll that materializes, smiles, and says – “lesser restoration.” 
And with that, the group heads down the stairs through the secret door in the gargoyle room. 

Wallace takes the position of honor and goes down first. He arrives at a landing and a moldy door. He pushes the door open. 
He looks around the open area with a few chairs and shelves that may have had some valuable objects. Still, now the only thing that remains is rusted pieces of metal. Wallace touches one, and it turns to dust. 
Everyone piles in and heads to a scintillating wall of shades of orange and red. The wall radiates heat.
Unla notices an inscription – “Sing praises or be tested.” He clears his throat and is about to sing some old show tunes he loved in his youth. 
Thistle stops him and sings a Fey song of love. 
Orsik scowls, “out of tune screeching. Dwarven music is what we want. That’s not going to work.” 
The ranger is irritated and marches through and is burned. 
Orsik smirks.
Thistle glares and then looks around. He is in a 20’ room that has a brass brazier in each corner and a circle of arcane symbols inscribed in the center of the room. 

On either side of the walls of the passageway he entered are shelves. Thistle examines them and sees some fabrics that he touches. Much like the rusted metal, these too turn to dust. 
The eastern wall is pierced by a wall of white and grey light that smells of incense and ozone and occasionally flashes silver with lightning. 
He looks around for an inscription and finds one – “*Songs of our ally may be treated gently.*”
Unla suggests instead of singing just complimenting, “perhaps the singing is a metaphor?” 
Thistle dismisses that thought. He sees the swirling effects and thinks, “water.” And sings the inscription for the water goddess Istishia from her altar. 
He steps through the wall and is zapped. 
Thistle swallows some berries.
Orsik turns to Vardis, “I really hope there is a way to disable the traps.” 
Vardis says, “Yeah, otherwise we must go through all those damaging walls.” 

Thistle shouts, “maybe the folks who are just watching may want to look for some useful information?” 
Wallace takes a cue and starts looking, and finds an inscription. He turns to Orsik and whispers the information. Orsik shouts, “Look what I found!” He points to a note in the far wall – 

Vardis shouts, “I got it! Gods of Fire and Air are allies as the gods of Earth and Water. The gods of Water and Fire are enemies, as are the gods of Earth and Air.” 
Thistle can get through the walls without getting hurt by singing the fitting praises. Wallace, Orsik, Unla, and Vardis also follow, careful to repeat the songs Thistle got right. After getting through the remaining two rooms and their scintillating walls, they arrive in a room covered in mold. 
Unla Kee notices a foot-long piece of ivory wood. It draws his attention because it is the only thing that has not crumbled into dust. He picks it up and smiles, “a wand of webs! I knew it was magical!” 
Vardis shouts, “we’ll decide which spell caster can use it later!” 
Thistle, who is also a spell caster, agrees. 
Orsik looks at the door and is about to open the door when Thistle says, “wait, let me check for traps.” 
“none.” 
Thistle grumbles because he is sure there would have been a trap. This is such a prominent place to put one. “Probably thought invaders would be dead by now.” 
Unla Kee quickly looks inside the room and says, “a laboratory.” He points to the workbenches and supplies that are, again, too old to be helpful. He then notices the tables are made of stone, “Ah, what a good design choice, and very expensive, furniture made of stone to resist explosions.” 
Not all is destroyed, a set of jewelers’ tools are still in good shape, and he pockets them. 
But the prize, in Unla Kee’s eye, is the spellbook. He rushes to the table and grumbles about the height. He reaches over and grabs all three books. He feels the magic of glyphs being triggered and thinks, “oops.” 
The door closes, is locked, and two magmin appear. 
While that was happening, Wallace found several gems and coins totaling 200 GP. 
The last thing Unla Kee thinks about is – “so who is Bad Fuul? Why does the SEER think there may be books in an ancient tower to help them? And there is no way the SEER is getting this spell book.”

# Chapter 12 - Securing the books, and freeing Elquen 
## Orsik 3rd day of the 3rd Tenday of Tarskh
*I want to kill that Pseudo-dragon. *
Wallace can see the look in his eyes and turns to him, “he’s a member of the Lord’s Alliance and works for the SEER.” 
“So?”
“Well, the SEER can teleport this creature within 5 feet of us. I suppose it can teleport something more dangerous?” 
“Yeah, but think about how the Zhentarim might reward us.”
“Orsik, I’ve lived in Parnast my whole life, and I know a thing or two about the Zhentarim. They don’t like wars. They don’t like freelancers. And they are just as likely to hand you over to the Lord’s Alliance as a reward. Heck, they might offer to torture us on behalf of the Lord’s Alliance in exchange for something the Zhentarim want and then kill us.” 
Orsik grumbles.
“I mean, why not ask him for something? Like about the map?”
“Be in his debt? Orsik is no creature’s debt!” 
Vardis rolls his eyes. 

Unla Kee
Unla Kee coughs. 
Orsik stares at him.
“If you are all done debating your plans, can we deal with the magmins in front of us?” 
The Wizard is about to cast a magic missile when Orsik shouts, “a magic missile for these puny creatures?” 
The dwarf watched in stunned amusement as Unla ponders the situation for a moment and then swings wildly with his shortsword. The magmin retaliates and knocks the gnome out. 
Orsik stares and sighs, “I guess maybe the magic missile would have been the better choice.” 
But Unla can manipulate the fates, and the killing blow is not a killing blow. 
The battle rages for a few more moments, and then the puny creatures die. 
Unla muses, “probably worked better when the wizard could show up and kill the trapped thieves.” 

Just then, Thistle notices a secret door. Everyone struggles to open it, and when they are about to give up hope, he casually walks over to a stone and presses it. The door swings open. 
## Vardis

“I’ll go in!” 
He marches into what was obviously a treasure room. The words “The Wizard of the Crag’s Treasure Room” are inscribed on the back wall quite ostentatiously. 
Vardis stares and sees nothing of value.
There is a ledger, and he picks it up. 
Turns out that the Wizard of the Crag made real estate investments that didn’t quite pan out, construction projects that had overruns, etc. 
He also discovers that there was apparently some duel with Taskor that was supposed to help with the debts. 

## The Wight 
Oh dear me, more people are arriving. And they will want powerful magical artifacts that I will have to deny. And then more people will die. 
The wight begins to reminisce. 
“Elquen, The Wizard of the Crag is dead!” shouted the guard.
“How?” 
“Taskor!” 
“What?”
“Taskor killed him!” 
“What?” 
His manservant rolled his eyes, “Elquen, it’s not that complicated. The Wizard of the Crag died in a duel with Taskor and no longer has the money to maintain the castle.” 
“The water elementals!” 
“What?” 
“If I don’t control them, they’ll destroy everything.” 
And then he remembers the wild magic surge that turned him into a wight and trapped the two guards and his manservants. 

He tried to help them, and they turned into Skeletons.
Returning from his reverie, he mumbles. “never again.”
*And of course, they want the books. *
“I am afraid the disastrous effects of magic mean there will be no removal of books from here, and more importantly, there will be no use of magic. It is my curse and burden to protect the world from this.” He waves his hand at the library, “magic.”
## Unla Kee 
“Orsik, are you sure?” 
“Yes, I am.” 
Unla Kee, who had not yet entered the library, makes his entrance, and Orsik declares, “But what if the Wizard of the Crag lives?” 
Elquen stares at Unla for a moment, and Unla manipulates the fates 
“Your arrogance,” he shouts.
“Yes?” 
“It’s so good to see you are alive!”
“Can I have my library?”
“Why yes!” 
Elquen then stares at the gnome, “there is the small matter of back pay.” 
“How much?”
“You never could hold onto money, so how about you give me 150 GP for walking-around money?”
Unla stares at his allies and nods, “here you go!” 
“And best of luck, sir!” And with that, Elquen walks out through the hallways to the laboratory. 
Vardis opines, “well, that’s interesting. We released a wight into the world.” 
“Probably won’t hurt anyone,” opines Orsik.
“Yep!” 
Group
The group collects all of the books from the shelves. Realizing that they don’t have enough space to carry them, they use one of the bookshelves as a sled and pile them on it. 
Once on the surface of the island, the group turns to Thistle.
*
My mad mapping skills are fantastic! *He thinks. 
Thistle successfully leads the group to the same point on the map where they found the dead hobgoblin within a few hundred feet of the end of the Marsh of Chelimber.
“We’re done!” 
Except they weren’t. Turns out that the Water Weird rematerialized, and this time it had a bunch of Mud Mephits as allies. 
The battle was difficult, but Vardis’ quick thinking to call in the group’s mephit allies turned the tide. The Mud Mephits once again began their civil war and fought each other to stand still. The only issue was that the heroes had to avoid the muddy explosion associated with a mud mephit dying for a few moments. 
All that remained was to head back to Parnast and claim their prize. 

# Chapter 13 - Back to Parnast - 03-04-Taraksh
## Note to the SEER 
Did I mention how annoying it was to keep track of them? Seriously. All the agents in the area are tasked to track this group of annoying individuals. 
I know you will say they are the most promising group we have seen in these parts. And that the Parnast must not fall to the Zhentarim. And that there is a tumult brewing in the North. 
And everyone must do their part, and my part is to ensure Parnast is part of the Lord’s Alliance and that Parnast does not fall to the Giants. The strategic location as the last town before the Black Road is too important. 
Now, I must communicate some terrible news. Orsik is a Zhentarim. I know you were hoping to secure him for the Lord’s Alliance, but was there any noble anywhere that would want him on our side? 
The rest are free agents.  
## The Free Agents and the Zhentarim Agent
Meanwhile, just past the Marsh of Chelimber, a group of adventurers carrying a full bookcase is trying to decide how best to return to their destination. 
“Wallace, what do you think?” shouts Orsik.
Wallace is flustered. He wasn’t expecting Orsik to seek his counsel. 
“Well, we have three ways we go to Parnast. We could go through the Grey Peak Mountains. We could go through the Forbidden Forest, or we can go through Ambush Gap.”
“That’s the choices you give us? Scale the tall mountains covered in snow, dare the dangerous forest, or go through a place known as Ambush Gap.” 
Everyone stares at Wallace, and he shouts, “Ambush Gap.” 
The journey to Ambush Gap is mostly uneventful except in the evening when some bugbears and goblins appear looking well-fed, armed, and ready to fight. 
The lead bugbear shouts, “This is Bad Fruul’s territory, and you must pay a fee to continue unmolested.” 
The second in command turns to the first, “I don’t think Bad Fruul said – unmolested.” 
“We are not illiterates.” 
Orsik smirks, “how much?”
“Half of what you have!”
Orsik tries to bluff the Bugbear – “Very well, we have 10 gold. We can give you five.” 
The bluff is not that well done, but Unla manipulates the fates, and the gang leader sighs and is about to say yes.
At that point, the goblins are outraged and start screaming at the Bugbear. The Bugbears raise their voices in return, but after a while, they seem defeated.
“The group decided that wasn’t enough. We noticed you have fancy armor. We want some of it.” 
“But we had a deal,” exclaims Thistle. 
“Yeah, and we thought you had more than 10 gold.”
Unla Kee jumps in, “let’s be honest, you know how little gold we had.” 
The two Bugbears argue, pointing at the Goblins and then back to the North. 
Finally, the second in command says, “we can’t show up with 5 gold. Can you throw us a bone, like a sword?” 
Unla Kee whispers to Orsik, “try to convince them the marked cards are worth a lot!” 
But Orsik’s smooth tongue fails him, and the Bugbears become angry. 
“All right, all right. We were just joking about the cards.”
The group huddles, and soon Unla Kee hands over the short sword he used on the Magmin and Orsik hands over the deck of cards. 
The Bugbear shouts, “two swords! One for the deal and another for tricking us!” 
Just then, the goblins ask for food, and Thistle offers them goodberries. The feeling of fullness makes the Goblins much less interested in this ambush. 
With the Goblins less interested, the Chief declares, “A sword, a deck of cards makes for a worthy haul. And you have paid appropriate tribute to Bad Fuul!” 
“Where is this Bad Fuul? We’d like to meet him!” 
Thistle jumps in, “I want to commend you to your boss on how well the way to the pass was guarded!” 
The Bugbear scratches its chin. “Look, we don’t actually meet Bad Fuul. Our Chief meets some other chief who then meets Bad Fuul.” 
## Ambush Gap 
Ambush Gap is a particular part of the badlands at the foothill of the Greypeak Mountains and to the North of the Forgotten Forest. From hundreds of feet above, the surface looks like that ground has been tiled. The areas between the tiles are 5-10’ wide winding trails formed from the rain event that is said to have created the Marsh of Chelimber. The trails criss-cross each other and have tall 20’ stone walls on either side. 
The tiles’ surfaces vary in shape and size, offering plenty of hiding places on them behind trees, bushes, and crannies. And on one of those tiles is Grik, the Goblin chief. 
The Goblin chief stares at his gang. They look hungry, but not too hungry. And they definitely look like they are ready to kill some foolish travelers. 
“Bad Fuul has given specific orders; we are to ambush anyone who comes through this pass! Bad Fuul has promised that anyone who comes up the pass will be weakened by releasing poison!” 
The goblins cheer at this announcement and then get back to waiting. 
Chief Grik can hear the adventurers coming up the pass, “they are coughing and wheezing!”
As they arrive, he shouts, “This pass is under the control of Bad Fuul!” 
A fey indignantly shouts, “we already paid to pass through here!” 
Chief Grik looks at him and thinks, “ugh, a fairy.” Then assuming a commanding position, shouts, “you will hand over all of your equipment and all of your treasure and leave naked, or your bodies will be stripped naked and left here to be eaten!” He takes a dramatic pause and shouts, “thus spake Bad Fuul!” 
The fairy turns to the dwarf, “definitely not the deal we got from the other goblins.” 
“So!”
Group. 
Thistle turns to Orsik and says, “I’m gonna get me some goblins.”
Three goblins standing on a tile look down into the trail and target Wallace. One hits, and the other accuses Wallace of being short. 
“Damn, they have good aim,” thinks the fighter. Is the target shout, “die little creature!” and miss. 
Wallace stares back and wonders, “who are you calling little?”

Wallace then charges up to the sheer cliffs and kills a goblin. 
 Thistle’s drake charges up the cliff where Chief Grik is standing and snaps the air. Thistle’s arrow also misses everything. A goblin standing before Chief Grik strikes the drake, but his sword does not penetrate the armor. 
Just then, some wolves charge. They were hiding a little bit further down one of the trails. They snap at the cleric and miss but penetrate Orsik’s armor. 
Vardis ignores them and climbs up to one of the tiles and uses burning hands, killing two of Bad Fuul’s gang, and badly wounding the other, who then flees. 
Unla Kee surveys the situation, compares the position of the sun to a piece of the updated prophecy that he was given, concludes that the end of the world isn’t happening yet, and unleashes binding ice, killing two wolves, binding one wolf, and the Chief and his henchman on the tile. Unfortunately, it also hits Thistle’s drake. 
Orsik seeing the frozen wolf, kills it and then charges up the walls to the tile top to attack the Chief. 
The two goblins on the tile that Wallace climbed up to flee. The first immediately, the second after seeing his scimitar’s ineffectiveness. 
Thistle and the Drake attack the Chief, but the chief grabs the last remaining goblin alive next to him and interposes the poor creature between the arrow and the acid damage. The goblin then attacks the drake and knicks it. 
Vardis uses his bow and kills a goblin who is on the surface of another tile. 
Chief Grik wisely avoids Unla Kee’s spells, only to be killed by Orsik. 
With all the goblins dead, the group searches for treasure. On chief Grik, they find a note and a gold stylus. They almost ignore the letter but read it and discover its orders from Bad Fruul – “Defend the pass with your lives! We will seize Parnast!” 
## Thistle 04-04 Taraskh 

“That’s odd, stones in a circle. That can’t be what I think it is.” 
Thistle begins to advance quietly. He advises Orsik, Vardis, and Wallace to hang back and then sighs when he sees Unla Kee follow him. 
The gnome whispers, “If I don’t go to danger, the world might end. And I am feeling lucky today.” 
“And it’s a good thing too,” thinks Thistle because the gnomish wizard finds an inscription in fay that he missed. 
Thistle stares and now understands what he is seeing. It’s a gathering place for the Fey. It’s a place where they would drink, eat and revel. And it was a portal that could be used to move between the Feywild and this place. But its magic has disappeared for some reason. 
Thistle glares, “this is how Adimond Kroskas brought so many troops into the Feywild.” He mutters, “Uncle Yorn used to spin tales of how the North was a playground for the Fey, and these portals were plentiful, and they were how we came in and out of our place.” 
He reflects some more, is pleased to see signs of his home plane, and sad to see the decayed remnant of a gentler, happier age. 
He flitters back to the group, and they all head on to Parnast. The two-day journey to Parnast is uneventful. 

## Orsik 06-04-Taraskh
Orsik heads to the Golden Tankard with the books in tow for a drink. But before he can get one, Hsing, the blind golden pseudo-dragon, appears. Orsik is furious. 
“Hello,” it says. 
“You again!” 
“The books. Ah yes, I can feel the magical power going through me as I read them!” 
“You might want to discuss the books with the Wizard of the Crag.” 
“The wizard is dead,” he says frantically. 
“Nope!” 
“But he died in the duel. Wait, did he become a Lich? Are we dealing with a Lich? I have to notify the SEER immediately.” 
He blinks out and then blinks back in. 
“Very well, I didn’t understand that you meant that Unla Kee was the new Wizard of the Crag.” He then whistles, and a covered wagon rolls up, and two members of the Lord’s Alliance start moving the book into the covered wagon. 
Orsik looks at them and glares. These are the kind of men who kicked him out of every guard job he ever had. Men dressed in too much armor that had not seen a blade in anger.
One of the men stands before him, “this is Lord’s Alliance official business.” 

Orsik clenches and unclenches his hand and is about to start a fight. 
But Wallace gets in front of him and says, “Hey, look, Parnast needs protection, and my dad has secured help from the Lord’s Alliance. I want to get out of here, and I don’t want to get married to Elspeth, but this is my hometown, so can we just avoid creating an incident?”
The enraged dwarf stares at Wallace and then stands down. 
He then watches as Thistle asks about the armor they found that clearly belonged to the Lord’s Alliance. The two armed men stare at it dismissively and offer no information.
He then shouts, “so is this an officer’s armor? Or are you all equals?”  
Again Wallace stops Orsik from going over the top by saying, “what part of the Lord’s alliance do you not understand? They are all rich bastards who wear blingy armor.” 
Orsik laughs, “oh, you are all equal!” 
At that point, Orsik notices how Wallace grabs Thistle from the scuff and hisses, “can you please stop waving that armor around? This armor might belong to some second cousin’s sister’s husband of these Lordies, and we can either be forced to turn it over or be accused of crimes. Or what if my dad sees it and demands we hand it over so he can make an offering to the Lordies?” 
Orsik is pleased. Wallace has begun to come out of his shell but can’t hesitate to troll him, “what if Elspeth finds out?” 
And Wallace takes the bait, “Elspeth? Shit. Why did we come here?”
“To get our reward!” 
And with that, Hsing hands over 150 gold pieces for their trouble. Orsik shouts, “I deserve more.” And Hsing sneers, “I have been making deals with adventurers for a long time, and you are not the kind to be able to convince me to give out more than the SEER commands.” The creature then promptly teleports away. 
Orsik’s ax swings in the air, and he shouts, “I intentionally missed!” 

## The next two weeks 
The local militia was so pleased with Orsik’s training and actions that they asked if he would join their guard and train them some more. They offer free room, board, and two beers daily as he is a dwarf. Orsik is delighted as this is the first time any guard has asked him to join. He almost cries when they announce that they want to be known as “Orsik’s Guards.” He turns red and says, “we’ll talk about it later.” 
While he is training the guards, a man in a dark cloak appears, “I spoke to Zuul Clovis, and he said that you wanted to join us and you had the kind of moral flexibility that we look for.” 
Orsik glares at him – “Who are you?”
“I am a Zhentarim!” 
“Prove it!”
The agent flashes his badge. Orsik handles it and says, “these can be faked, but let’s go on.”
“Your paranoia commends you, Orsik.” 
The agent hands him a piece of paper that says – “ 
Kin;

We have lost contact with one of our Relatives in the Forgotten Forest. He was in service to Thornacious, a Royal Fairy Prince. We fear the Prince may have discovered his true purpose, or he may have been
captured by some of the local goblinoids. If you see him, please renew contact and ensure he returns to
Thornacious.

Your Family
“ 
Orsik stares, confused. 
“Yes, you would be. Oblivillish is our agent in Prince Thornacious’ court. Find him.” 
A day later, Orsik shares this information with his fellow adventurers. 
Thistle is surprised to hear about this agent in Prince Thornacious’ court. The Prince is in exile, forced. And Thistle’s good friend Blassios, the Satyr, is part of his court. But the idea that a Zhentarim agent would spy on a royal member of the Fey, even if a disgraced one, troubles him. He then reflects on the nature of Fey courts, where each Prince and lordling spy on each other, all in an attempt to gain favor with Titania by disgracing their rivals. He sighs. Prince Thornacious might even consider it a sign of prestige if the Zhentarim wants to spy on him.  

Unla Kee buys a new sword to replace the one he lost from Calder the Halfling. Calder’s runs a shop in Parnast that is unaffiliated with Lionel Coster. The halfling mutters as he sells the sword, “the Lionel Coster are not welcome in Parnast. They use their dimensional portals to move goods quickly and safely and put the little guy out of business.” 

He then spends the next week gambling at the Golden Tankard with travelers who come through the town. He’s had chiefly bad luck losing half of his initial stake but is curiously pleased. Every person that comes through has fun, as does he, except one day when a traveler accuses him of cheating. 
“But I lost!” observes Unla.
“You should have lost everything!” 
Unla is about to attack but wonders about this traveler. He could be a merchant, or he could be a member of the Zhentarim.
Ragnar intervenes, and the traveler leaves. He stares at Unla Kee, trying to commit Unla’s face to memory. 
Thistle, meanwhile, has taken to challenging travelers to an archery contest. In the first week, he wins more than he loses. He also acquires a bit of a fan club of the local children who have never seen a fairy and are delighted to see someone their size winning at what they call “bowery.” The following week, inspired by his fans, he wins against all comers. 
The children are so convinced of his prowess that they accost any traveler with a bow and shout, “I know someone who can beat you in a bowery contest!” 

## Hsing Returns 10-02 Taraskh 

It’s a quiet day. No one has shown up to gamble or be challenged to a bowery contest. Thistle is outside making trick shots that delight the children. 
Unla Kee continues to reflect on the signs and portents of doom inside the Golden Tankard. 
Orsik marches in, tired and exhausted from another day training his guards, and sits down next to the wizard. 
Thistle seeing Orsik enter the tavern follows him in. 
Seeing the group, Ragnar shouts, “come all of you, I have some important friends.” 
They all get up and follow him to a back room.
“You have a back room?” grunts Orsik. 
And then the dwarf sees Azam with Hsing. 
He grabs his ax and is about to strike, but Wallace stops him again. 
“Do you know who the SEER is?” 
“No, I keep meeting his messenger.” 
“Let me explain. The Lord’s Alliance has two groups. The first is the Lordies, and they aren’t dangerous. The second group is run by the SEER. They are the spies, assassins, and cut-throats who know all and do all. The SEER does the dirty work that allows the Lordies to keep their hands clean. Make a Lord your enemy, and you can live a long life. Make an enemy of the SEER; even the Zhentarim won’t protect you.” 
“But how would he know if I killed this thing?” 
“The SEER teleported a pseudo dragon in the middle of the Marsh of Chelimber on the day we arrived on the big island after we defeated the half-ogres. Don’t you think he might be able to find us?” 
“And, look, this alliance matters to my dad. He’s been trying to get people to stop hating him for the whole Cult of the Dragon, so can you do it for me? And your guards?” 
Orsik glares and shouts, “fine, but I will not talk to him. Wallace, you and I will wait outside.” He stares at Vardis, “and you, old friend?”
When the door slams shut, Thistle turns to Hsing – “you gotta up your game. These quests aren’t worth the effort given the reward you have offered.” 

# Chapter 14 - Into the Forgotten Forest
## Azam 10-02 Taraskh
As the door slams behind Vardis, Azam stares at the remaining adventurers and thinks, “oh dear.” 
And when Thistle demands something more than the excellent reward his provider and leader have provided for these adventurers, Azam is left speechless. 
“The audacity!” 
But then Hsing comes to his rescue, raising his head from a sleepy position; he says in his quiet old voice, “I have information on your missing uncle.” 
Azam is shocked when Thistle seems ungrateful. 
“I would prefer to have the information related to my friend.” 
Azam is ready to leave, but Hsing gestures him to sit, and then the pseudo-dragon says, “Yes, I know. But I think you want your uncle’s location first.” 
“Where is he?”
“That is the reward,” explains Azam.
“How do I know you have the information?”
Azam is outraged. He stands up and shouts, “you insult Hsing? You question the honor of the Lord’s Alliance? You question the all-powerful SEER?” 
Hsing shouts, “AZAM!” Azam is frozen in place. Hsing turns to him, “These are adventurers, and they don’t know me. And they can’t tell the difference between a lizard and me. And the Zhentarim wanna be thought he can kill me. Now sit down and let them ask their questions.” 
Azam is stunned into silence, regains his composure, sits down, and Hsing yawns. 
“How do you know who my uncle is?” 
This time, Azam smiles as Hsing swats the insult away, “The SEER spoke. And if she, he or they, we are not going to reveal their gender, that they have the information, then it is valid.” 
Azam then adds, “we can also offer gold as well.”
Thistle and Unla Kee convene and agree to the quest. 
Azam turns to Hsing, “let me see if he wants to talk to you. He is fatigued.” Azam strokes the pseudo-dragon and brings his ear to his mouth. 
“I have been told to tell you what the SEER wants you to know.”
Thistle and Unla Kee’s eyes roll.
“You may have heard of the Ettin Gralm?” 
Thistle can not remember his uncle saying anything about this for the life of him. 
“Well, Gralm has a realm in the Lonely Moor and has a force of Hobgoblins and Goblins under his control. His realm is a tenday journey through the Forgotten Forest.” 
Azam pauses and stares at Thistle, “Our agents have said that Gralm has been hunting down Fairies for food and not just because of the food shortage.” 
Unla Kee snorts, “a likely story.” 
Azam glares but continues, “We have received word that Bad Fruul is trying to force him to join his army that Bad Fruul intends to use to destroy Parnast.” 
“So, what do you need?” 
“We have two magical contracts, either of which we would like Gralm to sign. The first says that Gralm will not align with Bad Fruul and our preferred contract, an alliance of Gralm and his forces with ours against Bad Fruul.”
Thistle stares, “Ettins and contracts?”
“Ettin law is poorly developed. So we rely on these to enforce magical penalties if the counterparty violates the terms.” 
Unla Kee looks unimpressed. 
Azam turns to him, whispers something, and then speaks loudly, “Mr. Kee, would this be enough?”
Unla Kee smiles. 
“And the amount I promised, Mr. Kee, is between you and your friends.” 
And with that, Hsing teleports away. 
Azam relaxes in the chair in the back room of the Golden Tankard and says, “While Hsing travels on his missions, I will be here, waiting for you.” 
## The Group

Orsik, Vardis, and Wallace are drinking in the main hall of The Golden Tankard. 
“I hate that lizard.” 
Thistle flits up to them,  tells them everything he has learned, and ends by saying, “He’s giving me some vital information about my family, so we gotta go kill this ettin.” 
Unla Kee stares and corrects him, “get Gralm to sign a contract.” 
“Contract, kill, big difference.” 
Orsik muses, “so we hike 10 days to go kill an Ettin.” 
“Sign a contract.” 
“What’s in it for me?” 
“100 Gold?”
“Each?”
“For all of us.” 
Sensing that Orsik is about to bail, Thistle, says, “Uncle Joon is an excellent source of intel. He travels everywhere. He learns all of the local stuff. He knows what’s going on.”
Unla Kee adds, “He’s well informed, and I can easily turn that 100 Gold into 300 Gold.” 
Vardis coughs, “or zero – we saw that.”
“Well, it was more like losing half.” 
Orsik is not interested. 
Wallace pulls him aside, “we gotta do this. I need to protect my village.” 
“Protect your village?” 
“Bad Fruul is going to attack the village. And if he’s got the Ettin, it will be worse.”
“Okay so let’s kill Bad Fruul. I have nothing against the Ettin. I barely know him.” 
Wallace groans as he realizes Orsik is confused about what was asked, “we’re not trying to kill the Ettin; we are trying to get him to sign a contract to help OR to sign a contract to not join the fight.” 
Just then, Azam coughs.
“What do you want?” 
“Well, it would appear that I misread my notes. I was told to offer you a potion of healing and a potion of greater healing if during the attack during the festival, none of you put personal glory over saving a villager of Parnast, and if you successfully defeated the attack. My apologies.” 
Wallace tries harder and reminds him that although he doesn’t love Elspeth, she is a friend, and his Dad is here, and he can’t really let them die. 
Orsik is annoyed and wants to kill something. “What if we first killed the Wyvern?” 
Wallace scratches his chin, “see, having the Wyvern is a cost-saving measure. If we didn’t have the Wyvern, we would have to have a prison, guards, and food. And we don’t really have the kind of people that can imprison some of the folks that come by here. It’s much simpler to hand them over to the Wyvern and not think about it. And frankly, the threat of imprisonment with the Wyvern acts as a deterrent.”
Orsik smugly turns and says, “why would we want to change a totalitarian system like that?
Wallace, who seems keen to do what Azam wants, jumps in, “hey Thistle, could your Uncle Joon help Orsick and Vardis with their map?” 
“Absolutely. He knows everything about the North.” 
Vardis seems more interested, and Orsik picks up on that. 
“Go on.” 
“He knows all kinds of the craziest things. He’s been up and down all of Faerun and the North. He’s the guy we have been looking for! He’s going to find your family treasure!” 
“He better.” Orsik thinks aloud, “This is the one town where a city guard wants me to work for them. And it’s where I have found my first lackey.”
And with that, Orsik nods, and the group heads off. 

## Unla Kee 1st day of the 3rd tenday of Taraskh
The first day of the journey to Gralm’s realm is through the grasslands that abut with the great desert. The weather is pleasant, and the trip is uneventful until midday when they see a clearing over a quarter mile wide. In that clearing, the land has no living creature. And the birds choose to fly around it. But the most curious part of the clearing is a 50’ rusty sword.
Unla Kee immediately recognizes the sword as a sign from one of the many prophecies of the end times. He knows what he must do. He must attempt to remove the sword. 
His traveling companions refuse to join him. 
“The burden of saving the world rests on my shoulders alone,” he thinks, “and that is why everyone must address me as Mr. Kee.” 
He enlarges himself and then proceeds to the sword. A slight electric shock zaps him when he tries to remove it. He is undeterred. He pulls hard, and the blade refuses to budge. 
As he stands next to the sword, heaving and thinking about what to do next, he notices the ground being disturbed as if something wants to come out. Soon a decayed hand pops out. 
“Mr. Kee is no fool,” he thinks, running to the perimeter. When he arrives at the edge of the dead land, he looks back, and the land is as undisturbed as it always was.
The next evening the group arrives at the edge of the Forgotten Forest. The group remembers the rocks of the Fey Portal that they had found. And they realize that perhaps that portal is not as inert as they thought.
