The passageway that the orog used opened into a nursery full of orc children. Guarding the children was an orc claw of luthic. The companions at first considered killing them all until they realized the monstrosity of the crime. And the observation that they had leverage over the Orc Claw of Luthic as one of the whelps was theirs. 
Using that information, they threatened an attack, and the Orc Claw of Luthic grabbed her Whelp and moved away. 
The companions then noticed a pit full of human prisoners. They were two families. 

When they saw Orsik, they rejoiced, as they thought the militia had come to save them! 
They then quickly revealed that they were the families of Jarlor and Larrish. 
Orsik remembered the two men from his dealings as a town council member. 
“What did the orcs want with them?”
“They threatened to kill us if Jarlor and Larrish didn’t spy chief Kurglow.”

Seeing the murderous rage in Orsik’s eyes, one of the children shouts, “daddy didn’t want to do that, but the orc chief Kurglow said he would kill us all.” 

# Chapter 21 - The Battle Begins

“Let’s find some more orcs to kill!” shouts Orsik. 
Vardis looks into the southwest cave where he finds a dead human beaten by orcs next to a rock.
“Vardis, I never understood why orcs just sit in their caves! I mean you’d think if they heard combat they would come rushing out and try to help one another? It’s like if you steal from me, that’s bad, but if you steal from them… well who cares.” 
Unla Kee thinks, “I understand their motivations.” 
And he says – “it might be because of the insulation?” 
They all stare at him. 
As Vardis walks in, two Orcs that were hidden attack, from the shadows but he is not surprised. He recognizes them as followers Yurtus, the god of death and disease.  
Orsik rushes in and demonstrates his unique battle tactics and kills one. Except the creature comes back to life. 
“No matter,” says the Dwarf who kills him again. 
Wallace then charges in and attacks the other orc. 
Unla Kee and Thistle were still in the cave with the whelps and the villagers when two more Orcs attacked. 
The first orc attacks with a spiritual weapon and hits Thistle. 
The second orc hits Unla Kee with his spiritual weapon and the spear. 
Thistle looks at his companion, “wow how did they hurt you so badly.”
One of the orcs can’t resist, “you have armor, he doesn’t!”
Unla Kee smirks, “not enough” and hits them with a lightning bolt that they can’t dodge for 29 points of damage. 
Orsik turns to Vardis, “go help the halfling.” 
Vardis approaches Unla Kee and says, “get ready for my light touch” and then heals him. 
Thistle strikes one of the orcs with his bow, but before the orc can laugh, Thuul acid’s damage leaves the orc dead. 
He then pierces the orc’s defenses for 11 HP of damage with an arrow. 
Wallace charges against one of the orcs and kills him. 
With all the orcs dead, Vardis explores the room to the south east of the prison room. As he enters he is hit by the smell of a foul smelling concoction that is brewing over a fire. As he looks around he notices bones and ash litter that is piled up as an altar to Yurtrus. To complete the disgusting spectacle, there is a wooden frame that holds a pale white section of elven skin. Then the smell of a dead corpse grabs his attention and he sees a dead flayed elf. 
Going back to the room he first entered, he finds several gems and gold trade bars. 
Wallace is pretty excited with the new feat he mastered, and charges into the last remaining area of the cave complex. 
As he runs in, he notices how the cave is unnaturally dark. He shouts, “guys, there’s someone here.” 
That’s the last thing he hears as an orc of the Red Fang kills him. The Orcs of the Red Fang are assassins for hire and followers of Shargaas. 
Unla Kee looks into the darkness, sees nothing, and then casts a fireball doing 29 points of damage. The two assassin orcs are unable to escape the blast. Nor is Wallace who is now clinging desperately to life.
Orsik screams, “Wallace!” and runs after him.
Unla Kee raises an eyebrow at Vardis, who says, “I know I have been replaced.” 
Orsik overhears that and says, “you’re BOTH my best friend!” 
Best friend or not, Orsik, is hit for 36 points of damage as he stands in the darkness over Wallace’s unconscious body. 
Thistle rushes into the darkness, stumbles around and finds Wallace’s mouth and forces a potion of healing down his throat healing him. 
Vardis sighs, and then channels the divine power of Larethien to bring daylight to the cave, banishing the magical darkness and causing 16 and 8 points of damage on the orcs. 
But the other Orc of Shargaas casts darkness again and disappears into the dark. 
“I just cleaned that up!” shouts Vardis. 
Unla Kee doesn’t hesitate, unable to see any target through the darkness, he runs into the darkness, under Orsik’s legs, jumps over Thistle, and past the outstretched arm of one of the orcs and then exits the darkness facing the other orc. 
Orsik uses his fighting spirit to gain an advantage over the Orc assassin, killing him. And then rushes to attack the last Orc that is outside the area of darkness and misses. 
Wallace who was knocked out, burned with a fireball, healed by a fairy, gets up from being prone on the ground, and then rushes at the last orc killing him. 
“You thought you were so clever!” 
With the death of the orc, the darkness is dispelled. 
Wallace takes a look around and points out a chest. Inside they find a miniature silver dragon skull and a small sack containing loose coins and gems. 
With the orcs dead, and the treasure secure, the group heads back to the main room with the whelps and villagers. 
“So how much time do we have before the battle?” asks Orsik.
“Two days.” 
“That’s plenty fo time for us to do what we need to do.
“Not if we want to bring all the children.” 
For a moment, Orsik thinks about it and then decides that he has to do the right thing and says, “We bring all of the children.”
Unla Kee raises an eyebrow. 
Along the way to Parnast, the group learns that one of the orc whelps is the son of the orc chieftain. The orc that guarded the whelps, thankful that her child wasn’t killed, tells them that Kurglow would be willing to abandon Bad Fruul to save his child. 
“He’s a terrible husband, but a good father.” 
Arriving in Parnast, Thistle suggests that they use Jarlor and Larrish to send a message to Kurglow through the spy channel. 
Jarlor isn’t interested until his wife explains, “if the orcs don’t attack we might survive so can you grow a spine and do what you need to do to protect your family?” 
He nods and listens as Orsik comes up with the worst threat he can – “if you attack, we send the whelp to bed without dessert and will not read a bed-time story!”
Jarlor stares at him, and wonders if he should correct the dwarf, but thinks, “never had kids and he’s the boss.” 
Thistle asks, plaintively, “Is there a Lionel Coster around?” 
Chandra looks at him queerly, and says, “my dear Thistle, this belonged to the Zhentarim who believed that power came from physical transport of goods. The transdimensional market that the Lionel Coster’s use is not available to folks here. Our poor local merchant Calder has been forced to use the Zhentarim physical transportation of goods.” 
Thistle sighs, and remembers what Uncle Joon had told him that the Lionel Coster was a devotee of KP Trade Equilibrium Theory. Apparently it| produced a more optimal general equilibrium because it considered all markets across all possible universes vs a single local physical market. Although optimal for consumers there had been violent resistance to them from merchants who preferred the profits of a more local equilibrium. The mathematics in the argument had involved a novel application of the Smalistin conjecture which asks if n-dimensional sphere’s basic symmetries are all the symmetries it has. But then again, for Thistle mathematics wasn’t his strong suit. 
The important point for Thistle, is that Calder didn’t have magic to sell. 
“Sorry about that!” the small halfling had told him. 
The next morning comes, and everyone in the militia arrives at their posts. All paths into the center of town where the well is are barricaded, windows have been boarded up, and pits have been dug to slow down the advance of any forces. 
The militia is terrified, the women and children are nervous, the refugees are worried, trapped in Parnast. 
As the sun begins to set, Orsik seeing how everyone is afraid steps forward and shouts – “my friends, they will not pass, they will not succeed, they will die! We will come through tonight alive, our town preserved!” 
The jingoistic speech is a rousing success and the militia cheer! 
As the moon begins to rise, Orsik and Thistle turn to Chandra, “our ace in the hole is wyvern.” 
She looks at them – “you do realize that the wyvern will kill everyone?”
“I thought you could control it?” 
“Um, no.” 
“Well I suppose someone could try to control it,” as Thistle tries to remember if he had learned how to control crazed hungry draconic creatures. Thuul whimpers.
Unla Kee scans through the book that SEER gave him and finds a relevant story about seven warriors who show up in a town and train the villagers to fight and together they all die in a glorious blood bath, their combined blood being the necessary part of some ritual that summons some extra-planar power. 
Unla Kee chooses to not share that, but instead shouts, “we must win!” 
Thistle from his elevated vantage point stares at the edge of the forest that he knows Bad Fruul must come.
And there he is. He is sitting on a mammoth, in bone-festooned plate armor, wearing a helm made from the skull of some unidentifiable beast. Thistle thinks, “I should know that.” Bad Fruul looks like a massive dark night, not like some hill giant. 

The giant dismounts, removes his helm, holds it under one arm and the eyes that gleam with intelligence scan the battlefield
“That’s the drow!” 
The giant shouts, “Little folk, give Fruul what he wants. The girl Chandra who hails from the folks of Evereska , the offspring of the horrid Luistarra and give us the vile statue of Agharradh. Comply with his demands and you will live. Failure to do so will mean the destruction  of this town after you have all been made slaves. ”
For a moment, the villagers ponder their choices, and then Ragnar and Gundalin shout, “NEVER! The Orsik’s Militia will never surrender one of our own! Go back before you die!” 
“Then death and destruction it is!” And at that the horde of golbins, orcs, ogres, and ettins begins howling and chanting. 
## Bad Fruul 
*They underestimate me! They think Bad Fruul is dumb! Bad Fruul will rule over all!*
He turns to a squad – “I want you to test their defenses. Go and kill them all!” 
Eight goblins, two wolfs and their goblin leaders march towards the town ready to attack the villagers. 
Bad Fruul watches as they attack along the main road – “they served their purpose.” 
The goblins fell into pits and died either on the pikes in the pits, or from the militia that attacked them from higher ground. 
The goblin bosses tried to rally their troops but were killed by the ever present archers of Orsik’s Militia. 
Bad Fruul shouts – “but I was told how badly their training went! My spies lied to me!”
The wolf tries to go around the pits and scale the wall. But the militia inspired by Orsik, and their successful defense stand their ground and hack it to bits. 
Bad Fruul thinks – “hmm… I must do something different, let me try this.” 
And now he sends ogres, half-ogres from Mama Mia and two cave bears. 
The ogres are able to walk around the pits full of dead goblins, and reach the walls. The half-ogres are again, killed by the Thistle Trained Archers. 
“You were saying, Vardis?” 
“Nothing.” 
“You were saying, Vardis?”
Although the ogres are able to navigate around the pits, the owl bears are not. 
Bad Fruul sees his ogres advance, and turns to his lieutenant, “Mama Mia’s ogres have breached the wall, send some more orgres with the owlbear.” 

# Chapter 22 - Release the Wyvern
“Die Unla Kee!” screams the Orc as his axe slices through the robes of the gnome. 
“The prophecy says otherwise,” thinks Unla Kee as he falls down. 
The orc chieftain stares at the dead halfling and laughs, seized with blood lust charges at Vardis. The cleric sees him advance and calls on Correllon to blind his enemy. The chieftain is blinded but still lands his blow for 15 hit points. 
Thistle looks at the two orc chiefs, the one wearing a blue sash and the one wearing a green sash, and says, “these look like they are pretty tough!” 
Vardis stares at the blue Orc, “he’s been hurt a few times. It’s not going to be too hard.” 
Thistle stares at the injured orc chief a little bit and realizes he’s the father of one of the orc whelps they brought from the caves. 
“Huh, why wasn’t he afraid of our threats?” 
“Maybe because Orsik’s threat was to *send the whelp to bed without dessert and will not read a bedtime story,*” mumbles Jarlor, the one-time spy. 
While this dialogue continues, Wallace is running various simulations in his head about the optimal path necessary to attack one of the orcs to maximize his charge. He runs 20 feet north, then turns around at a sharp angle, and then runs 10 feet east; charging at the Orc, he kills with one blow. The Orc flies 10 feet away from the force of the blow. 
Vardis casts a scorching ray killing an injured orc and seriously wounding another – “try threatening him like your mom used to?” 
Orsik nods and shouts in common, “if you don’t stop fighting, your whelp will be forced to eat all of his vegetables.” 
The entire town stares at Orsik. He turns to them – “I hated vegetables.” 
Kurglow, the orc chieftain of the Spinripper Clan, is unimpressed. 
Orsik shouts, “I AM NOT NEGOTIATING WITH MONSTERS AGAIN!” 
Thistle agrees and kills two orcs. 
Unla Kee, whom Vardis has since healed, casts a mind sliver on the Orc Chieftain with the Green sash to no effect. 
The Orc Chieftain then attacks Orsik and does 32 HP of damage. Orsik stands. 
Kurglow is now very angry at Orsik, partly because he threatened his son and because he insulted his fathering. The threats only made sense if Kurglow’s whelp was a soft, overly civilized, and pampered creature, not a wild, dangerous orc like his father. And that Kurglow spoiled his whelp. That list insult is too much for him to bear, so he charges at Orsik. 
Chandra, Vardis, Wallace, and Unla Kee each get a wack at Kurglow as he runs toward Orsik. 
Wallace hits the other orc chief and screams, “healing!” 
Vardis shouts, “many need healing. Let me see what I can do!” He walks up to Orsik and heals his wounds. 
Unla Kee tries his mind-spike again on the Orc with the green sash, and this time it penetrates the Orc’s mental defenses. 
Orsik mumbles, “enough talking, more killing,” and strikes twice with his blade at the green Orc, killing him. 
Kurglow seeing the other chieftain dead, shouts, “give me my son, or I will kill you, you piece of shit!” 
Orsik stares at him, confused. 
And then Orc that protected the whelps appears and starts screaming at Kurglow in orcish. 
After the screaming ends, Kurglow again turns to Orsik – “give me my child, or you will die.” 
“Does that mean you will also leave?” 
“Yes.” 
Orsik calls one of the villagers of Parnast to return the child. 
At that point, the female caregiver starts screaming at Orsik and Kurglow. Once done crying, she throws dirt at everyone. 
Kurglow snarls at her, then the chieftain points his finger at her and shouts at her. 
The female caregiver falls down on her knees and bangs her fists into the ground as Kurglow walks away.
The group stares, confused. 
One of Orsik’s militia, who happens to speak common, walks up and explains – “well, boss, here’s the deal. Kurglow was furious at Naz because she was alive, and his son was a prisoner. Naz insisted that she did everything to ensure Kurglow’s child was safe. Kurglow laughed at her and said that she only cared about her child, and if she could save hers and sacrifice his, then she would have. Naz denies the accusation and begs Kurglow to take her with him. He laughs at her and says – I think this is what he said – ‘Unlike you, I can take my whelp and leave you to die.’” 
Naz sits near the well in the middle of the town, sobbing. 
At this point, Bad Fruul howls in anger at the betrayal of Kurglow. He starts preparing for the final assault on his mammoth, with several ettins and a hoard of orcs. 
Thistle feeds everyone good berries – “Uncle Joon always said you shouldn’t fight on an empty stomach!” 
Raggnar looks at Orsik – “do you have any plan?” 
Orsik smiles – “it is time for us to release the wyvern!” 
Chandra shouts – “are you mad? He will kill us all!” 
Gundalin screams – “it’s a desperate ploy of a madman!” 
Orsik laughs, “yes, and it is time for us to risk everything. We will free the Wyvern and hope they and the giant fight.” 
Just then, Blassios and two sprites appear – “My friends! My great friends! My drinking buddy Unla Kee! We have come to help you fight this Bad Fruul!” 
Thistle is delighted, “Blassios, you came!” 
“Of course I did!” 
Meanwhile, Orsik talks to Chandra – “so how do I do this?”
Chandra scratches her head and says, “The Wyvern is tied to a post in the middle of the former stables. To free it, you will have to get past it. The good news is that this Wyvern likes fresh meat, and we have a lot of it right now.” She points to the dead orcs surrounding the battlefield. “Throw dead bodies in one corner, and then rush and remove the chain from the hoop. The Wyvern will notice it’s free and charge at the nearest living thing.” 
“So I better make sure it’s the giant!” 
“Yes.” 
As Bad Fruul climbs his mammoth and approaches the center of Parnast, Thistle shouts, “do we have time to rest?” 
“No,” shouts Chandra, “my family’s nemesis is guiding his actions. She knows that she must attack now, or she will lose her chance for vengeance.” 
Vardis says – “let’s drink our potions of healing.” 
“And the mud potions of healing!”
Orsik looks at the advancing forces, “they look big.” 
“And tough!” 
“There is no shame in fleeing,” says the voice in Orsik’s head.
Vardis sees his friend’s eyes and says, “yes, there is.” 
“Indeed!”
## Orsik 
Orsik stares at the battlefield. The orcs are coming in from the south and north, and Bad Fuul and his giants from the east. The last of the militia kill one advancing Orc from the south and then are overrun. 
It is up to him. 
As he wonders what help he can count on, Naz stops sobbing and stands up. 
“I will get my revenge on Kurglow after we kill this idiot of a giant and the orcs that follow him to their doom.” 
He smiles and heads to the stables. Chandra shouts, “I thought you were going to send the guards?” 
“No, it is my deadly plan, and I am the leader, and I will do it.” 
And for a moment, Orsik realizes that that thing that always got in his way may – possibly – be a thing of the past.
## Thistle
Thistle flies up and sees the field of battle in front of him. He thinks for a moment to attack one of the ettins and then reconsiders and strikes one of the orcs trying to block any escape. 
He watches helplessly as his friend Blassios complains about the rudeness of an orc who strikes him with a Javelin. 
And then, to his horror, the hill giant shouts – “I hate fairies that fly!”, smashes through the houses of Parnast and casts hold person.  
“I don’t want to plummet to my death!” thinks Thistle and wills himself to keep moving. 
“*Uncle Joon never said anything about spell-casting Hill Giants. He said Hill Giants were dumb and fat.*” 
“That didn’t work! That didn’t work!” shouts Bad Fruul as he bangs his head. 
## Vardis 
*I’ve been saving my fireball for a special occasion. This is it.*
The orcs from the north are caught up in the fire, wind, and heat. Three are dead, but three survive. 
And he smiles when Chandra casts bless on Orsik, who has promised to protect her, Thistle because apparently Bad Fruul sees him as a target and a target that is not her is a target to preserve, and Wallace, whom she has known from the day he was born and loves him like a son. 
## Wallace 
Wallace has a freaky ability to understand the specific distances between elements in a 60-foot radius, allowing him to plot movement that helps him charge at his enemies. 
He decides to attack the Orc that hurt Blassios, but he misses. 
“Need to rethink my planning.” 
From the rear, he hears the sound of a Javelin hitting Vardis. 
“T’is but a flesh wound!” 
And then Wallace is struck by an orc. 
As he turns to the Orc, he hears the roar of a mammoth charge. The creature smashes through the houses, sending glass, wood, and trinkets flying in every direction. Dust settles, and the beast is closer than Wallace would like. 
## Blassios
“For Thornacius!” shouts Thistle. 
*Certainly not for that buffoon. But why am I here? I am the bravest of all Seelies, but this is crazy. This is all Oblivilish’s fault. It’s his revenge for my indiscretion. And I am too close for comfort. I do not want to be fighting with my sword. At least if I don’t have to, and I most certainly don’t!*
Blassios takes cover and fires an arrow at the Orc that struck him with a Javelin.
He signals to the sprites to attack the mammoth, and they poison the creature but do not put it to sleep. 
And he smiles even more, when Naz strikes the mammoth with a guiding bolt. 
## Unla Kee
*No one can kill me until the end of times. At this point, time will end, and then who cares. *
He fumbles for his wand of web and toys with the idea of using it or casting another spell. 
The mammoth is caught in the web. 
Unla Kee, having dealt with that threat, moves directly in front of the giant. 
## Orsik 
He opens the stable doors closest to Bad Fruul. There is the Wyvern feasting on the dead corpses, and its feasting is more interesting than the dwarf. Orsik uses that distraction to go past the creature and free it. The Wyvern realizing they are free, perks its draconic head up, looks at the bloody mess in front of them, and smacks its lips upon seeing the giant outside.  

With a quick flap of its wings, it rushes out. 
In the background, Orsik hears Vardis getting attacked by an Orc from the north. And Thistle commands Thuul to approach one of the Orcs to the south. The next thing Orsik hears is the thud of the Orc’s body on the ground. And then the sound of a fireball. 
Orsik smiles. 
And then he rushes out the other door. 
He hears the wyvern strike the giant. And then hears an ettins mace connect with the Wyvern and scream 
– “I thought you said they would not free the Wyvern!” 

Thistle flies to a roof. He looks around and notices that there are plenty of wounded orcs. 

# Chapter 23 - Orsik
Thud. 
It was a satisfying thud, thought Chandra Stol. The death of a giant and a nemesis made her smile. And then she remembered that her pet wyvern was dead. 
Not of the body, mind you; Bad Fruul was killed while tied up. No, the sound of swords thudding on his body. 
That was satisfying. 
Raggnar shouts, “What have you done to this town?”
Gundalin is about to agree. 
Orsik, covered in blood, guts, and gore, shouts, “Yes, look what we did, saved your town!” 
Vardis, also covered in dirt, laughs, “and got rid of the wyvern, and got rid of some those architectural eye sores. You can modernize the look and feel of the town!” 
“That wyvern was a disappointment.” 
“Absorbed some blows?”
“Not a single attack landed; even Chandra caused more harm.” 
Meanwhile, the two sprites squeak, “We were more important than the wyvern! Our poisoned arrows saved the day! Bad Fuul was about to kill all of you when he finally succumbed!”
“Yes! We want to thank you for your work,” says a gracious Orsik. " Right, Vardis?” 
“We ever have to choose between a wyvern and sprites; we are so totally taking the sprites!” 
The Sprites are delighted and start tumbling, shouting, “Sprites FTW! Sprites FTW!” They high-five and start praising each other – “It was all you! No, you! You! You!” 
Blasius emerges at this point from his hideout, pulls out a flask, and shouts to Unla Kee – “my friend, my good friend, I have some alcohol. Some fine wine, would you like some?” 
“Yes.” 
Orsik stares – “You’re drinking anything today?”
“It’s a risk.” 
Blasius approaches Orsik, “My brother, my friend, my hero! This is a glorious day!” He waves his hand over the battlefield, and it is at this point that the cost of the battle is obvious. Most of Orsik’s militia is dead, half the town is devastated, the ground has seeped with blood, the air stinks with the smell of flesh, and the flies have started to come to feast. 
Orsik shouts, “Later.” 
Chandra, who was watching everything play out near the well, appears. 
“You were going to be the new head of the guard.” 
“But I never wanted the job, Orsik.” 
“Well, good, because after the performance of the wyvern, the job was no longer yours.”
“Win-win for everyone,” snarks Vardis. 
“My wyvern is dead,” Chandra sobs, “but, Orsik, he fought heroically?” 
“Nope.” 
“Yes, he did!” 
“You’re right; he did stand there and allow himself to be killed. 
Vardis blurts - He was a bold distraction
And Orsik completes - but I wouldn’t call that fighting.
“You’re a mean person,” the local cleric sobs.
“Hey, good outcome. You’re alive. We’re alive. The town is – saved.” 
At that point, the few remaining militia members, Raggnar, Gundalin, and the rest of the group that has internalized the scale of death and destruction stare at Orsik in disbelief.  
“We can rebuild the town. No problem.” 
And Vardis asks, “How many casualties did you suffer?”
“Half the militia, I think,” mumbles Ragnar.
“Well, civilian?” 
Chandra erupts in anger, “Mr. Vardis, I am not sure how things work in your big cities, and I don’t really care. But in this town, the distinction between civilians and militia is lost. These were our friends, our family, and our townspeople.” 
With that, Chandra, Ragnar, and Gundalin begin to argue about what will happen to the town and how to fix it. 
Everyone else decides to just sit down and breathe. Exhausted from fighting and just happy to be alive. 
Orsik takes that moment to sneak over to Bad Fruul. He pulls out a headband he had won in a game of cards while still a guard in the city. “I’ll swap this with that one on the giant’s head, and all will be well.” 
 After all, he’s never trusted the SEER. And he’s never trusted the pseudodragon. No one notices him as he approaches the giant’s head, so he smiles. He pulls the headband off Bad Fruul, stares at it, and thinks, “The SEER set up this complicated plan that almost killed me to find this band. And I am supposed to believe she isn’t going to use it for some evil purpose? No. I am going to swap it with this other band.” 
Unfortunately, Orsik spent too much time in his internal monologue and didn’t notice Thistle fly up and see him with the two bands. 
But Orsik may have gotten away with it if he hadn’t taken the other headband from between his legs and fallen on the ground. Orsik grumbles and stands up. He puts the cursed one in his mouth, picks the fake one from the ground, and tries to shove it on the giant’s head. 
Thistle sees the dwarf with two headbands and says, “Um.” 
“Look, Thistle, I don’t think we should give this headband to the SEER. That’s a bad plan.” 
“She said she would discuss it.”
“Why should we trust her?”
“She said we would get it back.”
“yeah?”
“Has she broken her promises? She’s led us into danger for no good reason without warning, but I don’t know if she’s broken her promises yet.” 
Orsik thinks momentarily, “Look, this artifact has an evil soul. How do we know she will not just do something with that soul? Like, what if she has plans?” 
At this point, Chandra explodes, “That soul has been tormenting my family for too long,” and waves her hand at the destruction, “Did this? Have the SEER get rid of it!” 
“You believe the SEER is going to do that?” 
And just then, Unla Kee says, “I want the soul in me.” 
Thistle turns – “you want what?” 
“To be a vessel? Is that what I am hearing?” 
“Yes, Vardis.” 
Vardis looks at him, confused.
“With the soul, I will have access to the magic of the drow and the lore of a 500-year-old wizard. Of course, I want that!” 
At this point, Vardis notices the other headband, “where did you get that?” 
“Not important.” 
At this point, things get very upsetting for Orsik. Everyone wants him to hand over the headband to the SEER, and no one wants to explain why they trust her. 
First, Chandra accuses him of putting her life in danger with his decision not to give it up, and when Orsik asks her why she trusts the SEER who put her life in danger, she sneers at him. 
Then, one of his guards claims that he’s putting the town at risk by not doing what the SEER wants. When Orsik asks why his guards trust the SEER more, the guard rolls his eyes as if it’s the dumbest question you could ask.  

Wallace is the next person to try to convince Orsik, and he points out that the SEER is an essential member of the Lord’s Alliance, that the town needs their support, and that he should be willing to give the headband to the SEER. Orsik is confused; Wallace wants to provide the SEER with the headband. The same SEER who put the town in danger, whose plots almost destroyed the city. So he asks why and once again is dismissed.  
At this point, Hsing shows up, and the gold pseudodragon demands that he hand over the headband. When the townspeople and his allies side with that creature, Orsik is fed up. They won’t listen to him, and what they want makes no sense. 
Orsik is confused and upset. No one answers his question, and they treat his concerns as if they don’t matter. Everyone acts as if he’s the bad guy for not trusting some stranger who put the town in acute danger. All his hard work to save the city is being ignored, and he’s being treated like another dumb sword. He had poured his heart and soul into this town and its people, who were willing to trust some strange woman over him. 
He had invested all of this time and energy in being redeemed for all the things he had done wrong, and at the very end, he was marginalized and distrusted. This was his redemption, his chance to turn it around, and still, nobody trusted him enough to answer his question. 
“I’m done.” He drops the magical object and marches out of the town.
Everyone stares at him as he marches off. 
Thistle flies up to him, “done with Parnast?”
“No, done with everyone. I am out.” 
“Wait, wait, what’s the problem?”
“I never liked the pseudo dragon. And I don’t know what this ring is; all we know is what this strange lady who put our lives and the town in danger tells us. And we plan to hand it over to her even though I don’t trust her. And nobody thinks the fact that I don’t trust her is important. And I can’t do anything to get anyone to trust me, so I am done!” 
Ragnar, racing after Orsik and overhearing him, says, “My dear friend. You make some excellent points.”
Racing after Orsik, Chandra mumbles, “What do we really know about the Lord’s Alliance?” 
“What good have they brought us? What I understand from Wallace and Orsik is that they were the ones whose machinations resulted in all this death and destruction to our town,” says a Gundalin, out of breath. 
Thistle says, “They did defeat the Cult of the Dragon?” 
Chandra shrugs, “so did the Zhentarim. They didn’t even see how they could profit. They figured they either help defeat Tiamat or get devoured by a five-headed dragon.” 
At this point, everyone is beginning to see Orsik’s point. 
Hsing is annoyed and shouts, “Do you doubt the SEER? She’s the top spymaster in the whole world.” Hsing pauses, “I guess you shouldn’t trust a spymaster. They do lie.” 
Orsik is smug; even Hsing sees the absurdity of trusting the SEER.
“So what are we going to do with this?” 
And just then, a door materializes, and the SEER steps through; my dear Thistle, give it to me!” 
“No, we won’t.”
“Why not step into my garden?” 
“Nope.” 
Orsik glares and points at her with the cursed ring, “What are you going to do with the evil soul?” 
The SEER laughs. 
“My dear boy, send it to where it was supposed to go.” She stares at Orsik and realizes that he’s changed profoundly. “My dear Orsik, you want to ensure I don’t do something with that soul and endanger this community! Why, then, I will perform the ritual in this town tomorrow morning!”
Orsik stares, confused, at the wall from which the SEER had just appeared.
Later that evening, the town throws a party, with the usual carousing, cheering, crying, drinking, and eating. 

An unexpected guest, Prince Thornacious, informs Thistle that he has mentioned him to Queen Tatiana of the Seelie court and that she was pleased. 
“You are being noticed!” 
Thistle is confused, “is that good?” 
“To be noticed by the Queen?” the Prince laughs, “that is all we should ever want.” 
“I just want my beloved.” 
A drunk Blasius and Unla Kee are still singing in the morning when the SEER arrives.
“Well, let’s get this done.” 
And a few moments later, Orsik is holding a headband of intellect.
As the sun sets, Wallace approaches his mentor and announces, “I’m going to call it quits. Elspeth and I decided to hook up, and the town needs a new guard captain. And…” 
Orsik grunts his approval and sheds not a single tear.
A few days later, Orsik and the other companions begin their long trip to Triboar to find Uncle Joon.
There is one slight difference: Orsik, who had the intelligence of an ox, now has a genius-level intellect. He was the only one who wanted the headband. 
As he marches forward, he thinks, “Well, my dear Orsik, it would appear this was an utterly unexpected outcome. Yes, I know you had this clever little plan to redeem yourself in the eyes of that old dwarf. To be a hero, not an outcast, you pulled that off without me. It was touch and go, but you did it. You completed a redemption arc. I am almost shocked that you could do that without me. I suppose redemption doesn’t need intellect, just a good heart. Yes, you probably have one. And then you had this insane plan with the headband, but this is better. A much better plan.” 
He remembers some old stories he had heard that had made no sense, and he smiles, “Yes, I am going to enjoy knowing more about the world and its history.”
He mumbles loudly, “I wonder if I should become a mage?” 
A few moments later, he hits himself on the forehead, “Oh, that’s why people kept getting upset at that thing.” 

# Chapter 24 - Uncle Joon
Vardis shouts – “Zephyr – OS!”
And the cloud giant laughs and then bellows – “The name is Zephyros. Not Zephyr Oh-Es!” 
Vardis laughs a little, to which the giant responds, "I can hear that!” 
As the last of the cultists fly off, Zephyros asks – “Still think it is the right decision?” 
“World is fine the way it is, warts and all,” shrugs Vardis. 
“Yes, we definitely think,” intones Unla. 
“We’re not fans of cultists,” says Thistle. 
And with that, the journey to Triboar continues. Zephyros can’t resist pointing out landmarks and sharing his knowledge of the terrain.
“We are traveling over the High Forest. It is vast and mysterious, larger than most kingdoms, encompassing mountains. It is said that the forest is full of enormous animals to you little people. Why I have heard that the brown bears are larger than sheds and the stags have antlers as wide as a wagon.” 
A day later, he shouts, “To the northeast, you can see the Star Mounts in the heart of the High Forest. We can’t get any closer to them because of the powerful winds that swirl through and around the mountains. These mountains are so tall you can see them from any forest edge.” 
As the days pass, the group realizes that their rations are their only food and worries that Thistle’s good berries will be their only sustenance. 
Zephyros takes pity on them and sends down some of his food. 
“And here we are reaching the edge of the forest and passing over the Iceshield lands, and beyond that is the Dessarin Hills. I do remember fondly spending time with the dwarves that mined those lands. But that was so long ago, I am not sure they are still there.” 
Thistle peers over the edge of the cloud that makes up the Cloud Giant’s castle; he notices a silver dragon approaching the court with several dwarves hanging off it.
“Guys, we’ve got incoming.” 
A minute later, the dragon lands before Thistle, Vardis, Orsik, and Unla Kee. 
Thistle takes stock of their visitors. The dwarves are shield dwarves, and the silver dragon appears uninjured. The dragon has a harness that the dwarves used to hang onto. Thistle concludes that they must be working together. 
The dwarves, upon landing, quaff some potions and become gaseous. 
Thistle stands before the dragon and asks, “Are you here to talk to Zephyros?” 
The startled dragon brings its head very close to Thistle and says, “You’re not a giant.” 
Vardis can’t resist and says, “Far from it.” 
“You’re not very tall for a fairy.” 
“No, I am quite normal-sized.” 
“I didn’t say you were short. I said you weren’t very tall.” 
“Are you here to talk to Zephyros?”
“What are you doing here?” 
“We struck a deal with him!” 
“Ah, you are enemies! We will fight you in combat, defeat you, and feast on your bodies. It was nice knowing you!” 
Thistle is shocked. That went a lot worse than he anticipated. He quickly shouts, “We’re just travelers. If you want Zephyros, he’s over there,” pointing to the inside of the castle. 
Orsik interrupts the dialogue, “Why are you enemies? He seemed nice. Is there something we should know?”
The dragon pauses, surprised. “Are you allies of the giant?” 
Thistle takes advantage of the pause, “We’re just adventurers, and he offered us a ride. I’m looking for my Uncle Joon.” 
Orsik shrugs, “I have this map with what I am looking for.” He then thinks out loud, 
We are our own allies. And to be quite honest, we’re not sure if we are allies of each other. Not a lot of allying going on here.”
“I don’t even like these guys,” blurts the gnomish mage.  
The dragon, about to breathe fire on the group, pauses and says, “In that case, seeing as you are innocent bystanders, I will ask you to step aside while the strike team and I, Clarion, destroy the orb.” 
At that point, the group hears the apparent sounds of combat coming from Zephyros’ room: swords clanking, furniture breaking, and spells being cast. 
“Did you say orb?” says a suddenly interested Orsik. 
“Is the orb that makes it fly? I have a tower, see. Can I get an orb in my tower?” asks Unla Kee. 
“I don’t know about that. I have been sent on a strict mission to stop this tower by staging a secret mission to destroy the orb and prevent this giant from bombing towns and villages across the north. I have not been sent to help gnomes fly their castles.” 
And with that, the dragon prepares to fly up to help the dwarves. 
Unla Kee shouts, “So what happens when the orb is destroyed?” 
“Oh, the castle stays motionless for several months while the cloud giant assembles a new one.” 
Unla Kee looks horrified at the idea of no alcohol for months while eating good berries. 
Clarion feels sorry and says in the tone of a parent whose child had burned their hand in the fire, “This is what happens when you ally yourselves with evil cloud giants.” 
“But we’re just passengers. You keep calling us allies,” says an annoyed Vardis. 
“And to be quite honest, I haven’t seen any rocks. Have you seen any?” he asks the group.
The dragon is surprised, “we have seen distinct evidence of cloud giants dropping rocks on settlements. And this is a Cloud Giant’s castle, so we made a reasonable inference and decided to take action!” 
Vardis looks at Orsik, who knows this kind of reasoning: governments that have no idea what to do other than send in the army and attack something so that it looks like they are doing something.
“Do you have proof that it is this giant? You’re making a lot of assumptions here,” says the dwarf. 
“Could be a different giant,” agrees the elf.
“I haven’t seen any rocks,” says the fairy. 
Seeing the dragon still talking to Thistle, “Well, the Lord’s Alliance.” 
Vardis looks at Orsik, who says, “Oh, we know that group.”
“We have been working for that woman,” says Thistle as he looks at Orsik, trying to remember a name, “the SEER? We did a few missions for her.” 
“You know the SEER?”
“Yes.” 
“And would you say that this is official SEER business?” 
“Yes?”
At that, the dragon stops, thinks, and says, “My commander will want to talk to you.” 
“Your commander? I assumed the person with wings was in charge.” 
The dragon smiles, “a common misconception.” 
A few seconds later, a female shield dwarf removes her helm and says, “ I am Diana, and I am in charge of this mission. The dragon says you are working for the SEER?” 
When Thistle nods, Diana turns to the dragon, saying, " It would appear we interfered with the wrong castle.” 
Vardis can’t resist and asks, “Would you say your princess is in another castle?”
Diana stares at him, “I am guessing that is supposed to be funny?” Then she pauses, “Oh, that’s human humor.”
“Yes, we like to be subversive that way.”
“A princess in a tower, no self-respecting dwarf would want a princess in a tower,” she grumbles. 
Orsik decides to help Diana decide: " Have there been any rock droppings over the past few days?” 
“Yes.” 
“Well, none from this castle.” 
“No threatening to eat you?” 
“No, no threats.” 
“No long-winded speeches about how Cloud Giants will rule the world?”
“Are you racist towards Cloud Giants?”
Diana is in shock. " Oh no. Cloud Giants are both good and evil, so I am double-checking that Zephyros is one of the good guys.” She pauses. “I am just trying to do my job. There is no need to fling accusations at me.” She then turns to the dragon and says, " Well, this was a bust. It's time to go.” 
Seeing things settling down, Thistle asks, “Do you know anything about the Cult of Dragon and Adimond Kroskas?”
Diana looks at him – “Well, we did fight them to a standstill.”
The dragon says, “I don’t think I ever heard of a person named Adimond Kroskas, but I wasn’t heavily involved with the efforts against the cult.”
Diana then declares, “Well, unless there is anything else?”
Orsik shows the map again, and the dragon looks at it carefully – “I am sure I have seen something like the Northwest. No, I saw it to the northeast.” 
Unla Kee tells her about the evil cultists that had come earlier to get Zephyros to be their ally. 
She laughs, “Should have led with that.” 
And with that, she flies off. 
Soon after, the castle’s journey to Triboar continues over the Dessarin Hills and the Dessarin River, and it lands near Triboar.
Orsik shouts, “TRIBOAR? This is a dump. It’s where Waterdeep’s nobility comes to hang out when they need the country. SERIOUSLY? THIS IS LIKE PARNAST 2.0!” 
Vardis nudges Orsik and points to a local militia member. Orsik smiles. 
“Sir, I am a member of the Twelve, and I do not take too kindly to that description of my fair city.” And then the guard feels compelled to share everything about his town, reflecting his civic pride. “This is a bustling mercantile town on the intersection of two important highways, The Long Road and the Evermoor Way. Although our town is quite small, half the population lives on sprawling ranches and farmsteads outside the town. And as you can see, plenty of traders come through. And although this appears to be a small, quiet village, we have many entertainment options here.”
At that, Unla Kee perks up. 
“Now, the Market Square and the Tower of the Lord Protector are straight ahead down the road. The current Lord Protector is Daratha Shendrel. She’s from Waterdeep!” 
Vardis and Orsi,k, who are both Waterdhavians, just smile. Is she a good leader?” 
“She’s fair and clearheaded and widely regarded as good.” 
Thistle can’t wait anymore and asks about his Uncle Joon.
“A fairy? Looks like you?” 
“Unfortunately.”
“Well, he started drinking at the Everwyvern House but has now been reduced to drinking at the Talking Troll. He seemed very happy when he first came here but has gotten progressively sadder and is now reduced to drinking in our local dive.” 
“I’ll need a small bucket.”
“Why?”
“To dump water over his head.” 
The guard laughs and says, “You can’t miss it. It’s passed the market, next to The Cart and Coin **T8, **which sells and swaps horses and draft animals. And it’s across from Ransor’s Open Road **T7**, which has been left to ruin since its owner passed away.” 
Thistle, very determinedly, flies towards the Talking Troll **(T12)**.  
As they walk, Orsik and Unla Kee note the entrance to the town of the Triboar Arms **T16**, a tavern next to Wainwright, which is unimaginatively called “Wainwright’s Wagons **T5**. " This tavern is across from The Happy Horse Ranch **T4.**
They note caravans in large fields as they pass through the market (**T2** and **T3**). 
Thistle steps into The Cart and Coin and politely asks for a bucket that the owner, Syreen Widdens, is happy to lend and marches into the Talking Troll.
And it is indeed a dive. It’s a dim, smelly, low-beamed place crammed with mismatched, battered old furniture and drunks. The roof, Thistle notes, obviously has been burned and is yet to be repaired.
As he looks around the table, he notices the bird droppings and looks up to find birds nesting in the roof's holes. 
“Welcome to the Talking Troll; what can I, Kaelen Sarssir,  get you?”
His demeanor and flair cause Vardis to ask, IIshe an actor?”
“Why yes!” 
“The fairy?”
Kaelen points to a drunk fairy sprawled on a table, speckled with bird droppings. 
Thistle ssighed took his bucket ,and ddroppedthe water on his Uuncle
“I didn’t do it,” he shouts. 
“You did.” 
“I didn’t.” 
“Since I did, why don’t you buy me a drink? Kaele, this is my nephew. Did I ever tell you what a wonderful boy he is? He’s going to buy me a drink!” 
The tavern keeper is unmoved, “will he pay for all of your tab?”r
Uncle Joon waves him off and turns to Orsik, “if you’re willing to split the tab 50/50, I could double the size.” 
Orsik is confused, “how does this help?”
Uncle Joon realizes what he said and blushes. 
“Don’t worry, I used to be dumb like you. I understand.”
Kaelen shouts, “30 Gold. 15 is what he owes, but taxes, late charges, and that Imusto listen to his moaning all day.” 
“Why didn’t you make it 45?”
“Because I am not in it for the money. I’m a method actor, getting material for my off-High Road production.” 
Uncle Joon has heard enough: " Shut up. You’re never going to be an actor, you never were an actor, and you couldn’t act your way out of a wet paper bag.” 
Thistle stares at his drunk Uncle and says, “Adimond Kroskas.” 
“That two… “ and then he pauses.
Thistle glares at him, and Uncle Joon laughs. You never could intimidate anyone, my boy.” He laughs and shouts, “Kaelen, my credit just went to 30 Gold.” 
Orsik has had enough, glares with the same glare that stared down Bad Fruul, and says, “We have come a long way to ask you this question, and you will answer it.” 
Uncle Joon stares, crumples into a small ball, and whispers, “It’s worse than what you think, boy. Adimond was the alchemist who figured out how to make drakes from dragon eggs. His next plan was to combine faeries and dragon eggs to produce a more powerful and deadly drake. I was trying to make a deal with him.” 
Thistle interrupts, “wWhywere you doing this?” 
“To get your girlfriend back, my boy. Why else? You’re family, and he had captured her.” 
Thistle knows he is lying but chooses to keep that to himself. 
Orsik, meanwhile, managed to get himself a drink from Kaelen. The Talking Troll is a dive, but its wine cellar is extraordinary. It includes a scarce vintage of sparkling wine, Champagne du le Stomp, whose provenance is unknown. 
Kaelen explains, “I am told it comes from a hidden dimension where a Vampire rules. The elf who sold it to me said it was the last of its kind. I laughed.” 
Uncle Joon shouts, “Kaelen, you hack, you found the cellar full. That bottle is just some random bottle from some random vintner.” 
And then Uncle Joon turns to Thistle, “The thing is that the ancient dragons don’t have the same relationship that other people have towards their gods. And when Tiamat showed up, not every one of them was pleased. And Adimond he was working for some of those dragons. His plans to combine faeries and dragon eggs were too twisted for even the Cult of the Dragon. And so he found other sponsors, or maybe he worked for them the whole time. Those sponsors allied themselves with the ancient dragons, or maybe the ancient dragons allied with them. I have a name – the Kraken Society. And what I know is that the Kraken Society was working with those ancient dragons. I am not sure what kind of power or madness you need to have to work with ancient dragons; all I know is that I don’t want to be anywhere near such a dragon.”
“So where are they?”
“If I knew, I wouldn’t tell you. Ancient Dragons are the most powerful creatures in the north. Their lairs are impregnable. Don’t be a fool.”
“What about Laela?” 
“Last I checked, Adimond had her.” 

Thistle stares at Uncle Joon. He wants to scream at him, perhaps even dump something more caustic than water, but …

Before he can consider his next act of vengeance, two brothers walk in and discuss matters of Justice. 

Jacoby and Persson Hysstryn are upset that nobody is looking for their brother, Mirak Hysstryn.

Thistle watches Orsik try to minimize his presence so as not to be drawn into another small town’s politics.

The brothers notice Thistle’s bucket and turn to him, “You’re new around here. And you have a fine bow and may be willing to help us. We have a magical bow we would be willing to trade for information about our brother.” 

“Sound interesting.” 

“About time. We went to the Lord Protector, Delratha, and do you know what she said?” 

Jacoby interrupts his brother, “Let me see if I can imitate her.” He adopts the voice of the Lord Protector, much to the amusement of the clientele, “I understand that you’re still searching for answers, but I don’t think there is anything to it. Mirak ran away with a ladyfriend, much to the embarrassment of his wife, and hasn’t seen fit to contact you. However, I promise I’ll look into it, but if you bother that poor lady again, I’ll have the Twelve throw you in jail for a fortnight!” 

Persson grumbles, “My brother is in this town, and she is not looking for him because she thinks I’m crazy.”

Orsik, demonstrating his law enforcement credentials from Parnast, says in his city guard tone, “Sir, I think you have had a little too much to drink.” 

Jacoby then snarls, “We haven’t even started drinking. We’re sober.” 

Thistle ignores Orsik, “I am interested in helping.” 

He thinks it will be a simple tracking job, except Jacoby explains that his brother has gone missing for 10 years. Jacoby explains that Mirak’s wife claims he probably ran off with one of his lady friends. Persson adds that the enduring rumor that the lady friend was Urgala Meltimer, an innkeeper in Triboar. 

Jacoby mutters, “She’s always denied that.” 

Persson then points at another drunk, “We paid that drunken priest to ask his god, and he says that Helm says that Mirak is still at home and never left.”

Thistle notices that Orsik has gotten up and is paying close attention to the story. He turns to the fairy and says,” I think it’s a plane of shadow over the house.” 

Persson and Jacoby look at him, and Orsik declares - “I am smart.” 

Unla Kee whispers to Thistle, “We could lie. I mean, he wants information. Do we really need to find it?” 

Jacoby sees the peculiar gleam in Thistle’s eye and mutters, “I want proof. Not a pile of dust,t and you say that this is all that’s left of Mirak.” 

Thistle thinks that the best place to start is with the last clue. The cleric isn’t forthcoming or talkative until the fairy pays his tab. 

Thistle sits at his table and introduces himself. 

“I’m Sivlarren Loomshank, Loomy to my friends. I lived in Neverwinter, got into trouble with the hierarchy, and was banished to the Allfaiths Shrine in Red Larch. It’s not my fault.”

Loomy starts to cry. Thistle sighed; he knew that pattern of being drunk very well.

“I can’t leave because of my debts.” 

“Which?” asks Orsik.

“To this tavern.” 

Thistle smiles, “We just paid them.”

Loomy smiles, “I guess I better be going, then.” 

“But before you go, what did you tell the brothers?”

He repeats what the brother’s said and adds, “Helm said something about being… about not being buried in the ground, but above the ground. I didn’t tell the brothers because I thought it would anger them.” 

Thistle stands up and turns to Orsik, “We have two clues. Let’s go chat with Urgala.” 

Kaelen asks Thistle, “You taking your uncle?” 

Vardis answers, “I speak for all of us, no.” 

A little bit later, the group is at the Northshield House. It is a fine local inn — stately, clean, and quiet.

Standing at the door front is a large, powerfully built black woman with strong features and greying hair. 

“Fellow adventurers!” 

“You used to adventure? Where?” asks Orsik.

“Mostly in the Dessarin Hills. The place is full of mines and fortresses that have been abandoned. There is always a tomb to be plundered. But everyone’s got to retire eventually.” 

Thistle probes, “Any regrets?” 

Urgala's eyes unfocus, “That’s an odd and good question. Well, there is this one mine that I visited years ago.” She then goes on and on. Orsik tries to understand the story's point, but all he intuits is that a demon was involved. The story ends when Urgala mutters, “And I had to abandon the mine when my wife plunged into the Underdark.” 

Orsik acts horrified and mutters, “When did that happen?” 

“Over ten years ago.” 

Orsik taps his headband and then points at Urgala. 

Urgala stares at him, confused. 

He asks, “Did you work for the Lord’s Alliance?” 

“Those pompous fools? What makes you think I would? The North is about freedom. And freedom is antithetical to strong state powers. And the Lord’s Alliance is about strong state power.” 

“So you are an anarchist?” 

“I believe in small local governments.” 

Thistle then turns to ask her about the Dessarin Hills. “So where is this mine that goes into the Underdark? And why is there so much opportunity for plunder?”

She sighs and pulls out a piece of paper with a raw map and some notes about landmarks along the way. 

Thistle carefully copies the notes. 

He then asks, “Is there a lot of mining in the hills.”

She shifts her body uncomfortably. “The Dessarin Hills are rich enough to make you rich if you are poor and just poor enough not to be worth owning. So wild cat miners go there and try to make a killing. When they do, they get out but leave the mine and the settlement behind. Then, sometimes, monsters from the Underdark or other passers-by use those mines for different purposes.” 

Thistle nods. He then tries to shift the conservation to the investigation of Mirak but discretely.

Urgala sits down, “You’ve been talking to Jacoby.” She sighs and waves his protests off, “Okay, okay, please. Yes, Mirak and I were involved for a while. It was after my wife disappeared. I was grieving. He consoled me. But I didn’t have anything to do with him disappearing. I never believed the rumors, either. Mirak had no other woman than me. He was madly in love with me.” She then pauses, and an angry glint appears, “You ask me, I think his wife, Tolmarra, found out and … well, I shouldn’t accuse anyone of something I can’t prove.” Seeing Thistle smile and realizing she said too much, she adds, “ Either way, please don’t tell anyone. I don’t need the trouble. 

Thistle looks at her and says, “So much tragedy. You’re grieving, then get caught up in something, and then your other lover dies. I can’t imagine.”

Vardis, on the other hand, looks at Orsik knowingly and says, “Guilty. One dead lover is an accident. Losing two lovers is carelessness or a pattern.” 

Thistle mutters, “Let’s check out Mirak’s wife's house.” 

Urgala tells him, “It’s the Six Windows Inn.” 

A little bit later, Thistle arrives at what appears to be the low-rent inn. The building is two stories high, chilly and decrepit. 

From a distance, he flies up, intending to look around. 

What catches his eye is that there are no windows into the attic. 

“I need to sneak in.” 

Confident in his stealth, he flies towards a partly ajar window. 
And then it happens, that 1 in 400  chance event, and he doesn’t just make a racket, but breaks the window, then trips on a beam, and then falls out the window hanging by a rope. 

Vardis stares at the events and is confused, “What is he doing?” 

Unla Kee looks and shouts, “Maybe it’s a distraction?” 

Orsik nods his head, “Yes, it is. Let’s go in.” 

Meanwhile, Tolmarra is screaming at Thistle - “Thief! You were trying to sneak into my house to avoid paying rent! I will call the Lord Protector! I have rights.” 

Vardis and Unla Kee are hesitant until they see Tolmarra rush outside, past them, waving a rolling pin and screaming at Thistle. 

“Now’s our chance,” whispers Orsik. And the three of them rush in. 

“Let’s examine the attic,” urges Unla Kee. 

As they head up the stairs, many residents exit their rooms to investigate the commotion. 

Unla Kee whispers, “I could cast sleep?” 

Orsik growls, “A bit of overkill?” 

Adopting his best Chief of Local Militia tone, he growls, “Nothing to see here, folks.” 

The folks seeing Orsik and habituated to seeing many strange residents shrug and ignore the three adventurers. 

Orsik stands at the door to the attic and shoves it open. He walks up the stairs and discovers bones. 

Outside, Tolmarra is upset. She brought a rolling pin and a bag of potatoes. She throws potatoes at Thistle, shouting, “It’s because of people like you that we can’t have nice things, ” and “I never trusted a fairy.” 

While caught in a rope and hanging from the second-floor window, Thistle tries to explain, “It was a bumblebee, a giant Bumblebee.” 

Tolmarra suddenly stops. For some reason, the story of a bumblebee resonates with her, and she mutters, “My poor boy! A giant bumblebee! Let me get you a salve.” 

Tolmarra rushes into the house. 

Meanwhile, in the attic, Unla Kee’s investigation of the bones is conclusive, “This fits Marik’s description. But we’ll have to take the bones to speak with the dead; can’t find any evidence of what killed him.” 

So he reaches out to the bones, and the skeleton animates just as his fingers touch. 

Orsik strikes it down with one blow. 

Tolmarra, meanwhile, has arrived at the attic door that Vardis barred, screaming that she will call the police against the trespassing adventurers. 

Orsik opens the door and tells her, “I think we will be calling the Twelve.” 

She tries to put some resistance and asks for help from her renters. But the residents are more than happy to see her go, one muttering, “Good riddance on that peeping tom.” 

As they leave the building, Orsik shouts, “Good work on that distraction, Thistle!” 

With Tolmarra in tow, Orsik, Thistle, Unla Kee, and Vardis head toward the Tower, where the Lord Protector resides. As they arrive, they see a large crowd. The two brothers and a large group of onlookers are there. Everyone is screaming, arguing, and pointing fingers. 

Just then, the Lord Protector walks out and screams, “Silence!” There is a moment of silence, and the crowd roars again with theories. 

Delrathat squares her shoulders and shouts, “You four, and you two, and you, inside. Now.” 

A few moments later, the Lord Protector, the adventurers, the brothers, and Tolmarra are in her office. 

“Who is in charge?” 

Orsik says, “Thistle Underwood.” 

The Lord Protector tries to stand over Thistle, but he flies up to her eye level. She then mutters, “So?” 

“We were investigating a murder.” 

Delratha is furious. She turns to Jacoby and Persson and says, “I have told you once and a thousand times there is no murder. And now you get these adventurers to go on your wild goose chase, disrupting peace.” 
She’s about to go on a long rant, but Thistle cuts her off, “We found a murdered body.” 

Frustration toward the Lord Protectors explodes. Jacoby and Persson scream at Delratha, lamenting how they were ignored for a decade. They insist that their family's lack of wealth is the reason, claiming noble families would have been treated differently. 

Delratha sighed, “We’ll check this tomorrow when we can get a cleric to cast speak with the dead. In the meantime, why don’t you all leave my office.” 

Tolmarra starts to leave as well. Delratha notices, “Not you.” 

After leaving the tower and re-entering the marketplace, Tolmarra hasn’t left the building, which results in cheers and groans from various vendors. 

Unla Kee observes, “They were betting on who was going to spend the night in the tower!”

Orsik, ever eager to buy goods, arrives at the local Lionshield Coasters outpost. The existence of these outposts with their well-stocked goods is a mystery. Their competitors claim they rely on an interdimensional trading firm that supplies them with goods. The Zhentarim have been trying for years to figure out how the firm keeps its stores stocked while avoiding paying the tolls others must pay. 

To Orsik, this is the kind of place where you can get whatever you want at a fair, if not reasonable, price. 

To his surprise, the place is in shambles. The two owners, Alaestra and Narth, are distraught. Seeing Orsik walk in, Alaestra blurts out, “I’ll give you a 10% discount if you figure out who stole our treasure.” 

Orsik, always looking for a discount, agrees. And he’s convinced that he needs to re-establish the pre-eminence of his intellect after Thistle’s detective work. 

“So tell us what happened.” 

“Our vault was breached, and our profits and earnings were stolen. Our only clue is a leather harness and a masterly crafted grappling hook, which the thief left behind. These items were used to scale the outer wall of the store soundlessly.”

“Why do you need our help?” 

“We were going to consult the expert harness makers, but …” 

“But?”

“Some folks don’t like the Lionshield Coaster. They think we are predatory. We aren’t.”
“So some money was lost? What’s the big deal?” 

Alaestra blurts out, “They stole the key.” 

“The what?” 

Narth tries to get her to shut up, but she continues, “The vault contains our money and the key. The key is how we get more goods. We write a list and then put the key on the list. And then the goods arrive, and some of the money is taken. The company doesn’t like it when the key is lost.” 

Orsik suddenly realizes he has leverage and smiles, “I think 10% is not enough of a discount.” 

Narth glares at his partner, “How about 20%?” 

Orsik stares impassively.

“And 50 Gold?” 

Orsik stares impassively.

“I can’t go higher.” 

Orsik pulls out his hand, “done.” 

He puts the money in his bag and asks, “What harness maker did you want to talk to?”

A little bit later, the group is at the local harness maker’s place of business, “Othovir’s Harness Shop.” 

Once introductions are made, Othovir admits he didn’t make the harness. “It’s an impressive make, however. I’m pretty sure it’s made in Waterdeep since I don’t know anyone outside of Waterdeep who can rival my harness-making skill. I reckon it’s a decade old and would fit a person 150 pounds.” 

Unla Kee thinks, “I know a tavern owner who fits that bill.” 

Othovir notices the grappling hook and says, “You might want to talk to Harriet to get any information on it.” 

Harriet runs a smithy called Uldinath’s Arms, a rival of Forehammer's Forge, across the road. 

Harriet takes one look at the hook and mutters, “Not mine. Only Gherlyn could have made it. And don’t you dare accuse him of anything. I have known him since I was a child, and he’s a good man but a lousy smith.” The twinkle in her eye shows that she is teasing. 

Orsik takes the hook and marches into Gherlyn’s place of business, The Foehammer’s Forge. Gherlyn is too old to have been the cat burglar. 

Orsik knows how to impress dwarves like that, “We killed a hill giant!” 

Gherlyn’s eyes perk up, “I could tell you were a proud dwarf the minute you walked into my establishment.” He pauses, “Did he have any orcs? Did you crush their skulls?” 

“They mostly died in our traps.” 

“Good, good.” Then he stops and pauses, “Why are you here?” 

Orsik shows him the grapple. Gherlyn is initially coy about who he sold it to. Still, Orisk appeals to his Dwarven pride, and Gherlyn explains that he sold it to Darz Helgar, who lives in a cottage on the northern campgrounds as the caretaker.

 The group headed towards the Northern Campgrounds, which they had noticed when they arrived near the Market. The campgrounds are a fenced-in field where wagons can park and contain oxen. There’s also plenty of space to pitch tents and build campfires. To one side is a stone well for drawing water.

Orsik ignores all of that and marches towards the cottage. He shows Darz the harness and the grapple and asks him obliquely if he knows anything about it. 

Darz sighs and sits down on the steps of the cottage, defeated. “Yeah. It was me. I stole the gold and the bags. Didn’t wanna do it. Didn’t have a choice. That scum Kaeln - y’know, the guy who runs the shite tavern - told me he knew about my past and would go blabbering to the Lord Protector. He told me I had to steal it or he’d yap. But I guess the game’s up now, isn’t it? Good. I’m tired of running, tired of hiding. I did ten hard years in Waterdeep’s dungeon. I can do a few out here in Triboar. Just promise me you get that bastard; get him good!” 

Orsik’s eyes lighten, “You have contacts with the Thieves Guild? Give me whatever you have, and I promise you nobody has to find out.” 

“What about Kaelen?” 

“Oh, we will deal with him.” 

# Chapter 25 - The Murder and the Theft
A summary. 
- Jacob and Persson Hysstryn convinced Thistle to dig into the mysterious death of their brother Mirak. It turns out his brother had an affair with an ex-adventurer, Urgala Meltimer, who owns the Northshield House Inn. They then find out from a drunk priest of Helm, currently passed out in the Talking Troll, that Mirak’s body is in the Six Windows Inn. 

Thanks to Thistle's brilliant distraction at the six windows inn, the rest of the group sneaks in and uncovers, in the attic, the remains of Mirak. Using the remains, they convince the Lord Protector to at least put Tolmara in prison until a cleric can be found who can cast speak with the dead. The Lord Protector Darathra demands the heroes stay in town as they did cause quite a commotion until the next day. 

Urgala Meltimer tells them of the death of her first wife in the Underdark. And of the many places to scrounge for treasure in the Dessarin Hills. A place where there was enough to mine as a wildcat miner but not enough to be worthy of controlling. Also, bandits and uncivilized types lived there. 

She provides a map of one area she thought was of extreme interest. 

- When Orsik and Vardis decide to go shopping, they hit the Lionshield Coster Outpost, Lion’s Share. They meet the owners Alaestra and Narth, who are in disarray because their vault was breached. This vault included special keys to get inventory through their interplanar supply chain. 

The Lionshield Coster chain leverages interplanar trade routes. The famous gnomish sage KP first analyzed the impact on trade across the entirety of existence. KP’s research has been controversial because although achieving the multi-planar optimum for economic efficiency is possible, the equations ignore the impact on local social cohesion. Orsik and Vardis were now thrust into the exact kinds of social disruption that planerization and its advocates dismissed. The undercutting of local merchants made the locals look at Lion’s Share with caution and disgust. Alaestra is relieved to find outsiders who can help. 

The only clue was a leather harness and a masterly crafted grappling hook. 

The group first talks to the master harness maker Orthovir, who admits he didn’t make the harness. And he is sure it was made in Waterdeep. It can fit a thin person that is about 150 pounds. 

They then go to Harriet Uldinath (T17), who doesn’t recognize the grappling hook and insists it isn’t from her shop. She does admit that it is better than anything she could make, and it might be the work of her competitor Ghelryn Foehammer. 

Ghelryn Foehammer (T18) is a dwarf that believes in privacy. But is convinced that privacy ends at the limits of burglary. He reveals that it was sold to Darz Helgar. 

Darz Heglar is the campground overseer and an ex-criminal from Waterdeep. When presented with the harness immediately reveals that Kaelen, who runs the Troll Tavern, forced him to the caper. He was in Waterdeep over ten years ago, and spent time in their dungeons. Orsik is very interested and offers to keep this secret if Darz reveals anything about the thieves guild. 

“It was ten years ago, but I will.” 

# Chapter 26 - We are in showbiz!
## Orsik
Orsik stares at Darz Helgar, and the ex-con knows he will have to spill the beans.
A thieves guild in Waterdeep has to be a highly organized and secretive group, with a strict hierarchy, with the bosses giving orders that the rest of us have to obey. Any guild worth that survived for any period of time would rely on informants inside the city guard and connections into the local borough to stay one step ahead of the law. 
My guild was the Coffin Nail, and it was a well-oiled machine run by a ruthless leader who commanded a legion of skilled thieves and killers. They had a code, strict and unforgiving, and those who dared to cross them paid the price. Make no mistake, the Coffin Nail was a force to be reckoned with, and those who underestimated them did so at their own peril.
But that was ten years ago when I got caught, and since then, I have lost contact with my guild. And I am not even sure they exist. We had a lot of enemies, and a guild doesn’t survive long. 
Orsik is dissatisfied, “where could I find the trace of this guild?”
Darz takes in the dwarf, thinks, and then says, “I would go to Skullport.” 
At this point, Darz makes a hand signal that marks him as a member of the Zhentarim. Orsik reciprocates. 
“You’re a member of the Black Network? The Black Network in Waterdeep is a bit of a mess. There are two factions there. They say one is the real black network, and the other is led by Manshoon.”
He pauses, waiting for Orsik to recognize the name.
“Manshoon was killed by Fzoul but cloned himself. And the story goes that all the clones were killed. But I’ll tell you that I know Manshoon is alive and well. But then again, what do I know? And it’s been ten years.” 
Orsik, whose intelligence was recently augmented, recalls a story about a spell caster that could create copies of himself that he stashed around as a form of immortality. At the time, the story made no sense, but now he realizes the story was about cloning. And for all he knows, it was about Manshoon. 
Orsik turns to Thistle, “we promised him to keep quiet about his role in this crime. And we promised to deal with Kaelen Sarssir. We don’t have to keep quiet, but we did promise.”
“I’m here for the ride,” says the fairy. 
Orsik, Vardis, Thistle, and Unla head to Talking Troll, determined to discover more about the thespian’s plans. Kaelen Sarssir has dreams of putting on a plane in an off-high-road production in Waterdeep. 
## Thistle
“Let me see if I follow this. Darz is a city of Waterdeep criminal who was blackmailed by Kaelen to steal from the Lionshield Coster.”
“Yes.” 
“And Darz told us about Kaelen because he wants us to get some kind of revenge on Kaelen.” 
“Yes.” 
“And Darz doesn’t care if we rat him out.” 
“Yes.” 
“So then to the Talking Troll.” 
And Thistle, once again, picks up a bucket and fills it with water before he arrives at the inn. 
When he enters the Inn, Kaelen shouts, - “he’s already in debt. Drank the money you gave him; now he owes even more.”
“That’s uncle Joon.” 
Thistle sighs and dumps the bucket on his uncle. 
As Thistle stares at his drunk uncle in the other corner of the room, Vardis and Orsik talk to the drunken priest of Helm. 
The cleric raises his head, smiles, and asks - “Did you find the dead body?”
After Vardis nods, the priest mumbles, “Helm spoke truly to me.”
Orsik adds - “the mayor has possession of the body right now. We’re waiting for a cleric who can cast speak with the dead. You’re a cleric?”
“I don’t like talking to the dead.” Then the cleric starts to cry, “I used to live in Neverwinter. I had a real life there. I got to meet people, have a lively social life, and then I got into one theological argument with the high priest. And then I am called into a meeting, and before you know it, I am banished to the Allfaiths Shrine in Red Larch. I was a victim of temple politics!” 
Thistle turns back to his Uncle and mutters, “what have you done, Uncle?” 
Kaelen shouts, “are you here to cover his debt? He said you were good for it.” 
“No. He can stay in debt. In fact, spending time in a debtor’s prison might be a good thing for him.” 
The bartender grumbles, “will you have something to drink, or is this some kind of social call.” He then pauses, “unless you’re willing to review my script? Maybe you might be willing to do a few scenes with me?” 
Thistle is about to object when he realizes Orsik has a plan. The suddenly genius-level Orsik turns to the bartender with a malevolent smile and asks, “That sounds like a fantastic idea. But do you have a back room where we could act out?” 
Kaelen smiles. Thistle thinks, “not what I would be doing right now.” And when Kaelen leads them down to the cellar, the fairy is convinced that this will not end well for the thespian blackmailer. 
The cellar underneath the tavern is dark, dusty, and cramped. The ceiling is low, and the air is thick with the musty smell of old wood and mold. Cobwebs are draped over everything, and the dim light from a few flickering candles casts eerie shadows on the walls. The various shapes and sizes of the bottles are stacked haphazardly on shelves and in crates, covered in a thick layer of dust. Some bottles are caked in cobwebs, and others are empty and discarded. The whole scene feels of neglect and decay as if the cellar had been abandoned for many years. 
Orsik examines the collection and turns to Kaelen, “how did you afford this?”
“The former owner was desperate and sold it to me cheap. His name was, I know this sounds like a joke, Ha-Ha Pinkerton, a short man with bright, cheery cheeks, always smiling. He disappeared after selling it, and I ended up with this place. I was just as surprised as you were to find out how well-stocked the cellar was. Honestly, I didn’t know much about him, and I thought the place was overpriced given the required roof repairs.”
Kaelen pulls out the script and then describes the scene they are to act out. It’s a moment of high tension between a rogue, and his victim, the owner of the cellar, who is, in reality, a vampire. The Rogue doesn’t know that he, not the owner is the real prey. 
After his unfortunate acting performance earlier that day, Thistle defers to Unla Kee, who performs brilliantly as the vampire. 
Kaelen is delighted and is about to propose they try another scene when Orisk interrupts - “we’re looking for a special key, a key we are told you might know about, a key that belongs to the Lionshield Coster.” 
Kaelen turns pale, and despite his poor acting skills, Orisk immediately knows that his denials are all lies. 
Thistle smiles, the poor actor is about to have a new scene for his play. 
“Look, I am an actor, and the keys are worth a lot of money to the right buyer. And I just wanted to sell them to turn this bar into some kind of theater that people would come from all over to see.”
He sits down, “ever since I saw that play The Inn of Shadows off Highroad, I have wanted to be on that stage. And I thought I would get my big break if I put on a play here, where famous people from Waterdeep come.” 
Thistle turns to Orsik, “we could just take the key. But there is value in having the owner of a dive bar in our pocket.” 
Orsik surprises Thistle with a better plan, “what if we had an actor who had a theater house that the rich and powerful supported, and we were the people who discovered him?” 
Thistle has no interest in social prestige in Waterdeep, “we could sell the key? I mean, it’s worth over 5000 gold to the right buyer.” 
The old Orsik would have gone for that, but this one thinks through the problem and says, “this looks like the kind of thing that the Lionshield Coster would hire bounty hunters for. I mean, I would take that job.” He pauses, turns to Kaelen, and says, “I want the key and a bottle of Champagne du le Stomp.” 
“That’s my prized bottle.”
“That’s the price of harassing my friend.”
“Your friend?”
“Yes, and we want to be investors in your dream.” Orsik's investment is more of a loan-sharking arrangement, but Kaelen is so pleased with the idea of investors that he misses the malevolence in Orsik’s eyes.
He walks around the cellar, “I’ll be able to fix the roof, a special effects crew of magic users, not ALM, mind you, but still.”
He then pauses, “that will be at least 5000 gold!” 
Thistle whistles, “are you trying to con us? That’s outrageous.” 
Orsik steps forward, “how about 500 as an initial investment?”
Kaelen is disappointed. “Very well, I suppose I could put you on the leadership circle plaque. The founder’s circle is reserved for investments over 2000 gold.” 
Vardis is about to object, but Orsik has plans. “That’s fine. But when are we going to get a return?” 
“Well, it will take about a month for me to fix the roof and put on a show.” 
“And how many people will be at the show? You have to do marketing.”
“I have to hire actors and bouncers and put on a beautiful production. We will make money from the tickets and from the cellar! See a show, and drink some of the finest wine!”
“And what’s our cut?”
Kaelen suddenly loses his far-off dreamy look and turns ice cold. And Orsik realizes how this actor could have blackmailed a criminal. 
“We’re your partners. It’s an investment. This is not a charity. And for that cut, we’ll keep quiet about your role in the lost key.”
Kaelen stares.
“We’ll just say we found it under a rock or it fell off a wagon.”
Kaelen gulps.
“I think that’s worth a 70/30 split.”
“Of the profits.”
But Orsik has worked with actors and knows that no production ever has a profit “of the gross.” 
And with that deal, the companions head out to Lion’s Share. 
The owners, Alaestra and Narth, are delighted to see their key. Alaestra, in particular, looks relieved. Sensing Orsik is trying to renegotiate their deal, they don’t budge. Orsik gets the sense that as scary as he is, their employers are scarier. 
With their 15% discount, the companions buy healing potions and new weapons. Thistle splurges on some boots of elvenkind that he has to wait for until tomorrow. Apparently, they need to get new inventory, and for that, they need the keys. 
The companions head out to Urgala Meltimer’s Northshield House Inn as the sun sets. 
Urgala is curiously well-informed about what had happened in the Sheriff’s office. When asked why, she says, “rooms are not as private as people think in a small town.” She cries for a moment and then says, “I suppose that affair was never going to end well.”
She stops. She wipes her tears away, smiles, and says, “you brought some measure of peace to me. For that, you can stay one night for free with breakfast in my inn.”
But Thistle  can’t resist pushing his luck, “and your eternal gratitude?”
