And then Urgala flashes the fearsome temperament that made her a deadly warrior, “You’re a sweet talker fairy. But I was an adventurer for a long time, and I have dealt with your kind before. One night, a free dinner and breakfast, and say yes before I change my mind.” 

# Chapter 27 - Battle Outcomes
As the battle raged on, Urgala aimed at the fire giant with her short bow. She released the arrow, hoping to hit her target and do some damage. But unfortunately, her aim was off and the arrow flew by the giant, missing its mark.
Orsik, wounded in the battle, watched the arrow fly by and calculated the marginal wound it would have inflicted on the giant. He shook his head, thinking, "Wow, nice aim there. The arrow would have barely pierced his armor even if you had hit."
Vardis, also on the battlefield, saw the arrow fly by and muttered to himself, "We need those sprites with their sleep arrows. Maybe they could give us an advantage in this fight."
Watching the battle from the top of a two-story tower in the middle of the market, Thistle saw the chaos unfold below. The market itself was a shambles, with shattered stalls and scattered goods everywhere. He saw another fire giant frantically digging with their longsword to the north of the market. The giant had been holding a large rod, like a diving rod. The creature had marched towards the location it was searching. The rod was now tossed to the side.
Close to the entrance of the campground, Thistle saw Darz, the former thief from Waterdeep, lying dead on the ground. He sighed, knowing that the battle had already claimed the lives of several brave warriors.
To the northeast, an orc mounted on an axe beak attacks Urgala, who seems to ignore him. Beyond Urgala, her home, the Northshield House, is burning. Staring at the former adventurer, Thistle notices that she has burn marks.
“Probably fought off the magmin.” 
To the east, a giant is frozen in ice. The Lord Protector is dead. And Orsik is about to die. As for Unla Kee, he is barely alive. While Vardis has his sword extended, ready to attack the giant. 
Thistle knew that he would have to find a way to defeat the giants and protect their realm. He just hoped that he would be able to come up with a plan before it was too late.
“I got it!” 
Thistle casts a spell to revive the Lord Protector, then fires his bow twice, and the arrows hit. He smirks and thinks, “Urgala needs to attend my classes.” 
Unla Kee stares at the giant, and thinks, “The giant should be able to resist this spell with its constitution,” and then casts Thunderwave. The sound explodes from Unla Kee, and the ice that held the giant is shattered as he falls back. 
Vardis and Orsik swing with their blades but are unable to kill him. 
Darathra stood stares at the immobilized giant, her heart racing with fear and uncertainty. She knew that this was a defining moment - a moment that would determine the course of her life.

If she stayed and fought, she could become a legend. She could prove her bravery and skill to the world, and earn the respect and admiration of her fellow warriors. But there was a chance that she could lose, and that thought terrified her.
On the other hand, if she ran, she would probably live. She would be able to avoid the danger and the consequences of her cowardice. But she knew that she would have to live with the guilt and shame of her actions for the rest of her life.
Darathra weighed her options, trying to decide what was the right thing to do. Should she stay and fight, or run and live? The decision was not an easy one, and she knew that either choice would have its consequences.
Orsik shouts, “run!” 
And she runs and, as he runs into the tower, shouts, “stay away from the giants.” Unla Kee is hiding and asks, “are we safe?” Darthara stares at him and mutters, “as long as the giants don’t want to come inside.” 
Vardis points his bow at the giant, and the arrow misses, but his feet move him fast enough, so he is in the tower.  
And sees Orsik being crushed by the giant’s sword. Orsik’s last thoughts were,  “I told them that we shouldn’t fight these giants. Frankly, I don’t think these giants are important.” 
The wounded giant flees, but Thistle won’t let him get away, bringing him down to the point where the magic missiles of Unla Kee kill him. 
Vardis runs out, grabs Orsik, and pulls his friend into the tower. 
Meanwhile, the other Fire Giant is still digging. Thistle prepares to fire arrows, but Darthara stops him, “you will not fire an arrow at that giant. We will sit here and pray that it leaves without killing anyone or destroying anything else.” 
“But I could kill the orc.”
“You will not fire another arrow.” 
Orsik nods, “pointless battle. I told you guys we didn’t need to fight the giants.” He pauses, looks at the crafty fairy, and mutters, “not your best work.” 
Thistle nods, “not sure what I was thinking.  It’s like I was possessed by someone else.” 
“Anything we need to worry about?”
“No.”  
Darthara stares at Thistle, “the rest of us almost died while you were unscathed.” 
Vardis turns to Orsik, “hey, I patched you up. Go out there and fight, soldier.” 
“Nope, I’m out.” 
Darthara mutters, “we should monitor the situation and take copious notes.” 
Thistle sighs and realizes this battle is over, so he flies discretely toward the giant to monitor what it is doing. 
A few minutes later, the giant drags a large 11-foot metal band. Orsik and Unla Kee determine how much it weighs and conclude that it is between 1 and 10000 pounds. 
Thistle flies in closer to get a better angle but cannot figure out what the band is made of.
The giant, fortunately, doesn’t notice the tiny fairy. 
On his way back, Thistle takes in the damage to the village. Urgala’s inn, The Northshield House, is burned, as is the Apothecary and several cottages and farms next to it. 
Orsik meanwhile feels wrong about Darz and commits to raising him from the dead. 
Darthara seeing no danger, heads to the market square where Orgala, Narth Tezerin, the goods store owner, and Othovir, the harness store owner, discuss what happened. 
Narth shouts, “what the hell happened here And your house, it burned to the ground.” 
“Houses burn,” Urgala whispers, “but they can be rebuilt, especially if I am in a strong community of friends.”
They all hug. 
Darthara then stares at the mess and asks, “how are we going to clean up this place.” 
## Orsik
"We need to collect the treasure of the dead fire giant before anyone else shows up," Orsik said, his voice filled with urgency. "And we need to raise Darz. That's very important to me. And we should also cast speaks with dead on the giant, to learn more about its plans and motivations."
He paused for a moment, his mind racing with ideas. "And we should also meet this powerful cleric! A powerful cleric cannot often be found in a small town like Triboar. We should make the most of this opportunity."
Thistle nodded in agreement. "We can sell tickets to see her," he suggested. " 
“Maybe she can join the Founder’s circle,” he sneers back. 
Thistle doesn’t register the sneer and smiles, “yes! And we can promise to make some play about her god.” 
Orsik glares. 
Orsik glared at Thistle, clearly annoyed by the fairy’s enthusiasm. But before he could say anything more, his attention was drawn to a tall, striking woman who had just sat down at a right angle to them.
She had a smirking green grin and long ash blond hair tucked under a large brim hat, exposing an elven ear. Her leather armor was dyed in various places with accents of blue, red, and yellow, offering an aesthetic, if not strategic, value to her ensemble. Orsik's eyes turn into slits at the sight of her, and he couldn't help but wonder who she was and what she was doing in Triboar. 
A silky smooth voice says,  “what a fine mess you have gotten yourselves into.” 
Orsik decides she is of no value and ignores..
She turns to Darthara and says dismissively, “it’s good to see that only half the town was burned to the ground.” And then the woman has an evil grin as she says, “I don’t think you’re going to make today a touchstone of your re-election campaign? Not exactly the kind of achievement you want people to remember. Fortunately, I will be there to remind them of it. ” 

She then turns to Orsik, “I saw a battle from my ranch, and decided to come and see if you needed any help.” 
Orsik stares and then realizes that she is the cleric. 
Thistle asks, “are there other candidates for mayor?” 
“Me, Cindell Trueshot.” 
Darthara spits towards her, “she’s a carpetbagger.” 
But unbeknownst to Darthara, a farmer mutters next to Orsik, “Darthara isn’t from around here. She is from Waterdeep. We voted her in because we thought she brought some Waterdeep magic.” 
“You’re both carpet baggers,” blurts Orsik. 
Cindell smiles. The town of Triboar has two primary factions, those who have lived here some the beginning and are mostly farm people, and those who have come here to take advantage of Waterdavian nobles who use the town as a vacation area. 
The woman in green is most definitely not a farmer. Her bearing is too formal as if she is accustomed to being in charge. “I am Cindell Trueshot, and I was relaxing in my vacation home.” She pauses, “it’s a small place. Nothing special. Just six rooms.”
Unla Kee asks, “Yard?” 
“Yeah, a couple of acres. Nothing extraordinary. Nothing spectacular. When I saw the giants come, I figured I should come down, but I wasn’t dressed to fight, and I know Darthara would never let the guards leave the city unprotected.”
Darthara glares at Cindell – “I sent the twelve to protect the ranches!” 
Cindell ignores the comment, “It took me a while to get ready.” 
Orsik turns to Cindell, “so you were here earlier to speak with the dead with a skull?” 
“Oh, no. The skull was brought to me. ” Cindell is confused by the idea that she would come to a meeting to talk to Darthara. “I don’t usually come into town. I have people for that.” 
Unla Kee smirks, “because why leave your palatial residence?” 
“I wouldn’t call it that, more of a pied-a-terre.”  
Vardis laughs, “A bungalow.” 
“Yes, that’s what I would describe it as.” 
## Vardis
“What is it with this Cleric?” he thinks. 
Orsik, who is really impatient, asks, “so oh, great and wonderous cleric.” 
Cindel seems frustrated. Vardis groans internally, “one of those clerics who doesn’t want to be a cleric.” 
“I don’t like to be defined by my profession.” 
“So how much money for the services a raise dead and a speak with t dead spell of the profession you are not defined by?” 
The cleric thinks for a moment and then says, “There is some woodwork that I need to be done around the house, 90 gold for the speak spell, and 1250 to raise someone from the dead.” She pauses, “unless Darthara needs those spells?” Then feigning concern about Darthara’s wounds, she says, “you need some healing. Maybe I should heal you first? We can’t have the Lord Protector too wounded to defend the town and hide in a tower while a giant rampages around the town.” 
Thistle, who keeps track of the kitty, winks at Orsik and Vardis and announces, “Well, we have this fantastic theater package! Three free admissions to the opera house!” 
Vardis look at Cindel, wondering how she will take it. 
“Not a big fan of theater and Kaelen’s work. I don’t think that will work.” 
Vardis points to the Fire Giant. Orsik nods.  
On the body, they find a ruby gem that should help them cover the raise dead spell. 
Thistle removes toenails to make potions of giant strength. Thistle sighs as he remembers that Apothecary is destroyed. 
Vardis finds a bag of salt, “probably a gourmet who liked his food seasoned.” 
Ghelryn, a smithy seeing Vardis look over the body whispers, “the heart can be turned into a living lamp. It would allow me to cast light in any room.” 
Thistle overhears and says, “would be useful for the theater.” 
Vardis tried to remove the heart and fails.
## Unla Kee
Unla Kee, the narcissistic neutral evil magic using halfling, had always been fascinated by the power of death. And so he knew that he had to take advantage of this opportunity. “Let’s see what this giant has to say!” 

He thought, "I want to learn the secrets of the fire giant's plots. I want to know what he was planning, and how I can use that knowledge to my advantage.” 
The dwarf, Orsik, whose newfound intelligence is a welcome surprise to everyone, asks the first question, “why did you come to Triboar.” 
As the mouth of the dead giant moved and its eyes turned to Cindell, Unla Kee's heart raced with excitement. He knew that this was the moment he had been waiting for - the chance to learn the secrets of the fire giant's plots.
The giant's voice boomed, and Unla Kee quickly translated its words for Cindell. "To rule over the giants! Duke Zalto will soon rule over all giants!"
Darthara’s eyes widened in shock. She had never heard of Duke Zalto, but she knew that this was not good news. If a single giant, let alone a duke, was able to gain control over all of the giants, it could spell disaster for the realm.
Unla Kee, on the other hand, was thrilled. He saw this as an opportunity to gain power and influence, and he was determined to use this knowledge to his advantage.
"We must do something about this," Darthara said, her voice urgent. "We cannot allow Duke Zalto to gain control over the giants."
Unla Kee nodded, a sly smile crossing his face. "Agreed. But first, we must gather more information. We need to know more about Duke Zalto and his plans. And then, we can decide on the best course of action."
Unla Kee asks, “what was the other fire giant digging from the ground?” 
“Why do you want to know? What is it that you seek?”
“A question with a question,” grumbles Vardis. 
Unla Kee asks Orsik, “If we answer his question, does that count as a question?” 
Cindell says, “yes.” 
As Unla Kee and Darthara discussed the information they had learned from the dead fire giant, Thistle interjected with a question. "What was the band that was carried out?"

Unla Kee paused, his mind racing. He had almost forgotten about the mysterious band that the giant had mentioned. "A piece of the Vonidod," the Fire Giant declares, his voice laced with intrigue.
Thistle's eyes lit up with recognition. "The Vonidod? That's an artifact that is related to the killing of dragons. It's said to be incredibly powerful, and it's been sought after by many throughout the realms."
Unla Kee nodded, his thoughts turning to the implications of this information. If the fire giant had been searching for a piece of the Vonidod, it could mean that there were more pieces out there, waiting to be found. And if all of the pieces were brought together, it could lead to the world's end.
He glanced at Thistle, impressed by the halfling's extensive knowledge of the realms. He knew that Thistle's uncle Joon had traveled far and wide, and had likely shared many stories with his nephew. Unla Kee couldn't help but feel a twinge of jealousy - here he was, working hard to gather information, while Thistle had access to a wealth of knowledge thanks to his uncle's travels.
But then he shook the thought away, reminding himself that he had his own strengths and resources. And he knew that, if they wanted to stop Duke Zalto and his plans, they would need to work together and use all of their knowledge and skills to their advantage.
Orsik asks, “where can we find Duke Zalto?” 
“In his throne room!” 
Unla Kee asks, “where is the throne room?”
And then the fire giant turns angry, “I will not answer any more questions from the likes of you. You are nothing compared to the fire giants and Duke Zalto. We will rule overall!” The dead giant then begins cursing the other giants, particularly the storm giants. 
Cindell 
“So about the raise dead.” 
*The dwarf tries to scam me with a claim that the ruby he just found is worth thousands. But I know a guy, and let’s just say I am a good judge of character. But then there is the more exciting opportunity to make Darthara look bad. *
“My dear Orsik, you want to raise Darz? He’s such a sweetheart! Of course, I’ll do it for a significant discount. Because we are a community, a family.” 
*Did he just flirt with me?*
“We’ll have to have dinner sometime.” 
*I misread that.*
“With your friends? I don’t want this to be awkward.” 
*Now he’s trying to get me to go to the Talking Troll. Apparently, it’s got a great wine list. That’s why not that he owns the place and will get a significant discount. And that my going there will get the other ranch owners to visit. *
Cindell then turns to Darz and raises him from the dead. 
“I’m alive.” 
He then notices how everyone tries to ensure the right people get the credit. First, the dwarf says how he paid to raise him from the dead. 
*Right, because he likes me, not because he needs more information. I know the type. *
Then there is Cindell. 
*Seriously, lady, you’re a cleric. Raising from the dead is what you do. And a discount? *
And Darathra
*No, I don’t think this was a play for my vote, but damnit, she raised me, and I like living. *
After recovering a bit, Darz stares at the hole in the campground. 
*Really? They think I knew there was this massive artifact 12 feet down in the middle of this campground? I would have dug the hole and sold it if I had known about it. *
The following day, Darz goes to the Talking Troll to talk to his benefactors. Seeing them at a table eating breakfast, he stops and sits and down.
“I’ve never been dead before.” 
They stare at him. *Alright, this was going to be my retirement job, but I owe them. *He sits down, and then after exchanging some pleasantries and talking about the afterlife, he shares his story.
“An old friend, a merchant from Mirabar, saw a dwarf cleaning stable in Xantharl’s keep.” Sensing their confusion, he says, “It’s a fortified village on the Long Road. My buddy recognized the dwarf from a wanted poster while traveling south through Longsaddle. The dwarf is Weevil, and the wanted poster makes him worth 5000 gold to the Axe of Mirabar.”
And to answer Orsik’s question, “I was keeping this job for myself, but I am old, and you guys did bring me back from the dead.” 
He swallows his drink and then leaves. 
## Urgala 
As Urgala walked into the dilapidated tavern, she couldn't help but feel a sense of nostalgia wash over her. The place had seen better days, that was for sure. The wooden floorboards were worn and creaky, and the bar counter was stained and scratched. The roof had holes in it. 
But despite its rough exterior, the Talking Troll still had a certain charm to it. It was the kind of place where travelers, adventurers, and outcasts gathered to share stories and ideas, to find companionship and solace.
As she scanned the room, her gaze landed on a group of heroes sitting at a table in the corner. They were a motley crew - a human warrior, a dwarf with a thick beard, a fairy with shimmering wings, and a human mage. They were deep in conversation, their expressions serious and focused.
Urgala her way over to the table and sat down, drawing the heroes' attention. She had seen their bravery in action and knew that they were the ones who could save the realm from the fire giants.
“Good on killing the giant, but you could have helped us deal with the magmins. The giants were just looking for something.”
“1st of the tenday quarterbacking,” mumbles the dwarf. 
“And your poor uncle.” 
“He’s fine,” says the fairy. 
She waves to Kaelen for a drink, and after he brings it, she continues, “well, after seeing what you did for my poor Miraz and for the town, I wanted to offer you something. Something that should help the next time you fight a fire giant. One of my former adventuring companions is a wealthy knight named Harthos Zymorven, and he had a giant slaying longsword. The last time I spoke to him, Harthos was living in Zymorven Hall, near his ancestral keep on the Rauvin Road northwest of Silverymoon.” 
The dwarf is interested. 
“He’ll part with it if you drop my name and tell him that you plan to defeat giants and save the realms.” 
“He’ll just give it?” 
“Well,” she smiles, “we go way back, and he’s always willing to do me the right favor.” 
She finishes her drink and leaves. 
A few moments later she returns, “I did save the map.” They look at her confused. “The map to the mine I didn’t explore that my wife died near.” 

## Orthovir 
The harness maker marches up to the table the adventurers are sitting, looks around as if he is worried about something, and then begins talking. 
“You seem like good folk, and you did me a favor. I don’t have any money, but I will tell you where to find it.”
They stare at him, shocked.
“I hate the Margasters. We have a history. And that’s all I will share.”
They nod. 
“Well, I know where the magic stash is kept for emergencies. The family owns a three-story tower in Silverymoon that has a detached carriage house on the property. You interested?”
They nod.
“The carriage house is a 30’ square room with two floors. The door is protected with an arcane lock. Two human guards are upstairs. And the carriage is protected with an alarm spell. The magic items are hidden in a compartment under the passenger seat.” 
Othovir gets up and leaves. 
## Darthara, The Lord Protector, 
She enters with two members of the 12 and sits at the adventurers’ table. 
“I’ve been told that the locals have shared information about treasures as a reward?” 
“You could say that.” 
She rubs her chin and waves for a beer. 
“I belong to an organization,” and she makes the harper hand gesture, “that needs to know what happened. The idea that fire giants would make it so far into the Derrien Valley without anyone knowing is worrisome.”
She pulls out a platinum badge with the Triboar insignia, “visit Danivarr’s house in Everlund. Everlund is east along the Evermoor way and gives the badge to Dral Thelev, the one-eyed half-orc proprietor.” 

# Chapter 28 - To Yartar
## 1st day of the 2nd tenday of Kythorn
### Vardis 
The Troll Tavern was particularly active that morning. 
It wasn’t the patrons. 
Although Uncle Joon was still there. 
“Did that guy ever leave,” wondered Vardis. 
Vardis laughs as Thistle dumps another bucket of water on him. 
And the drunk priest was still there, moaning about his new assignment. “I suppose I should help him,” he thought, then remembered, “nope, that’s not what I do. People need to take accountability for their own lives.” 
As he scraped some bird droppings from his shirt, “and the birds, they birds are still here,” he muttered. 
The parade of locals had come to offer them information about where they might get help, money, or both.
“Where to next?” 
As the debate continues, Vardis endures it only because there is ale. His meditation is interrupted when Ghelryn Foehammer, the smithy, coughs. 
Vardis turns to the Dwarf and stares as he presents a sealed letter.  
“I have a letter of mark for you. At great personal danger, you killed a fire giant. Now it would have probably been safer to let the Giants be and defeat their allies, that was trying to destroy the town, but your refusal to back down from the hated enemy is a tribute to you and your bravery. Take this to King Morinn or Queen Tithmel at the Citadel Felbar. They will reward you for your heroism.” 
Vardis takes a swig of ale, “more choices.” 
Vardis stares at Unla, who is interested in the map. “If we could figure out how to make my tower a flying tower, that would simplify these travels.” 


“And you, more powerful,” Vardis thinks but only mutters, “choices.” 
“And of course, Orsik, the newly intelligent Orsik, has to add more choices to the mix,  he thinks just as the Dwarf says, “we do that too.” 
Vardis stares at his friend, “you had straightforward plots in your head; now you have very complex ones.” 
As he looks at the map, he thinks, “whatever, if we are going to have choices,” and says, “and don’t forget Urgala’s dungeon or cave complex or whatever it was in the Dessarin.” 

## Orsik
“Let’s go east to Yartar, then we can take a boat north on the Surbrin River and hit Zymorven Hall. We can go to Silverymoon and then deal with the Harpers.” 
They all stare at him.
“It’s pretty simple, we minimize travel time, hit most of the quests, and are closest to the reward from the dwarves.”
He is confused. 
“Look, you must apply a simple bit of mathematics and optimization.”
More staring.
“It’s just the Travelling Orc Hoard Problem.” 
He shrugs.
As they start to leave, Thistle reminds Orsik that they should ask their new partner when the play will be ready.
Kaelen is non-commital until the Dwarf threatens to bruise the playwright’s skin. He agrees that there should be a production in 45 days. 
“So let’s say 5th day of the third tenday of Mirtul. We can use the Summer Solstice to advertise to all Waterdhavians who are coming to escape the crowds.” He pauses, “Can you get Cindell to come for dinner? If you could, it would help ticket sales among the Waterdhavians!” 
Orsik snarls. 
“I guess that is a no.” 

And with that, the group starts their long walk to Yartar. And it was a walk because although Orsik tried, he could not convince Narth to hand over some horses. 
Though the journey was uneventful. A raiding party of black-haired, blue-eyed Uthgardtians tried to ambush them, but that ended poorly for the Uthgardtians. 
“I almost had convinced them to run away,” but it turns out that these raiders hated Magic Users. So when Unla Kee tried to intimidate them, it enraged them.
Looking over their corpses, he could see why they were so desperate, for they had nothing of value. As he examined the bodies more closely, he noticed their totem, an elk. 
“What a waste of time.” 
Even Thistle’s encyclopedic knowledge of the area was of no use. 

## 3rd day of the 2nd tenday of Kythorn 
### Thistle 

“Well, Uncle Joon had visited this place,” he remarks. “At the intersection of the River Surbin, the Dessarin River, and the Evermoor Way, Yartar serves as a fortified crossroads waypoint for caravans and merchants. Yartar is also renowned for its expertise in river barge construction, their fine craftsmanship seen from Silverymoon to Waterdeep and along riverbanks between the two.”
Orsik smiles, “I knew it! We can take a river.” 
Thistle glares, waits for Orsik to realize that he was rudely interrupted, realizes that won’t happen, pauses, and then continues. 
“The city has seen an influx in population lately with more and more merchants choosing to stay and set up their wares at the Fishyard rather than continuing on their way.” 
Thistle then turns to Orsik, “this is where the information gets a little bit dodgy. According to my Uncle’s tales, along with an increased population, there has also been a steady rise in crime and shadowy business dealings.”
Orsik smiles, “my kind of town.” 
“And first, we have to get through the YSA.” 
Thistle worries that Orsik will make a fuss, as he tends to when the Dwarf insists on talking to the guards. 
“We’re just here to put up some posters for our play and hand out some playbills.” 
The guard looks at him, confused, and says, “okay, but remember, this is a law-abiding town.” 
And, what Thistle worried about happens; the guard recognized Orsik.
“Hey, you’re that guy who did that thing.” 
Thistle grabs his money a little bit tighter and takes a step back.
Orsik looks at the man, smiles, and says, “I’m not him.”
“No, you’re not. Orsik was a short dumb dwarf, and you are anything but. He would have pulled his sword and thrown himself out of Yatar.” 
Thistle smiles and hands the guard a playbill, “No harm, no foul! And enjoy our play!” 
The guard looks at the playbill and asks, “is this an expressionist, naturalist play, or is it a more classical production?” 
Thistle realizes that Kaelen hasn’t yet written it and steps forward confidently, “it’s an original production about the struggles of Uthgardtian tribespeople facing the onslaught of civilization that is taking their ancestral lands. Civilization is presented as a river boat from Yartar, the fastest, most unsinkable boat that will carry civilization everywhere. The boat gets destroyed as a result of the hubris and callousness of the captain. There is a romance angle between one of the officers and an Uthgardtian tribal leader.” 
Vardis smiles and says, “it’s spectacular. It’s both well crafted and has a timely message.” 
The guard seems impressed, excited, and wants to help. “You know, given what you said, this play has the potential to hit a broad cross-section of people. You should go to *The Three Rivers Festhall*. It’s at the crook of where the River Surbin and Dessarin River meet. It’s seen better days, but it’s still popular.” 
Thistle smiles and then asks, “sounds like a place sailors might frequent, so yes.” 
“Oh, in that case, I would go to the *Fishyard*. It’s an open-air market with dozens of vendors offering all sorts of imported goods, local catches, and delicacies. You can also find other traders with goods from Calishman, home remedy potions, luxurious fabrics, and more unique magical components there.” 
Thistle nods in approval. 
“Oh, and Yartar is home to Lionshield Costers.” 
Thistle really wants Orsik to stop, but he doesn’t. 
“We have one of those in Triboar; they get around?” 
The guard’s face darkens, “their use of pocket dimensions avoids the use of traditional trade routes. And that impacts local trade and local guards who need some money.” 
Thistle concurs but doesn’t tip the guard. 
He turns to Vardis, Unla Kee, and says – “to the Fishyard.” 
## Unla Kee 
He arrives at the open-air market and sees all sorts of goods and treasures among the stalls and tents. Calishman perfumes are bottled in shimmering crystal infused with the essence of exotic flowers. At another booth, home remedy potions brewed by a red-haired self-proclaimed Wise Woman named Talni Barcoba, who is quite beautiful, despite a large scar, are advertised. These potions will cure any ailment! 
Another merchant, Imber Chen, sells luxurious fabrics dyed in rich hues and woven from the finest threads. Imber is a lavender-haired gnome whose clothes match his violet eyes. 
Orsik asks – “paper?” 
Unla Kee whispers, “there was a merchant named Baash’a Kweelani who sold magical goods for the discerning magic users. She was bald and had bright flaming red skin, which is somewhat exotic and unique.”
But then Unla Kee notices the food. Stalls offer the freshest catches from the three rivers that glisten in the sun and smell of the wild. And for his sweet tooth, there are treats made from the finest ingredients and drizzled with sticky honey.
Kee knew this was a place where anything was possible, and the most fantastical dreams could be made real. 
“Not, too, end of the world, real,” he thinks. 
“We need to find someone who can tell us of the goings on of Silverymoon. A merchant, perhaps?” Thistle mumbles. 
“To Imber!” shouts the magic user. 
Imber has a clear Calishmanian accent and is startled when he sees a fairy in front of him, and says, “why I haven’t seen a fairy in months!” 
“Not my uncle?” 
“I did not know that ferries have uncles.” 
Unla Kee is surprised when Orsik rolls his eyes and says, “casual racism rolling off the bat. Lovely.” 
But then Unla Kee notices that Imber is offended. “I thought that they were born through asexual reproduction in egg creches. At least, that is what I read in the great sage’s work. Not every species must have binary sexual relations.”
Unla Kee enjoys watching Orsik put off balance, “fair.” 
“There you go, with heteronormative assumptions about binary reproduction. Just because it works for Dwarves does not mean that it’s how other species work.” 
But then, Unla Kee knows that Orsik will seize the moment, and he does, “oddly enough, we are doing a play precisely about that.” 
“A progressive play!”
“Yes, on the 5th day of the 3rd tenday of  Mirtul.”
“The name?” 
“Unnecessary,” shouts Vardis, “All you need to know is that it is progressive, and it is at the Talking Troll.” 
Unla Kee admires the chutzpah of the two. 
“Well, this sounds very exciting. It’s a two days journey.” Imber pauses, “are there places to eat and stay there?”
“Absolutely,” Orsik assures him. 
Thistle adds, “this is going to be a major transdimensional event.” 
“Now, back to trade, I have plied the rivers for many years,” and rattles off many cities, and among them is Silverymoon.
Unla Kee nudges Thistle, who asks, “tell us about the city if you have recent news.” 
“Silverymoon is a spectacularly beautiful city and long a powerful and influential member of the Lord’s Alliance. Our Waterbarron has allied itself with them to stay in power. And as he should, and as any goodly person should, they are the only power trying to bring order into the anarchist chaos that the Harpers promote and the Zhentarim exploit through their taxations.” 
Unla Kee smiles a little when he sees Orsik clench his teeth at the mention of the Lord’s Alliance and the dismissal of his new friends. 
“It’s a peaceful realm where many races live together for common knowledge, celebration, and defense.” 
“And they all have uncles,” says Orsik. 
“Well, yes, they do. Because it is populated primarily by the goodly races. Humans, Dwarves, Gnomes, Elves, Halflings, and Half-Elves.”
“Half-orcs, too?” asks Vardis.
“No, I said the goodly races.” 
Unla Kee almost coughs when Orsik says, “Oh, you did say that.” 
Thistle now remembers what his Uncle had told them. Silverymoon is one of those lovely places that like to have the “right” kind of people living within its city wall. 
When Orsik hears it, he says, “it’s a cultural fit. I get it. So only humanoids.”
“No, I said goodly races.”
“Yeah, I am not going to use that phrase.”
Imber glares. 
Unla Kee thinks about how progressives can be so curiously blind to their faults. He has exploited this property for his benefit many times in the past. And now he understands what the issue the Margasters had with Orthovir. 
### Orsik 
“Will you take us to Silverymoon?”
“Oh no, I can’t.”
Thistle asks, “do you know a guy.” 
“
Yes, I do. I happen to know a guy. He and I used to be friends. I think the word friend is appropriate.” He pauses. “Friends come and go.  It’s the enemies that accumulate. We just drifted apart. He’s Cap'n Squint, a small halfling in a tricorne hat, barks orders at men twice his size and stomps the planks as if he were ten feet tall.  He was working on my boat, and then he made his fortune. Now he has his barge. He’s his own person. And I think he’s leaving tomorrow for the return trip.” 

“Where is he?”

“Well, he’s leaving tomorrow. Tonight he will gamble on the Grand Dame, the most famous and luxurious boat owned by Lord Khaspere Drylund.” Imber pauses, “It’s said that Lord Drylund wants to be the new Waterbarron when the old one dies. And he’s been working the voters.” 
Orsik is about to leave when Imber stops him, “I’ve told you everything I know. I made an introduction. I even let you tell me about my play. And yet, you repay my generosity without considering my wares or making a fair offer on any of my goods.” 
Imber takes out some silks that he shows to Orsik, trying to tempt him. 

“Paper?”
“No.” Then he pauses, “but I have this fine white cloth that you could easily paint a big banner on announcing your play.” 
“Oh, that’s a good idea.”
“And I could make an introduction to a guy I know who is a member of the Hand of Yartar. He knows someone who could get you a stall so you can hang your banner.” 
Thistle gets the point, “or we could ask someone who already has a stall?” 
Imber smiles, “I, well, it never even. I am so glad you brought it up, Thistle. I didn’t want to mention it, but I would be more than happy to.” 
Unla Kee asks, “do you work on commission.”
“I expect a flat fee.” 
Orsik thinks about it and then proposes a radical new scheme that has never been heard of in Yartar, “we’ll give you a special coupon code that you will give your customers. We will then use that coupon to identify people you sent and give you a 10% share of those tickets and those tickets alone.” 
Imber stares at him.
Unla Kee sees the wisdom and nods approvingly.
“No. I want half a gold piece to have your banner up for the next 43 days.” 
Orsik is frustrated, “but what does the conversion funnel work? How do we know how effective this ad spend is? There is just not enough data for us to value this spend.” 

Thistle pipes up, “well, in the Fey, we had this magical dust you could use that would allow us to keep track of anyone who touched something. We could use something like that?” 

Orsik stares at him, and Thistle shakes his head as he says, “but I don’t have any of that here.” 

So the Dwarf turns to Imber and shakes his hand, “deal.” 

Thistle took advantage of this information to discover what kind of play the folks of Yartar would find interesting. He quickly determined that they would like a play with many actors and action that explored the challenges of the mercantile class in a world set to steal their riches, confronted by powers always pursuing them. He also noticed that they intensely disliked dictatorial and hereditary governments. 

Orsik and Vardis used that information to reach out to the markets of Yartar to advertise the play. 

Unla Kee, on the other hand, tried to see how he could set up a pump-n-dump of the tickets 
related to the play. Thistle decided to follow him.

Thistle and Unla Kee were surrounded by members of the Hand of Yartar. A woman’s only thieves guild that operated in Yartar. 

There were three thugs and a powerful magic user who explained that it wasn’t personal, just business.

They also explained that Unla Kee was not part of the contract, but the business of running his scams without their cut was unacceptable, and they asked him to stick around while they killed Thistle. 

Thistle fled and disappeared. The magic user and two of the three thugs could not find him. 
Unla Kee negotiated with one of the thugs a deal where he gave him a cut of the VIP tickets at a much lower price that the thug could resell and thus make money.

Thistle arrived in the morning just as Captain Squint was about to leave with the group on their way north on the River Surbin.

### **Rumors and Stories of Yartar**

The city has a dictatorial ruler called the Waterbarron. The current waterbarron Nestra Ruthiol. She has aligned herself with the Lord’s Alliance to keep her power. 

At the intersection of the River Surbin and the Dessarin River and the Evermoor Way, Yartar serves as a fortified crossroads waypoint for caravans and merchants of all sorts. Yartar is also renowned for its expertise in river barge construction, their fine craftsmanship seen from Silverymoon to Waterdeep and along riverbanks between the two.

The city has seen an influx in population lately with more and more merchants choosing to stay and setup their wares at the Fishyard rather than continuing on their way. With the increased population has come a steady rise in crime and shadowy business dealings as well. While the Waterbaron works alongside the Lord's Alliance to try and preserve the peace, the Hand of Yartar and other less well-intentioned factions continue to swell in membership and prominence as well.

With the brewing tensions, the city's prominence as a whole has always been diminished and tarnished by vicious infighting and subterfuge. Since the Waterbaron serves for life, those who desire power often find means of disenfranchising the position's influence within the city. With conflicts and schemes constantly undermining the city from speaking with one unified voice, the Waterbaron is forced to rely on outside connections with other Lord's Alliance members to implement any meaningful change. And with some much competition between merchants, business is often loss and won by rumors, sabotage, or other underhanded means of hindering the competition.

The Shields of Yartar, a coalition of 150 mounted guards work to keep the peace within the city and protect it from the threats of the wilderness and reside in Shield Tower, the massive keep to the west of Yartar.

# Chapter 29  - Circles within Circles
The fiendish half-human, half-demon spawn smiles at Unla Kee, tries to charm the halfling, and fails as the diviner shifts the fate in his favor. They hiss their frustration. 
Orsik turns to the mage, “We are going to exploit this town’s racism against undesirable races and run to the tavern we left Thistle and Vardis in and scream – ‘ungodly races’ and have the mob deal with them.” 
Unla Kee smiles, “that is an overly complicated plan. I love it. But we need to say something that will catch their attention….” 
“I got it. We were trying to take a walk. And these attacked us, these MONSTERS. I thought that this was a town of good races. And LOOK at what we found!” 
The diviner smiles – “and I’ll cast a lightning bolt into the tavern and blame it on the Cambians!” 
“Pin a robbery, start a lynch mob, and blame them for murders.” 
“Yep.” 
The two smile at each other. 
The Cambian, for his part, is pleased to see the dwarven fighter still there and tries to charm him but fails. 
Now Orsik and Unla Kee run. 
Orsik turns to Unla Kee – “remember, we want them to follow us, so don’t hide.” 
Unla Kee turns to Orsik – “I love this plan, but did you notice that the creatures can fly?”
“No.” 
The Cambian keeps up with the would-be thieves of House Margaster and strikes Unla Kee with his spells. 
A badly wounded Unla Kee, who has lost 2/3 of his hitpoints, turns to Orsik – “This plan is not that good anymore.”
“Yes. We’ll run into a house and try and get help.”
“I’m not sure a commoner will offer much resistance to the demons.”
“How remarkably unselfish of you!” 
“No, I am just thinking about how ineffective your plan is.” 
Orsik growls and barges into a house. The houses in Silverymoon are not locked. After all, only good races live here.
Inside the house is a sharply dressed halfling, wearing finely crafted jewelry, drinking some fine wine inside his drawing room, which is well-appointed with expensive furniture. The room is lit with candles. 
“DEMONS ARE CHASING US!” 
The gentleman drops his glass of wine and shouts, “DEMONS!” 
Unla Kee shouts, “Yes!” but is struck dead by the other infernal creature.
The first half-demon follows Orsik into the house – “We want our master’s treasure. Give it to us, and our master will forget this.” 
Orsik is determined to get his treasure, “we steal from you? YOU’RE DEMONS!” He turns to the halfling, “I thought this was a nice town!” 
The  Cambian that killed Unla Kee shouts, “Found it.” 
The one in the house says, “If you leave and don’t say anything and leave the city by tomorrow, your friend doesn’t need to die. He’s almost dead.”
Orsik is too stunned and stares. 
“Very well,” the creature nods to his friend, who kills the halfling. 
The Dwarf, who remembers that Unla Kee had said the world would end if he died, stares at the sky, “Huh, I guess the world isn’t coming to an end.” 
He then walks out of the house and looks at the body. “Maybe if he stays dead, the curse will be true.” So he picks him up and takes him to the Dancing Goat.
He shows up with the corpse over his body, and the tavern keeper shouts, “What happened?” 
“Impossible, such creatures do not exist in Silverymoon.” 
Orsik glares and heads up to the room upstairs. 
In the morning, Orsik attempts to carry Unla Kee’s corpse and is stopped. 
The tavern keeper, Nyalah Uuseff, dressed in formal silk clothes and her gold, says, “I have asked the guard to come and investigate these tales of ungoodly races being present!” 
“I agree, but we need my friend alive to provide testimony.” 
“The city guard will decide that!” 
The Silverwatch arrives, and the Captain announces, “We are here to take you to city courts.” 
Orsik growls, “Look, my friend who was killed can provide a reasonable testimony. Can we go get him raised?”
The Captain is stunned, “a murder by a demonic power?” He then pauses and looks straight at Orsik – “yes. We have many different temples; which one would he prefer?”
Thistle looks at Unla Kee and realizes he is “unclear.”
Orsik, however, remembers the kind of person his companion was and whispered to Thistle, “Cyric?” 
Thistle remembers that Uncle Joon had said that the city of Silverymoon specifically banned Cyric from their town and says, “No, no, we don’t.” 
“Well, he’s a halfling; the Goddess Meilikki loves them.”
And so they head out to Her grove, where the high priest is shocked to hear that a demon has killed Unla Kee. She takes his body to the inner sanctum and returns with the halfling no worse for wear and lighter one magical ring. 
She says, “Captain, the tale of the demon is true. You must look into this.” 
Orsik turns to him – “Wasn’t there this theory if you died, the world would end?”
Unla Kee sneers and says, “But I didn’t die, did I? I can see the future. I wasn’t scared. I knew I would be revived.” 
He pauses and says, “I was in no mood to race through the city, dodging flaming bolts, trying to raise a mob. I figured getting killed and having you raise me was a much better way.” 
The Captain turns to the group and announces, “You are expected in the Star Court.” 
Thistle groans, “Let me guess, Lord Margaster is the judge?”
Orsik and Vardis stare at each other, “THOSE MARGASTERS?” 
Before they know it, they have walked up the steps and are in a room with a judge and a beautiful woman wearing fine robes with two gorgeous men standing next to her. 
She asks in an imperious voice, “Are those the thieves?”
The men who flank her in a silky voice say, “Oh yes!”  
Sitting as far away from her as possible is the same halfling whose house Orsik had barged into. The Judge asks him, “Vinby Hearthgood, do you have anything to add?” 
“That dwarf is the person who smashed into my house.” 
Unla Kee is outraged, “Dude, I got killed, and you are worried about breaking and entering?” 
The Judge turns to Lady Margaster and asks, “Xamlyn, I understand you wish to arrive at an agreement.” 
“Yes, I want to talk to the Dwarf and his friends about a possible arrangement.” 
Orsik crosses his arms in front of his chest, squints, and says, “I am making no deals with a literal devil.” 
Vardis turns to Orsik, “These Margasters are rich and powerful, and we don’t want to make enemies of them.” 
The Judge seems to echo that when he declares that these accusations against a paramount citizen of Silverymoon are unprecedented and require the most robust evidence.
“How about some magic spells.”
“An excellent idea, bring the court magister.” 
Xamlyn realizes that this will be a disaster and declares, “No. I will not subject myself to this intrusion. There was no crime. This halfling claims someone killed him with a bolt of fire. This other halfling claims that there was a dwarf in the house. I claim that a drunken party went wrong, and they want to blame me.”
She takes her men and leaves. 
The Judge turns to Orsik, “I know you. You’re the one that did that THING! And now you are creating another mess for our city. The Lady Margaster is making sizeable investments in our city. And we need her investments and the investments of the City of Waterdeep. The War of the Dragons was a disaster; we are still reeling from that. I can’t have you here. The real estate market has collapsed. We need her money, and I order you to be gone by sunset.” 
Unla Kee asks – “can we buy some properties?”
“I don’t want anything to do with this town,” grumbles Orsik. 
The Judge turns to Thistle and says, “You are a credit to your kind and are demonstrating more goodliness than these folks are.” 
And with that, Thistle flitters out and says, “I’m done.” 
A little bit later, the group is leaving Silverymoon through the Sundbar gate and heading south on Rauvin Road to Everlund. The journey is mostly uneventful. 
As they leave, Thistle says, “Uncle Joon said that the Silverymoon betrayed the Silver Marches and their dwarf allies. So it’s cool that you hate them.” 
Orsik looks at him, “Look, I come from an upper-middle-class family, and I am not into these blood feuds of my ancestors that my parents keep talking about.”
Thistle stares, “It was five years ago.” 
“Yeah, you’re right; they are on my shit list.” 
Later in the day, before sunset, the heroes arrive after an uneventful journey to Everlund. The road to Rauvin Road from Silverymoon has the Nether Mountains to the east and the Silverwood forest to the west. The dark, brooding peaks of the Nether Mountains are the source of many legends about lost Netherese magic and a repository of tangible riches such as granite. The mountains are also full of tribes of orcs. At the same time, the eastern peaks are home to several blue dragons chased out of the northern Anauroch by a dragon known as the Doom of Desert. As for the Silverwood, it has grown atop the rugged, hilly terrain between the Evermoors and the Nether Mountains. The Silverwood was once part of the High Forest. Still, loggers working the woodlands on either side of the Evermoor Way have carved a great wound through the terrain over centuries. Bare hillsides littered with stumps line this gap.
They take in the sights as they arrive at the city of Everlund. The town is situated on the banks of the Rauvin River. It is one of the North’s most active mercantile communities. A thick stone wall encloses the city, pierced in five places by gates. Like the spokes of a wheel, broad, straight avenues lead from each entrance to the Bell Market at the city’s center. The streets are clean and wide enough to accommodate large caravan vehicles. Soldiers of the city’s army make a show of patrolling the walls to reassure citizens and visitors as well as to discourage attackers. The buildings of Everlund are stately and well-maintained, with steeply pitched rooftops and tall spires that sport colorful banners. Two bridges span the river, with parks and trees along its shores.
Orsik calls the gate guards and shouts, “Tell us something.” One of the guards, a younger human, squints at him and says, “Hey, are you Orisk?”
Orsik deceives him with his charm, and the guard apologizes. Unfortunately, the second, older dwarven guard says, “Orsik was a dumbass, you fool. This Dwarf is a knowledgeable and thoughtful person.” 
Orsik swallows his pride, “What do you think of Silverymoon?” 
The Dwarf starts to speak – “Until recently, Everlund was a member of the Lords’ Alliance. The five leaders who currently comprise the city’s Council of Elders voted three to two in favor of separation and, in a symbolic show of support for Sundabar, condemned the alliance for failing to come to Sundabar’s aid during the War of the Silver Marches. Silverymoon sat on its hands and did nothing.” 
“So you don’t like them?”
“Don’t like? I want to see the city ground and burned into ashes.”
Orsik smiles and continues to talk to the Dwarf. 
Thistle sighs and pulls Orsik away, reminding him of their mission to go to Danniver’s tavern, find a half-orc, and give him a platinum medallion. 
Orsik tells Thistle, “I’m thinking of signing up.” 
Vardis rolls his eyes. 
As they head down the main thoroughfare, the group notices an older woman talking in front of attentive young men about a monster that ravaged the city a long time ago. As Orsik passes by, she shouts, “One day, that monster, the Kraken, will return to destroy our city with its powerful tentacle and mind control! The Kraken will come with his allies; we must prepare because they are more powerful than anything we have seen!” 
“Are they giants?” asks Thistle.
“No. Much worse.” She pauses and looks straight at Orsik, “Do you want to join our group and fight against the ancient evil.”
“Yes!” 
She walks up to  him, grabs his arm to test his strength, smiles, and says, “Well, that will be five gold pieces, and you will get a certificate declaring you a fighter of the light!”
“We don’t have five gold pieces.” 
She looks sad. “Any money?”
“We’re broke. Spent our last gold piece and badger pelt to raise our friend from the dead.”
Thistle concurs, “Nothing in the kitty.”
“Can we do something?” asks Orsik. 
“Bus tables?” asks Vardis.
“No, that would not be appropriate. You must go out to the town, and there is a cave a few miles out that you must find, and in that cave, you will find some kobolds that have stolen an artifact, the star of light that belongs to our order! If you brought it to me, I would be willing to waive the fee.” 
“Is this L-I-G-H-T or L-I-T-E?” asks Unla Kee. 
She ignores him.
“So what does it do?” asks Orsik.
“Nothing, it’s just of symbolic value.” She dismisses them, “When you have the artifact, return it to me.”
“What’s your name?”
“Miery – not Mary. My mother worshipped the goddess Mielikki and wanted my name to reflect it.” 
“Okay, see you soon.” 
As they approach the Tavern, Orsik announces that they will not drink because they are broke. Unla Kee won’t accept that – “I could sell my spell books? Or my tower?” The group, seeing his earnestness, tells him no. 
As they walk in, they notice that it is an old and prominent place to eat that was once a noble’s mansion. As they walk, they realize this rambling inn is a favorite haunt for adventurers from the clientele. 
The manager, Dral Thelev, a half-orc who looks masculine, asks them, “What will it be?” 
Orsik presents the platinum brooch. Dral sees it and smiles – “Something must be amiss with my friend Darathra Shendrel. But before we discuss this,” he starts to pour wine, “a bottle of my finest Silverymoon wine.”
Orsik almost spits out the wine, “Do you have something that is not from Silverymoon? And you aren’t a goodly race.”
Dral smiles, “Yes, you are correct. I like to drink the finest bottle of wine and remind myself how much the citizens of the good city would be appalled.”
“How did you get it?”
“I have friends. The racist owner would be appalled to know I am drinking his wine.” And then he begins to talk about the city some more. He explains how the town is populated by people living on the reflected glory of having one of the Silver Sisters be their founder and that they are stuck in the past. And what’s worse, the younger sister is now the open Lord of Waterdeep and head of the Lord’s Alliance. 
“And that bridge is such a scam. You go to see the bridge, and you can’t.” 
Dral nods and holds up his cup. 

“Bottom’s up!”
And with that, the heroes find themselves teleported to a room. 
“Welcome,” says a Turami man dressed in the distinctive garb of that area.
“Where are we?”
“In Moongleam tower, of course.” 
Vardis says, “Yeah, I know that.”
“Of course you do. It’s the most famous building in the city.”
Vardis looks at the man more carefully and realizes he is a harper. 
“Who are you?”
“Krowen Valharrow.” 
“Why are we here?” 
“You told Dral something important. He teleported you here.”
“Why?”
“Because if you have a secret agent coming in from the outside world, having them show up at the door of the Moongleam tower is like waving a red flag at the Zhentarim and Lord’s Alliance and telling them – looky hear! But if an adventurer goes into a hangout for adventurers and then gets teleported here, no one is the wiser.” 
He pauses, “Each faction is busy monitoring every other faction, and no one is paying attention to the giants.” 
Vardis explains, “Well, the Ordning of the Giants has been sundered. Unclear as to why.”
Krowen clicks his lips, “Well if you are willing to find out and fix it or tell me how, I can help you quite a bit.” 
They stare at him, “Let me show you something.” He points to a teleportation circle on the floor and hands them a map. “These circles will provide instantaneous travel through the North. I am sure that would be of help.” 
![image]()
Orsik is shocked. The Zhentarim do not have things like that. 
And, he says, “Drathara had some gifts for anyone who would take this quest on. More gifts to encourage you to take the quest. They are yours to keep.” 
He hands them a coffer, “it was a birthday present for exactly such a moment.” 
They open the coffer and find several spell scrolls.
“So?” 

# Chapter 30 - FInally working for the Harpers
## 5th day of the 3rd day of the month of Kythorn
### Krowen Valharrow
Krowen stares at the adventurers in his tower and inner sanctum, and he wonders if they are the tool he can use to unlock the secrets behind the fall of the Ordning.
Since Ezra had discovered that using mercenaries derisked core Harper agents and provided plausible deniability, the Harpers had completed missions that would have been impossible in the past. Krowen was an old-timer and believed in the old ways, but these were desperate times, and this group had been sent by Dalthara herself. 
“So, are you in?”
They stare at him. 
“Damn it,” he thinks, “I am coming off desperate. How did Ezra pull this off with the First Faction?” 
The dwarf looks at him and says, “we have been doing this for some time, so yes.” 
“Excellent!” 
After a few moments, the dwarf asks him – “so, how do we go out?” 
Krowen looks at him and thinks, “the reports on the dwarf are that he is close to the Zhentarim.” 
“The front door!” 
His friend, the elf, looks at Krowen confused, “but you went to all of these lengths to send us through a special teleport spell involving some alcohol to hide our identity, and now you want us to walk out the front door?” 
“Drat,” Krowen thinks, “he saw through my ruse to burn Orsik.” 
Krowen thinks, “excellent point! This entirely slipped my mind.” 
The elf stares at him, confused. Krowen puts on his best confused old mage look and says, “I can’t believe I forgot that!” 
“Well, where should we go next?”
“Yartar to find the sword.”
Vardis asks – “so much must we pay?”
“Nothing!”
“Only our loyalty,” mumbles the dwarf.
“Is that a problem?” 
“No!” says the dwarf apologetically, “I always wanted to work for the Harpers.” 
And then the fairy asks Krowen, “off-topic, have you ever enchanted a flying tower?” 
Krowen explains that the orb necessary to turn a tower into a flying castle is a device that can only be obtained from the Cloud Giants, either by persuasion or violence. 
When Vardis tries to suggest that they could handle a cloud giant, Krowen blanches – “those fools will get themselves killed. Let me see how to dissuade them.”
“My dear young man, if a fire giant is a fully grown adult, then a cloud giant is superior specimen of a human being, trained to fight like the greatest of fighters and armed to the teeth with magical weapons and defenses of all kinds.”
Seeing the look on the elf reassures Krowen that he has nothing to fear from this.
And with that, Krowen smiles as they teleport to his agent in Yartar.  
### Thistle 
“Time to get that sword!” shouts Orsik.
And with that, Thistle finds himself in a garden with statues of frolicking dryads and satyrs situated among the flowers and stone benches. Thistle stares at it and groans, “this is all wrong! That fairy would never be frolicking with Alciabes! She hated him!” And then the illusion falls apart, and he sees that he is in a 40-foot-square guardian patio enclosed by an 8-foot-high wall of ivy-covered stone. The garden is in disrepair, with broken benches, weed-infested flowerbeds, and shattered statuary surrounding the circle they just arrived in. 
As Thistle stares at this embarrassing attempt to turn his culture and people into props of a garden, a masculine-looking person arrives and shouts, “who are you?” 
Before he can answer, Orsik shouts, “Agents of the Harpers.” 
“Agents or Contractors.” 
Thistle stumbles, “I don’t recall signing an explicit contract.”
The old man grumbles, “where did you come from?”
“Everlund?”
“You must have spoken to Krowen.” 
Vardis affirms that. 
“That pompous two-bit self-aggrandizing … He’s decided that having agents, even agents that are not sworn to the cause of the Harpers, is better than no agents. Being a Harper meant something. It wasn’t just a job!” 
“Sounds about right.” 
The old man grumbles. 
“I am Kolbaz and your host. Welcome to Yartar.” 
### Orsik
“Harthal? Do you know where we can find him?” 
Kolbaz stares at him. 
Thistle nudges Orsik and whispers, “hey, I think Harthal was dating a rogue, and the local rogues guild probably knows where he is if we can get in touch with them.”
Orsik nods, “should we ask about them?”
But Orsik can tell Thistle is uncomfortable meeting with the Hand of Yartar after their attempted assassination.
As Thistle and Kolbaz talk, Orsik notices how Kolbaz is dumbfounded that Thistle is an assassination target. “They don’t do that kind of wet work.”
Thistle, visibly annoyed, says, “the pointy end of their blades were pointed at my neck.” 
“Well, you must have really pissed them off. Perhaps you could tell me what you have done?”
“I did nothing!” 
“Well, fine, then, can you tell me about yourself? Perhaps we can find out.” 
Orsik nudges Thistle, “ask about the Lord’s Alliance.”
“How do you feel about the Lord’s Alliance?” 
“The Lord’s Alliance is a league of wealthy lords whose only interests are the powerful and their powerful families. They do not care for the little people. We are just bodies to be used to fight their wars and sacrifice in their vainglorious conquests. They are attempting to create a supernational state that will unite the various city-states, but it will never happen because those of us who value freedom will never let it happen.”
Thistle is satisfied and then asks, “You aren’t allied with the Cult of the Dragon?”
Orsik smirks, that faux-pas is what he used to do all the time. But then he remembers that Thistle doesn’t know the history of the War of the Dragon. The Harpers first identified the risk and rallied the various factions to fight. 
Kolbaz straightens his back, “I FOUGHT THE CULT AT THE WELL OF THE DRAGONS!” 
And Orsik is impressed as Thistle doesn’t even miss a beat, “so you know Adimond Kroskas?” 
“Nope never heard of him.” 
“But he was a lead cultist. I thought you harpers knew about him. Look, the cult of the dragon is trying to assassinate me.”
Kolbaz laughs, “that sounds preposterous. Why would they want to kill a fairy.”
Orsik is intrigued, “why is that preposterous?”
“Because the cult was destroyed. Why would they waste time on him?”
Thistle is indignant, “because I am looking for him, and they want to clean up their mistakes. A key ingredient for the ritual was the spring’s first Blossom that Adminod collected.”
Koblaz thinks. 
“The Spring First’s Blossom is a powerful artifact.” 
The old mage sputters and shouts, “I know what the Spring’s First Blossom is. I got a first-class education. I’ll have you note!” 

He then pauses, “but that wasn’t used in the ritual. I was involved in the research, which was not used in the ritual.”
“Are you sure?” 
“Yes. Very odd.” 
“Are you really sure?”
“YES!” 
Orsik realizing that Kolbaz’s professional pride has been hurt attempts to soothe him by praising the quality of the illusion and his role. “Only Thistle saw through it.”
“Yes, it was an inaccurate representation of the Seelie court. Look at all of these errors and that!” Thistle starts to point to all sorts of mistakes of the illusion.
Kolbaz stares at him – “why we should meet up and have some tea and discuss how to improve it.”
Thistle glares, “to make your magic fantasy land a bit more realistic as you treat my people as props to amuse?” 
Kolbaz is taken aback. Orsik turns to him, “about the Hand of Yartar?”
“Yes, I don’t have links to the Hand, but I know where you can find them. The Wink and Kiss is their hangout. Go to the tavern and ask a server. The rogues are all over the town.”
Vardis and Orsik are surprised, “there are a lot of rogues in this town?”
Thistle stares at them, “you mean you didn’t notice?” 
“Alright, let’s go,” shouts Orsik.
Thistle demures, “I don’t want to go to a bar full of people who want to assassinate me.”
Vardis and Orsik tell him not to worry and that they will defend him.
Even Unla Kee, still recovering from his ordeal, shouts, “they will have to get through me to get to you, Thistle. All 6 of my hitpoints.” 
Thistle stares at his companions and realizes he has allies, “I love you guys.” 
## 6th day of the 3rd day of the month of Kythorn
The Wink and Kiss Tavern is near the easternmost gate of Yartar and is famous as a safe place to do business. Inside, it has a maze–like collection of compact rooms, concealed side passages, and sequestered passageways. The fest hall was a bawdy and fun establishment that didn’t take itself too seriously. It was an excellent place for those who wanted to find easy-going friends for the evening. 
Due to its distinguished patrons, the Wink and Kiss is declared a safe haven for anyone who enters. Weapons were checked at the door, and rowdy behavior was not tolerated. 
“Good thing we have spells,” mutters Unla Kee as they are led to one of the rooms. 
### Vardis
Vardis notices an old painting of the former owners, “Asklar and Beldorm, I think I knew them. Can’t remember for the life of me.” 
As the group settles down at a wooden table, with finely crafted wooden chairs stained dark cherry, Orsik mutters, “we are broke.”
“Completely?” 
“Well, we sold everything to get him raised.” 
“And I love you guys.” 
Vardis turns to Orsik, “so we have no money and have to bribe someone. Maybe they’ll want something else?” 
“Loyalty? I am so done with that.” 
“I was thinking companionship.” 
Thistle gulps.
“I am the best looking.”
“That’s true. You do have that goth elf look,” says Orsik.
“Thank you.” 
A tiefling barhop arrives at the table and asks for their orders. 
Vardis gulps and says, “we’ll split an order of water. That’s free, right?” 
She glares.
“Just joking. Can I have a look at the menu?” 
She hands him a menu and then comes back 10 minutes later.
“So?”
“I need to go use the restroom,” says Thistle, and Vardis thinks, “coward.” 
“We haven’t been this broke since you did the THING, Orsik,” mutters Vardis. 
The tielfling sizes them up and says, “if you are that broke, then I know a gal.” 
“You’re going to offer us a job even though you know nothing about us?”
She looks at them – “you’re the group that defeated the giant in Triboar and has been advertising the play? Right?” 
Thistle nods vigorously, “that’s us!” 
Vardis turns to Unla Kee – “the pump part of the pump and dump cycle is going well.” 
“Yeah, the thing is, you don’t have a name. See, I have a name, Maia, short for Qumaia. And folks have been calling you the ‘three half-pints and a pint.’
Vardis laughs hard at Orsik’s discomfort. 
Thistle flutters, “No, our name is – Thistle Wendrod and Companions!”
Vardis laughs harder when Orsik blusters, “so now it’s all about you!” 
“Yeah, you guys are local celebrities.” 
“So buy us a round.”
“Not that famous.” 
“Think of the traffic we will bring in,” Unla Kee whispers.
“See, I know this gal who needs people to get some other people. And you guys are handy with a sword, and you took down a giant.”
“Two,” insists Orsik.
She smiles and corrects herself, “two fire giants. I’ll be back in a few.”
Unla Kee glares at Vardis, “we should gamble. I change the fates.” 
“Yeah, but if you die, we can’t raise you. And that’s a problem because the world will end.” 
“No, I won’t be there to STOP the end of the world.” 
And so over the next five days, “The three half-pints and a pint” are bounty hunters. They split into two groups. Unla Kee stalks his prey, exploiting his ability to twist the fates, and captures “Dashing” Dallon Flek, a handsome redhead that has bright eyes and rogueish good looks. The others capture Tink Urbelle, a grubby gnome with curly hair and animated facial expressions. 
## 30th day of the 3rd tenday of the month Kythorn
The Hand of Yartar handing out the bounties, Elicia “Kal” Kallaphan, is a severe half-elf that sees through the “Three half-pints and a Pint” attempt at humor. She has blond hair, wears a cloak at all times, and carries a finely crafted rapier. 
“Here’s your payout, 200 Gold, 50 for me, and so 150 for you.
The group takes their money glumly. That’s a lot but not enough.
And so later that evening, they are again at the Wink and Kiss, and the tiefling Maia is also there. 
“So now, will you give us the information we need?” 
“Oh, no, I’ll give you the information you need to get the information you need.”
“And?” asks Vardis.
“That will be five gold.” 
“Seriously?”
“Yes.”
So he hands her the five gold, and she points to another lady dressed in white at the bar named Eltio. Vardis sizes her up as a sullen, distant, and dangerous player. 
As Maia leaves, he says, “this is the best beer I have ever had.” 
Vardis turns to him, “longest run of sobriety?” 
Meanwhile, Unla Kee had been on a lengthy drinking binge and has now been sober for two weeks. His eyes are sharper than usual, and he mutters, “the longest.” 
Eltio explains that it will cost them 500 gold to get information on Harthal, or they can get Tamlin Zoar to waive the fee. 
“Who is that?” 
Eltio calls Maia, “I thought you said they were serious people?” 
Maia tries to defend the introduction, and ultimately Eltio sends her off with a warning. 
“Tamlin is a legendary member of the Hand of Yartar who built her own town on the Evermoor Road on the way to Everlund. If she thinks we should help you gratis, then we’ll help you.”
Vardis can’t resist, “are you a patron of the arts?”
“Yes!” 
“Well then, how about we give you some tickets to the up-and-coming production of our play!” 
“I don’t think so.”
“We will make you a GOLD sponsor.” 
She looks uncertain and says, “No, that’s not what I want to spend my money on.”
“But you are a patron?” 
Orsik turns to her and grouses, “look, we’ll get this letter as long as you promise to bring 50 friends to our play!” 
She looks at him and says, “deal.” 
## 2nd day of the first 1st tenday of the month Flamerule
### Koblaz
“Oh gods, no. That’s not possible,” he mutters. 
A flash of lightning, and then he crumples on the ground. The feet of a small person sound on the floor next to Kolbaz, and an ominous deep growl of a voice that sounds impossibly loud for the person’s body says, “you meddling little fool.” 
## 2nd day of the first 1st tenday of the month Flamerule
The three half-pints and a Pint arrive at Calling Horn. 

# Chapter 31 - Into the Moors
## 2nd day of the first 1st tenday of the month Flamerule
After a two-day journey, our heroes arrive at Calling Horns and head straight to the Inn. Calling Horns, as Thistle recounts, was nothing more than a trailside inn until a few years ago, when Tamalin Zoar bought the establishment and retired here. 
She attracted settlers to the region, giving rise to a small village whose citizens paid monthly fees for her protection. She’s still spry at 60 and is the law.
According to [Eltio](https://www.worldanvil.com/w/the-forgotten-realms-kostadis/a/eltio-person), she can provide the 3HPP a letter of mark that can be redeemed for the service they requested.
Broke, and desperate to get their sword, the heroes knock on the door of the Inn. Outside the Inn are a gruesome pile of bones from dead orcs who wandered too close and a large stable that houses several horses. 
Orsik turns to everyone – “she sounds like an amazing person.” 
They are let in and look around. The main room has several large tables and a kitchen in the back. The tavern is full of local villagers enjoying the food and company. 
As Tamalin enters the room, Orsik approaches her and declares – “I’m really interested in meeting you. Your business skills and vision lead to this investment. You created this thriving economy.” He pauses, “I’d like to learn from you.” 
Tamalin is surprised and flattered. She turns to the patrons at the main table and tells them to find other accommodations, shouts to a person in the kitchen to bring drinks and food, and begs the 3HPP to sit down so she can talk and share her experiences. 
It’s clear that as an adventurer at the end of her career, the idea of sharing her stories with others beginning their career is appealing. 
Thistle brings up the play in Triboar as an initial investment they made. Tamalin is impressed but mutters, “hits driven businesses are not very sustainable. But if you get a big hit, it’s worth more than a dragon’s hoard.” 
The conversation drags into the night, and eventually, Tamalin mutters about not getting older and says, “well, you brought me good company, and I have a soft spot for folks like you. Here have two rooms in the house for free.” 
Orsik and Vardis stay in one room, Unla Kee and Thistle in the other. As they all are about to get a good night’s sleep, Tamalin bangs at their doors, “trolls! I have a pair of trolls attacking my stables and several fine horses there.” 
Thistle flies out of the window and strikes one of the trolls outside the stables with his bow, directing Thuul to cover the troll in acid to stop its regeneration.
Unla Kee opens a window and then, from a distance, uses firebolts to kill the troll.
Orsik and Vardis grab their shields and weapons and, in their pajamas, rush out to face the 
The first troll dies outside of the stable. The second troll is killed inside the stables, but not before it slaughters two horses.
Seeing the 3HPP in action, Tamalin invites them to have a drink before they go back to sleep, and while they are drinking, she says, “I have a problem.”
“other than trolls?” asks Orsik.
“No, it’s trolls.” 
Orsik demures, “No, your problem is a lack of entertainment options for your patrons. You need a theater production.” 
She looks at him, thinks, and says, “I like your gumption, and I like your style, and I like all of that. But right now, I have got a more immediate problem.” She then turns to the group, “And you gotta know when to make the sales pitch. I think you are being too aggressive now.” 
Orsik is taken aback.
“You did ask for advice?”
“Not in front of everyone. I thought that was a 1x1 conversation.” 
“Oh, I wanted everybody else to benefit.” She looks confused, “oh, this was not a private lesson.” 
Orsik glares at her, “I don’t think this mentee thing is working out.” 
Tamalin realizes she made a  mistake and upsets the dwarf, so she turns to him and apologizes. Vardis is surprised to see his old friend accept the apology. 
Once everyone has settled down and drunk their first glass, Tamalin picks up the conversation again, “the trolls have started coming down into Calling Horns a lot more frequently of late. There are a lot of trolls in the Evermoors, or Trollmoors as we call them. It’s a region to the north that is a big swamp. I just need you to go there and find out what’s going on and why the Trolls are coming down. It would be great if you could solve my problem, but I just want to know what is happening. And if you can do that for me, I can provide you a Letter of Mark.” 
She pauses, “oh, you may not know this, but I am a member of the Zoar family.” She pauses, waiting for recognition. 
Thistle remembers something about the Zoar family and that a Zoar ran the city guard of Everlund. 
“Yes, my brother is the captain of the guard. If you go to Everlund and show Dral my note, he’ll get the captain to do whatever you need or want. Dral runs the tavern Danivarr’s House that the Zoar family owns. “ 
At this point, Unla Kee, who is no longer sober, reacts to what was just said. Now it turns out that Unla Kee had been alcohol-free for two weeks and that sobriety was starting to hurt his brain. The fresh alcohol soothed his nerves. 
He turns to Tamalin – “let me get this straight, we do this work for you, and we get a letter? We have better things to do.” 
Orsik pulls his hair out, “are you paying any attention? We get the letter, which gets Eltio to lead us to Harthal Zymorven, who gets us our sword, and when we drag him back to Zymorven Hall, a 1000 gold piece reward.” 
Unla Kee stares at Orsik and then says, “I guess we can do this.” 
As Orsik falls asleep, he thinks, “scoundrel. She’s a scoundrel. She went from thief and crook to scoundrel. I want that kind of arc.” 
## 3rd day of the first 1st tenday of the month Flamerule
The 3HPP head out into the Evermoors, looking for something to explain the trolls heading out of the moors to hunt for food. 
After a day of walking in mud, dealing with bugs, and sweating under the hot summer sun of Flamerule, a campsite is found near a stream. 
“Other than bugs,” Orsik grumbles, “we found nothing.” 
Thistle, on the other hand, is less convinced. There are signs, but he’s not sure what. He knows that all they have to do is go deeper into the marsh. 
“Got it,” he thinks. 

He points to Unla Kee and some people walking in the marsh. Thistle concentrates some more, “they are the same nomadic humans that attacked us when we left Triboar. Can you understand what they are saying?” 
Unla Kee casts comprehend languages, and he rushes off. Thistle stares, “damn it!” and follows him. 
And it’s a good thing. Unla Kee overhears them talk about Trolls moving out of their usual hunting grounds and encroaching on the nomad’s territory. Unfortunately, in trying to get into position, he didn’t pass with the grace of a ranger from the Feywild. 
At first, the warriors stare at him, and he stares at them and tries to be friendly. Except that one of the survivors of the last attack recognizes him as that annoying wizard and charges at Unla Kee and Thistle. 
Vardis, who saw Unla Kee’s noisy schlepping, notices the danger and hits the warriors near the shaman with a fireball lighting up the night sky. Soon the smell of charred flesh and burned trees fill the night air. 
Thistle strikes one with his bow. And then he strikes another.
Unla Kee looks at him, “wait, you can shoot two arrows that quickly?”
“Yep.”
“For how long?” 
“Ever since before we faced the hill giant in Parnast.”
“And you just did that now?”
“Yes.” 
“Why?” 
“Wanted the challenge.” 
“SERIOUSLY?”
The mage laughs and thinks, “I like this guy. I really like this guy.” 
He then pokes through the jungle to see where the remaining warriors are fleeing and finishes them with magic missiles. 
Orsik arrives on the scene ready to fight and sees nothing but dead bodies.
“But I didn’t get a chance to kill anyone!!!” 

# Chapter 32  - Murder Hobos
The sun rises. The Evermoors stretch before our heroes’ eyes and ears like a plate of overcooked pasta. Humidity sticks to their skin, bugs crawl over their tents, and the stench of decaying plants and flesh overwhelms their packed food. 
“So, all we have to do is find the cause – a bit of recon, and we can return to collect our reward.”
Thistle acknowledges Orsik, straps his bow to his back, and buzzes off, flying around to find something. 
He comes back a while later. 
“I heard some snarling, whooping, and hollering, but I am unsure what it is.”
The suddenly intellectually enhanced fighter thinks momentarily, strains to hear, and declares, “That’s them.” 
Thistle stares, “huh?”
“Look, I don’t want you to feel bad, but I am **really** smart. There are two trolls and a fire giant.” 
The fairy stares, dumbfounded. 
Vardis shakes him, “My friend used to be so cool until he got this headband.” 
And then an argument breaks out. Thistle is determined to kill the Fire Giant and the trolls. Unla Kee has to be held back from charging at the monsters. Whereas Orsik is adamant that they don’t have to fight, they just have to ensure that the trolls are coming out because of the Fire Giant. 
They agree they should sneak up on the creatures to figure out what they are arguing about. 
So Thistle flutters off quietly. His boots of elvenkind silenced the air. And there, in an opening in the swamp, is a Fire Giant shouting at two trolls. The Fire Giant is waving a rod identical to the one the Fire Giant that acted Triboar used to find some peculiar artifact made of adamantium at the trolls. The creature looks hungry, frustrated, and annoyed. The trolls just stare at the Fire Giant uncomprehendingly. 

“They are working together! Like Cats and Dogs!” he thinks.
