And he flies off to the campsite, where Orsik is swatting away flies with his sword. 
“We should kill them!” 
“It was a bad idea last time, and we almost all died.” 
“There’s only one, this time.”
“Just a recon mission!” 
Thistle considers that Orsik was the only person who argued against the last attack, shrugs, and turns to Unla Kee: " Do you have a comprehend languages spell you can cast on me so I can figure out what they are saying?”
A few minutes later, Thistle once again arrives at the squabbling humanoids. This time, he overhears the giant shout, “I want something tasty. Not mud pies.” The trolls are meanwhile looking at the mudpies at her feet and are confused as to why she doesn’t find them tasty. She glares at them and their incomprehension and whines, “I can’t go back to Duke Zalto until I find a piece of the Vonindod, and this Rod isn’t helping!” 
Thistle returns to the 2HPP and explains the situation again. This time, the debate rages even longer. The fairy and mage are determined to kill the giant, the cleric is ambivalent, and Orsik wants to return and collect his reward. 
“The quest is completed,” he shouts.
“But treasure!” 
“We almost got killed.” 
“Details!”
Thistle is insistent, “they have treasure.” 
Unla Kee pipes up from the floor of his tent, where he is lying down bored. " Wait, we aren’t going to attack?” 
Everyone just glares at him. He glares back and then closes his eyes. 
Seeing the resistance to the idea, Unla Kee proposes that they entice the trolls with the bodies of the Uthgardt nomads. Then, he will sneak up to the giant and try to steal his rod. 
Orsik and Vardis discuss the situation amongst themselves. 
“I’ll do what the group wants,” muses the elf.
“I have grown attached to them,” grouses the fighter. 
“Alright, let’s do this. But I gotta admit, Thistle, I am amazed that you draw the line at stealing, but just going and killing creatures that have done nothing to us to harvest their organs and steal their magic items is okay.” 
He then mutters to Vardis, “Murder hobo.” Vardis nods. 
Thistle stares at Vardis and then thinks, “Well, it is a different Fire Giant that attacked Triboar, and I don’t have a vendetta against this one, so I suppose we could move on.” 
A few hours later, with the sun setting in the east, the heroes, covered in the stench of the Evermoors, arrive at the Calling Horns Inn. 
Tamalin hears what they say and says, “Well, that is very odd. Fire Giants are working with Trolls. Cats and Dogs, working together.” 
“That’s what I said,” says Thistle. 
Tamalin smiles at the young fairy and continues, “Learn anything else?”
“They are part of Duke Zalto’s tribe. And they are looking for something called the Vonindod.” 
Tamalin sighs, “Never heard of him. The funny thing about Fire Giants is that all their leaders are dukes. And since I am on the topic, hill giants are led by a chief, stone giants by thane, and all cloud giants by a count.” She then thinks for a moment, “Never heard of a Vonindod. Still, you could find something out about in a major city like Everlund and Silverymoon.” She stares at Thistle and then turns to the other three, “Well, the three of you might get something useful from the good folks of Silverymoon.” 
Orsik grumbles, “Yes, the good people of Silverymoon with their Cambion demons.” 
“Tell me about it.” 
As the heroes head out, she says, “Well, I have a brother who is the High Captain of Everlund, and that note I just gave you is good for a favor. He might be able to get what you need.” 
The following day, the heroes head out to Yartar on the Evermoor Road, leaving Calling Horns behind. 
The road is dusty and a lot less busy than they expected. They are unsure if it’s because of the weather, the trolls, the giants, or something else. As they trudge along, they see a hill giant with a sack picking up anything edible and eating it immediately or stuffing it in their bag for later. 
Thistle scouts out ahead and sees two bugbears and an ogre following the hill giant, looking obviously bored, uninterested, and unhappy. 
“Shall we kill this giant and do the travelers on this road a good deed?” 
“You’re bloodthirsty today?”
Thistle ignores Orsik’s comment. 
And so they set up an ambush. 
Thistle, Orsik, and Vardis hide in the bushes off the road while Unla Kee stands in the middle. 
The other three plead with Unla Kee to hide, but he shouts, “I am hiding.” 
They stare at him, stunned. In plain sight, he’s standing in the road with his hands over his eyes. 
And it works. 
The giant, seeing that Unla Kee can’t see him, decides that he can ignore the halfling. Instead, the humanoid focuses on an edible fungus on the side of the road. The bugbear and ogre, meanwhile, notice that their boss is about to be attacked and slow down their pace. 
Thistle fires two arrows from the trees and hits once. Orsik strikes three times, doing three times the damage that Thistle did while wearing a unique shield he crafted overnight in the Calling Horns Inn. 
Vardis uses his guiding bolt to help whoever strikes next. Unfortunately, despite his luck, he fails to hit. 
“It happens.” 
Unla Kee then opens his eyes and thinks, “See, I knew the giant would be surprised!” 
And then casts a lightning bolt, and the giant, already a slow-moving lumbering creature, has the fates twisted against him and bears the full brunt of the blast. 
In pain and down to 15 of its 105 hitpoints, the giant strikes angrily at the creatures in front of him. First, he tries to swing his club at Orsik, who tricks the beast into attacking Thuul, and just before he strikes Thuul, the ever-watchful Vardis uses his mastery of light to blind the giant just for a moment, so he swings wildly and misses. The giant burps a vile concoction and turns to swing at Orsik again. The dwarf, again, points at Thuul. The giant thinks for a moment and swings at the guard drake again, but misses. 
Thistl,e seeing the Giant strike at his friend, aims carefully, and the arrow goes through the giant’s eye and lodges itself in what passes for his brain. 
A loud thud is heard, and dust is tossed into the air as the giant falls. 
The bugbears and ogre see their dead boss and Vardis holding onto giant toenails, looking at each other, and running the other way. 
The next day our heroes meet a goblin caravan and wave them down. A short middle-aged goblin, wearing a red velvet shirt and blue pants, held up by green suspenders, steps forward.
“My name is Fritz, and this is my store. My clan and I are heading east because the giants have gotten crazy, strutting, fretting, and trying to prove that they are the biggest and badest. And then the nomads attack anything that moves. Our goods are priced to move.”
“So it’s a going-out-of-business sale?” 
“My mother told me to never be that desperate and never sell for anything less than a reasonable profit.
“Wise words.” 
Fritz’s goods are of no interest to the 3HPP as they aren’t trying to set up a kitchen in a house. But Fritz, who is always on the lookout for knickknacks and doo-dads, is more than happy to buy a bag of salt that belonged to Duke Zalto, “salt fit for a duke!” an empty chest, and drinking horn – “if a giant thought it was worth carrying then it must be valuable” and fairy leather armor. 
Thistle is about to explain to him that it’s a fairy’s leather armor, but Orsik stops him. 
The next day, on the 8th day of the 1st tenday of Flamerule, our heroes arrive in Yartar late in the evening and head to the Wink and the Kiss. 

Maia is happy to see them and asks if they need more work. Orsik shows her the Letter of Mark, and she tries to high-five him, but he leaves her hanging. 
Unhappy, Maia takes the group to the bar in the center of the maze that makes the interior of this tavern and sits them next to Eltio.
Eltio, a member of the Hand of Yartar, is dressed in white, wearing a hat and a white mask. The clothes have carefully stitched patterns in white on the cloth. 
When they present the Letter of Mark to her, she asks, “so what did you do?”
“Recon.”
“No murder? Thievery? Highway robbery? Damn, Tamalin has gone legit. She used to be a legendary figure in these parts. Parlayed the terror she induced on the folks around here to a bigger, more lucrative gig.” 
The 3HPP stare at Eltio as she reminisces. 
The Hand of Yartar swallows her drink, tells Maia to bring drinks for the 3HPP, and heads out. 
While she’s out, the group debates how they will convince Harthos Zymorven, the heir to Zymorven Hall, to come with them. Orsik proposes intimidation, and Thistle proposes that they fake that he will be arrested and offer him a way to escape to Daddy. Vardis suggests that they tell him that his dad is ill. Orsik looks at his friend and says, “I don’t think that will work.” He then pauses, “Thank you for your idea. There are no bad ideas during the brainstorming phase. All ideas are welcome. Keep them coming.” 
While the group is still mulling over options, Eltio returns. 
“So your boy is in prison for murder. The bloke will be executed on the 10th. We do that sort of thing – head cut off to make it harder to raise them from the dead. As for the sword, it’s in the possession of a man named Tholzar Brenner. He’s a Damaranian, a land from the east, who is not liked. Tholzar is a guard captain who, if he went missing, no one would be upset. I can take you to him if you want.” 
They look at her.
“I can take you to him where an ambush would be possible.” 
Later that evening, the group was in the market near the Waterbarron’s house. Eltio pointed out a small group of armed guards with a brutish boss as they swaggered through the small stores and stalls. 
“He’s there collecting his protection money.” 
Orsik stares – “They are so close together, we could fireball them all.” 
Eltio looks at him and thinks, “You are that guy Orsik who did that thing. I knew it.” She stops, thinks about letting him burn, and realizes that the blowback might affect her or, worse, the Hand of Yartar. So she turns to him, “Do you want to create a 20’ radius fireball that will explode in the main market of Yartar near the Waterbarron’s residence? Are you insane? Or is this part of your master plan to free Harthal?” She sighs, “I don’t think they will wait until the 10th to cut your head off….” 
Meanwhile, Thistle thinks, “And he called me a murder hobo. He’s willing to blow up a small town, and me wanting to kill two trolls and a fire giant was too much….” 

# Chapter 33  - Rescuing Harthal
After arriving in Yartar, the 3HPP met their contact in the Hand of Yartar at the Wink and the Kiss. Eltio was surprised to see them alive and even more impressed to see them with the Letter of Mark from Tamalin Zoar, so she gave them what they needed. 
“The boy is in prison, probably about to be executed. As for the sword, Brenner, a thug of a captain of the guard, has it.” 
Fortunately for the 3HPP, Eltio knew precisely where to find him in the central market of Yartar that was just below the Waterbarron’s residence. The market was a series of interconnected alleys that spread out in a thousand little directions like a bowl of spaghetti, where each intersection had a store or a stall, and each street had its own history. Everything that was sold in Yartar eventually found its way here. 
It was pretty late in the evening when Brenner finally showed, bagging at the various small stores demanding his cut of their profits for the protection he offered. 
The giant slayer sword hung from his best, and he had the smug satisfaction of a man with some power and loved to wield it over the unfortunate within his grasp. Surrounding him were several city guards, who looked bored, frustrated, and annoyed. 
At first, Orsik wants Unla Kee and Vardis to use fireball. After all, a group of enemies tightly packed in a small space cried out for that spell. Eltio, no shrinking violet when it came to violence, was shocked. 
“Are you mad? Create a 20’ fireball just outside the Waterbarron’s residence? We’ll be hunted down and killed.” She paused and stared at Orsik, “you’re Orsik, the guy who does the thing.”
The angry dwarf stared at the female rogue dressed in white and muttered, “fine, no fireball.”
And it was unnecessary. All but one of the guards died. All but one. 
Eltio turns to the heroes, “damn, we need to get your boy out of prison tonight. When Brenner’s death gets announced, they’ll think it was tied to the brat’s friends and kill him.” 
Thistle mutters, “this would be a good time for that convenient entrance into the prison.” 
The Hand of Yartar looks at him, “well, not so much an official backdoor, more of an unofficial secret passageway through an ancient temple of Bane.” 
At first, the 3HPP isn’t convinced that this is a good idea. Orsik insists on fighting through the Waterbarron’s house, “front door or bust.” 
When Eltio explains the problem of assaulting a center of government and living long enough to escape, even Orsik is convinced that perhaps, this time, the secret door through the temple of Bane to the prison is the right strategy.
The first part of their journey is into the sewers of Yartar, to a passageway that Eltio had heard of from her contacts, “I’m sure this will get you to where you want.” 
Orsik and Vardis are unsure but proceed. 
As advertised, the passageway leads to the abandoned temple of Bane. It’s a large area with a  pool of water in the middle. Thistle, Vardis, and Orsik hug the eastern wall near the entrance, then walk along the southern wall, trying to distance themselves and the pool as much as possible. 
Unla Kee rushes to take a dip. Fortunately for him, he notices the water weird and backs away. 
Also, luckily for Orsik, Thistle notices three markings, one on the south wall and one on the closet pillars to the wall, that form a triangle. 
“Stop, it’s a trap.”
 “Well, how do we get around this trap?”
“The other way.” 
And the 3HPP walk around the room and arrive at the secret door that Eltio promised them. They quietly open the door and see just beyond a room with many very bored and tired guards. 
“Can we use the fireballs now?” 
A few moments later, the guards were dead, charred, and blasted. As the last one died, he shouted, “you have no idea what you have done.” 
As Orsik and Thistle pick over the bodies for treasure, they find a lot of silver coins. Thistle mutters about a tradition involving the condemned’s families giving a silver bribe to see their loved ones one last time before they were executed. 
To the north of the guardroom is a room with chests, the possessions of the condemned. The containers are trapped, and although irritating, nothing fatal happens. More annoying is how little treasure is found. In one, there is an unused great sword, a spell book with no spells but doodles, and a note which refers to a condemned man whose family took all of his possessions, a potion of healing, and some coins and cheap gems
The other chest is magically trapped, and Unla Kee dispels its magic. Inside are more silver pieces and a glass decanter with a rich red fluid, a potion of greater healing. 
Thistle heads back into the guardroom and goes west into a hallway with a door to the north that leads to a room where the condemned’s family met their loved ones one last time. There is nothing of value there but the tears of sorrow and despair. The hallway leads to the Waterbarron’s castle, so he returns to the guardroom. 
From there, Orsik urges them to try the eastern doorway.
Beyond the door is a large room with bookshelves, several cadaverous creatures standing around moaning, a table with a skeleton, and a desk with a pale-faced woman sitting behind it. The room looks richly appointed and out of context for a prison. 
Across from where Thistle is standing on the eastern wall is a door, and another door is on the southern wall. The sounds of desperate people and chains can be heard from beyond those doors. 
The woman stares at Thistle, “what can I do for you?” who in turn asks to see Harthal Zymorven. 
The creature stares at her book and then says, “I suppose I can have you meet him.” She turns to one of the cadaverous moaning creatures that are clearly zombies and orders it. 
Thistle offers to go with the creature, but she says, “No, I can’t have you contaminate the experimental work that we are conducting on the prisoners. Every bit of air, food, water, and sentiment is tightly controlled.” 
The 3HPP shrug and wait. 
When Harthal appears, he shouts, “Father, you came for me.” 
Thistle is about to protest when he notices Orsik looks precisely like Lord Zymorven. 
 Harthal runs towards his father, but the woman violently holds him. 
“He can’t leave. We need him for our experiments. Experiments that have been sanctioned by the highest authorities in Yartar. Although if you were to provide us with fresh bodies, I could let him go.” 
Orsik-Zymorven smiles and returns with several of the bodies of the guards.
The creature smiles, and this time its tell-tale fangs are unmistakable, “yes, these guards will do nicely.” 
Thistle is surprised to not hear her refer to them as her guards. She notices and says, “the Waterbarron is under the mistaken impression that these guards can stop me from traveling among the citizens of his city.” The vampire laughs. 
As Harthal prepares to leave, the vampire whispers, “Harthal, my boy, you must leave tonight. If you were to be found in the city, I am afraid I would have to kill you the minute you came down here. Do you think, Lord Zymorven, you can find a way to get out of the city?”
Orsik-Zymorven remembers the portals and says, “yes.” 
An hour or so later, as the 3HPP and Harthal approach the Harper hangout, Harthal shouts, “wait, you’re Thistle Wendrod.” Then after a scream of frustration cries, “your girlfriend is why I ended up here.”
Thistle is stunned. Leila wanted to marry this guy? He wasn’t sure whether to despair about that or to be delighted she was alive. But he called her “Thistle’s girlfriend!” There was hope! 
Harthal then explains that Leila was the rogue who convinced him to steal the sword and head out on an adventure. Lord Zymorven’s heir mutters that he only wanted some experiences like dear old dad. He ends the story by explaining that after he killed someone who badly deserved it, Leila abandoned him, saying she needed to go find the absolute love of her life. 
“Why Yartar?” 
“She wanted to talk to the Hand of Yartar and join them. Thought they could help her find you. But they were too local of an operation, so she decided she needed to go find someone else. I mean, she kept talking about Thistle. And that she was looking for you. And I really felt used.” 
“You were,” mutters Orsik-Zymorven. 
Thistle adds, in as condescending a voice as possible, “and that’s why you should stay in your castle and be Lord Zymorven. That way, no one would dare use you in that way.”
Harthal stares at Orsik-Zymorven and says, “dad, I am so sorry! I just want to go straight home and become the next Lord and never follow some crazy Fairy that wants to use me to find the love of her life!” 
A few moments later, the group enters the Harper headquarters. As they enter, Thistle announces to Orsik-Zymorven, “Lord Zymorven, if I recall, you had some urgent business to attend to and would be staying behind. Your ally Orsik would be waiting for us at the other end of the portal?” 
Orsik-Zymorven stares, befuddled, and says, “yes, of course.” 
Thistle notices that the hallucinatory terrain in the shape of a garden with misappropriate fairy culture is gone and replaced with a more conventional modern Waterdeephavian look of straight lines and austere surfaces. 
As he examines the terrain, a man appears, “my name is Gauche the Young, and you must be the group known as the Three Halfpints and a Pint.” 
Thistle and Orsik stare at him, confused. Gauche is of average human size, with a heavy build from eating well, amber eyes, a broad nose, and gray hair, revealing he is not that young. 
“My predecessor, sadly, was killed. And you are the agents that Krowen Valharrow hired. I’ll have you note that Krowen is a follower of the school of Ezra. After the end of the age of sundering, the gods could no longer enter the world on their own. And we thought we were finally safe from their madness. But then Severin, of the cult of the Dragon, found a tome with the aid of Tiamat that contained a ritual that would let her back in. The Harpers were weak then, and most of our field agents were dead. And so Ezra decided to pilot a new program of hiring adventurers and using Harper funds to get them to do missions. The success of the First Faction and Ezra can not be denied. But I disagree with the policy. You are not Harpers. You are mercenaries. Normally, I wouldn’t let you use our teleportation circles, but the success of Ezra’s program and his promotion to the upper echelons of society require that I help you. But I don’t like it. Not one bit.” 
The 3HPP stare at the obviously disgruntled Gauche the Young. 
 “What happened to the other guy?”
“Oh, he was killed by a lightning bolt.”
“What?”
“A mighty one. And what’s interesting is that the room Kolbaz was in was destroyed as if something substantial materialized in that room, destroyed everything in it, and then disappeared. It also happened near the teleportation circle, almost as if whatever it was came from there.”
Thistle asks, “Did anyone else come through the teleportation circle?”
“Yes, of course, they did. But that would be Harper’s business and no business of yours.” 
“Well, I am sorry for your loss.” 
Gauche is surprised, “Yes, his death was a tragedy. But this is a plum position, and I have been looking forward to it, so it’s – how sad and congratulations, I suppose.” 
Thistle takes leave of Gauche, examines the area around the teleportation circle, and notices that something massive materialized and then dematerialized. It smashed the walls, the roof, and the ground. The marks on the stone make it evident that this was not the work of a giant. 
The ranger then turns to the remaining pieces of paper on the desk. It’s clear from the papers’ arrangement that the research into Adimond Kroskas was the proximate cause of Kolbaz’s death. Thistle is about to leave when he finds a fascinating piece of paper. Apparently, Adimond Kroskas created the ritual that the cult of Tiamat used to first create Drakes. 
“That sounds about right,” thinks the fairy.
And then he notices something that surprises him, Adimond Kroskas is an alias for someone else. That he is not who he says he is.

Thistle then turns to Gauche, who is dusting the room, and explains his suspicion that his predecessor Kolbaz was killed by Adimond Kroskas. Thistle tries to figure out if Gauche knows anything, but Gauche is remarkably disinterested. 
“I tell you, sirs, Harpers will solve this mystery with the appropriate urgency and priority.” 
Orsik-Zymorven persuades Gauche to work a little bit harder, hoping that he will look into Adimond, but Gauche doesn’t get to this plum position because he is a fool, “Yes, you are right. I will look into the circumstances of my predecessor’s death. Looking into Adimond Kroskas, however, is a distraction, and I will not look into him.”
He then pauses and starts taking measurements of the marks on the room. He mutters, “I have this theory that the thing that killed Kolbaz was demonic in nature or a wizard.”
Orsik-Zymorven mocks him – “or a wizard disguised as a demon?”
But Gauche doesn’t take the bait, “yes, I must look into wizards who masquerade as demons.”
Thistle and Orsik-Zymorven are frustrated and smile politely. Gauche will up the priority into every possible angle of the investigation except looking into who Adimond Kroskas is or where he went. 
A few moments later, the group emerges from the teleportation circle in Everlund into the Moongleam Tower, standing in front of Nespril Menk, a tall, coltish, gangly-built, green-eyed apprentice of Krowen Valhorrow. He snorts through his broad nose and ties his light brown hair into a knot.
And then, when he speaks, he speaks with the sophistication of a Chondathan for whom the common tongue is their mother tongue. 
“The 3HPP have arrived,” he says. 
Orsik-Zymorven, still disguised as Zymorven, is about to object when Thistle says, “Orsik, it’s so good to see you here. Lord Zymorven had to stay behind and would meet us in his hall.” 
Orsik remembers that Lord Zymorven never left his hall and quickly transforms back to himself before Harthal can see the deception.
“Right, Lord Zymorven had to stay behind.” 
Vardis stares at his friend and almost laughs. His friend has intelligence and the raw power of genius, but he doesn’t quite know how to use it effectively. “The sudden increase in intellect is like a suit of armor that you just bought and aren’t quite used to using,” he thinks. 
“You are Krowen’s latest project,” Nespril sighs. “Well, apparently, Krowen didn’t tell you about the exit through the sewers.”
They stare at him.
“He wanted you to go out from the front door. Krowen, doing the big picture stuff, never pays attention to the details. I will introduce you to our secret passageway. This way, the Zhentarim won’t spot you.”
Orsik can’t shut up. “So, can you tell me how you avoid the Zhentarim spies?”
“We use a special random system.”
“Is it described in a book?” 
Nespril stares at him, “Krowen Valharrow would be very upset if the book were to go missing or if he suspected that you were involved in the book’s disappearance.” 
“I am just saying you need independent contractors to ensure it’s secure.”
And then Nespril explodes, “I am not a believer in Krowen’s support for the Ezra model. Hiring anybody to do a job and saving the world if the check clears. I believe in the old ways where being a member of this group meant you believed.”
Thistle seeing that his companion will press the issue points out that they still need to collect the money from Lord Zymorven and that this could get in the way. 
Orsik sighs and declares, “Okay, first we go to Danivarr’s House and talk to Dral Thelev and give him the letter of Mark.”
A few minutes later, Orsik and Dral catch up in the well-appointed tavern about their mutual friend Tamalin. Orsik says, “she really helped me see how I can better monetize my play. Brilliant business lady.” 
Dral tells Orsik that the Zoars are exceptionally well connected and that the captain of the army, High Captain Horix Zoar is Tamalin’s brother. 
Orsik asks, “and what is their relationship to Silverymoon?”
Dral laughs, “The High Captain has never forgiven High Marshal Methrammar Aerasumé for abandoning his allies. As a result, Zoar broke the compact and dissolved the alliance. Although he would like to raise an army and raze the city to the ground, the High Captain also believes that you must let bygones be bygones. He could get you into the city as a diplomat of Everlund, making it impossible for the local police to arrest you.” 
And with that, the heroes head north on Rauvin Road. On their second day, north of Silverymoon, they are attacked by some Crag Cats. 
## At Zhentarim HQ in Yartar
“Orsik’s a great agent. He was determined to find the Sword of Giant Slaying without revealing us.”
“Yep, could have just asked, and we would have told him where the sword was. And helped him get it. But he’s a stand-up guy who wants to do things independently.”

# Chapter 34 3HPP Productions
## 10th day of the 1st Tenday of Flamerule

The Rauvin Road to Zymorven Hall north of Silverymoon is typically relatively safe except for the odd creature that sees a small group that looks like it might be a tasty morsel.
Such a group is the 3HPP. To four crag cats, they look like a father with his three children. With their massive claws and a hide that deflects magic, the overgrown cats slaver in anticipation of eating the tasty morsels they see in front of them. 
They crash through the underbrush and surround the group. 
Harthal Zymorven, hides behind Vardis.
The first creature attempts to pounce on Thuul and lands inches before him. The guard Drake hisses. Another white cat bites Thistle, damaging his armor – “nothing that can’t be fixed.” 
Vardis scorches the creatures with his rays. 
And then, when another cat attempts to attack, he summons the light of Corellon and blinds the creature, causing it to swing its paws uselessly. 
Unla Kee is curiously distracted, looking at the stars. Harthal screams, " Are you going to do anything?” 
The wizard looks at him – “I am.” 
And just then, Orsik and Unla Kee kill the remaining cats with well-timed blows. 
Harthal stares at Unla Kee in awe.
Thistle mutters, “I think Uncle Joon said these are worth something.” 
Orsik and Vardis, who only bought their pelts preskinned and tanned, stare at him with the blood of crag cats dripping from their swords and armor and grumble. Orsik shouts, “Seriously? That’s disgusting. We are hours away from a thousand gold pieces, and you want to carve up an overgrown cat to make leather armor?”
“And eat the meat.”
“Eat the meat? We can eat at the finest taverns after today.” 
 With the crag cat safely in their bags, Thistle announces that he will fly ahead. Orsik gets the point and mentions that Harthal is doing some scouting.
Harthal is relieved, especially after the last incident. 

## At Zymorven Hall

Lord Zymorven is surprised to see Thistle Wendrod and sad because he is alone.
“Is my son dead?” 
“Oh no! We had to spring your son from prison.”
“What ?”
“Yes, he was on death row for murder.”
“Whaaatt??””
Thistle is obviously annoyed at the interruptions. 
“So yes, we were able to complete the mission, but to convince him to come, we had one of our allies disguise themselves as you.” 
“What!!!!” 
“And it wasn’t really a breakout. Instead, we bargained with a vampire who was the jailer and was conducting some experiments.”
“Well, all is well that ends well. If my son comes, I will give you your reward.” Lord Zymorven pauses, “I suppose I must avoid visiting Yartar for a while.” 
“Not really, just avoid the vampire.” 
He laughs. 
A few hours later, Harthal and the 2HPP arrive. Lord Zymorven is delighted to see his son, who is equally happy to be back at home. Lord Zymorven turns to the 3HPP, “a good old fashioned prison break, the kind of adventure that I had never done that before! Well done, team.” He winks. Then he turns to Thistle, “Here’s your reward for your help!” 
Harthal stares at Lord Zymorven, “That was a great bit of adventuring, Dad!”
“Yes, but I do think that my days are over.” He notices Orsik carrying the sword, “may that sword bring you better luck than it brought my son.” 
A few days later, on the Rauvin Road north of Everlund and south of Silverymoon, the 3HPP met a group of orcs. 
Orsik turns to Vardis, “We don’t need to talk to these people.” 
Thistle ignores him and approaches the group. A matronly-looking orc stares at him in the sun and shouts, “Well, well, it’s a fairy.” 
Another orc, possibly its mate, points its spear at Thistle and shouts, “Well, you may be right, you may be right.” 
The orc then pauses and shouts, “Would you know a good place to put up a homestead? What with the giants and all we had to pick up and leave.” 
Thistle stares, “a homestead?”
“Yes, what did you think that we are a bunch of marauding barbarians killing all the “goodly” races? You don’t subscribe to that Silverymoon nonsense, do you?”
Thistle blushes. 
Orsik doesn’t overhear the comment. 
Then the first orc shouts, “Well, our kids have only heard of your kind if in fairy tales. Could we call them so they can look at you?” 
Thistle winces at the term “fairy tale” but decides to let bygones be bygones and shouts, “Sure.” 
And the young orcs are indeed delighted to see him. Thistle is inspired and does some flying tricks that impress the group. 
Then another teenage-looking orc shouts, “I heard fairies are good with the bow. I bet you I am better. I’ll toss a rock, and whoever hits it gets bragging right.” 
Thistle laughs and prepares to fire his bow. Unfortunately for him, not only does he not hit the rock, but his fingers slip, and the arrows fall to the ground. 
The teenager shouts, “I AM THE CHAMPION!” The rest of the tribe applauds him. The matronly orc winks at Thistle, thinking he failed intentionally, but Thistle is flabbergasted at his performance. 
Later on, the young orc became well known among the local tribes, and Thistle became a bit of a legend, known as the fairy that couldn’t fire. The pun works better in orc. 
But that is for the future. In the present, the matron hands him some jam to make him feel better. Thistle, betraying his biases, blurts, “orc jam?” 
She is offended. " No, berry jam.” She pauses, “They really have you confused about Orcs.” 
A few days after that, on the road to Yartar on the Evermoor Way, the 3HPP sees another band of Uthgardtian barbarians. They are almost spotted until Unla Kee tricks them and thus overcomes the loud noises made by Vardis and Orsik. This is interesting because they both were trying hard not to be noticed. 
## 5th day of the 2nd Tenday of Flamerule

### Triboar at last. 

The journey to Triboar has been long and arduous, and the promise of profit is excellent. The town is overrun with folks from all over. The inns are overflowing with people, and the markets are busier than ever. The rich folks from the outlying ranches are strolling around with their bodyguards and retinues. 
Orsik thinks he can find some old friends, but then he decides that might be a bad idea and disguises himself magically. 
The group enters the Talking Troll, where Kaelen, their business partner, is supposed to have been working on their play. 
Thistle checks out the stagehands and actors, and his time in the fey tells him this will be a disaster. The sets are barely painted and have bizarre shapes and colors. The actors struggle with staying awake and sober, not acting. 
And then he hears a line of the play and winces, “This play would probably cost someone’s head or two, if they had two if Queen Tatiana was forced to attend. Even Prince Thornacious would demand exile,” he pauses, “at the very least.” 
While Thistle checks out the sets and actors, a note is dropped into Unla Kee’s hands. It is a piece of paper with a hand stencil and the simple message, “Meet me outside.” 

Thistle again anxiously declaims, “The Hand of Yartar? The same group that tried to assassinate me?”
Orsik grouses, “they were framed.” 
Thistle grumbles, “they would say that.” 
Before they can leave the Tavern, Urgala approaches.
Orsik turns to Thistle, “Who is that?” 
“She’s the woman who had an affair with a man whose wife later killed him. We found the murderer when their brothers offered a reward of this magical longbow.” 
Orsik grunts. 
Urgala smiles at Orsik and asks him to visit her Tavern. When she remarks on his giant sword, Orsik is very confused. When she insists that everyone should come, he is downright flabbergasted. 
Thistle groans, “She’s inviting us for tea.” 
Orsik dismisses him, “Maybe she’s inviting you for tea. She wants to see my sword.” 
Urgala glares at him, Vardis sighs, and the group heads to the Northshield House. 
“It’s looking nice.” 
“Yes, we did repair the damage the magmin’s inflicted on us.” 
She sits in her foyer, calls for some tea, and begins – “I am not sure how to say this, but Kaelen has borrowed a lot of money. He borrowed 5000 gold pieces.”
“So?”
“Well, Kaelen said you guys were good for it. And your exploits here and, more importantly, the stories from Parnast encouraged folks to give him money. He’s a good lad, but I think he’s over his head, and if he can’t pay them back, some folks may forgive him, but the Hand of Yartar, the Zhentarim, and some local criminals might not.”
“So?”
“Well, once they deal with Kaelen, they will look for you. And they will want their money, their profits, the cost of killing Kaelen, and some money for being inconvenienced.” 
“So we charm everyone, the play is a success, and we are done.” 
Urgala laughs, “My dear Orsik, to raise the money you need, you will need not one performance but an entire month of sold-out performances.” 
They stare at her bleakly, “But you are adventurers, and you don’t surrender.” 
“Oh no, we do.” 
“Well, I like to think my good friend Lord Zymorven didn’t make a mistake in giving his sword to you.” 
They stare at her glumly.
“The play can be fixed. We need someone to fix the writing, plot, acting, and directing. I believe a powerful magical item, Chancellor Geoffrey’s Invitation, can be used to create a background set with animations and music that appear almost real.”
“An illusion spell?”
“Yes, I suppose. But it lasts longer and doesn’t require the services of a mage.” 

## 5th day of the 2nd tenday of Flamerule 1492

Thistle is annoyed. Not as much as Orsik, mind you. Orsik wonders how his brilliant plan to run a play for profit is turning into a potential disaster. 

As he stands across the road from The Troll Inn, he looks at the theater. 

“Looks nice,” mutters the dwarf. 

Thistle groans, “Apparently, he bought the property?” 

“Bought? Why did he buy the property? Couldn’t he have rented it? We are so going to need to take over this production.” 

Urgala, who had told them about the bad news, looks at Orsik and says, “Let me get this straight: you made a deal with an out-of-work actor to put on a play and trusted him?” 

Orsik taps his Headband of Intellect, “This headband isn’t working as well as I would like.” 

However, for a moment, he wonders if the problem is that he’s been listening to people with worse ideas.

Urgala interrupts his deep thoughts, “The good news is that the play exists, and many people are here. At least Kaelen delivered the play. The buzz you created and the fame of your exploits have made this opening the event of the season.  If you guys can get this play to have a second night, you’ll make the money you need to make. If not, the Zhentarim and Hand of Yartar will get their pound of flesh.” 

She pauses and mutters, “The problem is the dialogue and story are a mess, the actors can’t act, and the set is a disaster.” 

Orsik asks, “Can we delay the opening?” 

Urgala laughs heartily. And then she says, “No.” 

Realizing that they will have to parallelize, Unla Kee will take on the script, Thistle will take on the direction, Orsik will do the acting, and Vaardis will collect the CGI. 

### Unla Kee

Unla Kee marches up to Kaelen, who is pleasantly surprised to see him. 

Kaelen smiles, “What an excellent script! I am so pleased!” 

The diviner looks at him, shakes his head, and says in an authoritative voice, “I have seen the future, and the future says this is going to be a disaster.” 

Kaelen’s confidence on a good day is poor, and this dismissive tone breaks him. He whimpers, “It’s that bad?” 

“Yes.” 

Kaelen grabs the script and rushes into his chambers in the The Talking Troll. A few hours later, he showed up with a new script. 

“Better,” mutters Unla Kee. As the mage reads it, he realizes that Kaelen has decided to use an archaic form of the common tongue that nobody would understand. He turns to the actor/producer/playwright and says, “I am very impressed with the use of this dialect. It’s very authentic, and nobody will understand a word the actors are saying.” 

“But it’s authentic! I was going for authenticity,” he argues. 

Unla Kee looks at Kaelen, and a stroke of genius hits him, “The trick isn’t to speak archaic common. Using just enough words creates the illusion of speaking archaic commonly. So instead of saying ‘You,’ we’ll say ‘Thou’ and ‘Thee.’” 

“BRILLIANT!!!” 

A few hours later, a cleaned-up script appears in Unla Kee’s hands, and he finally begins to see why the play is a disaster. The protagonist's motivations are wrong, and his interactions with the other characters are unmotivated and garbage. The play makes no sense. 

Unla Kee realizes the entire play can’t be rewritten; he must devise a trick to make this work. And then it hits him: “How about a play within a play about a bad play?” 

Kaelen stares at him; Unla Kee creates an awful outer play about the actual play. So, the audience is watching a play about a dreadful play. 

“Brilliant!” Kaelen is ecstatic. “This way, with a few lines of dialogue, this will be perfect, and we will protect the existing investment.”

Unla Kee then mutters, “The ending is terrible.” 

The wizard can’t make heads or tails out of it. Kaelen seems touchy about the bizarre, incomprehensible ending, as if it matters more to him than the entire play. Unla Kee realizes that he will have to awe the playwright with his intellect, so he manipulates fate to figure this out and help him brainstorm what he needs. And it works. 

Kaelen is pleased. 

Unla Kee is relieved. The play is no longer a mishmash of every bad avant-garde idea from off-High Road and has coherence. However, the story itself seems oddly elusive. 

### Thistle 

An unimposing, heavy-set man wearing a multi-colored shirt and a purple scarf over his head, barely hiding a buzz cut and his enormous ears introduces himself, “You are one of the investors! I am Everard Barney and completely committed to Kaelen's vision.” 

Thistle looks at the director and says, “Me too.” 

The director then starts to sob frantically.  “Kaelin, he creeps me out. And I can’t deal with the main actors! I give them directions, but they don’t listen. My entire family are bookkeepers. And I decided to branch out. And if this fails, I will have to return to Waterdeep and be an accountant.” 

Everard is now sobbing uncontrollably. 

Thistle realizes that Everard cannot understand his actor’s motivations, “Have you talked to your actors and asked them what they want?” 

“No?” 

Thistle sighs. He walks around the set, absorbing everyone’s body language and how they are talking. The fairy realizes that the lead actor, Amy, likes to pretend that he’s all fluffy and joyful, but she is status-driven and cares about what everyone thinks. Whenever anyone tries to get him to commit to something or make a definitive statement, it’s always “I don’t know, and I am not sure.” It’s almost as if he is hiding some mysterious past. 

Thistle tells Everard, “I am going to talk to Amy.” 

“How is talking to the actor going to help?” 

Thistle rolls his eyes, “For this lead actor, you must pander to their sense of being a star.” 

Everard listens and mutters, “So that scene where he gets on stage and says this soliloquy.“ He pauses thoughtfully, “I shouldn’t have her sitting on a pile of dung.”

“Correct.” 

“But I am trying to contrast between the heroic message and the crap we have to deal with on a day-to-day basis,” he whines pathetically. 

“What if we had him standing on a hill? And the dung is below him? So there is some separation?”

“Right, so they don’t make an association that I think Amy is shit.” 

“EXACTLY!”

“And that Amy elevates us from the shit?” 

Thistle nods. 

Everard is super excited at this point. “Since you seem to have such insight, perhaps you can help me with these other actors. They aren’t very good, and they keep forgetting their lines.” 

“Why?” 

“Because they are drunk.” 

“What if we make the fact they are drunk part of the play? That way every time they get a line wrong, it’s not a mistake, but part of the play?” 

Everard is tempted and then shakes his head. “Nah. What I am going to do is scream at them louder in the morning.” 

Thistle, who has dealt with Uncle Joon’s alcohol binges for years, agrees, “It will help with their hangovers. But instead of screaming for my drunk uncle, I just pour buckets of water. It works brilliantly, just like yelling.” 

Everard smiles, “My uncle screams at the drunk apprentices. I’ll have to tell him about the water trick.” 

They both smile. 

As the day winds down, Everard sheepishly approaches Thistle, “Any ideas on the finale?” 

Thistle stares at the incomprehensible staging and direction, hand gestures. He feels like something is wrong, but he has no insight. And then it hits him. This ending works great and says, “Change nothing.” 

### Orsik

While Thistle tries to fix the direction, Orsik concludes that the actors are not athletic enough. There is too little emphasis on physicality as if they treat this running race like a stroll. Their lack of energy is hurting the play, sapping its vitality. 

Thistle has told Orsik about Amy’s needs. Orsik considers being nice but mutters, “Terror is always the better option.” 

Casting a spell to disguise himself as a human acting coach, he marches up to Amy. 

“Who are you?” 

Orsik looks at her, “I am here to get your acting back on track. The name is Geoff Mamet.”

“What? I’ve never heard of you. Did Kaelin sign off on this? Is this under budget? Or am I paying this out of my pocket?” 

Geoff-Orsik growls, “You are not here to ask questions.” 

Amy deflates, “You’re the boss. Whatever you want…” 

She then describes how she wanted to do the main lead. Kaelin has been pushing back on his ideas, but she is a very timid, milquetoast kind of person who can’t push their ideas.

Geoff-Orsik realizes that he’s going to have to give her some spine. Unfortunately, his shout, “GROW UP AND STOP ACTING LIKE A WEAKLING!” doesn’t have the desired effect. 

She sighs, “I’ll just do what Kaelin wants.” 

Orsik hopes that Unla Kee and Thistle have fixed the script and direction enough to compensate for her acting. 

Then she starts explaining that she doesn’t like this scene, whining about how it doesn’t work and wanting it done differently. 

Geoff-Orsik stares at her and realizes her problem is a lack of energetic obedience to the director, not the director's direction or the script. 

She then explains that at the end of the play, she’s confused because the lines are complicated—“And I have to deliver these weird lines, and I have to make these over-the-top gestures and...” 

Geoff-Orsik has had enough and just shouts, “ENOUGH! You aren’t the director. You aren’t the screenwriter. Just deliver the lines, put some energy into it, and you’ll be fine.” 

She stares at him and meekly assents.

As Geoff-Orisk watches the rest of the rehearsal, his decision to focus on the energy level of the acting pays off: “What a brilliant idea I had!” 

He walks out of the theater, dispels the illusion, and walks back in. 

Amy sees him and shouts, “Oh, Mr. Orsik. How wonderful that you were willing to pay for Geoff Mamet to come and give me pointers. He’s made all the difference. Would you like to see it?” 

Orsik nods. 

### Vardis

Vardis walks to Urgala’s Inn and asks, “So tell me about this magical device?” 

She smiles, “Chancellor Geoffrey Invitations is what you need.  Chancellor Geoffrey would use the device to create these elaborate invites with sound and animated visuals.” 

“Wouldn’t an illusionist work just as well?” 

“Perhaps, but this won’t cost you anything.” 

“And where is it?”

She shows him a location south of Yartar in the Dessarin Hills - “A tribe of Kobolds has it. They don’t know what it is. They pilfered it off of one of Geoffrey’s messengers. And poor Geoffrey has been looking for it ever since.” 

“How do you know about this?” 

She smiles, “I wasn’t always an innkeeper.” 

A few hours later, Vardis is standing at the entrance of the Kobold Warrens. He pulls out a scroll of disguise and transforms himself into one of the creatures. 

Kobold-Vardis walks up to the entrance and, as he does, notices that there are dozens of Kobold footprints leading to a small three-foot cave. 

The cave entrance leads into a warren of cave tunnels illuminated by giant fire beetles. 

He stares at the ground, “Let’s see where these footprints go.” 

Eventually, the tunnels open into a cave, and another kobold is therein. The creature speaks to Kobold-Vardis, who looks like a Kobold but doesn’t speak the language. 

Kobold-Vardis shrugs, which confuses the other creature. It turns and runs deeper into the caves. The cleric follows. 

The tunnel opens up into a chamber with a few dogs. Kobold-Vardis thinks, “Pets?” 

He moves deeper into the cave complex and arrives at an enormous chamber with a 20-foot-high ceiling. Kobold-sized tables and chairs are scattered throughout the southwestern part of the room. On its northeastern side, a one-foot-high altar stands before a 15-foot-high monolith made of rusting iron.

“Let’s take a look at the altar.” 

The altar has an image of the Kobold god Grumsh strangling Tiamat. As he stares at the carving, he sees that Grumsh is a new addition to the monolith. 

“Focus on the mission.” 

He peers down the northeastern passageway from where he thinks the Kobold originated. 

The tunnel curves away from the cave with the monolith and then opens up into another cave. This one has tripwires stretching from one cavern's end to the other. 

Kobold-Vardis thinks to himself, “I could go back. But that would mean sacrificing our entire fortune. And I am not losing that money.” 

And he fails.

A bell rings, and a few Kobold guards appear. They are surprised to see one of their numbers caught up in the tripwires. 

Kobold-Vardis tries to imitate a confused and unintelligent Kobold who has gotten hopelessly lost. 

And pulls it off. 
One of the Kobold guards starts yammering at the other. Although Kobold-Vardis doesn’t understand anything he’s saying, he gets the intent - that Kobold-Vardis wasted his time and that the Kobold guard will have to spend all day cleaning up this mess. 

Kobold-Vardis successfully deceives the guard with a look of shame. 

The Kobold guard frees him, pushing him deeper into the complex. 

After stumbling ahead, Kobold-Vardis enters what looks like a large cavern, but then he realizes he’s outside. The sky above is starry. The moon is visible. The ground below is that of any valley he’s seen in this part of the north.

Then he realizes it’s all an illusion—an illusion projected by a ball with tiny, mirrored shards across its surface. 

“Gotcha.” 

And then he notices a large ogre sitting beside the ball with mirrors. 

Upon seeing him advance, Kobold-Vardis walks up to the ogre, who shouts something in Kobold. Kobold-Vardis continues to walk up to him. The ogre gets annoyed and starts yelling and waving his club. Kobold-Vardis stops before the ogre, bows, and then grabs the ball. 

The ogre is stunned and swings his club at what, to him, is an impertinent Kobold that needs to have its skull smashed.

Except this Kobold is anything but. Vardis ditches his disguise and uses his warding flare to stun the Ogre. Then Ogre blinded, misses. 

And Vardis starts to run. 

His first stop is the cavern with the tripwires. As he enters, he wonders how he will get through that. He stops just before the first trap and smiles. The Kobold Guard who freed him disables the entire mechanism. Vardis smiles and dashes past him. 

For some reason, he takes a left instead of a right and rushes into a chamber with Kobolds banging on the floor. 

He looks at them confused and then notices swarms of centipedes approaching him. He also can hear a pack of Kobolds rushing up against him. 

“Fireball.” 

A fiery mote flies off his hand and lands on the ground, and a blast of fire explodes in every direction, incinerating all of Vardis’ enemies. 

The fireball stops the on-rushing Kobolds, and Vardis scrambles through the caverns, carrying his mirror ball.

# 6th day of the 2nd tenday of 1492
## Bob Saget

The play was a smashing success the following evening. Vardis, Orsik, Unla Kee, and Thistle chose not to sit in the theater. They spent their night in the Talking Troll, ready to run or fight. 

And so they learn that a new character appeared at the end of the play: a demon-looking creature that introduced itself as Bob Saget. Using the effects of the CGI, it started to crack jokes that made everyone laugh. 

Thistle is confused about the outcome and looks at the original play more carefully. 

“Damn.” 

Thistle shouts, “This was a summoning ritual.” 

Thistle explains that the original play was a summoning ritual. The theatergoers were intended as sacrifices to tempt the demon to enter the gate, which is why the original ending was so confusing and bizarre. 

“What happened?” asks Orsik. 

“Well, we improved the play, so I don’t know?” 

Thistle mutters, “We should go check it out.” 

And there is this demon in purple smiling and laughing. It’s got a purple top hat and a purple formal jacket with purple tassels that seem almost alive. It’s wearing paints that go up to its waist and have embroidery that seems to move the more you stare at it. Its hands are covered in white gloves. But it’s the face that’s most disturbing. Two giant eyes with a purple iris, a mouth with jagged teeth, and purple skin that seems stretched over a skull. It looks every bit the part of a demon of the Abyss. 

The crowd should be terrified. 

The crowd is ecstatic.

Unla Kee approaches it, and the Demon introduces himself in a thick, heavy voice: “I am Bob Saget, my pronouns are he, him, and I am the tormentor of the kings who killed their jesters. I have been tormenting Kings for eternity with my dad jokes. I understand that I have you to thank for arranging my escape from the Abyss. You are the first people to summon me in eons.” 

Orsik steps forward, “So do we fight?” 

“Oh no, Orsik. I love this new job. I get to practice my material on a new audience. The Abyss is so - boring.”

Meanwhile, Urgala tells Vardis, “WE HAVE A HIT! Everyone wants to come tomorrow to see it again.”

The 3HPP is delighted. They have their hit, and they have a new star. Most importantly, the coin will flow. 

Kaelin, however,  is confused; he had planned this play as revenge on the theater-going public that never admired him for his creative genius, and now they are. He planned to summon a demon that would have laid waste to the entire town, consuming the theatergoers in hellfire. 

He pauses and shouts, “I AM GOING TO BE FAMOUS!” 

# Chapter 35 - The Play
## 5th day of the 2nd tenday of Flamerule 1492

Thistle is annoyed. Not as much as Orsik, mind you. Orsik wonders how his brilliant plan to run a play for profit is turning into a potential disaster. 

As he stands across the road from The Troll Inn, he looks at the theater. 

“Looks nice,” mutters the dwarf. 

Thistle groans, “Apparently, he bought the property?” 

“Bought? Why did he buy the property? Couldn’t he have rented it? We are so going to need to take over this production.” 

Urgala, who had told them about the bad news, looks at Orsik and says, “Let me get this straight: you made a deal with an out-of-work actor to put on a play and trusted him?” 

Orsik taps his Headband of Intellect, “This headband isn’t working as well as I would like.” 

However, for a moment, he wonders if the problem is that he’s been listening to people with worse ideas.

Urgala interrupts his deep thoughts, “The good news is that the play exists, and many people are here. At least Kaelen delivered the play. The buzz you created and the fame of your exploits have made this opening the event of the season.  If you guys can get this play to have a second night, you’ll make the money you need to make. If not, the Zhentarim and Hand of Yartar will get their pound of flesh.” 

She pauses and mutters, “The problem is the dialogue and story are a mess, the actors can’t act, and the set is a disaster.” 

Orsik asks, “Can we delay the opening?” 

Urgala laughs heartily. And then she says, “No.” 

Realizing that they will have to parallelize, Unla Kee will take on the script, Thistle will take on the direction, Orsik will do the acting, and Vaardis will collect the CGI. 

## Unla Kee

Unla Kee marches up to Kaelen, who is pleasantly surprised to see him. 

Kaelen smiles, “What an excellent script! I am so pleased!” 

The diviner looks at him, shakes his head, and says in an authoritative voice, “I have seen the future, and the future says this is going to be a disaster.” 

Kaelen’s confidence on a good day is poor, and this dismissive tone breaks him. He whimpers, “It’s that bad?” 

“Yes.” 

Kaelen grabs the script and rushes into his chambers in the The Talking Troll. A few hours later, he showed up with a new script. 

“Better,” mutters Unla Kee. As the mage reads it, he realizes that Kaelen has decided to use an archaic form of the common tongue that nobody would understand. He turns to the actor/producer/playwright and says, “I am very impressed with the use of this dialect. It’s very authentic, and nobody will understand a word the actors are saying.” 

“But it’s authentic! I was going for authenticity,” he argues. 

Unla Kee looks at Kaelen, and a stroke of genius hits him, “The trick isn’t to speak archaic common. Using just enough words creates the illusion of speaking archaic commonly. So instead of saying ‘You,’ we’ll say ‘Thou’ and ‘Thee.’” 

“BRILLIANT!!!” 

A few hours later, a cleaned-up script appears in Unla Kee’s hands, and he finally begins to see why the play is a disaster. The protagonist's motivations are wrong, and his interactions with the other characters are unmotivated and garbage. The play makes no sense. 

Unla Kee realizes the entire play can’t be rewritten; he must devise a trick to make this work. And then it hits him: “How about a play within a play about a bad play?” 

Kaelen stares at him; Unla Kee creates an awful outer play about the actual play. So, the audience is watching a play about a dreadful play. 

“Brilliant!” Kaelen is ecstatic. “This way, with a few lines of dialogue, this will be perfect, and we will protect the existing investment.”

Unla Kee then mutters, “The ending is terrible.” 

The wizard can’t make heads or tails out of it. Kaelen seems touchy about the bizarre, incomprehensible ending, as if it matters more to him than the entire play. Unla Kee realizes that he will have to awe the playwright with his intellect, so he manipulates fate to figure this out and help him brainstorm what he needs. And it works. 

Kaelen is pleased. 

Unla Kee is relieved. The play is no longer a mishmash of every bad avant-garde idea from off-High Road and has coherence. However, the story itself seems oddly elusive. 

## Thistle 

An unimposing, heavy-set man wearing a multi-colored shirt and a purple scarf over his head, barely hiding a buzz cut and his enormous ears introduces himself, “You are one of the investors! I am Everard Barney and completely committed to Kaelen's vision.” 

Thistle looks at the director and says, “Me too.” 

The director then starts to sob frantically.  “Kaelin, he creeps me out. And I can’t deal with the main actors! I give them directions, but they don’t listen. My entire family are bookkeepers. And I decided to branch out. And if this fails, I will have to return to Waterdeep and be an accountant.” 

Everard is now sobbing uncontrollably. 

Thistle realizes that Everard cannot understand his actor’s motivations, “Have you talked to your actors and asked them what they want?” 

“No?” 

Thistle sighs. He walks around the set, absorbing everyone’s body language and how they are talking. The fairy realizes that the lead actor, Amy, likes to pretend that he’s all fluffy and joyful, but she is status-driven and cares about what everyone thinks. Whenever anyone tries to get him to commit to something or make a definitive statement, it’s always “I don’t know, and I am not sure.” It’s almost as if he is hiding some mysterious past. 

Thistle tells Everard, “I am going to talk to Amy.” 

“How is talking to the actor going to help?” 

Thistle rolls his eyes, “For this lead actor, you must pander to their sense of being a star.” 

Everard listens and mutters, “So that scene where he gets on stage and says this soliloquy.“ He pauses thoughtfully, “I shouldn’t have her sitting on a pile of dung.”

“Correct.” 

“But I am trying to contrast between the heroic message and the crap we have to deal with on a day-to-day basis,” he whines pathetically. 

“What if we had him standing on a hill? And the dung is below him? So there is some separation?”

“Right, so they don’t make an association that I think Amy is shit.” 

“EXACTLY!”

“And that Amy elevates us from the shit?” 

Thistle nods. 

Everard is super excited at this point. “Since you seem to have such insight, perhaps you can help me with these other actors. They aren’t very good, and they keep forgetting their lines.” 

“Why?” 

“Because they are drunk.” 

“What if we make the fact they are drunk part of the play? That way every time they get a line wrong, it’s not a mistake, but part of the play?” 

Everard is tempted and then shakes his head. “Nah. What I am going to do is scream at them louder in the morning.” 

Thistle, who has dealt with Uncle Joon’s alcohol binges for years, agrees, “It will help with their hangovers. But instead of screaming for my drunk uncle, I just pour buckets of water. It works brilliantly, just like yelling.” 

Everard smiles, “My uncle screams at the drunk apprentices. I’ll have to tell him about the water trick.” 

They both smile. 

As the day winds down, Everard sheepishly approaches Thistle, “Any ideas on the finale?” 

Thistle stares at the incomprehensible staging and direction, hand gestures. He feels like something is wrong, but he has no insight. And then it hits him. This ending works great and says, “Change nothing.” 

## Orsik

While Thistle tries to fix the direction, Orsik concludes that the actors are not athletic enough. There is too little emphasis on physicality as if they treat this running race like a stroll. Their lack of energy is hurting the play, sapping its vitality. 

Thistle has told Orsik about Amy’s needs. Orsik considers being nice but mutters, “Terror is always the better option.” 

Casting a spell to disguise himself as a human acting coach, he marches up to Amy. 

“Who are you?” 

Orsik looks at her, “I am here to get your acting back on track. The name is Geoff Mamet.”

“What? I’ve never heard of you. Did Kaelin sign off on this? Is this under budget? Or am I paying this out of my pocket?” 

Geoff-Orsik growls, “You are not here to ask questions.” 

Amy deflates, “You’re the boss. Whatever you want…” 

She then describes how she wanted to do the main lead. Kaelin has been pushing back on his ideas, but she is a very timid, milquetoast kind of person who can’t push their ideas.

Geoff-Orsik realizes that he’s going to have to give her some spine. Unfortunately, his shout, “GROW UP AND STOP ACTING LIKE A WEAKLING!” doesn’t have the desired effect. 

She sighs, “I’ll just do what Kaelin wants.” 

Orsik hopes that Unla Kee and Thistle have fixed the script and direction enough to compensate for her acting. 

Then she starts explaining that she doesn’t like this scene, whining about how it doesn’t work and wanting it done differently. 

Geoff-Orsik stares at her and realizes her problem is a lack of energetic obedience to the director, not the director's direction or the script. 

She then explains that at the end of the play, she’s confused because the lines are complicated—“And I have to deliver these weird lines, and I have to make these over-the-top gestures and...” 

Geoff-Orsik has had enough and just shouts, “ENOUGH! You aren’t the director. You aren’t the screenwriter. Just deliver the lines, put some energy into it, and you’ll be fine.” 

She stares at him and meekly assents.

As Geoff-Orisk watches the rest of the rehearsal, his decision to focus on the energy level of the acting pays off: “What a brilliant idea I had!” 

He walks out of the theater, dispels the illusion, and walks back in. 

Amy sees him and shouts, “Oh, Mr. Orsik. How wonderful that you were willing to pay for Geoff Mamet to come and give me pointers. He’s made all the difference. Would you like to see it?” 

Orsik nods. 

## Vardis

Vardis walks to Urgala’s Inn and asks, “So tell me about this magical device?” 

She smiles, “Chancellor Geoffrey Invitations is what you need.  Chancellor Geoffrey would use the device to create these elaborate invites with sound and animated visuals.” 

“Wouldn’t an illusionist work just as well?” 

“Perhaps, but this won’t cost you anything.” 

“And where is it?”

She shows him a location south of Yartar in the Dessarin Hills - “A tribe of Kobolds has it. They don’t know what it is. They pilfered it off of one of Geoffrey’s messengers. And poor Geoffrey has been looking for it ever since.” 

“How do you know about this?” 

She smiles, “I wasn’t always an innkeeper.” 

A few hours later, Vardis is standing at the entrance of the Kobold Warrens. He pulls out a scroll of disguise and transforms himself into one of the creatures. 

Kobold-Vardis walks up to the entrance and, as he does, notices that there are dozens of Kobold footprints leading to a small three-foot cave. 

The cave entrance leads into a warren of cave tunnels illuminated by giant fire beetles. 

He stares at the ground, “Let’s see where these footprints go.” 

Eventually, the tunnels open into a cave, and another kobold is therein. The creature speaks to Kobold-Vardis, who looks like a Kobold but doesn’t speak the language. 

Kobold-Vardis shrugs, which confuses the other creature. It turns and runs deeper into the caves. The cleric follows. 

The tunnel opens up into a chamber with a few dogs. Kobold-Vardis thinks, “Pets?” 

He moves deeper into the cave complex and arrives at an enormous chamber with a 20-foot-high ceiling. Kobold-sized tables and chairs are scattered throughout the southwestern part of the room. On its northeastern side, a one-foot-high altar stands before a 15-foot-high monolith made of rusting iron.

“Let’s take a look at the altar.” 

The altar has an image of the Kobold god Grumsh strangling Tiamat. As he stares at the carving, he sees that Grumsh is a new addition to the monolith. 

“Focus on the mission.” 

He peers down the northeastern passageway from where he thinks the Kobold originated. 

The tunnel curves away from the cave with the monolith and then opens up into another cave. This one has tripwires stretching from one cavern's end to the other. 

Kobold-Vardis thinks to himself, “I could go back. But that would mean sacrificing our entire fortune. And I am not losing that money.” 

And he fails.

A bell rings, and a few Kobold guards appear. They are surprised to see one of their numbers caught up in the tripwires. 

Kobold-Vardis tries to imitate a confused and unintelligent Kobold who has gotten hopelessly lost. 

And pulls it off. 
One of the Kobold guards starts yammering at the other. Although Kobold-Vardis doesn’t understand anything he’s saying, he gets the intent - that Kobold-Vardis wasted his time and that the Kobold guard will have to spend all day cleaning up this mess. 

Kobold-Vardis successfully deceives the guard with a look of shame. 

The Kobold guard frees him, pushing him deeper into the complex. 

After stumbling ahead, Kobold-Vardis enters what looks like a large cavern, but then he realizes he’s outside. The sky above is starry. The moon is visible. The ground below is that of any valley he’s seen in this part of the north.

Then he realizes it’s all an illusion—an illusion projected by a ball with tiny, mirrored shards across its surface. 

“Gotcha.” 

And then he notices a large ogre sitting beside the ball with mirrors. 

Upon seeing him advance, Kobold-Vardis walks up to the ogre, who shouts something in Kobold. Kobold-Vardis continues to walk up to him. The ogre gets annoyed and starts yelling and waving his club. Kobold-Vardis stops before the ogre, bows, and then grabs the ball. 

The ogre is stunned and swings his club at what, to him, is an impertinent Kobold that needs to have its skull smashed.

Except this Kobold is anything but. Vardis ditches his disguise and uses his warding flare to stun the Ogre. Then Ogre blinded, misses. 

And Vardis starts to run. 

His first stop is the cavern with the tripwires. As he enters, he wonders how he will get through that. He stops just before the first trap and smiles. The Kobold Guard who freed him disables the entire mechanism. Vardis smiles and dashes past him. 

For some reason, he takes a left instead of a right and rushes into a chamber with Kobolds banging on the floor. 

He looks at them confused and then notices swarms of centipedes approaching him. He also can hear a pack of Kobolds rushing up against him. 

“Fireball.” 

A fiery mote flies off his hand and lands on the ground, and a blast of fire explodes in every direction, incinerating all of Vardis’ enemies. 

The fireball stops the on-rushing Kobolds, and Vardis scrambles through the caverns, carrying his mirror ball.

# 6th day of the 2nd tenday of 1492
## Bob Saget

The play was a smashing success the following evening. Vardis, Orsik, Unla Kee, and Thistle chose not to sit in the theater. They spent their night in the Talking Troll, ready to run or fight. 

And so they learn that a new character appeared at the end of the play: a demon-looking creature that introduced itself as Bob Saget. Using the effects of the CGI, it started to crack jokes that made everyone laugh. 

Thistle is confused about the outcome and looks at the original play more carefully. 

“Damn.” 

Thistle shouts, “This was a summoning ritual.” 

Thistle explains that the original play was a summoning ritual. The theatergoers were intended as sacrifices to tempt the demon to enter the gate, which is why the original ending was so confusing and bizarre. 

“What happened?” asks Orsik. 

“Well, we improved the play, so I don’t know?” 

Thistle mutters, “We should go check it out.” 

And there is this demon in purple smiling and laughing. It’s got a purple top hat and a purple formal jacket with purple tassels that seem almost alive. It’s wearing paints that go up to its waist and have embroidery that seems to move the more you stare at it. Its hands are covered in white gloves. But it’s the face that’s most disturbing. Two giant eyes with a purple iris, a mouth with jagged teeth, and purple skin that seems stretched over a skull. It looks every bit the part of a demon of the Abyss. 

The crowd should be terrified. 

The crowd is ecstatic.

Unla Kee approaches it, and the Demon introduces himself in a thick, heavy voice: “I am Bob Saget, my pronouns are he, him, and I am the tormentor of the kings who killed their jesters. I have been tormenting Kings for eternity with my dad jokes. I understand that I have you to thank for arranging my escape from the Abyss. You are the first people to summon me in eons.” 

Orsik steps forward, “So do we fight?” 

“Oh no, Orsik. I love this new job. I get to practice my material on a new audience. The Abyss is so - boring.”

Meanwhile, Urgala tells Vardis, “WE HAVE A HIT! Everyone wants to come tomorrow to see it again.”

The 3HPP is delighted. They have their hit, and they have a new star. Most importantly, the coin will flow. 

Kaelin, however,  is confused; he had planned this play as revenge on the theater-going public that never admired him for his creative genius, and now they are. He planned to summon a demon that would have laid waste to the entire town, consuming the theatergoers in hellfire. 

He pauses and shouts, “I AM GOING TO BE FAMOUS!” 

# Chapter 36 - The Hit
“Let’s stay for the party,” grumps Orsik as they look at their options. 
And so the 3HPP head out that evening to where the rich and powerful live, in a series of ranches that ring the town of Triboar.

Their destination is Westpharl Ranch, the home away from home of Cindrell. The ranch has sprawling grounds and a large master’s house. By the time they arrive, the party is in full swing. There is music, Bob Saget the demon is cracking jokes, folks are dancing, and there is food of every kind and variety.
As the party continues through the night and the group is having fun, a page comes to the 3HPP and invites them for a private audience.
Orsik is shocked, “are you sure she said all of us?” 
She smiles when they are taken into her study, a large room with bookshelves, art of various kinds, a lush carpet and a fireplace. 
Orsik can’t help but ask, “You want all of us simultaneously?” 
Cindrell realizes that Orsik thought this was a different kind of private audience. She looks at him with sorrow, the type of sorrow a rich person looks at someone who doesn’t have the class to be seriously considered as someone they might be interested in and doesn’t have enough money to make up for that lack of style. 
Orisk is oblivious. Vardis tries not to laugh and mutters, “Old sport.” 
And then, with all the grace of her noble upbringing, she turns to the 3HPP and says, “Sit down.”
And so they sit on the couch and chairs around her desk. 
“I don’t want to speak ill of my neighbors, but Hyuth Kolstaag, ever since he has arrived, has been a frightfully bad presence for the town. He’s a rich retired adventurer, unbelievably arrogant, with an astonishing sense of his own importance, utterly undeserved, and without a touch of class. He has rejected every one of my invitations to introduce him into Triborian society.” 
Orsik is confused, “okay?”
“Now, normally, I wouldn’t concern myself. Yet another misguided adventurer, who is a misanthrope with too much money and no class, has become a nuisance. He made several enemies, and they periodically showed up to try and kill him. The violence will cause a drop in the property values of Triboar.” 
She stares at them, expecting to hear a gasp. When none is forthcoming, she continues. 
“A few weeks ago, a pair of assassins slipped into town and attempted to kill him. We were awoken to peels of thunder and blasts of lightning. When the assassination attempt was foiled, the assassins stole a horse from the Cart and the Coin and murdered the stable hand.” 
“We will track down and kill the assassins,” grunts Orsik.
“Oh, no. I mean, if you want. I have a different job for you. This arriviste brought his trouble to our town and refused to recompense the owners of the Cart and Coin for their lost horse and buy a table at the luncheon in support of the stable hand’s family I threw.” 
“I am not sure what the issue is.” 
“He’s not a good person. He’s a bad person. And he’s bad for the town, and people are getting killed. And the  Lord Protector of Triboar,” and she pauses as if in pain, “Darathra Shendrel has only thought it appropriate to warn him with notices and stern words.”
Still, nobody understands what she is getting at.
“Let me explain. I ran for high office and was defeated by that nobody. I wouldn’t care, but nobody is a Harper Agent. I believe Triboar must be allied to the Lord’s Alliance, which will not happen while Darathra is here. I need to win the next election, and getting rid of this problem would help me win the election. It would show her ineffectual leadership and unwillingness to deal with the riff-raff.” 
Orsik gets the hint, “So you want us to finish the job the assassins botched?”
“Whatever you think is appropriate.” 
Orsik turns to Cindrell, “We need to talk.” 
Orsik is not in favor of killing random people. It’s not who he is now. But Thistle tells him he has a plan, so the fighter shrugs and nods.
The fairy turns to Cindrell, “Could we have an IOU made out to us? We know you are good for your money, but we’ve had some bad luck with other people.” 
She’s outraged and tries to solicit Vardis to intervene, but he refuses, “They have a mind of their own.”
Thistle insists.
Cindrell then tries to appeal to Orsik directly by offering to clear up his name in Waterdeep. Still, when he asks for all of the North, she laughs and says, “I am not a miracle worker.” 
Finally, she concedes, “Name your price.” 
Orsik proposes 10,000 gold, and she snorts, “With that price, I could get a magic user to erase him for every timeline of every existence.” 
“How about 500? 550 if I win the election?” 
Orsik shrugs, “Well, good luck with your election. You have a lot of schmoozing and hanging out and glad hanging out and pressing the flesh to do if you want to win the election.”
Seeing him leave, she shouts to Thistle, “What about you?” 
Thistle stares at her, “I’ll do it if you sign the IOU.” She’s resistant at first but relents when he unleashes the twinkle of his fairy eyes, the same eyes that Thistle’s lost love fell in love with. 
“Is this really necessary?”
“Ever since the Dragon Wars, you can’t rely on people to pay their bills.” 
She sighs and hands him a paper outlining the nature of the deal without getting into too many specifics. 
As they leave the ranch, Orsik asks, “What are you thinking?”
“Look, we don’t want the Lord’s Alliance here. So I think we go to this guy and tell him about the assassination, then to Darathra and tell her about this. And basically, ensure that Cindrell doesn’t win the election.” 
Orsik fists bump him.

Orsik and the rest of the gang don’t realize that Thistle is a member of the Seelie court. It’s why Prince Thornacious recognized him, and Blasius was his friend. He was a minor member but still a member. For him, intrigue is what members of the court do for fun. And seeing how bad Cindrell was at conspiracy, he had to show her how it was done. 
The following day, Thistle heads out to Urgala and tells her about the seriously odd shenanigans that Cindrell has asked him to do without revealing anything. He makes it known to her that Cindrell’s plans for the next election of the Lord Protector are underhanded, to say the least. 
Urgala is confused.  
Thistle says, “Well, she did ask us to kill Hyuth Kolstaag.” And then presents the contract. Urgala is curiously not surprised, “I do expect anything from her.” She then pauses, “But why him?” 
She stares at a corner in her drawing room in the Northshield House and says, “Hyuth was one of the more pain-in-the-ass magic users who went on the adventuring circuit. It was always about him. But you could rely on him during a fight. And now he is retired and for reasons he decided to come here. Seemed like a good idea, right? No. A guy like him has enemies. And he brings trouble, the kind of trouble that makes people feel unsafe.” She stops.
“Did you hear about what happened a couple of weeks ago?”
Thistle lies, “No.” 
She repeats the story of the assassins and then adds, “When you’re an adventurer, and you set up shop in a new time, and your old enemies come around to try and kill you and then kill a stablehand and steal a horse, it’s kind of expected to give the folks some money and sound sorry. This guy, Hyuth, summons some gargoyles to protect him and refuses to pay anything. He won’t even send a note to the family. Seriously, that’s some pretty shitty behavior. Makes all of us retired adventurers look bad.” 
“So we should kill him?”
“Now, I wouldn’t say that. I am not the person to judge. My book has some bad pages, and I have sinned.” 
Thistle feigns surprise.
“Yes, I have. And I don’t think I should judge. But if someone were to get him to be a better neighbor, that would be a good thing….” 
As they leave, Urgala mumbles, “I never imagined that Cindrell would be that cold-blooded.” 
Thistle then rejoins the 3HPP crew at the Talking Troll, and they all head to Darathra at the central tower. 
She is pleased to see them and congratulates them on the success of their play. She then says, “All this new business you brought into town! People are going to love you. Thank goodness you aren’t running for office,” she winks.
Thistle doesn’t waste any time, “we have a contract she signed to kill Hyuth, and as Lord Protector, we know you might object. And since we are blood brothers, I thought we should bring this to you.”
Darathra smiles like a cat about to pounce on a rat, “You did the right thing. I am going to bury her with this.
