Thistle then tells him, “We find a relic and come back, and the Oracle will tell us how to find a giant lord. But we don’t know which relic is where.” 
“That is correct.” 

# Chapter 47 - Quests Major and Minor
## Vardis
He groans when he hears his friend ask – “what’s your favorite book” and the Oracle responds with the “collected sayings of Annam” and the last torch winks out. 
“Well that’s it,” mutters the arrogant dwarf, as he marches towards the exit of the room.
Later that evening as they rest in one of the rooms of the faithful, the elven cleric turns to his friend, “what next?” 
Orsik pulls out two little note books from his pockets, one marked, “main quests” and the other titled, “side quests” he skims through them and turns to Yasmeen, “we should probably bring you back to civilization.” 
“That would be nice,” mutters the freezing gnome. 
Vardis turns to him, “but we can ask more questions.” 
“Six per day?” 
Thistle overhears them and shouts, “I want to ask where Adimond Kroskas is.” 
“You would.” 
“Yes.”
“Fine.” 
And the next morning the Oracle answers the question, “Maelstorm.” 
## Thistle
“And how do I get to this Maelstorm?” 
“King Hekaton’s conch will take you there. The path you choose here will lead you to it.”
Thistle reflects on what he has learned so far, that the Storm King’s court is in chaos, that a conch of teleportation will take him to the Maelstorm which may be the name of the court, that his enemy Adimond Kroskas is there. 
And of course, Orsik asks – “what act can I perform that will most impress the Zhentarim?” 
There is a moment of silence, and Annam says, “End this Giant War thus restoring the trade routes and secure preferential treatment from the Storm Giant King for Zhentarim trade merchants.” 
Thistle turns to Unla Kee hoping for something better, and the halfling stares smugly back and whispers, “I know how the world ends, this Oracle has nothing to teach me.” 
Remembering a game he played in the fey court where he won the game two truths in a lie with the royal lawyers, Thistle asks, “tell us which of the many burial grounds contains the artifact that will lead us to Duke Zalto?” 
A deep laughter echoes in the chamber, and the Oracle says, “I have been doing this for a very long time.” 
And a torch winks out. 
He flutters his wings – “what do the evil giants want?” 
“Power, glory, and the favor of their gods, so that when the ordning is remade, they are elevated to the top.”
Orsik smirks at Thistle who is just a tad bit annoyed.
And then he asks – “what actually happened to Urgala’s wife?” 
“She fell to her death in a well that lead to the Underdark.” 
“Anticlimactic,” he thinks.
## Orsik

As they reach the entrance of the Eye of the World, Orsik notices a strange sight, a ladder hanging in front of him.
He looks up and there in the sky is a strange vehicle, hanging. It looks like a small ship with sleigh runners, held aloft by a giant red balloon.
Before he can do anything, Thistle flies up. 
Grumbling he starts the climb up the ladder, ready for combat, and is surprised to see a group of people clad in black leather, with black leather masks staring at them. 
When everyone else arrives, a female voice begins – “I am Delsephine, captain of this ship. And here is my crew, Nyzroth, Brassik, Laz, Oriskus, Perella, Tralt, and Zalthia. We come to you on behalf of Klauth, the Great Dragon of the North! Klauth offers you this vessel and our services as a gift, so that you may cross his vast dominion while avoiding the many perils of the land below. War against the giants is inevitable, but the Great Dragon commands you forge ahead and face your destiny! The ordning of the giants is broken, and they are not united. Slay their leaders, and you may yet prevail. If you succeed in quelling the giant threat, Klauth bids you come to his hidden vale, so that he can reward your bravery.”
Orsik turns to the 3HPP and shouts, “huddle.” 
He reminds everyone that the Giants and the Dragons are two different ethno-religious groups with a belief in their ultimate triump and domination of the world and as a result hate each other. Klauth, as a Dragon, is well aware that his group was terribly wounded at the well of the dragon and that the last thing he wants is the Lord’s Alliance to come sniffing into the Spine of the World. 
“The enemy of the enemy,” he says, and they all agree. 
“So you’ll take us anywhere?” 
“Yes.” 
“Then to Everlund.” 
Delsephine looks at him, “I am not sure how this helps us in our mission.” 
“It’s really complicated, and we don’t trust so you for reasons of operational security, we’ll just keep this to the 3HPP.” 
She stares at him and commands her crew to set sail.
As the sky boat floats, Orsik notes how the fairy is increasingly uncomfortable about something.
“What do you feel about the Cult of the Dragon?” 
Delsephine glares at Thistle and for a moment she seems ready to hurt the small creature and then stares out into the sky and explains, “Severin was a fool who redirected the cult to invest all of its resources in the insane plot to bring Tiamat into the world. He walked away from the one divine purpose of the cult which was to end the insane war between Tiamat and Bahamut through the creation of a new purer kind of Dragon, the Dracolich.” She laughs, bitterly. “Tiamat thought the plan to enter the world was hers, but she was played for a fool by Zariel, her hostess in the planes of Avernus in the Nine Hell. She thought she could claim this world for her own, and thus end the war, but she failed.” Delsephine laughs, “and of course she failed.”
“So you were a member during these events?”
“No. I left after I was told that all Dracolich research was to be put on hold while we secured loot for the great ritual. Klauth offered me an opportunity, and I seized it.”
She then begins to talk about Klauth and what she feels for the great red dragon. Orsik, who is an open minded kind of guy, is a little taken aback by her deep sexual attraction for the great wyrm that she can barely hide.
Orsik is relieved when Thistle asks her about his nemesis.
She spits, “Damn him. Severin trusted that creature to provide him powerful magics. And he or was it a she? Did not.” 
Orsik is intrigued that Kroskas is someone who was important enough to be working with the powerful leader of the Cult of the Dragon. 
As they fly off, Orsik turns to his friend Vardis, “well here we are, and no frosty.” 
“He bailed on us.”
Thistle tries to explain that he felt that he needed to stick around and guard the eye but then shrugs, and says, “yeah. I mean he rarely entered the fight.”
“Like pulling teeth.”
Orsik
After several days of airborne travel, Orsik found himself once again in Danivarr’s House talking to Dral Thelev. 
“Have you solved the mystery of the Ordning? Have you brought peace to the world?” 
“We’re working on that.” 
Vardis adds, “it’s an ongoing project.” 
And Orsik finishes his thought, “the world’s a really big place. And there are a lot of cities you have to visit.” 
Dral’s face is covered in a momentary look of concern as he mutters, “I am sure Krowen Valharrow chose you for a reason.” He then smiles, “Some of Silvery Moon’s finest?” 
Thistle says, “we are looking for Captain Zoar.” 
Dral smiles, “that should be easy enough.” He tells one of the boys  in the tavern to go find the Captain. 
Orsik drinks the free wine waiting for the Captain who eventually shows up with three guards. He mutters at Dral, “why was I summoned?” 
Dral steps forwards and points to the 3HPP and Orsik who smiles as he tips a glass at the Captain.
“Smelly adventurers?” 
Things are about to go sideways when Thistle abruptly hands over the letter of Mark from Tamalin Zoar, Captain Zoar’s sister and the mayor of Calling Horns. 
The Captain is taken aback, “you have a letter of introduction from my scoundrel of a sister?” He smiles ruefully, “she started off as a cutthroat, a brigand, a cut-purse, robbed folks up and down the coast, became a member of the gang of women thieves in Triboar, and then got older, bought a house on a road, has built a town and is now mayor.” He then puffs his chess out, “she’s become a person of character and substance. I am very proud of her. If she thinks you are worthy of a letter of introduction, then you are.”
“Of course we are,” says the smoothe Vardis in a silky tone of voice.
Captain Zoar reads the letter carefully and then asks, “you need an introduction to the elders of Everlund? A fine meal?”
Thistle interjects, “we are looking for a passage to Silvery Moon. The last time we were there we had some difficulty getting in and out of town. Could you write a note saying we are in Silvery Moon as your agents?”
“So you want me to give you a letter with my mark, saying that you are on Captain of the Guard business and the agents of Silvery Moon should give you all assistance and grace?” 
Orsik tries to say yes, and groans when Thistle corrects him, “yes and we want to make sure there is a reference to a criminal organization operating in Silvery Moon that is trafficking demons.” 
To Orsik’s surprise, Captain Zoar is excited. “Dral get me a drink. I have a story to tell.” He sits down and talks, “the bastards of Silvery Moon abandoned Sundbar when the orc hordes attacked turning the city into a ruin. They didn’t raise a finger to help. And so we decided to leave the Lord’s Alliance which is not an alliance.” Seeing Orsik’s interest, he continues with a detailed and rambling discussion of the various battles that took place around Sundbar. And then he ends saying, “I am not surprised they are trafficking demons. That matches the blackness and depravity of their souls” 
Orsik waits to see what will happen next. The Captain stares at Orsik and then shouts at one of his guards, “go fetch me a letter so I can affix my mark on an introduction.” The guard groans and leaves. 
The dwarf is about to sit down and wait when Zoar shouts at Dral, “Bring foods and drink. And those little fishies that you make with the special sauce and the salad and the dressings.” 
When the guard returns, Zoar writes his letter Mark and asks, “ Your group’s name?” 
“3HPP,” mutters Thistle.
“You are the three half-pints and a pint?” 
Orsik stutters, “oh, no it doesn’t stand for anything.”
“Because I heard of the play. You are the producers of the play that has Bob Saget? It is an amazing production. I haven’t had a chance to see it, but friends have. And I do so look forward to it when it comes to town in a few weeks.” 
Orsik is delighted and is calculating the coin he will make, and then says, “you must see it several times!” 
“Rumor has it that you were silent backers?” 
“Yes. Although it involved quite a bit of work too. It was a little weird.” As he reflects on the events of the play. 
“Well it’s a smash performance every time. Although each time the play ends there is a different ending.” Then Zoar takes a sip of his wine, stretches back, and says, “And the lead actor, Bob Saget puts on a terrifying performance as a demon.”
Orsik worried that the demon may have killed folks, probes using an offhand question, “ and no deaths and murders!” 
“No, why would you ask?”
“The original plot had some murders, but we stopped that.”
“Is it family friendly? I was planning to take my children.” 
“Totally safe.” 
At this Captain Zoar is excited, “Given your previous encounters with the Silvery Moon constabulary, I was thinking of using a different name for your group. But now, given your fame, I think I should stick it to them. The idea that Everlund is allied with the producers of the smash production is going to make them eat some humble pie!”
As he finishes the paper work, Thistle asks, “have you considered running for me?” 
Orsik smiles, the smile of a wolf arriving on his prey, “you think about it and let us know how we can help you.” 
The Captain is taken aback, reflects on the offer and then says, “I am quite pleased with my role as High Captain. I fully support the current speaker, Vatrice Stormwright, and I don’t question her leadership and she’s up for re-election in a few years and many things can happen. Although if you were to be seen as defeating the Giant threat that would greatly increase my prestige.” 
With the business concluded, and High Captain Horix Zoar, finally returning to his duties, fed, a little bit inebriated and quite happy, Orsik turns to the group, “let’s go get ourselves an artifact.” 
Orsik, as a dwarf, and a cosmopolitan itinerant sale-sword, had learned much about the Nether Mountains. The first is that they looked dark and brooding. He never understood that. He saw the mountains as a rich repository of tangible riches like granite. That was not motivated his interest in following up on the quest from the Mary and the Fighters of Light. The Nether Mountains were said to have magical items that once belonged to the ancient Netheril. It was entirely possible that the Fighters of Light had lost such an artifact. Although why Kobolds would possess it was confusing. 
From the merchants that they bought supplies from, Orsik also learned that the mountains are also full of tribes of orcs, while the eastern peaks are home to several blue dragons chased out of the northern Anauroch by a dragon known as the Doom of Desert.
“I’ll have to ask Delsephine if she knows anything about that,” he mutters. 
## Vardis

Much to Vardis’ relief, Thistle found the trail and lead them directly to the entrance of the Kobold cave. As he stared at it he remarked to himself that Kobold warrens tend to look  the same. He wondered if it was some kind of instinctual behavioural pattern that drove that or something else. 
“Kobolds,” thinks Vardis. And watches as Unla Kee and Thistle walk into the warrens with no effort. 

He and Orsik are able to squeeze through the small entrance, despite their height and armor. 

Vardis is again annoyed to discover that the tunnels are barely tall enough for him to squeeze through, reaching 5-6 feet in height and sometimes 4-5 feet. He will not be able to move as quickly as he wants through these warrens. 
What he immediately notices is that there is a dim light in the tunnels, created by fire-light beatles. 
“No matter, that’s what our light sources are for.” 

# Chapter 48 - The end of a relationship
## Orsik 
He was pleased with himself. After a day and a half in Silverymoon, the 3HPP had armed themselves with magic and weapons. They had spent their coin and eaten in the fine restaurants of the fair city. 
“Yes, life was good.”
When a patrol of the guards of Silverwatch stopped in front of him, what appeared to be a captain addressed him. 

“The High Lord of the Silver Marches as well as the ruler of Silverymoon and the High Marshal of the Argent Legion, the army of the League of the Silver Marches, High Lord Methrammar Aerasumé wishes to speak with you.” 
“He wants an audience with us?”
“No. You are diplomatic envoys from the City of Everlund. And you are expected to present your credentials to his lordship.” 
“Okay, we’ll get to it eventually.”
The captain coughs.
Orsik sizes them up and then thinks better of it, “Diplomatic Immunity.” 
With a tinny nasal voice, the captain says, “And that’s why we are here to escort the 3HPP and ensure that nobody gets in your way.” 
“We don’t need protection. Very nice of you, though.” 
The captain continues, “You may not know this about diplomatic immunity, but you can be expelled regardless of said immunity. And more importantly, his Lordship wants to talk to you.”
Orsik, realizing the opportunity, agrees, as does Thistle. 
Ten minutes later, Orsik and the 3HPP enter the audience chamber. 
Thistle. 
Thistle and the 3HPP are ushered into the audience chamber of the High Lord. The room is filled with courtiers discussing matters of state, budgets, and trivia of all kinds. 
The aide coughs and introduces the 3HPP; the letters of introduction are handed over to the High Mage – Taern “Thunderspell” Hornblade, who examines them and declares that they are to his satisfaction. 
“We are here to offer you a cultural exchange with the city of Everlund,” answers Thistle to the High Lord’s question about why they are here. 
The courtiers laugh. “That’s a mere village town, a hovel,” declares a lord. 
Thistle overhears Orsik outraged, muttering, “Does he know he just insulted Everlund’s diplomats?” For a moment, he worries that the dwarf will do something foolish, but then he is quiet. 
The High Lord smiles and asks, “What could the city of Everlund share with our city?” 
“We have a traveling play.” 
The High Lord interrupts, “You didn’t tell me, High Mage.” Thistle is about to continue, but the High Lord interrupts him, “You are the play's producers?” 
Thistle smiles and nods.
“I am in love with it. I have seen it twice already.”
“Twice? And are you a fan of Bob Saget?” 
“Yes! I love the segment in the middle of the play, Mordekainens’ Best Home Illusions, where local mages present a short imaginary tale, and the audience votes on the best one. And the play ends differently every time. It keeps bringing the audience back for more.” 
Vardis is super excited and shouts, “There are 20 different endings!” 
“No spoilers,” demands the High Lord. “That would cause a diplomatic incident!” The High Lord is excited and says, “I demand we arrange for a long run of this show. Chamberlain, please work with these producers and arrange for an extended run in the royal theater.” 
Thistle is excited and about to say something when Unla Kee shouts, “And 50,000 gold pieces. And if you can’t front that cost, we’ll return to Everlund.” 
The fairy and the dwarf try to stop Unla Kee, who can’t stop talking. “It’s for the pre-production costs, not for us!” 
And that seems to have done the trick; the High Lord says, “If that’s what it takes!” 
The High Treasurer is appalled and interrupts the High Lord with words about budget, fiscal policy, deficit spending, and inflation. 
High Lord Aerasumé sighs and says, “I can’t give you 50,000, but I can give you a thousand. And I can certainly tell the Chamberlain that the Royal Theater will be offered for free.” 
Thistle notes the Chamberlain’s tight and very fake smile. 
Unla Kee, to Thistle’s chagrin, doesn’t. “For that amount, we consider giving you a discounted pair of free tickets.” 
That does not land as well. Thistle watches as Orsik steps into smooth things over, “what my friend Unla Kee was trying to say was that we thank you for your support, and you will, of course, have two reserved seats for the play.” 
With that taken care of, Thistle turns to another topic. “Can you help us with the extradition of Xamlyn Margaster?”
Sensing confusion, Thistle continues, “She has committed unpardonable sins in Everlund, and we have come to pick her up.” 
The High Lord presents a sad face, “Lady Margaster, of the noble house of Margaster, is an ally of the High Lord and a member of a respectable Waterdhavian noble house. Lady Margaster is a guest in our city, and I will not deport her.” 
Thistle worries that they might run into trouble if they talk to Margaster and asks, “Is it okay if we talk to her?” 
“Lady Margaster is a member of this community, and she is free to speak with anyone or not speak with anyone, like everybody.” The High Lord looks at the Chamberlain and then back to Thistle, “I am truly delighted that the city of Everlund sent such famous producers as their emissaries. High Captain Horix Zoar corrected our mistake when we through you off the city because of a terrible misunderstanding.” 
And with that, Thistle and his companions are shown to the outside of the High Lord's Residence. 
Once outside, Thistle turns to his companions, “Killing Xamlyn is too easy. We need to disgrace her and to do that, we need to expose his devils.”
“Dispel Magic?” asks Vardis. 
“No, because magic is used to alter the appearance of folks and is equivalent to pulling off someone’s wig,” he mutters.
“How did you know that?” 
“Uncle Joon.” 
“Don’t want to know.” 
Finally, after some discussion, they decide that perhaps an approach would be to ally themselves with the Senneth, the Cleric of Mielikki, who raised Thistle from the dead. 
Surprisingly, Senneth is not surprised to discover that there are devils in the city. “In a city of 40,000, at least one mortal is a devil; why not a fiend?” But she dismisses the idea of any cults operating, “after the cult of the dragon was defeated, there was a general crackdown on cults. The smart ones packed their bags and left the city.” 
Thistle decides to take a risk and describes the appearance of the Cambions that showed up at the trial. 
Senneth is shocked, “those are the men who follow Lady Margaster.” The cleric then pauses, “I do recall some unpleasantness with you and the Margasters.”
Thistle, realizing this might go badly, changes the topic and distracts her from this line of questioning. 
Unla Kee brings up the play, trying to change the topic of conversation. 
But Senneth sees unwanted connections, “interesting that you bring up a story about a devil and a play with a demon.” 

## Vardis

Vardis, sensing a potential disaster, turns to Senneth and asks – “What library do you recommend we visit for research on Cambions?” 
Her train of thought interrupted, she says, “The Lady’s College!” 
The heroes leave Mielikki’s Glade, and the high priest crosses the Moonway, traverses the city, goes over the invisible bridge to the north, and reaches the college. 
Thistle, that repository of the obscure of the North, recalls from his uncle that the college was famous for not having a “master-apprentice” form of education but instead a classroom-style education. 
Arriving at the Lady’s College, many young mages, scholars, and teachers are running around.  
Vardis wonders where to go when a bright young tiefling wearing the “Ask Me” sign approaches him.
“Welcome to the Lady’s College! Are you a student? Parents? Or visitors?”
Vardis stares at her and says, “We want to learn about Cambions.” 
The young tiefling Anakis is giddy – “Cambions are my favorite! There’s a lecture by Sage Vusk on the topic just down the hall.” 
“Lecture?”
Anakis giggles and then composes herself, “Right, at the Lady’s College, material is presented in prepared speeches. Students can choose to attend or not choose to attend. Some lectures have not had a person show up in years, but the Sages keep giving the lectures because they are required to. Sage Vusk is a sweetheart, and nobody goes to his class.” She pauses, “Cambions are cool, but nobody in Silverymoon will ever see an infernal creature …” 
Anakis notices that Orsik is staring at her, “Yes, I know, not a goodly race. But the Lady’s College is not as narrowminded…” 
Vardis takes leave and enters the large, empty room. At the front is a podium, and in front are a set of sparsely populated rows of chairs. He sits down and listens.
Sage Vusk discusses the origins of Cambions and observes that a few centuries ago, it was assumed that they were the progeny of male demons and female humans. Still, now we know this was not the case and that a Cambion is the name given to the progeny of any union between a demon and a non-demon. He then continues that as part of this more profound research into Cambion anthropology, some modernist sages believe that a Cambion can have free will. In contrast, others like Sage Vusk believe that, ultimately, the infernal nature of the Cambion means that that is impossible. He bolsters that argument with the observation that the Cambion is the foot soldier of blood war. 
Vardis shouts, “Disposable? Who disposes of them?” 
“Their masters.” 
“You seem to be the kind of people who want to kill a Cambion. This might be of more interest. Although many have focused on the Cambion’s raw brutality, their ability to charm their enemies using their fiendish charm makes them valuable. A single Cambion did in many adventuring groups, like The Noble Daggers.” 
“Is there any way to resist this charm?” 
“Great question! There are the usual means. But I read in a monograph that some sages believe the Fiendish Charm requires the Cambion to see their enemy. It is possible magical darkness, or blindness or invisibility could provide sufficient protection.” 
Orsik asks, “So, Professor,” 
“I am not a professor; I am a traveling scholar.” 
“Sorry, Mr. Scholar, sir, I understand that Cambions can disguise themselves as humans using magic?” 
“Does Dispel magic work?” 
“Yes, but the problem is that so many people use magical illusions to disguise their appearance that casting the magical spell might not have the intended effect, and some consider it a grave breach of etiquette. It’s not unheard of for there to be duels!” 
Vardis is now curious about what Orsik’s next move will be. 
“Is there any movement to ban magic?” 
At that, Sage Vusk laughs, “In Silverymoon? The city that was ruled by the chosen of Mystara, the goddess of magic? The city that invented Magical Schools? No.” 

Vardis becomes frustrated and sighs. 
Thistle taps him and Orisk on the shoulder, and they walk outside, “we could tell Bob Saget to create a groundswell against magic as a hidden theme in his play?” 
Vardis likes the idea, as does Orsik. 

## Orsik

Late in the evening, an elf wearing a purple robe and quite obviously carrying a symbol of the Harpers approaches. 
“Krowen Valharrow has asked me if you ever plan to deal with the problem of the Giants. We have noticed that you went to the Oracle and discovered critical information to ending the war, but instead of ending the war, Silverymoon is doing goodness knows what.” 
“This feels a little bit like micromanagement.”
The Harper agent betrays a flash of anger, “A Frost Giant practically sacked Bryn Shander, and Hill Giants attacked Goldenfields. While you sit here and plot your revenge, people are dying, and the Giants continue to assemble magical devices to prove their worthiness to Amn. And Krowen wants to know if you plan to do anything?” 
“Absolutely.” 
“Perhaps I was unclear; there was access to some magical pathways given to you. If you don’t start moving today, you will lose access.” 
“We have other ways to move around.” 
“Yes, and you have chosen to ally yourselves with cultists.” 
Thistle bristles, “former.” 
Orsik sees Vardis looking at him and nods; he’s dealt with managers who think they can manage him – “So this is a performance improvement plan? I know a lot about them, and they are a farce. Every single one I was put on led to me getting fired. So I don’t do them.” 
The Harper smiles, “Oh no, we aren’t going to put you on a performance improvement plan. You are a contract. We wouldn’t renew. But Krowen still sees value and thinks if we made an offer of treasure maps. Or, as Krowen said – stop this random questing and go save the North.” 
Orsik looks at him and says, “You should take those maps and dig that treasure.”
“What?” 
“We’re done.”
The elf grabs the maps and glares at Orsik, “This idea of using contractors instead of full agents is because of Ezra. There are those of us who never thought that was a good idea. Mercenaries don’t do things because it’s right; they do it because they have to.” 
Orsik carefully controls his rage and turns to the 3HPP, “So, are we done?”
Unla Kee sips from a glass, “done.” The other two nod their agreement to Orsik. 
The elf picks up the maps and leaves.
Orsik turns to his friends, “I know what we do next.”.  

The next day, the group splits up. 
Thistle uses the time to find a scholar to research Adminod Kroskas and finds Rebecca Deakin, an archivist who works for Wyrmworks in their cataloging department. Her intellect is beyond doubt, and her mastery of all dragons-related topics is unparalleled. And she happens to have some free time. She agrees to look into the question for a modest fee of 1800 gold pieces. 
“Come back in a week.” 
Orsik uses his time to talk to the local Zhentarim agent, Uzorel “The Viper” Hornbalde. The Viper is, in fact, distantly related to Taern Hornblade, the former ruler of Silverymoon, and is reluctant to meet Orsik until he discovers that it’s the Orsik of Parnast and Triboar fame. Uzorel is a relatively diminutive figure with lanky limbs, pale skin, and a sharp, angular goatee. He makes up for what he lacks in physical presence with tact and charm.
Orsik then betrays the location of the circles he knows, the one in Everlund and the one in Yartar. 
“There’s no profit in that information for you? You give it?” 
“I am just sharing with friends.” 
The Viper reaches for his sword, “Friends?” 
Realizing that The Viper is paranoid, he reassures the Black Agent, “I had a beef with a high-ranking Harper. And as far as I am concerned, this gets back at him where it hurts.” 
“Revenge, I get that.”
“But no reward?”
“I just want the higher-ups to know I am one of them.” 
The Viper releases his grip on his sword in its sheathe and smiles toothily, “They will. And they will be pleased.” 
When Orsik tells everyone what happened, they realize that Yartar and Everlund may become too hot for them to visit. Thistle begins to see a pattern emerge and understands why Orsik is treated the way he is across the realms – “he gets angry when someone doesn’t give him the respect he thinks he deserves, and then lashes out by leaving.” 
Later that evening, the group has left Silverymoon and is talking to the crew on the Great Airship, a gift from the ancient red dragon Klauth. 
As they fly above the Evermoors, west of Silverymoon, Delphine asks – “where to?” 
Thistle shouts, “the Grandfather tree.” Everyone looks at him, “why?” 

“Because we need to find information that will lead us to a giant so we can get the conch and get to King Hekaton. And apparently, the only way we can do that is if we collect these artifacts. And because I have information that my beloved is with the Fire Giant Duke, we need to collect several because the Oracle didn’t specify which artifact would get that information. And the Grandfather Tree is right here.” 
“Touchy,” think Vardis and Orsik. 
As they fly towards the Grandfather Tree, Brazzik, one of the crew members, approaches the 3HPP and says, “I am a bit of a loresmith and know quite a bit about the grandfather tree and would be happy to share it, for a small fee.” 
Thistle looks at Brazzik, thinks for a moment, and then says, “I do know that it’s a big tree. And I do know about the Uthgardt Tree Ghost Tribe that treats it as sacred ground.” 
At that, Thistle laughs as he remembers something else. “There was a pair of Seelie Dryads, Halisa, and Helia, who misspoke at a ball, offended the Queen, and were punished for protecting this tree for some indeterminately long period.” 
Orsik and Vardis look at each other and give Brazzik 50 gold, hoping for better information. 

Brazzik takes the coin – “Your friend was accurate, but his information was incomplete. If you kill the Dryads, there are tales of powerful and dangerous consequences. Furthermore, there are patrols of the tribe that sometimes come through here. So you need to be careful.”
Thistle laughs, “great! Fey is coming on a pilgrimage with a cleric of light!” 
Orsik adds, “With a neutral evil wizard and a wanted criminal!” 
Vardis turns to him, irritated, “Aren’t you supposed to be disguised?” 
His dwarf friend responds, “We’re all friends here?” 
Everyone nods. 
At that point, Vardis realizes that the Cultists have become friendlier since Orsik hurt the Harpers, something he could not resist bragging about. For these ex-cultists, it’s a little payback for the events of the Cult of the Dragon and the role of the Harper Remalia Haventree in organizing the North against Tiamat. 
Orsik, seeing the approval, goes back to his annoyance of the Harpers, “Totally trying to micromanage us.” And then sarcastically says, “It’s as if the whole point of being part of a powerful organization is so that you can mindlessly do whatever your superiors say.” 
Vardis looks at him and mutters, “Don’t remind me of the work I quit.” 
One of the cultists, who looks elven, stares at him, “Is that why you were kicked out of Lorien’s cult?” 
Brazzik interrupts the elf, adding, “The other thing to be aware of is that these dryads have allied with various creatures around the forests to ensure the trees protection.”

# Chapter 49 - Killing a dragon
## Vardis 
“Well that was awkward.” 
His master plan to obliterate the army of kobolds that was surely hiding in the chamber, foiled by the fact that only a single kobold was hiding in the chamber.
Worse, his arrogance had caused him to fall into a pit. 
After clambering out of the pit, he picked over the charred remains of the kobold. A shiny idol of Tiamat piqued his interest. 
Outside, his friend Orsik shouts, “hey, let’s go down this passageway.” 
Vardis groans. That would mean squeezing for him. 
As they walk through the twisty passage, Orsik keeps pointing out the shoddy workmanship and how Dwarves do a better job, and “No Dwarf would ever make a mess of things this way.” 
Vardis tries hard to stifle a yawn. 
They reach an intersection, and Orsik shouts, “right hand rule.” And so they follow that simple rule and re-emerge into the same large area that they had left, just some 20 or so feet further down.
“What a waste of time.” 
 He hears something. A very specific sound that is unmistakable. And then he remembers something that Orsik had said and Thistle had repeated, that the Netherese Mountain Range was home to blue dragons. 
“That’s a blue dragon,” mutters Thistle. 
Vardis nods and tries to remember why he is here, “How did I get here?” And he remembers. When they had first arrived in Everlund, they stumbled across this old lady called Mary who was shouting – “*one day, that monster, the Kraken, will return to destroy our city with its powerful tentacle and mind control! The Kraken will come with his allies; we need to prepare because they are more powerful than anything we have seen before!” *She was head of an organization called the “Fighters of the Light” and it had a 5 GP membership fee. “We were broke,” he remembers. And then thinks, “I am about to fight a blue dragon to save 5 Gold.” 
“Seriously, saving 5 gold?” 
Orsik turns to him, “there are three things of value in these mountains, granite, dragon hoards and Netherese Artifacts. The first is the most valuable, but the last the most powerful magic items in the realms. If there is a chance.” 
Vardis nods, “So what's the plan.” 
Orsik and him start to sketch out a plan, when Thistle shouts – “Pro or anti-Tiamat?” 
The dragon roars. 
Thistle reaches for his bow, “you’re dead.” 
Vardis whispers, “what did he say?”
“Tiamat for-ever!” mutters Thistle. 
“I guess we are doing this.” 
“Yes, we are,” says Orsik as he gets ready to attack. 
## The Battle
Thistle flies off, tells Thuul to advance on the dragon, fires two shots that hit. The dragon roars in pain, just in time to get stung with a squirt of acid from the guard drake. 
Orsik dashes to the dragons, slashes at it four times causing the creature to bleed while sheathing the creature in booming energy. 
Before Vardis can act, two kobold seemingly blink into existence and attack. 
Seeing his situation as precarious, the dragon decides to disengage and flies from the eastern most part of this enormous cavern, 80 feet to the middle of the cavern, just before it turns south. 
Unla Kee is pleased, the dragon is just inside of Fireball range. And as he reaches for the spell components, Orsik shouts, “hey dude, you cast all of your third and fourth level spells.” 
“Huh?” 
“Yeah.” 
“Well what am I supposed to do with these first and second level spells?” 
“Kill Kobold.” 
Unla Kee visibly pouts and then casts sleep. 
Thistle orders Thuul forward, and then fires two arrows at the follower of Tiamat. Unfortunately his arrows miss. 
Vardis shouts, “it’s always the light cleric.” The fireball hits, but it’s a glancing blow. The dragon dodges most of the damage. 
Orsik, meanwhile, has ran towards the dragon, and although more than 30 feet away, throws his hand axes and hits once. 
The dragon howls and then breathes lightning on Orsik. The bolt courses through his body as it reduces his strength in half and then the dragon flies to the west, outside of their field of vision.  
Unla Kee and Vardis, meanwhile, are finding themselves surround by kobolds that keep blinking into existence. The wizard shouts, “good thing it’s just kobolds” as he unleashes Rime’s binding ice, killing four of the materialized creatures. 
Thistle flies towards the dragon and can’t see him. Rather than dash into combat, he decides to find a nook to hide in and prepare to fire an arrow if the dragon appears in his field of view. 
Orsik, seeing that he can’t reach the dragon, spots a corridor the group ignored before that should drop him behind the dragon. 
The dragon seeing that everyone is sneaking around, decides to investigate his surroundings and spots Thistle. He tries to move into position, but Thistle sees him and fires an arrow. Orsik holds his breath and exhales when he see that it misses. The dragon then breathes on Thistle, but this fairy has faced a lot of dragons and knows what to do. 
Meanwhile at the other side of the cavern… 
Unla Kee shouts, “running out of 2nd level spells.” 
Vardis shouts back, “magic missile?” 
And then three kobolds are dead.
And another light blinks and a kobold materializes and critically misses. 
And near the dragon, Thistle moves closer to where the dragon was and hides. 
Orsik meanwhile emerges from the tunnel that he had crawled into and hits the dragon with a Firebolt. Thistle stares at him, shocked. Orsik had a perfectly good bow that he could have used to hit the Dragon twice. But no, Orsik wanted to use a spell. Thistle shakes his head in disbelief. 
The dragon bloodied and bruised shouts in common, “do you think we could reach an accommodation?” 
Orsik growls, “No.” 
“I just felt I should ask.” 
“What a polite dragon.” 
“But still no?” 
“Yes.” 
Vardis overhears this and mutters, “he came out charging at us.” 
Unla Kee corrects him, “no, what happened is that Thistle asked him about Tiamat, and the Dragon choose poorly and Thistle attacked.” 
Vardis nods as he misses an attack on a kobold with his sword, “right.” 
The Dragon meanwhile flies south into a cave that has a 60 foot steep high climb and hides deep in the cave. 
Orsik knows that this is the last stand for the Dragon. There’s no where else for the dragon to run, so he charges up the hill and is just five feet from the top. 
Thistle who had hidden, finds himself too far away to move into position to hit the dragon. He chooses to fly closer and stay hidden. 
Unla Kee and Vardis, meanwhile, are still fighting kobolds. One of the dog like creatures charges at Unla Kee and misses. Another strikes Vardis and misses. Another snores. 
Unla Kee has had enough, and decides to run towards the dragon. 
Vardis kills a kobold that was about to strike Unla Kee with his bow. 
The Dragon laughs, and then strikes Orsik with a lightning bolt. Orsik dodges the bulk of the blast and is still alive. 
The Dragon stops laughing. He’s holding on by a thread with only 6 HP. Orsik is hanging by another thread of 8 HP. 
Thistle is very happy, he knows the Dragon is dead. But perhaps Thistle was counting his chickens before they hatched, because he misses twice. 
Orsik glares at the fairy, “twice?” 
Vardis who has been keeping an eye on the combat, mutters, “three times?” 
The dwarf was planning to use his spell, but decides that perhaps this is not the time. He rushes up the last five feet, charges at the dragon, and swings his sword at the creature, killing it. 
“Hallelujah!” 
Orsik is breathing heavily, Thistle is firing a few more arrows into the body while the cleric and magic user are racing towards them. 
Unla Kee finally arrives next to the dragon’s corpse, and smiles. “Time to extract the most valuable body parts.” 
Thistle and Orsik, meanwhile, head back to the dragon’s hoarde which is almost 300 feet away from their current location. There they find 2,000 gp, fourteen gems worth 250 gp each, a *potion of mind reading*, a *bag of beans*, and a *potion of superior healing*
They also find a peculiar gem in the shape of a star. 
By the time they arrive back at the dragon’s corpse, the bloodied Unla Kee is admiring his carving – “Let’s see, some dragon meat, dragon blood for potions of dragon strength, dragon teeth that can be turned into dragon arrows for our favorite fey, dragon bone that can be turned into dragon weapons and a dragon horn that we can use to craft a dragon wand.” 

Unla Kee shouts, “well shall we continue?” 
Orsik looks at the bloody mess and the fact that he barely survived, and that the spell casters are out of spells and announces, “nah, we got what we came for.” 

# Chapter 50 - Star crossed Dryads
## Unla Kee
Unla Kee is the first to notice the Grandfather Tree from the airship. The tree's crown is over 350 feet high and spreads 800 feet in diameter. As the ship approaches the ground, he notices four more trees under the canopy. Just before it lands, he catches a glimpse of the ground. Two large rings of raised earth circle the tree, an outer and inner ring. 
As he considers the long hike, he remembers that he could be the proud owner of Cloud Giant’s castle. He thinks that Eigeron had told him precisely where he could find Blagothkus. And then, he considers that his current allies might not be as willing to hand over the castle to him. They might want to share it or, worse, control it. 
Thistle might not care; he’s only on an errand of love and vengeance. 
“No,” he realizes, “this will have to wait.” 
## Thistle
Thistle is in a rush to find the artifact, get back to the Oracle, get the Conch, and find the Maelstorm where Adimond Kroskas is hidden. 
And, only if strictly necessary, deal with the threat to the realms. 
As he approaches the southernmost tree under the canopy, a dryad’s voice tells him to go no further. 
The dryad introduces herself as Halisa, explaining that she has been exiled from the Fey because she offered her honest opinion about the Queen’s dress. 
Thistle squirms visibly when he hears that, covering his eyes with the tips of his wings. 
Thistle tries to explain that they are pursuing an artifact of great importance and asks if she knows where it is. 
She says no and explains that the Tree cannot be touched because that was the terms of her punishment. 
“Tree Protection Schedule D,” asks Thistle. 
“The very one!” replies the dryad in her indirect light voice. 
Orsik intervenes and infuriates the dryad, who tells him to leave because he’s a dwarf. Orsik explains that he was once a dwarf scout and understands the importance of not damaging nature. But Halisa is unimpressed and shoos him off. 
Thistle sighs as Orsik predictably marches off in a huff. He did that in Parnast and now here. 
Halisa then explains to Thistle that other Dryads might be willing to help him. 
As Thistle is about to leave, he’s startled to see Prince Thornacious. 
Halisa is terrified at the idea that royalty is coming and demands they leave immediately. 
Thistle is still confused about how Prince Thornacious magically appeared here. Still, observing the awkward flying, the expression on the Prince’s face, and his inability to understand Fey, he realizes what has happened. 
“Orsik?” 
“Yes.”
“We can use this.”
“I hope so!” 
And with that, he arrives at the easternmost tree. There, another dryad sits in the tree and introduces himself as Helia. He is delighted to see Thornacious and let’s loose a stream of insults, hoping to provoke the sprite. But Orsik-Thornacious understands nothing and misinterprets some of the gestures. After a time, Helia gives up. 
He then explains how he got here. He was Halisa’s plus-one at the ball. When Halisa expressed herself freely, Queen Titania demanded that the plus one present itself. 
“I tried to explain that I wasn’t her date or interested in Halisa, and I even dumped her on the spot. But Queen Titania didn’t care. And now I am stuck here for an eternity!” He pauses, “is that long?” 
Thistle makes the same request, and he is once again rebuffed. But Helia offers an option, “I would give you permission if you could convince Halisa to apologize!” 
Orsik, Thistle, and Vardis discuss their options. Vardis points out they can bend the fates using Unla Kee’s powers. Orsik proposes violence. Thistle is unsure and suggests they check the other dryads in the trees. 
The tree on the northern cardinal point is an old dryad with a grumpy, breathy voice. Thistle introduces himself and has been searching for his Uncle Joon.
“Joon!” 
“How much does he owe you,” sighs Thistle. 
“He owes me a joke. A good joke! I want to hear a good joke.” 
Thistle ponders the jokes he heard throughout his travels and remembers an old joke he used to share with his beloved. The joke only works if you are a fey, and it’s about a Dryad and how the charm interacts with the movement of a tree. Thistle lands the joke nicely. And the Dryad laughs heartily. 
Seeing him laugh, Thistle asks for permission to complete his quest. And this elderly dryad turns him down, “unless you get Helia and Halisa to stop arguing. I am tired of hearing their bickering.” 
“How did she trap them here?” 
The Dryad ponders the question and mutters, “I am not sure. Queen Titania told them that if one left, the other would be safe, and the one leaving would die. They hate each other so much that they can’t find the love to sacrifice themselves. So they sit there bickering.” The dryad slouches and puts his head in his hands, “and thus we are canceling the moon festival.” 
Thistle is shocked; it’s performative, but shocked nonetheless. 
The elderly creature mutters, “Get them to stop bickering or to leave, and I will give you permission.” 
Thistle flitters off to the last tree on the western cardinal point. 
After the introductions, Thistle learns that her name is Sleth and that she is upset that Helia and Halisa are not caring for their trees. She tries to point out what is wrong, but Thistle only sees a perfect tree. 
Sleth rolls her eyes, “that tree branch is growing in the wrong direction. Why, in the next 30 years, it will create a terrible imbalance. And this is exactly what happens when Queen Titania sends whatever dregs she can muster.” 
Thistle is surprised, “what? You aren’t trapped here?” 
Sleth laughs, “Oh no, this is a positively amazing assignment. Or it was. This is the greatest tree in the Universe, and I get to tend to it.” 

Thistle hits his head with his forehead, and the Orsik-Thornacious stares at him, “Of course! Queen Titania would pull this kind of stuff all of the time. She’s told them a lie, and neither loves the other enough to sacrifice himself. So they sit there stubbornly for all time.” 
Orsik-Thornacious looks at him – “So now what.”
“So now we say that Prince Thornacious came to this tree to be cured of his muteness and to tell both of these love dryads that they can leave!” 
The dwarf in the sprite’s body signals to his friend to create the right effect as he touches the tree. Then Orsik-Thornacious shouts, “Blessed be Queen Titania, I can speak.” And then he mumbles, “but only in COMMON!” 
He then flies over to Halisa. Her reaction is surprising – “Helia is going to be stuck here, right? That knot dumped me just as Queen Titania was about to send me here. We could have been happily in love instead of rotting away.” 
Thistle’s eyes roll. 
She declares emphatically, “I plan to come here every 100 years and taunt Helia!” 
When Halisa is sufficiently far away, Orsik-Thornacious and Thistle fly to Helia. The dryad is upset that Halisa is leaving, which is odd given how much he insists he hates her. Orsik-Thornacious explains that this is a pardon and that he, too, can go. 
Helia stares at him momentarily and then flies before the Prince chases his mind. 
The old Dryad and Sleth appear next to the 3HPP when both dryads are gone. 
“FINALLY LEFT!” groans the elderly Dryad. 
“Yes, and as I kept telling them, there was no curse. They had cursed each other! Imagine a hundred years of aggravation because you believe it’s a prison. What’s worse is our aggravation, but now – finally – peace.” 
Thistle turns to Orsik-Thornacious and suggests they bid everyone farewell.
With the permission to search given, the 3HPP quickly finds the artifact hidden in a chamber in the tree's roots. It is a nonmagical electrum torc with Giant runes, formerly the nose-ring of a mighty hill giant chief. 
 As they leave, they notice a group of centaurs approach. The centaurs are rank and are muttering about a cursed temple deeper in the forest. It’s unclear where this cursed temple is other than somewhere in the woods. The centaurs, they learn, are here for the magical powers of the tree to cure them of the disease they acquired. Spending the night here has the same effects as the Greater Restoration spell. 
Just before they get to the airship, they sneak up on a group of barbarians of the Tree Ghost Tribe, led by a young man who they understand to be the son of the Great Chief Boorvald Orcbane, talking to Sleth. The leader is furious when he overhears that the precious artifact of his tribe has been stolen, promising vengeance. 
As they sneak away, Sleth says, “Prince Thornacious was so gracious and helpful!”

# Chapter 51 - Into the Feywild
Somewhere deep in Orsik’s brain, a part of him was contemplating the nature of intelligence. Intelligence was the process of inventing new ideas from observation. Wisdom is measuring the validity of those ideas against the world’s reality.
Orsik had suddenly become supremely intelligent; was his intelligence overwhelming his wisdom? His life experiences had not prepared him for the new ideas he had. Was he like a child who could see things without contextualizing them correctly? Would he become a force for good? For evil? Or for Orsik?
Was his desire to age an act of wisdom or foolishness? A brilliant insight or an impetuous action of a child?
But that is a thought for another time. 
Right now, Orsik was under a large tree, attacked by ghosts, and needed to focus. 
“It’s gone,” shouts Thistle. 
Orisk rests for a moment, and then his warrior training kicks in. He scans his surroundings and sees a human-sized shape barely distinguishable from the background. 
“Ghost!” he shouts. 
Vardis says the words of power, and a raw blast of coruscating divine energy flies from his outstretched hand, but the ghost slips away into the two arrows from Thistle’s bow.
The ghost glares and Orsik burns him with a firebolt, but it is still not dead. 
Vardis shouts, “FIREBALL!” 
Unla Kee prepares the Bat Guano and then has a change of heart and strikes with a mind sliver, shattering whatever mind was there. 
“Two more to go!”
At this point, Orsik’s inner voice reminds him that if they had paid the guide on the ship 50 gold, they might have avoided all of this. 
And Orisk says out loud, “We didn’t get rich by writing a lot of checks!” 
And the inside voice responds, “You got rich by spending money irresponsibly!” 
Orsik shouts, “It was a joke.” 
Vardis, seeing his friend troubled, “is this about the 50 gold?” 
“Yes,” grumbles Orsik. 
“Well, it wasn’t real estate.” 
Vardis suddenly runs from underneath the giant tree and shoots at something in the sky, “Missed!” 
Thistle’s bow strikes true, and he mutters, “Amateurs.” 
Orsik is annoyed at Thistle, so to show him who the boss is, he throws a hand ax at the ghost and barely scratches it. 
The ghost approaches the 3HPP, and the pleasant face of the dead Uthgartdian chieftain is replaced by the horror of the undead, scaring everyone and unnaturally aging Thistle. 
Orsik is annoyed, “damn it! Why didn’t I get older.” 
This time, Orsik is pleased to see Vardis’ arrow hit the creature, and as it dissipates, he bangs his sword against his shield and shouts, “Okay, one more to go, guys!” 
The air shimmers next to Unla Kee as the final ghost materializes next to him. 
Orsik observes the spell Vardis cast has no effect and mutters, “Bummer.” 
The ghost attempts to possess Unla Kee, but the diviner twists the fabric of time and space, and the spirit cannot overwhelm the mage’s mental defenses. 
Orsik advances on the ghost, whose peculiar existence shows the damage of Thistle’s two arrows, 
A few hours later, with the skull of some Giant that radiates necromancy in their bag of holding and covered in dirt from digging, the 3HPP approach their airship. 
Thistle
The Fairy was worried. Titania, the Goddess of the Fey and ruler of the Fey court, had punished someone for insulting her in that awful way the Fey goddess punished. And she wasn’t usually the forgiving type. 
“Forgiving? Hah! Vengeful, spiteful, cruel, and heartless.” 
And just then, the sky in front of him sparkled, and a sprite with a high-pitched squeaky voice, wearing pink pants and an orange camis, appeared and said in common.
“I appear here at the request of the Seely Court! It has come to our attention that you have interfered in the ongoing punishment that Queen Titania, in all of her righteousness, imposed on Helia and Halisa. She commends your subterfuge and subtlety in freeing the two Dryads and is pleased that it was to advance a personal and selfish goal.” 
Just then, Orsik-Thornacious appears and smiles, waving his hand. 
Thistle is worried, and his worries are justified when the Sprite says in fey – “Just because those ignorant Dryads fell for that trick, doesn’t mean I will. But Titania was pleased with the use of trickery. And she is also pleased that she can use Thornacious’ involvement in this sordid affair as another reason to keep him out of the Feywild.” 
The Sprite then returns to common, “Unfortunately, the Queen’s wroth cannot ignore the insult of you revering one of her decisions, and she has decided to give you a small little task. A Formorian of the Unseelie Court has taken refuge in the upper lands. You are to investigate and deal with the problem.” 
Orsik-Thornacious whispers to Thistle, “We can kill that thing?” 
Thistle turns pale white, “If this is all we have to do, we got off light.” 
The sprite squeaks in delight, “Wonderful! There were, of course, others arguing for more drastic punishments. In her infinite wisdom, the Queen appreciated how you dealt with your Uncle Joon.” 
Thistle can see the challenge between Orsik’s intelligence and his wisdom. The intelligence tells him that Orsik could probably kill the Sprite without anyone noticing and then deny the crime. And he’s about to do it when Thistle intervenes, “Do you really think that the Seelie Court won’t know who killed their emissary?” 
The Sprite then waves its hand, and a portal emerges – “In you go!” 
“I thought it was near this forest.” 
“Yes, it is near this forest in the Fey.” 
Orsik-Thornacious is pissed off and about to strike. 
Thistle tries calming him, “This is normal, totally normal!” But Thistle’s voice betrays his worry, yet Thistle somehow persuades Orsik-Thornacious to come through the portal. 
The ranger squirms when he hears Orsik-Thornacious mutter, “Prince Thronacious is delighted to come through a scary portal on behalf of the Queen. It’s not like anything bad happens when you go through a portal.” 
A disorienting moment later, Thistle is in the Feywild. And the colors finally make sense to him, bursts of bright oranges, yellows, purples, and greens flood his vision. As the colors continue to bloom, he begins to see kaleidoscopic patterns. The patterns spin in slower and slower circles until finally floating further from him and settling into the environment. The oranges and yellows press into leaves and flowers, the purples nestle into the sky, and the greens vein trees and the grass beneath your feet.
“Home,” he thinks.

Just then, an elven hunter appears, smiling and announces himself as Drauchanar, or Chan or the Knife. He quickly explains that he’s no ordinary hunter but hunts rare game like the Formorian! 
Just as Thistle accepts the offer, he realizes Orsik still looks like Thornacious. 
“The Prince will come? But if the Prince comes, I will not be permitted to keep the prize!” 
Thistle has a brain wave and says, in common, “Perhaps the Prince should disguise himself as something that nobody would suspect, perhaps a dwarf?” 
Drauchanar, in a sing-songy high-pitched voice, squeals – “Wonderful and how gracious! This way, the Prince can guide us in the hunt, the Knife can keep the head, and all the forms of society and culture can be preserved!” 
And with that, he shouts, “Follow me! A hunting Fomorians we shall go!” 
Thistle follows the Knife reluctantly but assumes this guide must have some idea where he is going. And yet, as they walk, he can not help but feel that something is watching him. 
As the sun sets, Drauchanar declares, “We shall rest here! And I shall sing a song!” 
“That is not necessary,” pleads Thistle.
“Oh, but it is!”
Drauchanar sings an old song, and Thistle notices how it enthralls Vardis. It is an elvish song that speaks to the elven soul in ways that surprisingly penetrate even the nearly impervious shell of cynicism Vardis has built up over the years. It’s about the love of Corellon and Araushnee before the sundering. 
Orsik wakes everyone up with a shout, “Where is he?” Thistle is immediately awake, bow in hand, “who?” 
“Your friend led us into the forest and abandoned us.” 
Thistle quickly scans the area and patiently explains that The Knife was ambushed and dragged off by some kind of giant-like creature and a four-legged beast. 
Orsik is annoyed and wants to abandon the quest, but Thistle, empowered by the strange magic found in the Feywild and his belief that pissing off Titania will not be a good thing, convinces him to continue. 
And so the 3HPP follows the trail and arrives at a cave opening 20 feet in diameter set into a cave wall that rises out of the forest. Thistle notices that a cyclops is standing guard. 

Orsik mutters, “Just when I finally need Harshnag.” 
And then Thistle points to a waterfall. Orsik mutters, “Yes, a waterfall. There is always treasure behind a waterfall.” 
A few minutes later, the 3HPP is wet and standing in a tunnel behind.
“I told you!” gloats Orsik. 
But there is no treasure. And so Orsik tries hard to disbelieve the walls. “There’s always a treasure chest behind the waterfall!” 

As he walks around the tunnel, a goblin dressed in studded armor pokes their head and asks, “What do you want?”
When they are informed that the 3HPP want to kill a Formorian, they squeal in delight and a few moments later return with a much larger goblin that speaks with a very nasal, squeaky voice and says, “Goblin #1, yes, we use numbers, said that you were looking for a Formorian?” 
Orsik growls, “It’s pronounced Treasure Chest.” 
“A Fomorian or a treasure chest?” they ask, confused. 
Thistle assures them that it’s a Fomorian. 
“My name is Tlega.” 
“Hello Tlega, I am Thistle.”  
The goblin is touched by Thistle’s flawless pronunciation. It explains that the typical Bully/Bullied relationship with the Fomorian Maegurn has fallen apart, as Maegurn has become abusive, not just a bully. 
“It’s time for him to go.” Tlega encourages the 3HPP to follow him into the caves. Orsik, being suspicious, cautions the Fairy who says, “I looked into their eyes, and I saw their soul. They are not lying.” 
Orsik nudges Thistle, “Then let’s get them to help us.”
Tlega is delighted with the proposal and urges that all of the Goblins assemble to attack. And with that, Mirk, another Goblin boss, appears. Mirk is obviously unhappy at Tlega’s decision and explains that the Bullied/Bully relationship is the core of Goblin society and that the line is just an imaginary line. The two begin to argue, and Tlega and the other goblins become deflated. 
Orsik is about to try his luck and intimidate Mirk when Unla Kee says, “I got this spell, Jim’s Glowing Coin.” Orsik smiles, “Yes, that’s awesome. That will work great.” 
Mirk and Tlega, distracted, quickly agree that dispatching Maergurn is the right next thing to do to advance the needs of this tribe.

# Chapter 52 - Banishment
The 3HPP, with the goblins, led by Tlega and Mirk, enter Maergun’s throne room. The throne room is newly finished with smooth walls, a massive throne, Drauchanar hanging from a chain, and a cyclops, displacer beast, and the Fomorian. 
Thistle stares at the Fomorian and tries to remember what he knows. He sighs. It’s from the official textbook of the Seelie kingdom, which is a tad bit biased. He recalled that the book said, “*They are the most hideous and wicked of all giantkind are the godless Fomorians, whose deformed bodies reflect their vile demeanors. Some have facial features randomly distributed around their misshapen, warty heads. Others have limbs of grossly different sizes and shapes, or emit terrible howls each time they draw breath through misshapen mouths. However, their wretched appearance rarely evokes sympathy, for the Fomorians brought their doom upon themselves with the evil that rules their hearts and minds.”* 

Thistle looks at Maergun and thinks, “Definitely got the ugly, right.” And then he remembers the official histories of the elves that were mandatory reading, “*The elves remember when the Fomorians were among the most handsome of races, possessed of brilliant minds and unrivaled magical ability. However, that physical perfection did not extend to their hearts as a lust for magic and power consumed them.*
*The Fomorians sought to conquer the Feywild and enslave its inhabitants, claiming those creatures’ magic for themselves. When the fey united to defend their realm, the Fomorians fought them and were subjected to a terrible curse.*
*One by one, the giants fell as their bodies were warped to reflect the evil in their hearts. Stripped of their grace and magical power, the wretched horrors fled from the light, delving deep beneath the world to nurse their hatred. Cursing their fate, they have ever after plotted vengeance against the fey that wronged them.”*
Thuul rushes to the Cyclops and bites. Thistle follows up the splash of acid with a pair of arrows that wound the one-eyed monster. 
The Cyclops howls in pain from the acid and smashes Thuul with his club. Thuul lives. 
Tlega, who is in the corridor leading to the chamber with the Fomorian, shouts to his troops, “Move ahead. I’m right behind you.” 
Orsik glares and then sighs when he realizes that that is all Tlega plans to do. 
Unla Kee steps to a vantage point where he can cast a Fireball that burns the giant, but the displacer beast ducks out at the last minute. 
The four-legged creature strikes at Thuul, killing him, and wounds Thistle with his tentacle. 
Orsik squeezes between the Cyclops and the Displacer beast and strikes the tentacled creature with his infused sword that projects thunder damage that hits both creatures. Seeing they are alive, he digs deep and attacks them again.
A goblin fires his short bow at the Displacer Beast and misses. 
Vardis, the cleric of light, grabs his spell focus and casts Fireball. A red sparkle flies from his hand, and some 50’ away, a fiery blast roars, obscuring the three monsters. Vardis looks at what happened: the Displacer Beast is dead, and the Cyclops and the Fomorian were too slow to avoid getting hurt. His god would be pleased. 
After that, the goblins fire their bows at the giant. Two of the arrows hit, and the rest miss. The goblins, however, are delighted with the outcome. 
Mirk shouts – “Well done!” 
Mirk’s decision to do nothing but heap praise is met with dissension amongst the ranks. Some goblins feel that Mirk isn’t engaged in the mission and is slacking. The debate is about to become disruptive, so Mirk throws a tantrum at them, grabs his javelin, and hits the giant. 
“Satisfied!” 
The goblins all cheer. 
The Fomorian shouts, “You killed my puppy!” 
Unla Kee shouts back – “Fluffy!” 
The giant roars – “No!” 
“Spiky!” 
“Haerbega!” 
Orsik interjects, “That’s a bad name. You really need a new one!” 
The Fomorian shouts, “Who do you think you are?”
“The group that will kill you?”
The Fomorian takes a hint and tries an evil eye on Vardis, who avoids the worst of the effects but not of the club. 
Thistle is annoyed that Thuul is dead – “More like dematerialized,” he reminds himself and shoots two arrows at the Cyclops, that falls down dead. 
Tlega shouts – “ATTACK!” 
Orsik turns to him – “Don’t you fight?” 
“We lead!” 
Orsik rolls his eyes and mutters, “From the rear!” 
With the help of a cantrip, an arrow from a goblin, and a devastating set of attacks by Orsik using the Giant Sword of Lord Zymorven, the giant *almost* dies. 
After a goblin arrow misses, Vardis uses a cantrip and kills the creature. 
With the giant dead, the goblins break into a peculiar dance, waving their hands in the air and shouting, “WE ARE THE CHAMPIONS! NO TIME FOR LOSERS! FOR WE ARE THE CHAMPIONS OF THIS CAVE!” 
Vardis frees Drauchanar, who declares – “I am free, exhausted, tired, and free! And thanks to my spectacular leadership, the giant is dead! I let myself be captured so you could find follow me here.” 
He pauses, “You came through the waterfall! Brilliant! I knew Vardis would see through that illusion.” 
Vardis smiles, knowing that Orsik is silent to maintain the illusion that he is Prince Thornacious, and says, “Thornacious is always trying to steal the credit!” 
Orsik ignores the comment, searches the body, and finds an iron key. He uses the key on a keyhole on the throne and discovers a staircase to the treasure vault. The vault has plain, smooth walls, and the room and its contents are free of mining dust or debris. 
The treasure is pleasing to Orsik and consists of a box of turquoise figurines, a brass crown, a carved ivory statuette, and a large tapestry depicting a scene of elves feasting. There is also a mass of gold, platinum, copper, and silver coins and a potion of greater healing and water breathing. 
Thistle notices a secret door to the Southeast, and the group decides that he should investigate. There is a room that appears to have been discovered thanks to some excavation. It is decorated with delicate bas-reliefs of elven nature designs. Thistle identifies the space to be a tomb. He approaches a raised platform upon which lies what appears to be a white stone statue of a sleeping elven woman. He notices two duergars who seem to be cleaning the tomb reverentially. 
Then, a peculiar mind reaches out, and he decides to kiss the statue. 
Vardis notices and shouts, “Thistle, what are you doing!” 
Thistle turns to him and says, “This looks just like my girlfriend.” 
“no touching rule! Remember we discussed with Unla Kee that there would be no touching!” 
“But kissing,” and with that, kisses the statue. 
The statue shimmers with a bright light, and a female elf is where the figure was. The elf looks disoriented. Vardis knows immediately what this is, “A vampire!” 
Thistle shouts to Thuul, “Come let me introduce you to my new friend, Aeglossil.” 
Aeglossil is tired and cranky and reaches for Orsik, whom she grapples extends her fangs, and bites, feeding for the first time in eons. The pallor of her body seems to recede a little. 
Vardis presents the symbol of Corellon, and Aeglossil snarls in fear at the power of The Protector. 
Unla Kee casts banishment, space, and light warp around Aeglossil as if reality has opened a sinkhole.  Then there’s a great bang, like a door being slammed shut, and a ripple seems to go out through the world before light, space, and time return to normal.
Orsik turns to him, “How?” 
“I can twist time and space.” 

The vampire is banished into another realm. With the Vampire no longer there, Thistle runs. Or instead, flies away. Without the Vampire to control him, the sight of so many Derro and his injured state pushes him to leave. 

Unla Kee wants to stand and fight. But Vardis convinces him of the folly of that plan. Orsik and Vardis follow him quickly. The Derro in the caves remain waiting for their Vampire mistress to return. 

The sunlight of the Feywild bathes our heroes. Thistle shouts - “Here’s The Knife.” 

Orsik can’t resist and replies, “That’s no knife.” 

The Knife is confused - “I am not a knife, I am THE Knife. It’s a nom-de-guerre.” 

“So you will lead us to the portal?” 

Thistle interjects with a slight tone of irritation, “I can do that. And whatever happened to that nice lady we met?” 

Orsik mutters, “Tomorrow, Thistle, ask me tomorrow.” 

Thistle starts to insist, “And why did Vardis send her away.” 

At this point, The Knife shouts him down, “How dare you speak so to Prince Thornacious! He gave you an answer. You should accept it.” 

The Knife is still under the illusion that Orsik is Prince Thornacious and is trying desperately to suck up to him and says, “You are so gracious, Lord, to speak so kindly to -- him.” 

The dwarf keeps a straight face and mutters, “Yes, it is true. I am very patient.” 

The following day, Thistle finally understands what happened. The lovely lady was a vampire, and he had been charmed. Thistle acknowledges the events. Orsik is disappointed that Thistle neither grovels nor demonstrates that he respects him more. 

Before they can argue, the colorful sprite reappears - “The Formorian is dead, and Queen Titania is pleased! She would like to give you a gift!” 

Thistle tries to signal to Orsik that accepting any gift from Queen Titania always comes with strings attached. But Orsik’s greed gets in the way, and he declares they are happy to take whatever gift she has in mind, but they would like to know what it is. 

The sprite is pleased - “It is a Blessed Horn! A magical item of raw and incredible powers.”

Thistle hears powers, and caring not one whit about deals with powerful, vindictive, godly powers that rule alternative planes of existence, takes it. 

With that, the portal is open, and the heroes go through. 

1st day of the 2nd tenday of Eleasis

On the other side, Delsephine is waiting. For her, a few moments have passed. 

She stares at the aged Orsik and asks, “Who is this?” 

Orsik answers her, “I am Orsik’s father.” 

She looks at him, confused.

“I am Orsic, with a c. You didn’t see this, but I met my son by the tree and gave him a nice whupping. He’s disgraced my name and reputation, and I have some cleanup to do.” 

She looks at him intently and smiles beneath her mask.

Orsik notices her smile but knows she will keep this secret because she understands a thing or two about second chances. 

Once on the ship, Vardis announces their next destination, Beorunna’s Well. 

Once again, the sage from Candlekeep negotiates a modest fee of 50 gold from the 3HPP, who offer 500 silver. 

The money has exchanged hands; he tells the tale of this place. 

“The spirit mound of the Black Lion and Red Tiger tribes of Uthgardt rests in the heart of the Druarwood. It’s not a mound per se but an ice-cold cavern whose ceiling is partially open to the sky.

According to legend, Beorunna, an Uthgardt hero, died fighting a demon in these woods. In the final moments of the battle, the ground beneath Beorunna’s feet crumbled, plunging him into a cavern. The triangular cavern contains, among other things, a heated pool with a steady geyser. This pool came to be known as Beorunna’s Well. Uthgardt, exploring the cavern for the first time, found what they thought were Beorunna’s bones, which actually belonged to a half-ogre.

Uthgar’s followers buried the half-ogre’s bones alongside a relic stolen from their enemies, the giants, and built an altar upon the burial site. When the Uthgardt call upon Uthgar for guidance or seek to appease their totem spirits, they slaughter a woodland beast and place its carcass atop the altar, where it slowly rots. Today, the tribes maintain a tenuous truce and take turns protecting their spirit mound. Each tribe has erected its own totem pole near the altar. The Black Lion totem pole is covered with dried pitch and has a lion’s skull atop it. The Red Tiger totem pole is stained with blood and has a tiger’s skull surmounting it.

A small stand of pine trees called the Sunken Grove grows in the middle of the cavern. Outside the perimeter of the grove, the cavern is hooded by a ceiling lined with stalactites and pierced with tree roots. The floor around the outside of the Sunken Grove beneath the ceiling is dotted with stalagmites.

The distance from the top of the pit to the cavern floor is 250 feet. The Uthgardt have tied a long, knotted rope to a tree stump and left it dangling in the cavern. The barbarians use this rope (and others like it) to climb into and out of Beorunna’s Well. 

I have yet to see these totems, but they do correspond to what one would expect from the literature.

The relic, I suspect, is beneath the 9 feet wide, 6 feet long, and 3 feet deep multi-stone blood-splattered altar.” 

Orsik turns to Vardis, “I don’t think they will mind if we dig under it.” 

The sage mutters, “I disagree. But I am so confident in your success that I have placed a modest wager on you returning with the artifact.” 

Despite gentle and not-so-gentle nudging, the sage is unwilling to increase his wager. 

## 2nd day of the 2nd tenday of Eleasis



The 3HPP arrived early in the morning near the cave. The cave forms a large triangular shape, and although the sage did a good job describing the place, its size still impresses everyone but Orsik, who has seen much more impressive dwarven constructions. 

The rope is as advertised. But before they head down, Thistle proposes to do some scouting. 

The fairy ranger discovers tents on a ledge to the east. The tents belong to Uthgartdian barbarians. To the west, he sees a manticore. To the south is the advertised altar. 

The 3HPP confer and agree to go down quietly. Unfortunately for them, the shaman hears them come down and rouses the Barbarians. 

The roused barbarians wake up the Manticore. The creature sees Unla Kee's 12’ wings and flies as fast as he can towards them. 

Orsik shouts, “I have a plan. Let’s run to the altar. We can use that as a defensive position.” 

The 3HPP is uncertain but agrees, except for Thistle, who will take a defensive rear guard position. 

As he hangs back, Vardis shouts, “NEVER SPLIT THE PARTY!” 
The group frantically runs towards the altar. Thistle stays back, hoping to defend against the Manticore. The Barbarians run after the Orsik group except for the Shaman, who heads towards the circle. 

## The battle in the cavern 
### 2nd day of the 2nd tenday of Eleasis 

The 3HPP rush to the wall and the altar. They are trying to get to it before the locals who are trying to defend their sacred lands from pillagers get to them. 

Vardis huffs and puffs as he runs and mutters, “Not so sure running to get cornered is the best plan we’ve had.” 

Orsik, still getting used to his 50 years of aging, mutters, “We do our best work with our backs against the wall.” 

Unla Kee is unsure, but he knows this is not where they will die. Is it bravado? Is it bravery in the face of death? 

“I know because I know,” he says to fate.

And while Vardis and Orsik dash to take cover behind the Altar, Unla Kee turns towards danger. 

Vardis shouts to Orsik, “Which way should we go?” 

The aged Orsik shouts, “The easy way?”

Vardis, who is huffing, asks, “And that is?” 

Orsik points at a path that leads up to the Altar. 

“Right.” 

Unla Kee is in range of the Shaman and casts “Enemies Abound.” The Uthgardtian can no longer tell friends from foe. 

The berserk barbarians, seeing their shaman go mad, run toward Vardis and Unla Kee.

The Manticore is hungry, cranky, and pissed off. And it has a job to kill flying creatures entering his cave. The tiny fairy is barely noticeable, but it struck him, so it was time to do the favor. With a shriek, it lets loose a volley of its spikes. And it howls in anger as they miss. 

Thistle sees the bolts swing past. “That is dangerous; time to kill it.” He fires arrows that strike the creature but don’t stop its murderous intent. He thinks, “Tools are better than natural weapons.” 

The shaman sees his tribesman approach him and, under the spell's influence, strikes at them. 

His clan members think it means redoubling their efforts to attack the 3HPP, which is running towards the altar and running faster. 

The Manticore is injured and goes to hide behind some stalactites. It ponders its next move. 

Thistle sees the creature fly away, sighs, and kills two of the on-rushing locals from a great height. 

Vardis hears the groans of the dying and mutters, “One fairy killing machine when it remembers it can shoot more than once.” With one last effort, he makes it behind the Altar. 

Orsik arrives right behind him. “That sage’s information better be accurate.” He picks up a shovel and starts digging. “Vardis, don’t you have some magical digging spell or spell to make the shovel bigger?”

The cleric, still huffing, says between heavy breaths, “No.” 

The righteous protectors of the tribal artifacts are confused. If they charge towards the Altar, the fairy will kill them. If they move toward the shaman, they will die. So they decide that the thieves will use the rope to escape and that the simplest solution is to hide behind rocks near the rope. 

The Manticore peeks from his hiding hole and unleashes another volley of spikes. This time, they are more effective. And he smiles. But it's not too effective because Thistle brushes them off. 

The fairy looks at the ground and decides that the Manticore is too well hidden, but these barbarians are not. He kills one more. From his vantage point, all he can see is the blood on the ground and its prone body. 

One of the barbarians wonders if the Shaman is under some kind of controlling spell and so punches him in the face. The Shaman is knocked prone and then stands up and hugs his assaultor. He turns to the group and shouts, “Protect the relics!” 

Vardis shouts to Orsik, “We’ll have to do it the hard way.” 

Orsik, whose body is drenched in sweat, shouts, “I know at the place.” 

Vardis nods, “After the thing.” 

Vardis notes, “Hey, they stopped chasing us.”

The dwarf peeks over the Altar, “The Barbarians can’t get past Thistle and are hiding behind rocks near the entrance. The Fairy is brilliant. It’s my impact on him.” 
Vardis rolls his eyes, “So we can keep digging and not exhaust ourselves?” 

Then Orsik taps the rock, “Given that we don’t need the altar for protection, how about we just smash it?” 

Vardis casts a spiritual weapon, “I love the way you think.” The sound of rock being hit echoes through the cavern. 

The Shaman understands that his ally, the Manticore, failed him. The creature will not be enough to provide air cover. He shouts at his people, and they respond. They take out their short bows and fire. A shortbow is a simpler weapon than a longbow but lacks range. And Thistle is just inside of their long range. The arrows miss. 

Unla Kee sees that the brawny types have taken to smashing the altar, so he turns toward the cowering creatures. He is a funny sight to behold: 3 feet tall, 1 foot wide, with twelve-foot wings spreading out from his back. He ponders his next move and moves in closer.

The Manticore sees the move, moves in closer, and aims a volley at the abomination. A creature with wings that size is an abomination. 

Thistle orders Thuul to get between Unla Kee and the Manticore. Unla Kee is annoyed; he can heal now and doesn’t need the protection. 

The shaman stops trying to cast spells and instead engages in a ritual spell. 

Vardis needs to be closer to figure it out. Thistle is not from this plane. And Unla Kee has a very dismissive view of Spiritual Magic. 

Thistle is finally in range.
