The red seed of light zips through the air faster than the Manticore can blink. He sees its trace against your eyelids as he shuts them for the last time, pointlessly, against the fiery blast that roars. 

The Shaman, who is nearby, opens his eyes and can still see traces of the crimson bead’s path toward its deadly end.

Orsik laughs. Vardis’ spiritual weapon misses. 

Vardis laughs too, “I can’t hit the - literal - side of a barn.” 

But no matter, Orsik’s blades smash the rock. And the altar cracks into many large chunks of rock that reveal the ground beneath it. 

“We have the artifact.” 

Orsik starts the run back to the rope. Vardis follows him from a distance because his friend uses “Expeditious Retreat.” 

Thistle looks around for a target. The Shaman appears dead. So, he aims at one of the shortbow-wielding antagonists. He misses. More precisely, his arrow slips in his fingers. 

A barbarian sees the arrow fall from the sky and laughs. His shaman is displeased and commands him to take the fight seriously. The barbarian, worried about dying in this life in a way that displeases the gods, scowls, stares at his enemies, and tries to get more cover. 

Thistle kills him.

Unla Kee casts yet another fireball and is annoyed that the effect is diluted by the stones' cover. 

2nd day of the 2nd tenday of Eleasis

Vardis runs towards the rope that will lead outside of Beorunna’s Cave. He pulls his bow mid-run and shoots, expecting the arrow to miss widely, but it hits. 

Unla Kee smirks, “I can do better than that.” 

The Shaman stares in disbelief as the fireball explodes in fiery red anger and hate. When the fire recedes, he sees the charred remains of his friends and the people he was supposed to protect. 

Only one of the protectors is left.

Orsik runs and shoots with his longbow. The arrow misses the target and bounces harmlessly away. In frustration, the dwarven fighter takes the bow and smashes it against the ground. He screams, “I hate this bow.” 

Vardis mutters, “You could have taken lessons from Thistle, but you were too proud.” 

The dwarf is too angry to have noticed. 

And while Vardis and Thistle are angry, the Shaman reaches for the Necrotic magic of his ancestors and raises the dead fighters as a horde of zombies to fight the intruders. 

Thistle shouts, “Do we target the Shaman?” 

Vardis shouts back, “No. These monsters exist even if the Shaman who cast them dies.” 

“Good,” mutters the halfling mage, and another Fireball explodes, incinerating the undead's bodies. 

Thistle flies closer to the Shaman and strikes him with a glancing blow. But the blow must have been far worse than Thistle imagines, and the Shaman tumbles and falls, the blood pools around his body. 

Vardis looks around for some other monster that might have killed him. “Do you see anything? Is there a dragon nearby?” 

Thistle is annoyed that his marksmanship is questioned and shouts, “He’s dead.” 

“Just checking!” 

Unla Kee stares at the last remaining living Barbarian, prepares another fireball, and realizes it might be worth saving for another moment. Instead, magical darts appear next to him and immediately strike his enemy—the barbarian winces from the pain. 

The shaman is dead, and his friends are all dead, thinks the last defender of the relics. He will not die on his knees, so he stands up to charge. But before he can let loose a blood-curdling roar or curse, two arrows pierce his body, the first through his lungs and the second through his neck, and all that is left is the sound of a defiant gurgle. 

Thistle lands on the ground. Everyone is exhausted, bloodied, and tired, but at least they are still alive. 

“Goodberries, anyone?” 

Thistle feels good about himself and decides to go and explore. To the North West, there is an encampment of the two tribes that shared this artifact: the Red Lion and Black Tiger tribes. The barbarians don’t have much treasure on them. And their weapons and armor aren’t that valuable. And so, after looking over things, he flies off to the manticore’s lair. And there, too, besides the odd bone, he finds nothing of value. 

While he’s flying, Orsik and Unla Kee have decided that there is an excellent opportunity to concoct a plan to blame the Red Lion Tribe for the attack. 

Unla Kee was initially reluctant, but when Orsik whispered the magic word, “Chaos,” he became animated and excited and suggested various refinements to the plan. 

By the time the fairy arrives, Unla Kee, Orsik, and Vardis have staged the battle to make it look like the Red Lion Tiger tribe attacked the Black Tiger Tribe. The staging is so well done that Thistle is at first confused by what he sees. But then he smiles.

“Shall we go up?” 

Orsik grunts in agreement and begins to climb, and then rocks start to fly out from the ground and hit him. 

Vardis shouts, “The damn ritual.” 

The Shaman had cast a spell that protected the area around the rope. The thistle tried to fly up but was attacked by rocks. 

“I guess we’ll have to fly them up.” 

Artifact in tow, the 3HPP stands at the lip of the Beorunna’s Cave. They stare down and think, “Well, that was a job well done. The two tribes will be too distracted to realize who the real culprit is.” 

A few hours later, the Shaman wakes up. He had faked death and is now awake. He looks around and stares up into the sky. Tears well up in his eyes. He realizes that if he does nothing, the tribes will engage in a brutal civil war. He stops and thinks that it is better to die killing the thieves who pillaged our sacred relics than dying in another pointless tribal conflict. 

 Delsephine welcomes them back on her ship. Orsik suggests that she might want to join them next time, but her definitive use of the word no cuts him off. 

Vardis pulls out Harshnag’s map and ponders their next move. 

“Let’s go check out the Wyrm Cavern?” 

“It's been a while since we attacked dragons,” mutters Orsik.

Vardis reconsiders, “How about the One Stone? It’s closer.” 

Bressik smiles, “That happens to be in the territory of the Sky Ponies. The location is in a geological feature resembling a Sky Pony from above.” 

“I want to go see the Sky Ponies.” 

Orsik cringes as he remembers Vardis’ collection of ‘Sky Ponies.’ Never mind his obsession with that puppet show group and the endless collection of slightly different Sky Ponies that Vardis had to own. And then there were the pamphlets. Vardis kept telling him this is pretty sophisticated fun, and that’s why Orsik didn’t appreciate it. Now that Orsik is super intelligent, he realizes his friend has some weird taste in art. 

 Vardis excitedly asks, “Thistle, you know anything about them?” 

Thistle mutters that the Sky Pony tribe and his Uncle Joon disagreed over some matter of honor, and he said, “Uncle Joon says that they referred to him in a way that was beyond the pale for a member of Queen Titania’s court.” 

“He was kicked out of the Court for being a drunk. Not sure what was beneath him,” mutters Thistle. 

Bressik, the sage of Candlekeep now reduced to working on an airship for a dragon, approaches, “Will you need my services?” 

“Yes, here’s five platinum.” 

“Is it possible to standardize on a single coinage?” 

“What?” 

“The spirit mound of the Sky Pony tribe lies in a grove of oak trees surrounded by the tall pines of the Moonwood, west of the Redrun River. The Sky Ponies’ spirit mound is a two-tiered plateau. On the lower tier, cairns cover the buried remains of the tribe’s honored dead. The upper tier is shaped like a sky pony (a pegasus), though the form of this feature is readily apparent only from the air. A round, 15-foot-diameter boulder planted firmly in the ground in the northeastern part of the area represents the eye of the sky pony. The boulder is covered with line engravings chiseled by an unknown hand, and it’s this stone that the spirit mound’s name refers to. The Sky Ponies use the stone as an altar, and Sky Pony shamans believe they can commune with Uthgar simply by touching the boulder while its surface is bathed in the full moon's light.

A swift but shallow stream of sparkling freshwater encircles the spirit mound like a moat, although it is not defensive. Overlooking the stream at the northeast and southwest ends of the plateau are unpainted totem poles, the tops of which are carved to look like sky ponies with spread wings. The withered and moldering corpses of trespassers are lashed to these poles as warnings to others.”

Vardis coughs; we have to dig under the rock. 

Bressik nods, “It’s unclear if it’s under or the rock. The sages suggest it is the rock.”

“It’s under the rock,” declares Orsik, with the newfound confidence of his intelligence. 
Delsephine shouts, “Will you go now or wait until morning?” 

“Morning.” 

Eleasis 3rd day of the 2nd tenday 

“I have a plan,” declares the ranger. He points to a beach on the main island 100 feet south of the totem and 100 feet from the boulder. “We will swim across the river to the beach, go up the legs, and get to the boulder without anyone noticing us. The enemy is encamped on the banks of the Northern River.” 

Orsik turns to Thistle, “You know the elevated platform looks like a pegasus.” 

Unla Kee observes, stares intently, and mutters, “I am a pegasus!” 

The rest of the 3HPP needs to be more convinced. 

After crossing the river, the 3HPP carefully walks towards the creature's feet. There, they take a gentle, sloping climb. 

After a few minutes, they stand at the boulder. Unla Kee turns to the others and says, “Well, dig.” 

Before they can dig, the ground beneath their feet begins to tremble. Seconds later, a bullet erupts from the ground 50 feet away from them. Then, another Bullet breaks through the ground. On each is a human who appears to be giving them commands. 

## The Sky Pony Avatar

As the Bullette emerges from the ground, Unla Kee is captivated by the thought of a magical spell he wants to create, the Point of Power. It is a spell that, when cast, can augment the ability of anyone’s investigation, deception, and persuasion skills, and the amount of augmentation would increase with the spell level. The spell requires him to concentrate and talk on the topic at hand, which lasts for an entire hour or until he chooses to stop it. 

It’s unclear what triggered that thought, and it’s unclear if he will remember it tomorrow. 

While Unla Kee is daydreaming about spells, a huge boulder bursts up through the earth like an arrow piercing a shield, but then the rock opens an enormous toothy maw and roars. Powerful legs pull a bulky body out of the ground after the beady-eyed head, revealing a humped and finned back with muscular rear legs that claw their way atop. Orsik is thrown to the ground a few feet from the creature. 

Orsik gets up, stares at the situation, and moves masterfully to strike the creature where it cannot defend itself and where his green flame can attack the other. He hits four times, and once with a critical, the green flame bounces from his sword to the other Bullette. The first creature loses a significant part of its health. 

The armored human on the creature’s back attempts to cast hold person, but the spell fizzles as Unla Kee, flying above with his Pegasus wings, notices and stops her. 

Vardis summons a spiritual weapon, and it slashes at the wounded monster, killing it. He then moves to flank the other creature. 

The other human on the still uninjured Bullette commands Orsik and Vardis to flee, but they see through the magical tones of the voice and ignore him. 

Thistle casts Hunter’s Mark and safely strikes the Bullette with arrows from the sky.

Orsik grumbles at that because the Bulette strikes him with its jaw, even though his buddy uses blinding light to distract the subterranean monstrosity. 

“When you aim for Orsik, don’t miss,” gnarls Orsik, who then kills the creature and the rider whose last words are, “You shall not seize the stone from the cult of the black earth!” 

Orsik can’t resist as his opponent bleeds from the wound, “How do you know we were taking the stone? We were just walking along the field. You attacked us for no reason. 

The fairy chimes in, “The stone belongs in a museum!” 

The other human shouts, “You would let us keep the stone?” 

“I don’t trust somebody who just attacks me. And you’re both dying. So we’ll just continue with the killing.” 

Thistle, Thuul, and Unla Kee strike the two humans from the safety of the sky. Before they die, both try to command Vardis and Unla Kee. The first fails, and the second tells Orsik to flee. Unfortunately, the command doesn’t stop Orsik from striking him down when the last surviving combatant tries to run away. 

The battle is over, and the 3HPP scavenges equipment from their assailants. The stone breastplate is a strange construction that is unheard of. 

Sky Pony tribesmen appear as the 3HPP pluck the treasures from their dead enemies from the tree line. 

Unla Kee, Thistle, and Orsik debate how to deal with the situation, and while they are arguing, Vardis, who has some experience with religious matters, picks up the body of a dead assailant and shouts, “We defended the stone from these thieves!” 

The Shaman shouts, “The Sky Pony avatar has saved our relics!” And then he shouts, “Where is the one that had the wings of a Sky Pony!”  

Unla Kee thinks, “Should we go for the I am the vengeful avatar or the super-cool avatar that everyone wants to be friends with?” 

He dismisses his invisibility and shouts, “I am here. Bow before me.” It’s the most amazingly cool entrance the Shamans and Sky Pony tribes have ever seen. Suddenly, a halfling with the 30’ wing span of a Pegasus appears over their heads. They are in awe and immediately start bowing. 

The Shaman shouts, “We will stage a massive festival in your honor. We will hunt, get drinks, and prepare! And to protect the totem, we will leave 20 of our best warriors here while we bring a thousand more!” 

Orsik is aghast. This has gone terribly, and he thinks, “Great work, Unla Kee; instead of 20, we now have a 1000.” 

Unla Kee is now very annoyed. This was such a brilliant plan: Convince the tribe that he was the Avatar, and then they would let him steal their rock. But now that they see the almost theft as a failure, they weren’t there to protect the rock; they want to deploy an entire army to defend it. 

Orsik shouts, “Let them have the party; we can ask them questions while drunk.” 

That’s when Unla Kee hits upon a great idea; they failed him, so they should do something as penance—“I command you to go on a vision quest, three days out, then spend a whole day fasting, and then return in three days. The vision quest will tell you how to protect this rock, always!”  

The Shaman stares at Unla Kee, decides that this avatar of the Sky Pony speaks the truth, and shouts to his tribesmen. 

As they leave, they salute and cheer the Sky Pony. 

Unla Kee turns to Vardis, “So we have seven days to figure out this rock.” The mage pauses, “I want us to do something nice for them, like make them a new totem or something.” 

Vardis stares at him, “Are you getting soft?” 

The halfling smirks, “Of course not. I want them to worship me, and a benevolent god always attracts more followers than one that does not bestow gifts.” 

With the Shaman and the Sky Pony tribe gone, Unla Kee and Orsik examine the rock. 

“We can’t carry this.” 

“Nope, too heavy for the airship.” 

Then, Unla Kee notices some peculiar lines on the rock. He stares at them for a bit, and it hits him: These are trace lines. He’s read about this somewhere and remembers that you must trace the lines in the proper order, and the artifact's power will be unlocked.  

The diviner asks a power, and it tells him how to trace the lines. When the last line is traced, he is covered in oozing blisters. Fortunately, thanks to his blessed horn, he can cast cure wounds, and the illness disappears as fast as it came. 

Unla kee realizes the rock can be shrunken and enlarged thrice daily. It also can create an effect similar to the wand of wonder. 

“How glorious!” 

The rest of the 3HPP, upon hearing that, groan.

Unla Kee then turns to where the rock was and uses a stone-shape spell to craft an image of himself. Orsik places a magical bean and an enchanted egg in a bowl, which, when broken, creates a water pool. 

“I hope they figure out how to use this.” 

# Chapter 53 A deal is made, and the Ravens cry Nevermore. 
## 1st day of the 2nd tenday of Eleasis at Raven’s Rock 
Bressik watches the Dragon’s Breath, the airship of the cult whose commander is Delsephine, approach Raven’s rock.

The area is a geographical anomaly. Regardless of the time of year, it is always covered in snow. It’s unclear what the cause is. And his reading has revealed nothing. 

It is a mystery, and there are so many in the north. 

But he has a paid gig as a scholar, which is way better than being a deckhand, so he pulls out his map of the area and tells the 3HPP of his research.

He coughs, takes a swig of some brew, and begins - 

“On the icy slopes of the Spine of the World stands the spirit mound of the Black Raven and Gray Wolf tribes. Raven Rock gets its name from a 100-foot-tall stone carved in the likeness of a perching raven, sitting in the center of the highest plateau of the mound and facing west. The Black Ravens leave carcasses by the stone raven’s feet as offerings. At dusk, giant ravens gather to feast on the remains. After devouring the carrion, the giant ravens perch on timber roosts and squawk at one another until nightfall, when they fly off. Warriors of the Black Raven tribe sometimes ride these giant ravens into battle.

Surrounding Raven Rock are four 50-foot-tall menhirs that the Uthgardt shamans use to track the changing of the seasons and the movement of the stars.

The Gray Wolves aren’t allowed atop the high plateau. Instead, they gather around an altar mound in the heart of a wolf-shaped depression southwest of Raven Rock. Their altar is a rectangular slab of stone with the phases of the moon carved into its sides.”

 Orsik, irritated at all the digging at Morgur’s Mound, declares, “To the altar.” 

Bressik is surprised, “Yes, but what about Raven’s Rock?” 

Orsik cuts him off, “The altar is where it is. It’s always at the altar. We aren’t looking anywhere else.” 

Bressik splutters but remembers Orsik's blade and temper and is silent. 

Unla Kee flies to the altar with the wings of a pegasus. The comical effect of the 30’ wingspan attached to a 3’ tall, 1’ wide halfling never ceases to amuse Delsephine. 

Orsik and Vardis climb down the rope ladder and begin the long trudge to the altar. 

Thistle flies around, scouting with Thuul.

Bressik, who found the relationship between the two fascinating, spent the evening chatting with Thistle. He discovered that the Ranger’s relationship with his drake was more of a partnership. Thistle described it as anarcho-socialist and said they take turns being in charge. 

“Fascinating,” he mutters. 

While the cleric, the fighter, and the mage dig, Thistle notes some ravens and approaches them. 

They are these giant creatures flying, just out of range, and yet just in range. 

“Have you seen any Uthgardian Barbarians?” 

The darkest of the ravens craws. “Who are the barbarians, the people who respect their ancestors or the group tearing up a religious site to steal an artifact?” 

Thistle pauses, “Well, I mean to say the nomadic peoples that are not residential to a specific location, and the term is…” 

He realizes he has just opened his mouth and firmly planted his wingtip into his mouth. 

“Craw! Craw! Craw!” the murder of giant ravens goes. 

“You mean the people who respect the Raven’s Rock? Those who treat it’s residents with care?” says the other Raven. 

“Yes?” asks a meek Thistle. 

“Craw! Craw! Craw!” go the ravens. 

“Who knows? It could be today, it could be tomorrow, it could be five from now,” says another raven.”

Thistle says, “Well if you would be so kind, do not tell them who took this artifact.” 

“Craw! Craw! Craw!” go the ravens.

“Nevermore!” says the first raven that spoke. 

Thistle is alarmed. He doesn’t know what that means and can’t remember what Uncle Joon said. 

He flies to Vardis and Orsik and shouts, “When will you guys be done?” 

A wet, snow-covered, steaming Orsik shouts, “We found it!” 

“Then we need to get out of here ASAP. I think we may have incoming.”

Vardis and Orsik hoist the thigh bone on their shoulds and march back. 

Thistle and Unla Kee talk to Delsephine, who agrees to bring the Dragon’s Breath closer to where the rest of the group is. She fears the Ravens may not view her as an innocent bystander. 

Delspehine points her airship towards Great Worm Cavern. 
## 2nd day of the 2nd tenday of Eleasis The Great Wyrm Cavern
As they approach the Great Wyrm Cavern, flying over the world's spine, Bressik ponders how to make his last presentation memorable. 

Somehow, the conversation turns to the fact that Bressik and Delsephine have a contract that Klamath the Red Dragon has in his hoard. 

“He’s very supportive of your plans.” 

Orsik is surprised, “He’s supportive?” 

“Oh, yes. Dragons view the Giants as an obstacle to their eventual domination over the North. They are uniquely capable of surviving in similar terrains and territories that dragons are.” 

Orsik can’t help but poke Thistle, “You okay with us working with Dragons?” 

“Yes,” says the fairy. “I am not opposed to all dragons. I am opposed to the Cult of the Dragon.” 

Bressik interjects, “And as I clarified earlier, we were not members of Severin’s faction that misread Samaster’s text on Dracoliches. Tiamat or another Devil manipulated Severin to create a summoning ritual for Tiamat. That would have prolonged the eternal conflict between the dragons. We believed in the original Dracolich vision of creating a new undead race of dragons that would transcend the petty differences of the different groups. The war of the Dragons was a coup by Severin and Tiamat to subvert our original plans.” 

At this point, Thistle is annoyed: “This is all academic nonsense. It was about power-hungry individuals.” 

Bressik is offended, “I beg to differ. It was about a contrasting view of the natural order. We believed that the undead Dracolich represents a unifying force between the dragons and death and that unification will lead to peace and prosperity.” 

Unla Kee is intrigued, “Why is it Dracolich and not Lich?” 

“Excellent question. My linguistic research suggests it was done to emphasize the different nature of the ritual and the draconic aspect of the undead creature.” 

Unla Kee looks at him, bored. 

Bressik returns to the initially planned presentation, realizing he's losing his audience. 

So he stops, pulls out his final map and begins his narration.


“The spirit mound of the Great Worm tribe is inside a spacious cavern at the northeast end of the Valley of Khedrun, deep in the Spine of the World. Thick ice hangs above the pillared entrance to Great Worm Cavern, and the interior walls of the place are covered with an icy glaze and lined with massive icicles that gleam and glitter in reflected light. The walls are too slippery to be climbed without gear or magic.”

Delsephine interrupts, “We do have such equipment for sale.” 

Orsik mutters, “We’ll take two sets.” 

The captain pockets the 40 gold into her purse and crampons and other equipment materialize from under the hold. 

Bressik continues. 

“The cavern floor is a sheet of slippery ice flanked by 70-foot-high ledges of rock, atop which stand two totem poles of chiseled ice with carvings that resemble winged snakes. The walls of these ledges are riddled with caves in which the Great Worm tribe members live. Natural steam vents in the rock keep the caves warm throughout the year. Above the ledges, dug into the cavern walls, are narrow crypts where the honored dead are placed, their frozen corpses propped up in a standing position.”

Bressik pauses for a minute and wonders if he should continue helping with this assault on ancient artifacts and this destruction of ancient tribal norms. He then remembers what he was paid for and his audience and continues. 

Rising from the back of the cavern is a triangular promontory of ice-covered rock, 120 feet above the cavern floor at its peak. Thereupon stands an altar of frost-covered stone, carved in the shape of a coiled serpent with great wings. The altar represents Elrem, the Great Worm. Here, the tribal warriors make sacrifices to the Great Worm in honor of Uthgar. Doing so, they believe, ensures that Elrem doesn’t return to devour them.

Orsik asks, “What can you tell me about the gong?” 

“Two possibilities. One is that it is the artifact itself. The gong a circular disk 13 feet in diameter, was once the shield of a frost giant champion, but it was broken in combat and is now missing a large piece. What remains of it weighs 250 pounds. The shield is made of red dragon scales bolted to a beaten copper frame. Its leather arm straps are long gone. The ropes holding up the gong can be cut with two swings of a sword.”

Bressik pauses, “Given that all other artifacts were under the altars, I don’t give much credence to that theory.” He then assumes his professorial tone and says, 

“The second theory says that the gong is a gong. It’s decorative and serves no purpose but as part of some local ritual.” 

Orsik nods in agreement, “To the altar.” The dwarf turns to his friend, “We should get the gong. We have a bong and a gong, which would be a great addition. And we could give it to Harshnag if it’s a shield.”

Bressik takes his notes and descends into the lower decks of the Dragon’s Breath.

Orsik walks up to Delsephine with a sly smile, “Bressik is a very capable man of artistic and literary pretensions, and I was wondering if we could hire him into our show.” 

The captain looks at him, her eyes blazing, her face covered in her dark mask, “He has a contract. But there is a payout clause.” 

Delsephine chuckles, “No one reads the fine print. See, Klamath is a necromancer. And teh contract is for two lives, a living and an undead one. So we assume ten gold a year, and he’s 30 years old, that means we get to 300 gold for the living part. And for the undead part, we assume another hundred years, so we get to 13 000 gold pieces.” 

She pauses, “Klamath is willing to negotiate, and I am happy to negotiate for him.” 

“So what would happen if he died?” 

“His value would decline clearly, but remember the undead part.”

“What if his body was desecrated?”

“Well, we can use the parts in our necromantic constructs.” 

“Can we buy the living part?” 

“Certainly. But you must understand buying the living part releases him from his obligations to Klamath. The obligations don’t transfer to you. He is free at that point to do whatever he wants.” 

Orsik is intrigued, “I have got to meet this dragon. Where is he?” 

Delsephine laughs, “I won’t tell you. But I can promise that he will talk to and reward you once you have dealt with the giants. Now, do we have a deal?”

Unla Kee says, “We need to structure this contract differently than just a straight-up cash deal. How about we give you residuals in the play we are running? So, a residual stipulates how much money you will get from future earnings. These can be bounded or unbounded.” 

Delsephine thinks, “Very well. But since residuals are inherently risky, I will demand an upfront payment of 500 gold, and then the residuals will be capped at 6000.” 

Unla Kee shouts, “Done.” 

And then he turns to Orsik, “Of course, she doesn’t know that I signed a deal for the current play. Our deal will not burden the sequel with her residuals.”

Orsik looks at him admiringly, “You can do that?”

“It’s called showbusiness.” 

Thistle wonders, “Do we want to piss off a Red Dragon?” 

Orsik laughs, “We won’t. What will be funny is that we’ll have to tell Bob to wind down the play so we can start a new one. We have two gongs, and we’ll call it the Gong Show.”

Later that day, they finally arrive at the Great Wyrm Cavern. 

As they enter the cave, they notice members of the Great Wyrm tribe getting ready to defend their totems.

Unla Kee and Vardis exchange a glance and incinerate them all with fireballs. 

They smile smugly. The defenders are dead. The altar is theirs. 

Walking closer, they hear a rumbling sound—the ground trembles. A creature bursts from a snowbank, a careening, blue blur of death. Something akin to a mingling of worms, centipedes, and dragons, the beast moves in a bewildering serpentine pattern, its dozens of legs hurtling it forward at a speed that belies its immense mass. Wing-like membranes grow along the beast's sides, unfurling, probing for prey or obstructions in the path. With a screech, the worm rises up on its many legs, mandibles clicking in ravenous anticipation.

# Chapter 54 When the 3HPP Met Harry and Mary, and then Orsik got his fingers dirty.

## 5th day of the 2nd tenday of Eleasis 1492 at Flint Rock

Orsik grumbles, “It’s under the rock, it’s always under the rock. And the rock is always inconveniently placed.” 

He stares up ahead and sees Thistle and Unla Kee landing. 

Vardis sighs and starts marching. 

After a few minutes, sweat dripping from their foreheads, Orsik asks, “So where is it?” 

Thistle stares at Orsik, “Under the rock?” 

Vardis casts detect magic and confirms the location. 

Orsik looks at him, “No, that’s not how detect magic works. See, with 1 foot of stone, you shouldn’t be able to detect anything.”

Vardis stares at him, “You’re right. Something is wrong. We need to abandon this entire quest and start all over again.” 

Unla Kee sniggers, “This would have been undetectable in the past, but you’ll notice that the stone surface is weather-worn. So, although originally the stone was 1 foot, the stone now is just ever so slightly less than 1 foot. And as a result, the spell worked.” 

Orsik scratches his head. Vardis stops walking away. 

They head back to start digging when two giant elk approach. 

Thistle casts spells to speak with animals, flies within shouting range, and asks, “Have you seen any other creatures?” 

The male elk responds, “Well, there were the Joneses. They are the most irritating giant elks.” 

The other female elk intervenes, “Harry, how many times must I tell you to stop speaking ill of the Joneses.” 

“Harumph. Mary, they are the ones who like to strut around with their horns acting all superior to everyone.” 
Unla Kee laughs, “Keeping up with the Joneses…” 

Orsik, meanwhile, is furiously digging.

Then the female Elk squints into the sky and shouts, “Are you a fly?” 

“No, I am fae from the feywild.” 

“Fae from the feywild? Is that a particular river nearby? A watering hole? A grove of trees?” 

Harry, the male giant elk, says, “Oh no, it’s one of those creatures that think we worship some god with funny horns. I’ll have you note we are part of an atheistic club and do not worship the gods of the Fae. “ 

Mary groans, “I’m so sorry. My husband and I are trying to enjoy a quiet meal.” 

Thistle asks, “Anything dangerous?” 

Mary pauses, “No, not that I recall.” 

Thistle thanks them and flies off. 

He does hear Harry moan, “Another bipedal stealing our grass!” 

Orsik and Vardis are now trying to smash the rock. 

As they make more noise, Mary shouts, “Yoo-hoo! Pretentious flying creature!”

Harry mutters, “Calls himself a Fairy from the Feywild. Whoever heard of such nonsense. The Feywild is just a tool of the oppressor to keep the Giant Elk subservient to a doomed religion.” 

Mary ignores him, “We have noticed that other bipedal creatures think this is an important area for them. Are you sure you are allowed to do what you are doing?” 

Thistle manages to persuade them that they do. 

Harry says, “Well the sooner you get out of here, the sooner we get our peace and quiet.”

Mary and Harry approach the 3HPP and push the stone out of the way. 

A bit later, the 3HPP is on Delsephine’s ship, the Dragon’s Breath, with a mithral spear tip wearing 75 pounds. 

“Where can my ship take you next?” 
Vardis mutters, “Morgur’s Mound.” 

## 6th day of the 2nd tenday of Eleasis at Morgur’s Mound
As they approach Morgur’s Mound, Bressik is told to provide the information he has. He pulls out a map of the location that he found while at Candlekeep. 

“The spirit mound of the lost Thunderbeast tribe, Morgur’s Mound, stands in the Crags and is named after Uthgar’s brother. The Thunderbeast tribe disappeared. We're not sure why. It may have been tribal conflict, their ineptitude, or a dragon who swallowed them all. Nobody knows. 

The mound is shaped like a long-necked, wingless dragon — the Uthgardt impression of a thunderbeast. A ridge of dragon bones juts from the mound's head, neck, back, and tail. Enormous cairns encircle the mound out to a range of a quarter mile. Beneath them lie the bones of revered Thunderbeast tribal warriors.

The Thunderbeast tribe has not visited its spirit mound in years, leaving it ripe for plunderers. Several dig sites left by previous expeditions attest to the mound’s popularity among treasure hunters.”

Vardis mutters, “And they call us grave robbers.” 

Bressik coughs uncomfortably, “As I was, through no fault of my own, a member of a cult planning to end the world and bring about the age of dragons by bringing an evil goddess into our world and avoided the just punishment I deserve by serving an evil red dragon, I am not sure I am capable of casting aspersion on anyone’s character or their activities.” 

When they arrive at the mound, Vardis asks, “So where do you think it is?” 

Bressik answers, “No clue.” 

After casting detect magic, Vardis gets a hint that something might be below his feet, and says, “I am feeling something and it’s not the gas.” 

Orsik sniggers. 

Vardis asks, “Unla Kee, you’re up. You have mold earth?” 

The halfling shrugs, “No.” 

Orsik is furious but resigned and starts to dig. Vardis joins him. 

Thistle and Unla Kee stand watch. Unla Kee is too weak to dig, and Thistle is too, proud. 

Eight hours pass, and they find nothing. 

“Tomorrow?”

## 7th day of the 2nd tenday of Eleasis

Again, Orsik and Vardis are digging and the mage and the ranger just watch. 

Vardis gets annoyed, “Maybe you can dig?” 

Thistle says, “No.” 

Vardis gets annoyed, “Why don’t you take your pretty little eyes and check if you can see anything?” 

After a few more hours, the two friends find a magic item. Not the artifact they are looking for but an item. 

They identify it as an abjurer’s bangle, a device that absorbs damage from attacks. They hand it to Unla Kee. 

But still no artifact.

## 8th day of the 2nd tenday of Eleasis 

Orsik and Vardis move to the creature's head and dig some more. 

And once again, the find another magic item, a Helm of Comprehend Languages. 

Orsik curses, “Seriously?” 
Delsephine sighs, “Still no artifact.” 

Vardis gives her the stink eye. 

Thistle and Unla Kee shrug. 
## 9th day of the 2nd tenday of Eleasis

Nothing.
## 10th day of the 2nd tenday of Eleasis 

Orsik finds a giant tooth. It's a gold-plated fire giant’s tooth, an ancient giant relic of giantkind. The tooth is nonmagical and small enough to fit in the hand of a human.

Orsik waves at Thistle to come towards him. 

As Thistle approaches, four animated thunderbeast skeletons erupt from the mound around the hole Orsik is in. 

“The scouting was oh so useful.” 

Thistle sees what’s happening and flies as fast as he can, and no faster.

Orsik scrambles out of the hole. He sees four creatures and attacks the first, bringing it one tiny step close to oblivion. 

Just then, two arrows from Thistle strike another of the beasts that he identifies as ankylosaurus’. 

Thuul dashes closer. 

Three of the creatures attack. Orsik’s military skill and armor allow him to avoid the jaws of the monsters. Vardis casts a warding flair and distracts his enemy. Unla Kee is bitten.

Vardis casts a fireball. The burning blast destroys the one that Orsik had injured and wounds two more. 

Unla Kee pulls out his artifact, allowing him to produce effects similar to the wand of wonder. 

He points the rock at one of the creatures and performs the ritual, and a gust of wind blows out of the rock. Orsik laughs bitterly. 

But Unla Kee sees the opportunity and uses the wind to push one of the skeletons into the pit that Orsik and Vardis had dug. 

Orsik strikes with his sword twice at one of the monsters. 

Thistle moves in and impales another with two of his arrows. 

The creature in the pit tries to climb out, but the wind blowing from Unla Kee is too strong. 

Orsik sees what’s happening and mutters, “Excellent!” 

Vardis strikes one of the creatures down. 

Unla Kee uses a gust of wind to force one of the creatures away from the dwarf and his buddy. The skeletal monster loses its grip, and Orsik and Thule take the opportunity to miss striking at it. 

As the creature tumbles into the hole, Unla Kee ponders what to do. He considers using his artifact of wonder but casts a fireball in the hole instead. 

A sudden rush of heat explodes from the ground, and then silence. 

On the airship, Delsephine asks, “Should we take you somewhere where you can clean yourself, Orsik?” 

“No.” 

Orsik is annoyed at the cult leader. His body is covered in dirt from five days of digging, and all she cares about is her pretty little ship. 

He finds the cleanest spot on the ship and plops himself down.

Pulling out the tooth, he mutters, “Five days of digging in the dirt to find this.” 

Delsephine grimaces, and tells her crew to give the grumpy dwarf space. She then turns to Vardis, “The only places left are Raven’s Rock and the Great Wyrm Cavern.” 

“To Raven’s rock.” 

# Chapter 55 A deal is made, and the Ravens cry Nevermore. 
## 1st day of the 2nd tenday of Eleasis at Raven’s Rock 
Bressik watches the Dragon’s Breath, the airship of the cult whose commander is Delsephine, approach Raven’s rock.

The area is a geographical anomaly. Regardless of the time of year, it is always covered in snow. It’s unclear what the cause is. And his reading has revealed nothing. 

It is a mystery, and there are so many in the north. 

But he has a paid gig as a scholar, which is way better than being a deckhand, so he pulls out his map of the area and tells the 3HPP of his research.

He coughs, takes a swig of some brew, and begins - 

“On the icy slopes of the Spine of the World stands the spirit mound of the Black Raven and Gray Wolf tribes. Raven Rock gets its name from a 100-foot-tall stone carved in the likeness of a perching raven, sitting in the center of the highest plateau of the mound and facing west. The Black Ravens leave carcasses by the stone raven’s feet as offerings. At dusk, giant ravens gather to feast on the remains. After devouring the carrion, the giant ravens perch on timber roosts and squawk at one another until nightfall, when they fly off. Warriors of the Black Raven tribe sometimes ride these giant ravens into battle.

Surrounding Raven Rock are four 50-foot-tall menhirs that the Uthgardt shamans use to track the changing of the seasons and the movement of the stars.

The Gray Wolves aren’t allowed atop the high plateau. Instead, they gather around an altar mound in the heart of a wolf-shaped depression southwest of Raven Rock. Their altar is a rectangular slab of stone with the phases of the moon carved into its sides.”

 Orsik, irritated at all the digging at Morgur’s Mound, declares, “To the altar.” 

Bressik is surprised, “Yes, but what about Raven’s Rock?” 

Orsik cuts him off, “The altar is where it is. It’s always at the altar. We aren’t looking anywhere else.” 

Bressik splutters but remembers Orsik's blade and temper and is silent. 

Unla Kee flies to the altar with the wings of a pegasus. The comical effect of the 30’ wingspan attached to a 3’ tall, 1’ wide halfling never ceases to amuse Delsephine. 

Orsik and Vardis climb down the rope ladder and begin the long trudge to the altar. 

Thistle flies around, scouting with Thuul.

Bressik, who found the relationship between the two fascinating, spent the evening chatting with Thistle. He discovered that the Ranger’s relationship with his drake was more of a partnership. Thistle described it as anarcho-socialist and said they take turns being in charge. 

“Fascinating,” he mutters. 

While the cleric, the fighter, and the mage dig, Thistle notes some ravens and approaches them. 

They are these giant creatures flying, just out of range, and yet just in range. 

“Have you seen any Uthgardian Barbarians?” 

The darkest of the ravens craws. “Who are the barbarians, the people who respect their ancestors or the group tearing up a religious site to steal an artifact?” 

Thistle pauses, “Well, I mean to say the nomadic peoples that are not residential to a specific location, and the term is…” 

He realizes he has just opened his mouth and firmly planted his wingtip into his mouth. 

“Craw! Craw! Craw!” the murder of giant ravens goes. 

“You mean the people who respect the Raven’s Rock? Those who treat it’s residents with care?” says the other Raven. 

“Yes?” asks a meek Thistle. 

“Craw! Craw! Craw!” go the ravens. 

“Who knows? It could be today, it could be tomorrow, it could be five from now,” says another raven.”

Thistle says, “Well if you would be so kind, do not tell them who took this artifact.” 

“Craw! Craw! Craw!” go the ravens.

“Nevermore!” says the first raven that spoke. 

Thistle is alarmed. He doesn’t know what that means and can’t remember what Uncle Joon said. 

He flies to Vardis and Orsik and shouts, “When will you guys be done?” 

A wet, snow-covered, steaming Orsik shouts, “We found it!” 

“Then we need to get out of here ASAP. I think we may have incoming.”

Vardis and Orsik hoist the thigh bone on their shoulds and march back. 

Thistle and Unla Kee talk to Delsephine, who agrees to bring the Dragon’s Breath closer to where the rest of the group is. She fears the Ravens may not view her as an innocent bystander. 

Delspehine points her airship towards Great Worm Cavern. 
## 2nd day of the 2nd tenday of Eleasis The Great Wyrm Cavern
As they approach the Great Wyrm Cavern, flying over the world's spine, Bressik ponders how to make his last presentation memorable. 

Somehow, the conversation turns to the fact that Bressik and Delsephine have a contract that Klamath the Red Dragon has in his hoard. 

“He’s very supportive of your plans.” 

Orsik is surprised, “He’s supportive?” 

“Oh, yes. Dragons view the Giants as an obstacle to their eventual domination over the North. They are uniquely capable of surviving in similar terrains and territories that dragons are.” 

Orsik can’t help but poke Thistle, “You okay with us working with Dragons?” 

“Yes,” says the fairy. “I am not opposed to all dragons. I am opposed to the Cult of the Dragon.” 

Bressik interjects, “And as I clarified earlier, we were not members of Severin’s faction that misread Samaster’s text on Dracoliches. Tiamat or another Devil manipulated Severin to create a summoning ritual for Tiamat. That would have prolonged the eternal conflict between the dragons. We believed in the original Dracolich vision of creating a new undead race of dragons that would transcend the petty differences of the different groups. The war of the Dragons was a coup by Severin and Tiamat to subvert our original plans.” 

At this point, Thistle is annoyed: “This is all academic nonsense. It was about power-hungry individuals.” 

Bressik is offended, “I beg to differ. It was about a contrasting view of the natural order. We believed that the undead Dracolich represents a unifying force between the dragons and death and that unification will lead to peace and prosperity.” 

Unla Kee is intrigued, “Why is it Dracolich and not Lich?” 

“Excellent question. My linguistic research suggests it was done to emphasize the different nature of the ritual and the draconic aspect of the undead creature.” 

Unla Kee looks at him, bored. 

Bressik returns to the initially planned presentation, realizing he's losing his audience. 

So he stops, pulls out his final map and begins his narration.


“The spirit mound of the Great Worm tribe is inside a spacious cavern at the northeast end of the Valley of Khedrun, deep in the Spine of the World. Thick ice hangs above the pillared entrance to Great Worm Cavern, and the interior walls of the place are covered with an icy glaze and lined with massive icicles that gleam and glitter in reflected light. The walls are too slippery to be climbed without gear or magic.”

Delsephine interrupts, “We do have such equipment for sale.” 

Orsik mutters, “We’ll take two sets.” 

The captain pockets the 40 gold into her purse and crampons and other equipment materialize from under the hold. 

Bressik continues. 

“The cavern floor is a sheet of slippery ice flanked by 70-foot-high ledges of rock, atop which stand two totem poles of chiseled ice with carvings that resemble winged snakes. The walls of these ledges are riddled with caves in which the Great Worm tribe members live. Natural steam vents in the rock keep the caves warm throughout the year. Above the ledges, dug into the cavern walls, are narrow crypts where the honored dead are placed, their frozen corpses propped up in a standing position.”

Bressik pauses for a minute and wonders if he should continue helping with this assault on ancient artifacts and this destruction of ancient tribal norms. He then remembers what he was paid for and his audience and continues. 

Rising from the back of the cavern is a triangular promontory of ice-covered rock, 120 feet above the cavern floor at its peak. Thereupon stands an altar of frost-covered stone, carved in the shape of a coiled serpent with great wings. The altar represents Elrem, the Great Worm. Here, the tribal warriors make sacrifices to the Great Worm in honor of Uthgar. Doing so, they believe, ensures that Elrem doesn’t return to devour them.

Orsik asks, “What can you tell me about the gong?” 

“Two possibilities. One is that it is the artifact itself. The gong a circular disk 13 feet in diameter, was once the shield of a frost giant champion, but it was broken in combat and is now missing a large piece. What remains of it weighs 250 pounds. The shield is made of red dragon scales bolted to a beaten copper frame. Its leather arm straps are long gone. The ropes holding up the gong can be cut with two swings of a sword.”

Bressik pauses, “Given that all other artifacts were under the altars, I don’t give much credence to that theory.” He then assumes his professorial tone and says, 

“The second theory says that the gong is a gong. It’s decorative and serves no purpose but as part of some local ritual.” 

Orsik nods in agreement, “To the altar.” The dwarf turns to his friend, “We should get the gong. We have a bong and a gong, which would be a great addition. And we could give it to Harshnag if it’s a shield.”

Bressik takes his notes and descends into the lower decks of the Dragon’s Breath.

Orsik walks up to Delsephine with a sly smile, “Bressik is a very capable man of artistic and literary pretensions, and I was wondering if we could hire him into our show.” 

The captain looks at him, her eyes blazing, her face covered in her dark mask, “He has a contract. But there is a payout clause.” 

Delsephine chuckles, “No one reads the fine print. See, Klamath is a necromancer. And teh contract is for two lives, a living and an undead one. So we assume ten gold a year, and he’s 30 years old, that means we get to 300 gold for the living part. And for the undead part, we assume another hundred years, so we get to 13 000 gold pieces.” 

She pauses, “Klamath is willing to negotiate, and I am happy to negotiate for him.” 

“So what would happen if he died?” 

“His value would decline clearly, but remember the undead part.”

“What if his body was desecrated?”

“Well, we can use the parts in our necromantic constructs.” 

“Can we buy the living part?” 

“Certainly. But you must understand buying the living part releases him from his obligations to Klamath. The obligations don’t transfer to you. He is free at that point to do whatever he wants.” 

Orsik is intrigued, “I have got to meet this dragon. Where is he?” 

Delsephine laughs, “I won’t tell you. But I can promise that he will talk to and reward you once you have dealt with the giants. Now, do we have a deal?”

Unla Kee says, “We need to structure this contract differently than just a straight-up cash deal. How about we give you residuals in the play we are running? So, a residual stipulates how much money you will get from future earnings. These can be bounded or unbounded.” 

Delsephine thinks, “Very well. But since residuals are inherently risky, I will demand an upfront payment of 500 gold, and then the residuals will be capped at 6000.” 

Unla Kee shouts, “Done.” 

And then he turns to Orsik, “Of course, she doesn’t know that I signed a deal for the current play. Our deal will not burden the sequel with her residuals.”

Orsik looks at him admiringly, “You can do that?”

“It’s called showbusiness.” 

Thistle wonders, “Do we want to piss off a Red Dragon?” 

Orsik laughs, “We won’t. What will be funny is that we’ll have to tell Bob to wind down the play so we can start a new one. We have two gongs, and we’ll call it the Gong Show.”

Later that day, they finally arrive at the Great Wyrm Cavern. 

As they enter the cave, they notice members of the Great Wyrm tribe getting ready to defend their totems.

Unla Kee and Vardis exchange a glance and incinerate them all with fireballs. 

They smile smugly. The defenders are dead. The altar is theirs. 

Walking closer, they hear a rumbling sound—the ground trembles. A creature bursts from a snowbank, a careening, blue blur of death. Something akin to a mingling of worms, centipedes, and dragons, the beast moves in a bewildering serpentine pattern, its dozens of legs hurtling it forward at a speed that belies its immense mass. Wing-like membranes grow along the beast's sides, unfurling, probing for prey or obstructions in the path. With a screech, the worm rises up on its many legs, mandibles clicking in ravenous anticipation.

# Chapter 56 - The return to Silverymoon
## 2nd day of the 2nd tenday of Eleasis
As Orsik passes the totem, a Coatl emerges. It demands that they destroy Wormblod, who has perverted the tribe, and the creature promises a boon for this act.
Thistle, remembering killing Wormblod in the Eye of the All-Father, says, “We did it!” 
Vardis tries to stop him, thinking, “We should haggle for something more.” 
But it’s too late; they have all been blessed with a Charm of Restoration. 
Orsik sighs, “I guess that’s useful.” 
The creature’s eyes bulge, “you are not appreciative of my boon?” 
The 3HPP nods their heads like annoyed teenagers when their parents ask them to say thank you to the annoying uncle and say, “Yeah, we appreciate it.” 
The creature slithers its tongue in and out of its mouth and mutters, “Usually, they appreciate me more!” 
Further down in the cave, the 3HPP finds the Remorhazz’s lair. As they steal the gold and gemstones and the minor magical artifacts, Vardis notes that if they had not bothered to attack with the fireballs, they might have been able to get the gong without fighting the creatures. 
“But fireballs are fun,” mutters Unla Kee. 
Back on the Dragon’s Breath, Bressik is delighted about his newfound freedom and the opportunity to work on background research and the play.
Ironically, Thistle never thinks to act Bressik about his girlfriend. Bressik finds that odd. It’s not like he’s a well-informed Candlekeep scholar with deep knowledge about the occult and goings on of the area. And of the curious case of the fairy, Uncle Joon, and his journeys. 
Bressik is pleased and thinks “that information will cost them.” 
“And the buffoons, Orsik and Vardis, who refused to pay me a fair price, and now they will never know where their precious little treasure map goes.” 
## 3rd day of the 2nd tenday of Eleasis
The following day, as the sun rises over Silverymoon, the 3HPP with Bressik arrives at the gate. 
There is a bit of a dialogue of what angle to use to enter the city, and they quickly reject the use of the letter from the Ambassador, and decide to mention that they are business partners with his [Lordship](https://www.worldanvil.com/heroes/campaign/npc/9857dbfe-91d8-41b5-a71a-3f7711bf7003). 
“Working with the non-goodly races?” spouts the guard. 
Orsik glares and the guard decides to confirm with his captain, [Sir Cedric Hollowcrest](https://www.worldanvil.com/heroes/campaign/npc/69b2ab4a-f8c2-4b19-aeef-ad714e17de68/), who arrives a few moments later.
“I am so sorry. These are the famous 3HPP! The redeemed Orsic!”
Orsic gruffly announces, “I am Orsic Sr.” 
He points to the Fairy - “The brokenhearted Thistle!” 
Then beams at Vardis and says - “The honest Vardis!” 
“Bob Saget’s play has made you heroes. His performance was divine!” 
And with that, the original guard says, “YOU’RE UNLA KEE! Omg! OMG! I am so in love with you! You are unafraid of anything, blast with fire and ice attacks, ignore all dangers, and laugh in the face of death!” 
Unla Kee stares at him, “What's the most dangerous thing around us right now?” 
“You.” 
“Am I scared of myself? Of course not! If I could look at myself in the eye and feel not an inch of fear.” 
The guard pulls out a note – “Could you sign this? Care of Borin Greycastle?
“One.” 
The guards sigh. 
“Cheer up; you can sell this in the open markets or collector for 20x!” 
“Sell this? Never.” 
As the 3HPP moves through the Mulgate and heads towards the Moonbridge on Moonway, they realize that Bob Saget has taken several liberties with their story. And in so doing has made them minor celebrities. 
Thistle, who paid good money, asks the group to head to Wyrmworks, where Rebecca Deakin has been working on some research for him. 
As they walk in, she’s excited and shouts, “Oh, you are alive!” 
Orsik raises an eyebrow. 
She answers, “I figured anyone stupid enough to look into Adimond Kroskas would be dead by now.” And then, unprompted, “My research into Adimond has reaffirmed my faith that Bob Saget is right. Silverymoon must abandon magic. The Spellguard is a crypto-fascist tool of the mages and a force of control.” 

Rebecca pauses and looks around furtively, “I spoke too much.” 
And then continues, ‘The thing is that the wizards treat us non-magic users as lesser people. Why else do you think we have the ‘goodly’ races? It’s a natural response to the wizard's oppression. Then the wizards create a school, and all of these out-of-towners going to the Wizard school act as if they are all better than the rest of us. They have their special classes, and their sages and their discussions. And it’s all fun and games until one of those idiots casts a spell or does some experiment with an elixir which turns into a plague.” 
She mutters, “Bob’s right. Just because they can cast spells doesn’t make them better. They should respect us. Those of us who don’t cast spells are valuable. And frankly, some of us can do what magic cannot do. I do research as an archivist and get information the old-fashioned way. I open books. I do research. I use my brain. I don’t snap my fingers, and some demon magically tells me the answer in exchange for the souls of 1000 of my co-citizens.” Seeing Unla Kee, she says, “I am talking about the Silverymoon and the Spellguard! The closest they get to dirty hands is if they touch a tomb that one of their apprentices had not dusted off before they touch it.” 
With that, she turns to Thistle, “So let me see. Adimond Kroskas is a powerful spell caster who knows the spell that allows one to transform Drakes from Dragon eggs.” 
“The what spell,” shouts Thistle. “This drake is not a spawn of a spell.”
Rebecca looks at him and says, “Right. He’s a lesser drake.” 
“Lesser Drake?” 
“Yes. Pure Drakes were created from a Dragon egg, and lesser drakes were their offspring.” 
Thistle glares at Rebecca, then turns to his increasingly annoyed Drake and says, “Maybe you should sit outside.” 
Rebecca continues, “Now he was hired by the Cult of Tiamat to work on a fay-dragon drake hybrid.” 
Thistle is shocked. 
She continues, “So this guy I met, who said he was your uncle, Joon, said that Adimond went into the fae to get the First Flower of Spring because it was a critical component of his spell research. The fairies were also important, unclear if it was as test subjects, components, or treats.” 
And then she pauses, “The weirdest part is what Adminod Kroskas is.  The Cult of the Dragon referred to him as a dragon, and some of them referred to him as a Dragon's pet. I am betting on the Dragon.” 
Thistle shouts, “UNCLE JOON! I am going to kill him.” 
Vardis remembers the death of Kolbaz, who did research Adimond last, and the odd damage in his chambers. “A Blue Dragon,” he mumbles. 
Thistle, when he recovers from his rage, suddenly understands with horror that the stories of his girlfriend’s violent behavior may not be as innocent as he initially thought. 
Orsik, more concerned with the play than a Blue Dragon, species experiments, and lost girlfriends, shouts, “C’mon, you got your answers.” 
And with that, they head out to the High Palace. Outside is a banner announcing that the play is sold out for the next two months. People are lining up to buy tickets, and the minute the 3HPP shows up, they are mobbed. 
Unla Kee is delighted and shouts, “FINALLY, THE RECOGNITION!” Orsik is also happy with the adoration, “I am a hero!” They both autograph the papers that are put in front of them. 
Just then, a pair of guards approach and announce that  [Lord Methrammar Aersume](https://www.worldanvil.com/heroes/campaign/npc/9857dbfe-91d8-41b5-a71a-3f7711bf7003) wishes to meet them.
He smiles warmly as they enter the room, “It’s so good to see you! The play has been a smashing success. Profits will delight all of you. Bob Saget had a brilliant stroke of genius to incorporate you into the play. At the end of the performance, when he’s about to destroy the world in some explosive, the four of you defeat him in a battle of wits.” 
He laughs. 
“Not a battle of wits, but a real battle. Orsik uses all his battle mastery, Vardis summons his god’s powers, and Unla Kee unleashes his spells.” He takes on a dreamy look, “It’s amazing. Especially the effects, the lights, and the explosions. There is a rumor that some CGI device is being used.” 
Orsik is intrigued, “who portrays us?” 
“I don’t know. You should ask Bob.”
Thistle wonders why he wasn’t part of the final combat, and his Lordship realizes that. 
Lord Aersume turns to Thistle, “We couldn’t find an actor small enough, so we used a puppet stuck on a stick. It’s an amazing likeness.” 
Orsik, who is gung ho on the idea of a new play, is shot down by his Lordship.
“People aren’t bored. The play has become a spiritual investigation. Bob has made the play about the dangers of magic and that all of us are accountable to ensure nothing bad happens. The heroes are four individuals of question repute who save the day. It plays into individual accountability, fears of magic, and the power of anyone.” 
Orsik is delighted. And wonders whether the son of Laeral Silverhand, one of the seven anointed of Mystara, agrees that magic is dangerous. Has Bob swayed him?
“There is danger in everything. There is danger in the sword. There is a danger in your questions,” he says menacingly. “The story is a metaphor about understanding the limits of what we can do and that anyone can be a hero. After all, the original playwright had wanted to write a play that would summon a demon that would destroy the entire town of Triboar and was thwarted by the four of you.” 
Sensing Orsik’s question, he glares, “I will not participate in these debates about Magic being too risky. Magic has served Silverymoon for generations. Lady Laeral Silverhand, my mother, favored of Mystara, once ruled the city, and if she thinks that magic can serve the city going forward, so say I. Those who say otherwise are traitors to Silverymoon.” 
Orsik then thinks that might be an opening to get the play changed to avoid paying Delsephine. “We have certain business concerns involving a red dragon that necessitate small changes in the play. And given how particular red dragons are about their money, if you could give us 6,000 gold pieces, we could get out of the situation or change the play.” 
“My dear boys, you don’t understand how profitable this play has become.”
He hands them 12,000 gold pieces and says, “You can cover that 6,000 from your share.” 
Unla Kee is worried, “what percentage is that? Is it from total earnings, profit?” 
His Lordship smiles, “There are only two truths in theater, Unla. The first is that there is never a profit. The second is when the producer gives you some money, you take it and don’t ask questions.” 
“I am never buying a flying castle,” Unla Kee whines. 
“We could always take Blagothkus’ castle,” Thistle points out.
Later that evening, the group meets with a very different-looking Bob Saget, who is delighted to see you. 
“My muses! And before you ask, everything is great. The theater is full every night to hear – my – story. Every night, their minds get twisted more, per your instructions against magic. It’s nothing more than a successful perversion of society that will achieve your, not my, stated aims in 30 days.” 
The 3HPP tries to probe who plays them, and Bob is very cagey. He is dismissive, “nobodies. They get their moment in the sun, and we replace them.” 
“The scene works best if it's amateurs rather than real adventurers. The actors show real gut-wrenching horror as they are making their attack. And it’s that terror that makes the whole scene work.” 
The heroes try to get some seats in the play, but Bob insists that none can be provided. When they press him, “I’m afraid not. It will not work at all. [Kaelen](https://www.worldanvil.com/w/the-forgotten-realms-kostadis/a/kaelen-sarssir-person) wrote the play that summoned me. He saved me from an eternity of bad dad jokes. And now we work together to improve his play. Your presence would be a – distraction.”  
Orsik smiles politely and decides to get in, so he purchases some invisibility potions. 
# Chapter 57 - The Theater

## Eleasis 4th day, 2nd tenday

The 3HPP, hidden from sight using the invisibility potions that Orsik provided, stands in front of the double doors of the Great Theater.
Each door has a small, grilled viewport. A shutter blocks the view of what lies beyond. The door is decorated with magical runes made of many thousand small mosaic pieces. 
Vardis asks, “Where is everyone?” 
Orsik agrees that it is odd that there are no other patrons, “where did all those patrons that bought the tickets and mobbed us go? Are we too early?”
“No, we arrived 5 minutes before the play started.” 
Orsik moves boldly to the door and opens it. As he is about to enter, he notices a glyph that the 3HPP turns off. 
Unfortunately, he did not notice the alarm spell that had been cast. 
The shrill sound of the alarm forces the audience to turn towards the door. 
Orsik notices that one of the audience members is a suppurating, bloody mess; one of its eyes is missing. 
Vardis observes that another is clad in blood-soaked peasant clothes, having clearly suffered horrendous wounds before their death. 
Thistle stares at the gaping wound in their rib cage, exposing the desiccated organs. 
They all think, “Zombies.” 
The theater itself has two levels. The first level has a stage at the end with a red curtain that has been raised. Benches are arranged to give the zombies an excellent view of the play. And there is a staircase that is right next to the door. 
Standing on the stage, Bob Saget, dressed in a purple jacket, purple tie, and purple pants, shouts, “We have guests? Could it be the Three Half Pints and a Pint?” 
He waits and hears nothing. 
“Well, it doesn’t matter.” He clicks his fingers, and the doors behind the 3HPP close. 
“You can still enjoy the show; if you are here to disrupt it, you might want to think twice.” 
And just then, several drow knights and two cambions come from backstage and stand in front of Bob. They obstruct the view of the stage for the Zombie patrons. 
They don’t seem to mind. 
Bob Saget then continues. “The fool Kaesen’s summoning spell brought me here. And the 3HPP thought it would bring joy and light to this world, and of course, profit.” Then, with a dramatic hand gesture, he spits out, “But that was boring.” His tone turns to rage, “Who wants to bring joy and light? I am a demon. I cause chaos and mayhem!” Then it turns conspiratorial, “I’ve been casting my magic to twist the folks of Silverymoon against their fascist oppressors, the Spellguards. But I’ve been working on my own little spell. And it needs some victims. And rather than just kill someone, why not have four aspiring thespians pretend to be the 3HPP and die trying to kill me?” 
The drow disappears backstage and then shows up with four commoners dressed like the 3HPP, except one dressed in black and carrying a stick with a fairy attached to it. They are terrified and crying and screaming. 
He turns to the audience, breaking the fourth wall, and says, “I love the authenticity of their terror.” 
As the faux-Orsik screams, he tut-tuts, “Weren’t you looking to be members of this unbelievable show?’ 
And then again breaks the fourth wall, almost as if he is taunting his uninvited guests, “Do you want to take their parts?” 
The 3HPP are silent. 
“Look at them, a member of the – ungoodly race. They come into Silverymoon, and they get a foul of the Spellguard and nobody cares if they disappear. Why the folks of Silverymoon might say thank you.” 
He spins in a circle and claps his hands, “I am not killing people. I am solving a problem for the city. And after every performance, the cast joins the audience and passes judgment on their performance!” 
The 3HPP stares silently. 
The crying cast members are pushed on stage, and Bob says, “They don’t know how to fight or cast spells, but I think the authenticity of desperation makes it work.” 
A gnome dressed like Unla Kee waves his hands frantically as he tries to cast something, but the poor gnome is not a spell caster. Bob laughs and then sticks his claw through the poor person’s chest. 
At that, the Tabaxi, dressed like Orsik, charges and swings his sword. 
Bob shouts, “I’ve been injured. I am going to die.” The Tabaxi smiles, and then Bob kills the feline and says gleefully, “No, I am not,” as he kills them. 
The 3HPP can watch no more and close their eyes. When they open them, there are four corpses on the stage. 
At that, a Drow Mage stands in front of the corpses and animates them using some kind of device. The sad victims then join the audience. 
Bob laughs and leaves the stage. 
The 3HPP leaves the theater, horrified. 
As they stand outside, invisible, a blue shimmer appears, and then their fans march out. 
The fans are delighted, excited, and very alive. 
Orsik understands what is happening: “He’s using the Theater to conduct his bloodthirsty ritual, and he’s creating some kind of demi-plane to send the theatergoers to.” 
Then, each member of the 3HPP sits silently and wonders what to do. 
Unla Kee thinks, “I wanted to attack right then and there. Now, I don’t.” 
Orsik decides that the people of Silverymoon did nothing for him. And he remembers quite vividly how the Knights treated him. He, of course, forgets what he did to deserve that treatment. 
Vardis will do what his best friend wants. The city of Silverymoon is not an elven problem. 
Thistle thinks to himself, “This is awful! I must do something!” Then he pauses, “But I also have to save my sweetie!” And then remembers there is time, “We have 30 days!” And then remembers that all those innocent people are going to die, “Can I really make that tradeoff?” And then he realizes, “I am the only one who can save her, nobody else. I will save her and then deal with this.” 

Vardis mutters, “I don’t think we could take Bob. He’s too powerful. We need allies.” 
“We seem to have accumulated enemies, not allies.” 
Unla Kee quibbles, “Sure, the harpers hate us. The Zhentarim aren’t exactly trying to help us. The city of Silverymoon won’t look kindly on us when we abandon them. There is the city of Everlund. And then there is the Hand of Yartar. We have friends. And I have a fireball. And I can assure you that this is not how the world ends.” 
Orsik says, “We could tell the Lord of this city?” 
And then they all realize that if they did, it’s unclear if the Lord would think they were innocent of these crimes.
“What’s the vote?” 
They all agree that they will deal with this secretly, first dealing with the giants, then Bob. 
And with that, they turn to Unla Kee – “Let’s get you that tower.”
“A flying tower?”
“A flying castle!” 
The following day, the 3HPP are once again the celebrated heroes of Silverymoon. Bob Saget is, once again, the toast of the town, and his play continues its fantastic run! 
The 3HPP, however, are aware of the evil they have unleashed on the town of Silverymoon. 
## Eleasis 8th day, 2nd tenday

Reaching Parnast, Orsik, Thistle, and Unla Kee recognize the castle as the Dragon's Breath floats past. It’s the same castle that buzzed them so many months ago when they went on a quest on the black road. That quest set Orsik on his peculiar journey of redemption and led to the 3HPP’s uncompromising hate towards the Lord’s Alliance and its head of secret police, the SEER. 
But that was not the only beginning. The castle was the home of Blagothkus, who triggered the chaos of the Ordning. Blagothkus had allied himself with Tiamat to destroy the world of the small people. The cult had used his tower to transport treasure until a group known as the First Faction stopped him. The First Faction then secured the castle from Blagothkus and a Dracolich. Again, how Blagothkus ended up with his castle is a mystery. 
As they float near the Castle, Delsephine asks, “Where is Brezzik?” 
Orsik looks at her, “He decided to look for work elsewhere as their play wasn’t going to have that rewrite we were hoping for.” 
And with that, she drops them on the upper tower of the castle. 
# Chapter 58 - Bob's Song
## Eleasis 8th day 2nd tenday 1490 
Standing on the Dragon’s Breath deck, about to land on the deck of the Cloud Giant Castle, Orsik becomes reflective.
“We seem to have collected a lot of enemies.”
Vardis nods.
But then, Unla Kee, to everyone’s surprise, reminds them that they have also collected a lot of Allies. “Well, here’s who we have cultivated. There is the new mayor of Triboar. There is Lord Zymorven. And  Tamalin Zoar, founder of Calling Horn, and her brother Hirax Zoar, High Captain of Everlund. We also have the SEER…”
“We tried to kill her,” grumbles Orsik.
“Unsuccessfully, and so we should add her to our friends list. And then there is Wallace and his dad. And then there is Queen Titania, and Prince Thornacious. And I think this Uncle Joon character is a friend.”
At the mention of his uncle, Thistle blanches. 
“And then there is the possibility of this fairy dragon hybrid that happens to be Thistle’s girlfriend?”  
Thistle is tempted to try and kill Unla Kee, who ignores the fairy but realizes that he still needs his curiously great ally. 
Orsik scowls, “Okay, then maybe I was too down on myself.”
Vardis turns to him, “The question is what to do about Silverymoon.” 
The group then toys with the idea of convincing the Uthgardtian Tribes that they were agents of Silverymoon and rejected the idea. They also consider whether to implicate the Harpers, but dismiss the idea. 
Orsik ponders a Zhentarim angle but discards it. 
Delsephine shouts, “So are you going?” 
Orsik looks down at the ground, grabs the ladder, and starts climbing. Thistle flies down. Unla Kee flies down with his 12-foot wings. Vardis admires his friend and the two flying half pints and proceeds down the rope ladder to the upper courtyard of Blagothkus’ castle. 
As he climbs down, he remembers the eerie song that Bob Saget sang. 
**Is this a bad pun you're hearing?**
**Opened your tomb, surprised you're appearing**
**Made you kill your jester, that was quite a blunder**
**Now prepare to face jokes that will truly thunder**
**Is this joke real, life is just a game?**
**You thought you were king, but power is a lame claim**
**Now you'll face puns of every kind, a neverending stream**
**From groaners to knock-knock jokes, they'll all be mean**
**So you think you can laugh it off?**
**You're trapped in this cycle, cough it off**
**No escape from these punchlines weak**
**For eternity, these dad jokes you'll speak**
**(Chorus)**
**Bohemian rhapsody is nothing compared**
**To the torture I've planned, jokes unfairly paired**
**Scaring children, making adults frown**
**This is your punishment, forever a clown**
**I see a small pun, of no consequence to some**
**But for you, my dear king, it's like a ton of plum**
**Your jester's ghost watches, a silent despair**
**As you butcher punchlines, filling the air**
**(Chorus)**
**Bohemian rhapsody is nothing compared**
**To the torture I've planned, jokes unfairly paired**
**Scaring children, making adults frown**
**This is your punishment, forever a clown**
**(Guitar Solo - filled with puns about famous guitarists)**
**So you think you can escape this hell?**
**No way, José, you're stuck in this cell**
**Forever condemned to tell jokes so bad**
**That even the dead will wish you were sad**
**(Chorus)**
**Bohemian rhapsody is nothing compared**
**To the torture I've planned, jokes unfairly paired**
**Scaring children, making adults frown**
**This is your punishment, forever a clown**
**(Outro)**
**You'll be telling these jokes for all eternity**
**A fitting end for your cruel history**
**So laugh it up, king, or cry it out loud.**
**Either way, you're stuck in this dad joke cloud**
Thistle

As Thistle flies down on Thuul’s back, he sees a collection of dead members of the Cult of the Dragon before him. 
“Good riddance,” he thinks. But as he gets closer, he realizes they aren’t dead. 
“Zombies!” he shouts. 
The 3HPP stop their descent, stare, and realize that they can just land out of reach of the slow moving undead near the iceberg top that protrudes through the upper courtyard. 
“Let’s go to the tower on the port side of the castle,” he suggests.
As he flies down, he sees that Delsephine has moved the Dragon’s Breath to drop Orsik and Vardis nearby. 
Unla Kee follows Thistle. Thistle notes that the mage wanted to cast cast fireball. 
The tower is ancient and appears to be crumbling. Ice has sealed the windows shut and cracks have formed in the walls and rooftop—a balcony of sculpted ice hugs one side of the tower, 75 feet above the base. The door at the base of the building has been smashed open and then boarded shut. 
Thistle enters the tower, and notices exsanguinated corpses of orcs. He flies down and inspects the bodies when Orsik shouts, “Vampires.” 
Thistle looks at the spot on the neck Orisk points at. He nods. 
Thistle, Orsik, and Vardis ponder their next move. Unla Kee has already made it. He’s flown to the upstairs balcony and entered the room. 
The fairy groans and follows him but peeks in, trying to be discreet. 
Thistle can see a half-elven moon elf vampire smiling at Unla Kee, like a cat smiling at a mouse. 
And the blood-sucker screams, “You can’t be alive. You’re an abomination.” 
The fairy is stunned and points at himself with his mouth open, “Me? Look, I just escaped the cult of the dragon …” he begins. 
She screams, “Don’t call that abomination that Severin did to the cult of the dragon, the cult of the dragon. That was a twisted, devilish plot by Zariel to eliminate Tiamat from her plane of Avernus. She was an annoying office mate.” 
The 3HPP nods in understanding. 
Vardis finally makes it up the stairs, “a friendly vampire?” 
“I am no such thing. I am Sandesyl Morgia.
She ignores the insult and turns to Thistle once more. Thistle succeeds in convincing her that she knows what she is talking about. 
“Severin hired Adimond to create a fey hybrid dragon.” 
“So you know where he is?” 
“No. And yes, I understand that Severin perverted everything I believed in. But I believe in making clean breaks. They decided to go their way, and I decided my way.” 
Thistle tries to get the vampire to reveal anything but is met with ignorance and indifference. The vampire wants to talk about the experiments. 
“Severin was successful, but it almost drove that fairy mad. She would transform between the two creatures. It was more a form of lycanthropy than a fusing of the two essences.” 
She pauses, “I think she looked like you.” Thistle blushes and describes the love of his life. She sighs, “Yes, it was her. Can you turn into a dragon?” 
Thistle tries and fails. 
She then changes topics, “This castle is owned by a certain cloud giant Blagothkus. I want you to know that I don’t care who owns the castle as long as there is a fresh supply of bodies to exsanguinate. I am happy to be the castellan and manage the savoir-vivre.” 

## Orsik
The dwarf is intrigued. “So as long as we provide enough bodies, you will leave us alone?” 
“Have you heard of Silverymoon?” asks Thistle. 
Orsik is surprised at the dark turn the fairy has taken. And then he remembers that the fey can be very dangerous. 
“Yes, a city full of pompous yits. Tasty pompous yits.” Sandesyl explains, “I just want a steady supply of sentient humanoids I can exsanguinate at my leisure. I want to hear their screams as they suffer and are in pain.” 

Orsik looks at his allies and is unsure if what he sees is indifference, interest, or fear.
“I like the screaming to be private, so I use my magic to silence the screams outside the tower.”
“How considerate,” Orsik mutters. 
Thistle then shifts the conversation. “You said the master of the tower is still here?” 
“Yes, and Blagothkus has been moping. It’s been weeks.”
“What about?” 
“It’s not like we have dinner together regularly. I wouldn’t mind dining on him,” she laughs. “A bit of vampire humor…” 
Orsik then remembers the story of Blagothkus. 

Eigeron and his father, Blagothkus, came to the Eye of Annam seeking wisdom. The divine oracle told them that a great upheaval would upset the balance of power in the world, allowing all giants to win the respect of their gods and bring glory to their race. The oracle told Blagothkus outright that he could never impress the gods enough to earn their favor, then urged Eigeron to step out from beneath his father’s “dark shadow.” Blagothkus was overcome with despair and envy. A terrible fight between father and son ensued, in which Blagothkus slew Eigeron. Blagothkus then retired to his castle to mourn.
Eigeron asked the heroes to avenge his death by killing his father. 
Orsik shrugs. 
And asks, “How did you become a vampire? And, like how many spawn do you have? 
She smiles and says, “That would be telling.” 
“We are asking…” 
She smiles, that smile of the hunter to the prey, “I think it’s important for you to know that should you accidentally think – “Oh, she’s an old helpless vampire obsessed with her necromantic research,” at that moment, there would be a vengeance that would make you wish that you had never opened this door, and had ever spoken to me.” 
Orsik nods in approval at the threat. 
Vardis then asks about the Ogres. She explains that they were part of her necromantic experiments after they were no longer viable as a food supply. 
Orsik tries to get her to have the zombies perform in his next play, but she refuses.
