She then explains that she has worked for Blagothkus for a long time. And then she rambles about Blagothkus. She shares, “He once tried to ally himself with the Cult of the Dragon against the little people, and unfortunately, that led to a group called the First Faction capturing control of the castle. The castle then crashed in the north. The First Faction captured the castle again. Then Blagothkus went to the Spine of the World to talk to his god, and when he came back, his son was dead. The First Faction had gotten rid of me before they left the north. It was an evil ploy. So I don’t know how Blagothkus got his castle back.”
As the 3HPP starts to leave the tower, she hands Orsik a book, “This is why I wanted to become a vampire.” He reads the title, “Dusk.” He sighs, as he remembers the young adult fantasy series about vampires and girls. 
He’s about to poke fun at her, and then he notices that Sandesyl is the kind of creature that goes from 1 to 50 level of anger in an instant. And he thinks the better of it. 
## 3HPP
With that the group heads over to the tower in the stern of the castle, the chambers of Blagothkus. 

When they arrive at the tower, they discover that it’s in a state of shambles. And inside are a pair of ogre zombies. At first the 3HPP want to ignore the zombies, but the zombies do not want to ignore them. These zombies are wearing a distinctive plumed helmets and its’ obvious they are not under the vampire’s control. 
 It takes a moment, but the zombies are soon all killed. 
# Chapter 59 - Enter Adimond. Exeunt Harshnag

## Eleasis 8th day of the 2nd tenday of 1492

Blagothkus sat silently in the control room.
The situation was clear; a vampire, [Sandesyl](https://www.worldanvil.com/w/the-forgotten-realms-kostadis/a/sandesyl-morgia-person-1), had been treating this castle as her domain. The vampire had been content to let Blagothkus travel around the world, engaging in whatever plots he might be interested in as long as he supplied a steady stream of victims. 
But more food was needed when Blagothkus got into a funk and parked the castle in its current location north of Parnastded. Sandesyl reacted by eating through all of Blagothkus’ minions. 
Over the intervening months, that supply of victims had been slowly and brutally harvested.
The 3HPP clearly understood that the only living things that could be eaten were themselves, Blagothkus, and a pair of Ogres named Worug and Tax. 
As they stood outside the tower, they could feel the eyes of the Vampire staring at them through the unseeing eyes of her zombie spawns. 
And now Blagothkus introduces a new character to this menagerie: his wife, who apparently controls the castle. 
As Thistle enters, he notices a staircase of ice leading from the tower’s ground floor to a higher chamber, the walls and ceiling of which sparkle and glitter. Jewels are everywhere in the room. He sees glowing moonstones, thumb-sized emeralds, shining silvery mithral wands, and strange spheres covered in turquoise and gold, and dozens of copper levers and golden spheres embedded in the walls. After a moment, the walls seem to disappear, providing a perfect aerial view in all directions, as if there were no castle and no cloudstuff around. More than a dozen glowing white runes wink into existence, drifting about the room like snowflakes.
The cloud giant Blagothkus is sitting cross-legged, looking harried, tired, unshaven, and disheveled. 
He points at the runes and screams, “SHE WON’T LET THE CASTLE GO ANYWHERE!” And then dramatically waves his hand through a rune, “See?” 
And with that, the voice of Blagothkus’ wife, Escarlotta, booms - “I am done with him.” 
Vardis asks, “Do you want us to drive?” 
“No, I want him dead. He had this insane plan to help the Dragons bring Tiamat, and then the idea was that when they failed, the little people would be so weakened that the giants could rule. And so he goes to the Eye of the All-Father and kills his son. He couldn’t just let his son kill him. Amn declared him unworthy, and he proved how unworthy he was.” She snivels, “And what makes it worse is that we aren’t one of the cloud giant families making a play for rulership.” 

Thistle asks, “And who is?”
“Countess Sansuri. She’s a vain, glorious, greedy, and ugly cloud giantess who lives in Lym Armaal, her castle. The Countess is untethered from reality, surrounded by enemies who yearn to topple her. And so she is full of herself. HER family head magical artifacts when Ostoria fell, and SHE will find them.” 
Blagothkus is about to say something, and she shouts, “Instead of going and playing your games with dragons, you should have seized the maps from the Countess!” 
The giant stretches his hands at the voice as if to say, “See what I am dealing with?” 
The fairy asks, “Would you like our help getting rid of him?” 
“No,” shrieks Escarlotta, “I want him dead as all of the floating icons surround Blagothkus and arrange in an arrow pointing at him.
The 3HPP is intrigued and discovers that Escarlotta is furious at Blagothkus for trapping her in this console. 
But there is something more, senses Thistle. 
“Let’s say we help you kill your husband here.” 
As Blagothkus rouses himself and prepares for combat, Vardis rushes to stand before him and says,  “Hypothetically! It’s just a hypothetical.” 
“The problem is that if we kill Blagothkus, the vampire will come in, which won’t work for us. 
Escarlotta starts to become petulant. “I don’t care. Maybe it would be better when he’s a vampire. He never had a soul anyways.” 
Vardis whispers, in a tone to soothe tempers, “That was a mean thing to say…” 
Blagothkus, realizing he finally has someone on his side in the endless arguments with his wife, sits down again. 
At this point, the 3HPP debates what they want and need to do. Unla Kee wants the castle because he’s tired of walking. The group learns that Escarlotta hates the vampire. But Syndesia kept the castle clean, and no rats were on board. However, at some point, when Escarlotta realizes that the 3HPP will not kill Blagothkus because of their fear of fighting the vampire, she screams at Blagothkus for not having gotten rid of the Vampire when Escarlotta had told him to or, as she put it - “Bring Tiamat into the world, he’s got time for,  but dealing with a Vampire in his house? No. Not important for the big shot!” 
Finally, the 3HPP decides to get Escarlotta to fly this castle. Killing the vampire is not something they can do at this time, so Thistle fills her in on their adventures. He makes a particular point of talking about the Fire Giants and mentions Duke Zalto’s ambitions and his collection of artifacts. 
Blagothkus and Escarlotta laugh when they hear that Duke Zalto is making a play for rulership - “Fire Giants? How absurd.” Then they proceed to crack jokes about Fire Giants and how silly they are, but the jokes don’t translate into common. 
Thistle is pleased, “laughter is the basis of any good relationship.” And so he ventures to ask for her help. 
There is a moment of silence. And then she explains her terms - “You have to kill Countess Sansuri. I hate her. She is a cow!” 
Vardis laughs, “So we are doing body shaming now?” 
“No. That was uncalled for. She’s an arrogant, selfish, horrible, absurd creation that should be dead.” 
Unfortunately, that then turns into Esclarlotta screaming at Blagothkus for his actions that led to them not making a play for the rulership of the Giants. He explains that it was never in the cards, and then she explains that if he had done the decent thing and let Eigeron kill him, maybe she would be queen now.
Sensing this could go on for hours, Thistle asks about the Fire Giants. That causes them both to laugh. Escarlotta agrees she will help but points out she doesn’t know where they are. 
When Thistle announces they can find that information at the Oracle, the castle starts to move.
“Well, why didn’t you lead with that?” 
As the castle starts to fly, there is a knock on the door. 
Thistle flies out and sees a vampire under an umbrella to avoid being burned by the sun. It’s a spawn dressed in the purple of the cult. 
“It would appear that the castle is moving. And our mistress would like to know where we would stop for a meal?” 
Thistle shouts, “Silverymoon.” 
“There are so many different establishments between here and Silverymoon. Why that one in particular?” 
“Because we have a vendetta against House Margastar.” 
The vampire is stunned. It says, “Well, that is a surprise.” And it leaves. 
As the night settles, Sandesyl stands in the courtyard surrounded by her zombies and several of her spawns. 
“Did I hear correctly someone has a vendetta against Margastar?” 
Thistle shouts, “Yes, that would be me!” 
“Come outside so we can talk about it.” 
“How about not?” 
“You don’t trust me?” 
“I can hear you just fine from inside here.” 
“I am so disappointed that you don’t trust me.” She smiles evilly and taps her fingers against her legs for a few minutes. “I am not interested in fighting a war with the Margastar’s. Asmodeus protects them. And I refuse to upset the archfiend’s plans. Only an insane person would do that.” 
Unla Kee shouts, “An insane plan? Cool!” 
The moments pass, and finally, Sandesyl suggests a village of humans along the way. Thistle counters with a village of orcs, and then they both remember the Uthgartd barbarians of Flint Rock. 
Sandesyl agrees and waits patiently for the Castle to arrive at Flint Rock. 

Seeing the deal that has been made, Delsephine notes the 3HPP, telling them that Klauth wishes them the best of luck in their pursuit of ending the war of the giants. 
## Night of Eleasis 1st to morning of 2nd day of 3rd tenday 1492
Sandesyl’s vampires find a collection of Uthgardtian nomads mourning their despoiled artifact. They are quickly captured and brought to the castle that has settled some miles away. 
The vampires leave convincing evidence and a survivor to spread the tale that this was done by monsters under the control of House Margastar in service of Silverymoon. 
Over the next several days, until the 3HPP arrives at the Eye of the All-Father, the sound of screams of horror of the victims of Sandesyl and her vampires echo through the castle. 
## Eleint 3rd day of 1st tenday of 1492
On the morning of the 3rd day of the 3rd tenday of Eleasis of 1490, the castle arrives at the Eye of the All-Father. The 3HPP march, hurrying past the domed entrance and the six pillars carved like giants, down the hallway to get to the main temple of Annam, where Harshnag awaits. 
They explain that they have a castle, and when he asks about Blagothkus, they are non-commital. 
He picks up Thrymm’s great axe, touches the Ise run on the portal, and walks into the Eye. 
There, the 3HPP meets Eigeron, who asks about his father. When he learns he is not dead, but his castle is here, Eigeron goes mad and tries to possess one of the heroes. 
Vardis invokes the power of Corellon, and the monster is turned.
Sensing that they don’t have time, the heroes deposit each artifact in turn, and the oracle reveals the location of the leaders of the giants who have taken the mantle to rule over all giants. 
For the hill giant chief, the Oracle says - 
“Travel south over tree, hill, and vale. Let the great river be your guide. On one of its eastern arms, you’ll find Grudd Haug — the den of the hill giant chief. The conch you seek is close to her.”
For the stone giant Thane, the Oracle says - 

“Travel southeast, o’er lands high and low. Cross the great forest to the gray peaks and search for Deadstone Cleft, a canyon of the stone giant thane. The conch you seek is in her possession.”
For the Frost Giant Jarl, the Oracle says - 

“Travel west, o’er mountains, to the Sea of Moving Ice. Among the glaciers, you’ll find Svardborg — the berg of the frost giant jarl. The conch you seek rests on his icy throne.”
For the Fire Giant Duke, the Oracle says - 

“Travel east, o’er mountains and snow, to distant spires. There, on a cold mountainside, you’ll find a village of yakfolk and, below that, Ironslag — the forge of the fire giant duke. The conch you seek is in his quarters.”

For the Cloud Giant Countess, the Oracle says - 

“Travel southeast to the untamed moors, and look to the sky for Lyn Armaal — the castle of the cloud giant countess. The conch you seek is in a secret chest. Search the highest spire!”
Before the turning of the ghost ends, the heroes march out of the portal, leaving Eigeron howling for his revenge and marching into the main temple. 
And there Thistle sees a wondrous sight, Adminond Kroskas.
Thistle prepares to attack, but Adminond Kroskas sees Harshnag and transforms into an Ancient Blue Dragon. 
Harshnag shouts, “I will distract the dragon. You must go and save the realms.” 
Thistle and Unla Kee hesitate. Thistle wants revenge. Unla Kee sees an epic battle that he knows he will win. 
Vardis and Orsic start to run toward the exit, but seeing their allies stop, they linger momentarily. 
Harshnag realizes that they aren’t planning to leave. 
Seeing his warnings fall on deaf ears, Harshnag swings his greataxe at the statue of Annam the All-Father and chips it. The entire temple shudders. The frost giant scowls at his enemy and the 3HPP. He dodges the dragon and strikes the statue again, breaking off a large chunk. This act of desecration causes cracks in the ceiling, and the mountain begins to fall around you. “Flee!” yells Harshnag. “Your fate lies elsewhere.” 
Seeing no other choice, the heroes run. The Ancient Blue Dragon seems uninterested in the fleeing humans and attacks Harshnag. As the heroes rush through the hallway to their castle, they see the hallway and the temple where the two monsters fight collapse behind them. 

# Chapter 60 - Enter Blagothkus

## Eleasis 8th day 2nd tenday 1492

After the death of the Ogre Zombies in the stern tower that was Blagothkus’ lair, Vardis and Orsik engage in a bit of intellectual debate about the nature of free will. 
“We have a Bob problem.” 
“No kidding.” 
“He’s evil.” 
“Well, he could choose to change?” asks Orsik.
“That’s unfortunately not how the outerplanes work. Creatures that are native to the prime material plane have agency. They can choose to be good or evil. It’s pure and rabid planism, I get it. But it also happens to be how the Universe works. It’s why Tieflings are always viewed as – evil – and Aasimar are always viewed as – good. Folks assume that a Tiefling is intrinsically evil because of their relationship to the outer planes.” 
“So the prime material plane is superior to other planes? Wow. That’s rabid planism.” 
“No, I didn’t say that.”
“But you said free will only exist in the prime material plane.” 
“And many outer planar entities view that viewpoint as bizarre and awful. It’s a bit of prime-material-plane bias.” 
“I see. That’s why clerics hate the undead because they lack agency?” 
The conversation becomes tedious, so Unla Kee and Thistle explore the rest of the tower. Unla Kee becomes invisible, and Thistle sneaks. They climb up to the second floor. The room looks like a series of battles have taken place. There are scorch marks from magical fires, gouges on the bed from swords, broken arrows strewn across the room, and frozen blood pools on the ground. The walls of this room are sculpted with icy murals depicting cloud giants riding giant birds. An enormous, smashed bed with a headboard of ice sculpted to resemble clouds dominates the room. The headboard has taken heat damage and is partly melted. Chunks have been broken off and are on the ground. Bear furs are heaped upon the bed, and two large wooden chests rest at the bed’s foot. 
Standing in the middle of the room is a large Frost Giant skeleton. Thistle stares, confused, “Why would a Frost Giant be in a Cloud Giant’s castle?” 
Unla Kee remembers a class he took, or was it a story he was told, or was it a vision of the future? And says, “This castle played a prominent role in the final defeat of Tiamat. At the time, Frost Giants were staffing the castle, not Cloud Giants.” 
Thistle is now confused, “How did Blagothkus get control of the castle again?” 
And then both realized that perhaps the friendly vampire wasn’t as pleasant as she appeared. Maybe she was the actual proprietor of the castle. Possibly, the Vampire wanted a base in the sky that they could use to conduct necromantic experiments. And what exactly was inside the glacier? 
Thistle quietly empties the chests of the treasure of Blagothkus. And the two of them, unnoticed, return to Orsik and Vardis, who are still debating the complex issue of agency, planism, and whether free will is good or bad. 
With the treasure in tow, Unla Kee shouts, “Screw Bob and his play.” He points to the haul, “This is enough to retire on.” 
They all stare at him. 
They exit the stern tower and sneak on the starboard side of the castle past a room that contains a Frost Giant. 
While everyone else debates what to do, an invisible Unla Kee stares. 
Unla Kee asks himself, “Are they immune to psychic magic?” He remembers that he was drunk when that topic was covered, and trusting his gut casts psychic lance. 
The giant skeleton is immobilized. And then promptly destroyed. 
Unla Kee stares at the dead creature, smiles, and declares – “it doesn’t matter what I thought I knew. I know the future, and the future said the spell worked.” 
Inside the room, there is nothing but detritus. A battle tore the room apart, and nobody came by to clean it. 
Now, in the central outer courtyard at the aft of the cloud castle, they see a staircase that leads to a second tower. The door is barred heavily shut. 
Orisk casts knock, and the door opens. 
Two gaunt-looking ogres scream in terror. The dwarven fighter stares at them. He is confused; everyone else is a zombie, but these are alive. 
He ponders momentarily and says – “do you speak common?” 
One ogre with a scar on his face asks, “My name is Worug. Do you have food?” 
And Orsik responds, “You’re not undead.” 
The other ogre with a bandaged right-hand says, “My name is Tak, and we are hungry.” 
Orsik turns to the ranger, staring at some gnawed ogre bones. Thistle squirms and says, “Goodberries?” 
After consuming several, the ogres are looking less feeble and not as hungry. Worug tries to hug Thistle and says, “No, dude, bad touching!” 
Orsik then asks, “Why aren’t you dead?” 
And Worug says, “It turns out that if you don’t let a Vampire in, it can not enter your house.” 
“And I guess all the other ogres let the vampires in?” 
“Yes, they were stupid. Didn’t keep the door closed. So this purple lady says, "I am the big boss lady. Let me in. And they say sure, and then she comes in, and there is chaos, mayhem, and blood. And well, we said no.” 
Unla Kee infers that the Ogre’s and Blagothkus’ chambers are safe from the Vampire. 
The ogres then continue, “The crazy vampire lady killed everyone, some quickly and some slowly. As for Blagothkus, he was crying and saying that he had killed his son. Frankly, I don’t understand what is going on. But the Vampires don’t’ come out during the day, so we use that to get food.”
The ogres explain the horrors of the last few months. “After Blagothkus came to Parnast, he’s been unwilling to move and is holed up in the control room. The vampire got hungry and decided to feast on Blagothkus’ retinue. When the remaining creatures decided to attack the vampires, the vampires turned the dead into undead so that we couldn’t move during the day. The last few days, there’s been a bit of an ogre and elf game, where we try to sneak around to get food and not get noticed.”
At this point, a bored Thistle asks, “What’s in it for us?” 
Vardis turns to him and asks, “When did you get so - Orsik?” 
Worug, the Ogre with a scratch on his face, says, “I have a secret. If you get us off this castle, we will tell.” 
Vardis shrugs, “It could be that one of you likes the other.” 
Tak, the Ogre with the bandage on his right hand, coughs a little too eagerly, “That’s ridiculous.” 
Worug looks at Tak, “Why didn’t you say something?” 
Then, there is a long, awkward pause. 
Tak turns to the group, “If you promise to get me and Worug off this castle, we will tell you. I promise.” 
Unla Kee shouts, “Sure, I’ll gamble.” 
Worug explains that inside the glacier the Castle is built around is a den of a dracolich, Glazhael. That white dracolich was originally the steed of Talis the White. The First Faction killed Glazhael. The dragon, now a dracolich, tried to capture the castle but was led on a wild goose chase to find some artifact. When the return of Tiamat was thwarted, Blagothkus took control of the castle and she came back here. 

Orsik whistles, “A vampire, a dracolich, and an army of undead. How badly do we want this castle?” 
The group sighs as Unla Kee puts on a sorrowful face. 
Tak puts his arm around Worug’s as they leave and declares, “We won’t leave this tower. Come here if you need rest and safety from the vampire!” 
As Orsik walks away, “Why would the vampire kill us?” 
Unla Kee remarks, “because the vampire might conclude it needs three, not four heroes.” 
Thistle remarks, “I am sleeping on the ground, gentlemen.” 
Vardis wonders if he can get Delsephine to come and pick them up. 
As the group descends from the stairs that lead to Worug and Tak’s tower, they arrive at the barracks on the starboard side of the castle on the upper deck. They see what Worug and Tak described: a scene of dead bodies, chaos, and mayhem. The room has been torn apart, and several ogres are still in a state of decomposition. 
Across the upper courtyard on the port side of the ship, they see another Frost Giant Skeleton in one of three guest rooms. And once again, Unla Kee incapacitates it with a psychic lance. But his allies cannot kill it this time, and it strikes back with a Freezing Stare. Unla Kee barely survives. The creature, however, is killed. 
To the aft of the guest rooms is a room that housed kobols. From the look, the Vampire used them as a snack room. The kobolds are tied to their beds and are dead. The vampires used them as a living blood bank,  slowly killing each kobold. The look of horror on the poor, miserable creatures would rend the heart of any hero who cared. 
Vardis remarks, “maybe we kill the Vampire?”
Orsik shuts that idea down, “We need the vampire.” 
And with that marches to the control room at the aft of the cloud castle and knocks on the door. 
A male giant’s voice booms, “I will not let you, Sandesyl, or any of your hellspawn enter my tower. Do you hear that?” 
“We’re not vampires!” 
The giant voice shouts, “That’s what she said last time!” 
The voice goes silent, and suddenly, a periscope appears over the top of the tower of the control room. 
The voice shouts, “Yes, you are not Vampires. The periscope works with mirrors, and I could see you.”
 Now, with a much calmer voice, it asks, “What are you doing here, and what do you want?” 
“Have you spoken to the Oracle?” 
The giant starts to cry, “That’s how it all began! Me talking to the Oracle!” 
“When did you talk to the Oracle? The chamber hadn’t been opened in a thousand years.” 
The giant laughs, “Harshnag! You must have talked to Harshnag! It’s a standing joke. Harshnag brings humans and claims that this is the first time in a thousand years that anyone has been there.” 

Vardis is aghast, “but we had to solve a puzzle and almost got killed. Items we needed were strewn across the dungeon.” 
“Harshnag likes to make things interesting for any little people. He hides the items he needs in the temple. He thinks they are worthy if little people can figure out and survive. If they can’t do well, they are unworthy.” 
Orsik interrupts them and says, “Okay, but why not go to the Oracle now?” 
“Because my, she won’t let us go!” 
“The Dracolich?”
The giant scoffs, “that interloper? No. My wife.” 

# Chapter 61 - The Yakfolk
Escarlotta, the sentient control center of Blagothkus’ Cloud Castle, “you need practice. I will not have you attack the Countess and lose. And I love a good love story; we will help Thistle find his love, Leala.” 
The other members of the 3HPP need clarification. They had hoped to go after Sansuri first, but Thistle had used his charming wit and sob story to sway the giantess. 
As the ship moved from the collapsing Eye of the All-Father, she shouted, “AT LEAST SOMEONE LOVES SOMEONE IN THIS SHIP!” 
Thistle smiles and watches through the viewport as the ship moves silently across the clouds. It’s daylight, so he steps outside, confident that the Vampire will not try and kill him. 
He tries to remember what he knows about Ancient Blue Dragons, but he only remembers blue fairies and blueberries. He doesn’t remember anything about blue dragons. 
Meanwhile, Escarlotta shouts inside the control center, " Does anyone have any ability to tell me exactly where I am supposed to go?”  
They share the information they have from Oracle. Three days later, they get closer. 

## 7th day of the of the first tenday of Eleint 1492

Escarlotta once again asks, “Anything more precise?” 

Orsik turns to Unla Kee, “Diviner?” 
The halfling shrugs. 
Vardis thinks, “Perhaps my god will help. And prays for divine intervention.” 
Correllon doesn’t listen, or maybe he does, and that’s why Vardis is somehow covered in bird shit. 
Vardis wipes himself clean, “not exactly endearing me to you right now.” 
But Thistle, still outside, spots a village on a mountain spire and shouts, “It’s there!” 
Up ahead, he spots the great dungeon forge of Ironslag. He remembers from Uncle Joon that the dwarves of Silvermarsh call it Mount Hamarhaast (“hammer of ashes”). Carved into the base of a 500-foot-high mountainside cliff are a pair of 50-foot-tall adamantine doors—that Uncle Joon says are airtight valves sealed by ancient magic. Atop the cliff is a village, the village of Yakfolk. 
As they approach, Escarlotta asks, “Where shall I stop?” 
Thistle tries to argue for the subtle approach and points out that there is a stairway they can use to climb.  But Orsik will have none of that and insists on what he calls a “show of force!” 
His enthusiasm convinces Escarlotta again. 
The ship glides over the village at the top of a mountain. 


The castle's front gate opens, and the heroes climb down and stand in the middle of the yurt nearest the iron gate. 
A young, frightened moon elf comes to greet them and says,  “Chief Kartha-Kaya would consider it beyond rude not to come and eat in her yurt!” 
Thistle stares at her and is convinced she is afraid for them, but he cannot say. 
At first, Thistle tries to avoid the meal, but the social graces he learned in the Fey court impose specific protocols on him. 
Orsik and Vardis are suspicious and so cast wards against poison and enter. 
Built on high ground in the middle of the Yakfolk village is the 60-foot-diameter hut with a beaded curtain entrance in the northeast wall, facing an outdoor campfire that is the Chief’s home. The hut's interior is one large chamber dominated by a shallow, 10-foot-diameter, circular pit. The pit contains a campfire that keeps the hut warm. Toward the back of the hut are piles of cushions, baskets laden with foodstuffs, and painted clay vessels filled with water, wine, and broth. Between the fire pit and the south wall is an ornately engraved bronze gong and a wooden mallet with a head padded with yak hide. The gong and the mallet hang from a wooden frame.
The food is spread across the hut and consists of fresh vegetables, cooked meats, and bread. 
Orsik starts to eat some, expecting it to be poisoned, but he is pleasantly surprised by how good and unpoisonous the food is. 
He nods to the group, and the rest of 3HPP dig in. The monotony of the cleric’s created food and water has begun to grate on everyone’s nerves. 
When the meal is completed, the Chief insists they must perform a cleansing ritual. 
Thistle and Vardis try to object, but the Chief is insistent and suggests that ill omens are likely if no food is eaten. 

Orsik will have none of it and stand outside. The Chief is disappointed and makes some snide remarks about dwarves. Then, he holds Thistle's left hand and Vardis's right hand. Imberu, his first hand, holds Thistle’s right hand and Unla Kee’s left. Nahala, his other wife, holds Unla Kee’s right hand and Vardis’ left, closing the circle. 
The chief begins to hum, and the heroes feel the cleansing. They also sense the Yakfolk minds trying to take them over. 
Orsik notices something amiss and warns his friends, who warned,  are now better able to repel the attempted possession. 
The failed attempt to control the minds of the 3HPP triggers a free-for-all melee. 

# Chapter 62 - Into Ironslag
The Yakfolk are not as easily killed as some of the enemies that the 3HPP have fought. There are many of them, and they are quite hardy. Worse, they have hostages making the use of the area of effect spell, the Fireball, dicey. 
And to make it worse, the Yalkfolk have priests that can summon elemental. And an earthen monster strikes, grabbing Thistle in his fist and tossing him against the ground like a rag doll, holding onto his body. Then he slams the puny fairy again. 
As that is going on, Unla Kee computes the space needed to hit the most number of enemies. “Four,” he shouts and the room explodes with a fireball. 
One of the Yakfolk screams in agony as it dies in the blast. 
Another seeing the blistering ball of fire, fires some arrows at Unla Kee, and hits with a glancing blow. The halfling smiles and says, “I am – after all – Unla Kee… For you.” 
Thistle manages to squirm from the Earth Elemental and uses his longbow kills one of the Yakfolk priests with an arrow to the throat. The other arrow wounds the other priest, but not enough to send her to whatever afterlife she may think she deserves. 
Thuul, squirms past the earth elemental to strike at the still living priest. The monstrous earthen extra-dimensional creature punches him, as he scoots by, and although there is a slight crunching sound, the drake survives and scalds the priestess with his acid.
She screams and dies. 
Unla Kee, who has recently learned the dark arts of Cloud Kill plots the trajectory of the spell. He mutters to himself, “you are getting in my way.” He sees two placements, one which gets the elemental and one which doesn’t but gets an extra Yakfokl. “What did my teachers say?” he mutters. “Right, immune to poison.” 
And soon a billowing poisonous cloud streams from his fingers and three of the half-human and half-Yak are hidden inside. Two thuds are heard, and some choking. 
Outside of the chief’s tent, Orsik is fighting the chief. Whom he kills. 
Thistle decides to try and kill the Earth Elemental who showed him so little respect. 
Slam! Slam! 
And the poor fairy is once again slammed against the ground. 
The cleric of Correlon seeing Thistle getting brutalized, uses his mace of terror. The elemental, is frightened, of what is unclear, and begins to burrow deep into the ground. 

And just then the last of the Yakfolk trapped in the billowing cloud of poison, dies with a thud. 
The tent is quiet. 
At this point one of the elves speaks, “I am Princess Halani Meliame, and this is Jevin and Vandar. We are from the Moonwood. The Yakfolk captured us. If you bring us to our homes, there is a great reward.”
Unla Kee turns to Orsik, “We made allies!” 
Thistle, who really doesn’t want to accumulate more enemies, interrogates the princess and learns that she was captured by the Yakfolk who had performed the ritual of possession on several of her guards. 
The halfling wizard seeing the profit says, “We’ve got a flying castle. Just climb the rope to the top level, and don’t go anywhere where there is no bright sunlight.”
“There are some vile creatures in our sky castle that we have not yet had a chance to get rid of,” offers Thistle. 
Before she climbs up the rope, Thistle asks her, “Any giants near by?”
She pauses, and explains – “Yes past the doorway to the north. There is an elevator that takes you down into the Giant’s home. Oh and another thing, the Yakfolk used their ritual to take over some dwarves from Citadel Adbar, and kill the dwarven king.” 
Thistle then wonders about the political situation in this part of the world and asks the Princess, “How do the Moon Elves feel about Silverymoon?”
The Princess expresses her bitter frustration that Silverymoon betrayed the Silver Marches in their hour of need. 
Thistle mutters, “I know!” 
And she smiles, and explains that the era of Silverymoon’s dominance has ended. Halani looks tired as she mutters, “They used to be a beacon of hope and light, with courageous and heroic leaders of society. But then they abandoned the Silvery March for their own selfish needs and exposed us to the raids of the hordes. Our people can no longer support or rely on the support of such fair weather allies. A new organization will protect the people of this world.”
“You wish to establish, A new world order?” the fairy asks. 
The Princess realizes she has said too much, “I have been in prison for some time, and I don’t know. And I am not sure where things are moving to.” She stares at the dead Yakfolk who had tormented her, and says angrily, “I would not be surprised if the agents of the Silverymoon put the Yakfolk up to their plots.” 

Vardis says convincingly, “This would not surprise us at all.” 
After talking a short rest, the group heads towards the door that leads down into the Fire Giant’s domain. 
The door is 20 foot tall, made of riveted iron plates and handles 9 feet above the floor. Even the Pint finds these doors too tall. 
After some discussion, Thistle flies to the handle, and the rest push the door open, which thanks to their combined might opens easily. 
Beyond the door is a portcullis, that Vardis lifts open. 
Unla Kee mocks him, “You should become a fighter.” 
Vardis stares at him, and remembers that the clerics of Corellon Latherian have to deal with a god that has trust issues. Ever since his first consort betrayed him, anything but true loyalty is a source of anxiety and affects the relationship. And for a person like Vardis who is already on the outs, a further demonstration of his lack of loyalty may provoke his god. 
Past the portcullis is a cold, dark and vast chamber with a 50’ high ceiling, and an 80’ diameter, 500’ deep shaft in the middle of the floor. An iron rig is bolted to the pit’s circular rim. Suspended from this rig by iron chains is a 60’ diameter circular platform made from oak beams held together by iron bands and rivets.
As the 3HPP stare, the notice that the platform ascends and descends the shaft constantly, powered by a wood water wheel located behind an opening in the southeast wall. 
Thistle hides behind some boxes and observes the platform’s movement. He notices that the  platform makes a series of 15 stops on its way up and down the shaft, each stop lasting 30 seconds. After 15 minutes, it is either all the way down, or all the way up. 
After some discussion, the group decides to look around for another way to go down. They find a staircase in an adjacent room. 
Thistle turns to the 3HPP and reminds them of something Uncle Joon talked about, “Ironslag is at the base of a 500 foot high mountainside cliff. The entrance is a pair of 50’ tall adamantine doors. The doors are airtight valves sealed by magic.’ 
Orsik turns to him, “so this is the only way in?” 
“Probably?” 
The descent into the mines is tedious and slow going. The first few levels are a maze of rough hewn tunnels having an occasional upward or downward slope. Orsik, whose experience in mines, comes in handy, guides the 3HPP deeper into the cave. 
The dwarven fighter thinks, “crappy construction, poor workmanship.” 
After descending some 500 feet, where nothing could be heard, Unla Kee hears the distinct dialect of rock gnomes. 
A strange hissing voice shouts, “don’t fool around! Keep working!” 
“Why aren’t the giants doing the work? Would be way more efficient,” Thistle wonders aloud. 
Thistle and Vardis misremember the little information they have about giants. 
“They suck,” mutters Vardis. 
“Not only do they suck but they suck in a very particular way – they are lazy!” adds Thistle. 
“That does make them relatable, though.”
Orsik turns to the group, “I can see two fire salamanders guarding dwarves and gnomes. We will sneak up and attack.” 
Thistle, hides behind Orsik who doesn’t notice him. Orsik is silent as a mouse. Unla Kee makes nary a sound.
Vardis, on the other hand, could wake up the dead. 
Orsik growls, “why did we bring you here?” 
And then casts Expeditious Retreat, catches up to one Salamander and strikes it with his sword. 
The heat of the Salamander’s body scorches the dwarf. 
Just then Thistle remembers something that Uncle Joon had said. It wasn’t about their fire damage, or their cold vulnerability but that they are really good smiths. 
Unla Kee steps forward and banishes the Fire Salamander furthest from the group. The creature doesn’t stand a chance, given Unla Kee’s manipulation of portents and fates. 

# Chapter 63 - Casing Ironslag
With the far Salamander banished, Orsik struck the one within reach. And kills it. 
Vardis rushes in, “DIE! SALAMANDERS!” 
Orsik shakes his head. 
Turning to the newly freed dwarves and gnomes, Orsik tries to get them to help. They all express their desire to flee. 
When Thistle points out they possess a cloud castle, the dwarves and gnomes shout, “Huzzah!” 
Orsik turns to him, “Really?” 
Thistle shrugs and then says – “Just be careful. Stay out of the dark places.” 
“And if someone asks to come in, just say no,” mutters Orsik. 
Finally, one of the Dwarves sees through the innuendo, “There are vampires in the castle?” 
“Only one Vampire and many spawn,” corrects Vardis. 
The dwarves and gnomes decide that going down the stairs is a safer bet and thank their saviors. 
One gnome, however, chooses to stay and introduces herself as Wiri Fleagol. “I have been here for quite some time.”
“Why? Did they take over your village or something? Or did you do something to deserve this?” asks Vardis. 
Wiri ignores the taunt, “Does anyone deserve to be locked up in chains mining for giants?” 
Turning to Thistle, she squints and says, “You are a fairy. I will help you. I know things about it down below. And I will do it for a price.” 
“Everyone wants a cut,” moans Orsik. 
“Money to buy a house in Silverymoon,” taunts Vardis. 
“I don’t want a house there. They are nothing but a bunch of bigoted, self-important, righteous bastards. Do you know what happened? I’ll tell you what happened: when the Orc tribes invaded, Silverymoon chose not to help the Silver Marches. The Dragons weakened them!” 
“Do you want vengeance?” 
The gnome recoils, “No, there are possibly innocent gnomes.” 
“Possibly living.” 
With that, Thistle explains the sordid story of Bob and the play in Silverymoon. However, he leaves out his involvement and, more importantly, makes it sound like House Margaster is to blame. 
But Wiri doesn’t believe a word he says. 
She then turns to Thistle and says, “I can tell you about what’s ahead.” 


“The rail tracks running through these tunnels end at a pair of wooden barriers in the first room of Ironslag proper. Three pillars of black basalt support the ceiling. The bucket chain passes through gaps in the western and eastern walls, its iron buckets dangling a few inches above the floor. A tunnel leading west connects to an iron gantry (18) that overlooks the foundry (34). It’s the job of the enslaved people to keep putting ore into the buckets. There are four dwarves with ankle chains. A fire salamander stands watch.” 
“From there, you go west to the Foundry. This area is scorching, and you will suffer extreme heat if you linger here. A rusted iron gantry clings to the north, west, and south walls 50 feet above the floor of the main foundry, which echoes with the sound of clanging metal, rattling chains, whooshing bellows, and bubbling molten iron. The ceiling looms 40 feet above the gantry. Cable towers support the bucket chain that runs near the path of the gantry.” 
“From there, you go through a southeast entrance to the Great Wheel (20). A fireplace in the middle of the north wall keeps this area warm and lit. An enormous wheel made of iron gears and wooden spokes dominates the room. The wheel is rigged to the bucket chain that travels throughout the forge. Turning the great wheel are ten shield dwarves and ten humans (all [commoners](https://www.dndbeyond.com/monsters/16829-commoner)) with iron manacles around their ankles. If you stop the wheel, everyone will come quickly to find out why.” 
The Giants' prisoners' sleeping quarters are south of that room. Iron portcullises seal off this long, foul-smelling hall. Three black basalt pillars support the ceiling, and thirteen 10-foot-tall iron cages line the walls. A padlock hangs from each cage door.
Thistle asks, “Can they fight?” 
“No, they are exhausted and dehydrated from the heat.” 
She then holds her hand, “That will be 20 gold.” 
Orsik shouts, “What a hard bargain you drive.” 
The gnome is puzzled, “Did I ask for too little.” 
“Nope, that was our maximum.” 
She glares at them, “I am beginning to sense that you are not the kind of guys I want to have a deep personal relationship with.” 
“Would you rather be on our good or bad side?” asks a mischievous Vardis. 
“Point taken.” 
She’s about to leave when she says, “I could help you?” 
Orsik convinces her that it will not end well. But Orsik can’t resist asking, “What will you do with the 20 gold? Buy a nice dinner?” 
She glares, “If I am going to tell you about what I am going to do with my money, you need to tell me about your hero.” 
Orsik declares, “My grandfather.” 
She smiles, “Well, I plan to buy a farm and never spend another day underground. Do you have any suggestions?” 
“Not Silverymoon,” says Thistle. 
“Yartar and Triboar are nice,” says Orsik genteelly. 
She pauses and thinks, “I think I’ll try them out. And if you are ever there, come find me.” 
Vardis gets a flash of divine inspiration and remembers that their objective is to get the Conch, not to kill the Giants. 
He asks Wiri, “Do you know where the conch is?” 
She looks at him, “I was a slave, but if I hazard a guess, it’s in his room, which is on the lower level. I never went there.” 
The gnome turns to Orisk. “So you’re Orsik, spelled with a K?” 
“No, a C. K is my derelict son.”
“And if I mention your name, will it be okay?” 
“Just mention the 3HPP. We helped the mayor in Triboar.” 
After she’s gone, the 3HPP thinks about what to do next. 
“We could case the place?” remarks Thistle. “Unla Kee makes me invisible, and then I sneak around until I find the bedroom.” 
“Then we can come up with a plan,” says Orsik. Orsik is pleased. In the past, he was the muscle; now, he is the brain. 
An invisible Thistle flies off and creates this map as he floats around the top part of Ironslag. 
Thistle
“I love sneaking around.” 
He begins his journey by floating through the eastern passage into the foundry, where the two tracks end. 
He scribbles down his observations - 

*A rusted iron gantry clings to the north, west, and south walls 50 feet above the floor of the main foundry. The foundry echoes with the sound of clanging metal, rattling chains, whooshing bellows, and bubbling molten iron. The ceiling looms 40 feet above the gantry. Cable towers support the bucket chain that runs near the gantry's path. *
He also notices four ogres and a bored-looking juvenile giant overseeing them. 
From his perch in the top western corner, he heads south and east through a large hallway to a large room. 
Again, Thistle takes notes. 

*Wide iron gantries extend a short distance into a vast chamber. Two lit braziers illuminate the western gantry, with iron stairs leading down to the floor. The steps are iron grills with holes large enough for a human fist to pass through. The steps cover three-quarters against any ranged attack that must pass through them to hit their target. The northern and eastern gantries are unlit. The ceiling is 40 feet above the gantries, and the floor is 50 feet below. The rattling of the bucket chain fills the room. Four cable towers support the bucket chain as it moves through this area.*
*The Vonindod's helmed head and upper torso hang in midair, held in place by chains bolted to the floor and ceiling. Cranes extending out from the western and eastern gantries have two other fragments of the colossus hanging from them: an enormous greatsword and a giant gauntlet-like hand and forearm. These fragments hang at the same level as the gantries. *
*An iron portcullis is set into the south wall at the top of the stairs, close to another lit brazier. Beyond the portcullis is a dark hallway leading to the prison.* 
Looking down into the room, Thistle observes Duke Zalto playing with his hellhounds, tossing a metal ball. The ball has a body in it.
Thistle examines the cranes and observes that a few well-placed reduced spells could cause havoc. 

From there, Thistle sneaks to the pressing mill. Again, he takes notes:
*Eight orcs work here under the watch of a fire giant. The bucket chain moves from north to south through this room, the iron buckets dangling a few feet above the floor. As buckets of iron ore pass by, two orcs with hooked poles tip the buckets and dump their contents onto the floor. Two more orcs place the ore atop stone slabs, and two more orcs pick out impurities. The last two orcs load the ore back into the buckets and shovel the impurities into piles. A fire burns in a hearth in the middle of the east wall.*
From there, Thistle heads down to the mustering room, where he takes more notes. 
*The walls of this room bear frescoes that depict fire giants forging armor and weapons, marching to war, and binding red dragons in chains. Four basalt pillars support the 40-foot-high ceiling, and iron chandeliers bereft of candles hang above a pair of basalt tables. A fireplace in the north wall provides only warmth and light.*
Three ogres are cheering two Fire Giants who are arm wrestling.
Thistle checks his internal clock and decides to return to Unla Kee and get another invocation of invisibility. 
He then finishes exploring the lower level of Ironslag 
From the mustering hall, he heads down into the kitchen, where he takes some notes 
*Standing between black basalt pillars are two 10-foot-tall basalt tables for food preparation. Underneath the tables are empty iron cauldrons weighing 100 pounds each, and above them are dozens of utensils hanging from iron hooks and chains. Brimskarda, the fire giant duchess, stands in front of a fireplace holding an iron spoon to her lips, sampling soup from a fat cauldron suspended above the flames. Five smoke mephits dance around the cauldron, cackling with glee. Two ogres wearing grease-stained leather aprons stand beside the tables, and twenty goblins scurry about, performing menial chores.*
*Brimskarda wears a gown from a young black dragon’s scaly hide and wings. She has wrapped her luxurious orange hair around a black dragon’s horn, giving her a hairstyle that looks like a curving pillar of flame. *
At some point, Brimskarda shouts to the goblins, who take food down the hallway. Thistle follows them through the door and ends up in the Dining Hall. 
When he enters the dining hall, he notices a room to the northwest and sneaks in. He realizes it’s the war room. 

*A basalt table, its base sculpted to look like a fountain of black fire, stands in the middle of this well-lit room. Bare marble shelves jut from the walls at 10 and 15 feet. Standing against the northwest wall is an iron golem with a rune-carved iron ingot embedded in its forehead*
“No treasure,” he thinks. 

From there, he goes back into the dining room. He remembers he has some poison. He sneaks towards Duke Zalto’s table and tries to pour poison into the liquids. Unfortunately, while trying to pour, he causes a disturbance. 

The nearby goblins look around, but Thistle has managed to fly away quietly. 
The goblins chatter and complain at each other. Thistle infers they must be convinced that one of them made the mess. 

Thistle then reaches an alcove against the East wall. There are two doors. 
“The bedrooms.” 
Thistle thinks about perhaps shrinking the door but then realizes that it will shrink, but it will be too big for him to hold in place and fall, making a considerable noise. Worse, he will stop being invisible. 
So he sighs, and heads back to Unla Kee with his map and notes. 



# Chapter 64 - The heist. 
## Orsic
Orsic was tormented. He was at a crossroads. From a disreputable hired hand known throughout the North, he had found inside himself the leader of the soldiers that defended Parnast. And then, thanks to the miraculous effects of a ghost that had aged him, he had become his father. 
He was free of his reputation both externally and internally. 
But he was conflicted. 
Bob Saget was in Silverymoon. And the demon was planning to destroy the city. 
“Should I abandon them to their fate?” 
He pauses. 
“The chaotic destruction of Silverymoon would be quite the feather in my cap and probably get a notable promotion within the ranks of the Zhentarim.” 
He pauses. 
“But do I want to be known as a member of the infamous 3HPP? Do I want to have to hide my face wherever I go?” 
His reverie is interrupted. 
“Okay, I think I found the location of the conch,” states the fairy. 
Orsic listens carefully. 
And then stops, “Thistle, tell us what to do.”
## Thistle
The fairy is shocked. Ever since the dwarf got his intelligence boost, he’s been insufferable. 
But he can see how the dwarf is distracted. 
And so reluctantly, he explains his plan. First, he shows them the map. 

“We have two problems. The first is that there are a lot of giants.”
Unla Kee shouts, “I have a fireball.”
“They are fire giants.” 
Unla Kee is not deterred, “I have cloud kill.” 
Thistle stares at him, “There are at least 20 Fire Giants.” 
Unla Kee mutters, “I could dominate them?” 
“They are giants.” 
Unla Kee is miffed, “What’s the point of knowing these spells if I can’t use them?” 
Thistle wonders if Unla Kee is toying with the idea of dominating him and then returns to his plan. 
“With all of the giants, a frontal attack will fail. Therefore, I propose a distraction.” 
He then points to the carts that circle the upper level and says, “The plan is you get into the carts and let the carts take you around the upper level. When you enter the tunnel leading to the pressing mill, you jump off and then carefully and quietly sneak into the elevator area. Then you go down the elevator shaft.”
“And what will you be doing?”
“Wiri mentioned that whenever the wheel breaks, the giants rush in. I will make the wheel break by magically reducing a key part. With the giants distracted, I’ll fly through the Vodinoid chamber and catch up with you near the elevator on the lower floor.” 
Thistle watches as Orsic mulls it over and is pleased when the dwarf nods. 

## Orsic
“What did he say?” he thinks. 
Orsic is too busy thinking about his reputation and the fun of not being hunted across the North to pay attention to Thistle’s plan. 
“So I get into the cart and then down the elevator,” he mumbles. 
“Good, good.” 
## Thistle
“There is one problem: where the tracks end, there is a fire salamander that we must kill quietly.” 
Thistle turns to Unla Kee, “Do you have a silence spell?”
“No.” 
Thistle remembers the last heist that Unla Kee was involved in, Silverymoon, and how he didn’t have a silence spell there either. 
“Figures.” 
## Unla Kee
The wizard is irritated but unbowed. 
“There are other ways to deal with this problem,” he mutters. 
And from his head, a shimmering lance of power flew out and struck the creature. It writhed from the psychic pain and was stunned. 
Thistle strikes him twice with arrows, and the creature winces, caustic blood dripping. 
Unla Kee casts a toll on the dead, but the creature somehow avoids doing so. 
Orsic strikes and also fails to kill him. 
But Thistle’s arrows finish the job.
“No noise,” mutters Unla Kee. 
He’s pleased when the rest of the group acknowledges his achievement. Although he does notice Vardis sprawled on the ground. 
It’s only then that Unla Kee notices the dwarven prisoners. 
“Please let us go.” 
Unla Kee turns to Thistle: " If they stop filling the carts, the giants will notice. Do we want them to keep filling them?”
“Nah.”
“Do we want to fill the cart with Salamander parts?” 
“Yes, the cart behind us.”
## Thistle
The fairy watches as the 2HP loads the carts and moves towards the pressing mill. He pauses and thinks, “No time like the present.”

He flies down to the whee lroom and observes the situation.  A large fireplace on the north keeps the area warm and list. The enormous wheel with wooden spokes dominates the room.
He looks at the wheel more carefully and notes that the wheel is rigged to the bucket chain that travels through the forge. 
Thistle then examines the prisoners moving the wheel. There are ten shield dwarves with iron manacles, and all look exhausted.
“They won’t be of any use,” he thinks. 
“And there is the muscle,” he mumbles. He notes two ogres clad head to toe in spiked armor, ensuring none of the dwarves stops working. 
## Orsic
As Orsic floats on the slowly moving bucket chain, he wonders, “Does Thistle realize that he needs to wait before breaking the wheel?”
And the bucket chain stops. 
“No, he didn’t.” 
## Thistle
Thistle is thrilled with the results. The wheel is destroyed, and the Fire Giants seem to be converging on it. 
He can hear Duke Zalto rushing up the stairs. 
“Time to go catch up with my friends.”
Then his heart turns cold. 
“Oops.”’
Thistle flies to his stranded allies.  
## Orsic
“This was your plan?” mutters the very irritated dwarf. 
Now that the dwarf is no longer distracted by metaphysical considerations of his reputation and is focused on surviving, he remembers why he always comes up with plans. 
“Well, we had a lot of plans that we were toying with, and it was easy to get confused,” explains a very apologetic Fairy. 
Orsic is unimpressed and frustrated. He grabs the map Thistle drew of the lower level, examines it, and then starts speaking. 
“Okay, so we have a new plan. We jump from bucket to bucket to the edge of the room with the Vodinoid, jump down and head down to where the elevator room is, and enter the dining hall from there. And then hit the bedrooms.” 
“Jump?” asks Vardis.
Orsik looks at him and realizes that his friend is not happy at the height. He thinks for a moment if he should tell him and decides that it’s probably for the best, “I can cast feather fall.” 

## Vardis
As he floats to the ground, he glares at the fairy, who whispers, “Okay, now that we’re here, we need to quickly go through the elevator shaft into the dining hall.” 
“Will the goblins be there?” asks Orsik
“Probably?” says Thistle.
Vardis notices the frustration in Orsik’s face. 
As they approach the dining hall, they huddle about how to get past them. 
“Intimidate or bluff?” asks Vardis.
Unla Kee can’t resist, “Heare me out, I could cast dominate person.” 
Vardis laughs as Orsik turns to him and growls, “Okay, so you dominated one of them. What about the rest?” 
Vardis watches as a disgruntled Unla Kee marches, acting as if he is in charge. The cleric observes how the goblins ignore them, even while watching them. “They probably see a lot of strange people.” 
Before Orsik can say anything, Thistle is holding the lever of the north door and pointing to Orsik, urging him to push the door. 
Thistle takes a bow when the door opens, thanks to the heroic feat of strength. 
Vardis watches how an infuriated Orsik mutters, “Good for you! You did such a good job!”
## Thistle
“I did. I flew up here and moved the door handle nine feet up in the air. It’s not like we have anyone else who can do that.” 
And with that, the fairy enters the room. 

Vardis takes one look at the room and says, “No kink shaming.”
Thistle is surprised, so he looks around, trying to see what is there. There is an enormous bed with a frame made from crisscrossing iron sword blades welded together. 
“What’s he talking about … “he asks, then notices that dangling above the bed are large chains and manacles, the purpose of which is known only to the duke and the duchess. 
Behind the bed, Thistle notes a 15-foot-diameter shield made from adult black dragon scales displayed on the north wall behind the bed. 
He looks up at the ceiling, where an iron chandelier hangs over the center of the room, near the foot of the bed. Thistle’s eye is drawn to a chain connected to the chandelier, snagged on an iron hook mounted to the east wall above a 10-foot-tall basalt table with some valuable items resting on it and a large iron chest tucked underneath it. 
Two wardrobes of fire-scorched wood stand against the west wall. A giant barrel in the southeast corner serves as a stool and rests in front of a burnished, full-length mirror mounted in the south corner of the east wall. An iron brazier hangs from a hook mounted on the south wall near the door.
Thistle is about to try to steal some of the treasure from the basalt table, but Orsik points to the chest and shouts, “Open it now.” 
Unla Kee casts a knock and then opens the chest. Thistle was too close and could not avoid the blast of incendiary fire. When the fire clears out, he notices that the mage is dead. He also sees the cleric flat on his back, having tripped trying to get out. 
“Typical.” 
At this time, Thistle is particularly pleased with his recommendation to close the door when they enter. He knows Orsik will be impressed with his tactical awareness.
## Orsik
He is not impressed.
Inside the chest is a giant-sized adamantine crown set with six black opals, which are worth a lot of gold. 
Thistle smiles even more when he notices thousands of gold pieces underneath the ashes in the chest. 
And he’s particularly pleased with the discovery of the conch. 
There are several more magic items in the chest: a javelin of lightning, a wand of the warmage, and adamantine-scale armor. Orsik picks up the magical ring and sighs, “Another ring of jumping.” 
Vardis nudges him, “I probably need one, given how clumsy I am.” 
“Alright, let’s go. Let’s get the hell out of here.” 
Thistle asks, “What about all of this stuff?” He points at the treasured items on the table. 
“We don’t need it.”
Orsik is surprised when the fairy says, “No, I am going to put this stuff into our bag of holding.” 
Orsik tries not to laugh as he sees the fairy struggle to move a 750-pound hairbrush. 
“I don’t think so.” 
“Orsik, come up here!” 
“I want to leave. Dude, we just made 15,000 gold. Let’s get the fuck out of here.” 
Orsik is confused by Thistle’s sudden greed. 
“There are 5000 more right here!” shouts the Fairy. 
“I don’t want to fight 20 fire giants.” 
And with that, Orsik groans as Unla Kee interjects, “Why is getting this last item going to produce 20 Fire Giants?” 
“Because it’s going to take time!” 
“let’s take a vote!” shouts Thistle. 
“YOU TAKE A VOTE! I AM LEAVING!” says the dwarven artificer and marches off. 
As Orsik marches towards the door, he notices Unla Kee reach for his rock of wonder. The wizard is about to invoke some spell when Orsik points at the door and shouts, “How are you going to open it without me?” 
Thistle turns to Vardis, “Do you want the treasure?” 
“Nah. Nothing piques my interest.” 
“I guess we could come back to kill all the giants?” observes Thistle.
“Yeah, like ram the cloud castle until the giants die!” mutters Orsic.
“Or unleash Bob Saget’s zombie army?” suggests Unla Kee. 
With that, the group heads out. They pass the goblins setting the dinner tables and use the elevator to go up to the top of Ironslag. There, Orsik demonstrates another feat of strength when he opens the massive iron portcullis that bars the exit to the Yak Folk village. 
He smiles and begins the long rope climb into the castle. As he looks from the walls, he notes that the Zombies are still there. 
Finally, he enters the control tower.
## Escarlotta
Escarlotta has been waiting anxiously for the 3HPP to return. She’s made her husband’s life miserable, complaining that she shouldn’t have to rely on little people to get her revenge. 
And then she feels the dwarf enter her control tower.
“Is she dead?” 
“We have the conch,” mutters Orsik. 
“I sent you to kill the Duchess! IS SHE DEAD?”
“Yes, she’s dead!” 
Escarlota imagines a suitably gruesome death and, for a moment, is happy. 
She’s even happier when Vardis starts singing, “Ding dong, the Duchess is dead.” 
Then he shouts, “Now let’s kill Sansuri! That horrible excrement of cumulous cloud dares to pretend she is favored among all other cloud giants!” 
## Orsik
Orsik once again is thinking about Bob. He’s thinking about how much better his life is now that he’s a hero and older. 
He turns to Vardis, “My son says we should ally with Bob against the city of Silverymoon.”
Vardis stares at him momentarily and thinks – “His method acting is getting a little bit weird.” 
Unla disagrees, “Let’s kill him.” 
Thistle reminds Orsik of the original plan – “That we take down Bob and frame house Margaster. To do that, we have to let some chaos reign.”
“Demons and the like slaughtering folks through the city?” 
Unla Kee surprises Orsik, “I was just gonna say that sound’s pretty fucked up, and I could get behind it.” 
“You wanted to kill Bob?” 
“I want the riskiest plan.” 
“There are a lot of things that could go wrong with trying to pint the slaughter of innocent hundreds of people on a greater devil.” 
The fallen cleric of Corellon interjects, “Normally, I am with Orsik, but this time, I think we just let it be. Bad things happen.” 
Orsik stares dumbfounded, “Aren’t we gonna take the blame because it’s our show?” 
The cleric who was banned from every temple of his god says, “I don’t know. There’s a cambion, and there will be a lot of chaos. There’s no guarantee we get blamed.” He then pauses, “And I am not sure Silverymoon is long for this world. Furthermore, our track record of being conniving is not as strong as you think. “
Thistle observes, “Bob is going to figure out how to pin the blame on us – unless we explicitly fight him.” 
Orisk hears this and thinks about his newfound reputation, “Taking him out would be the best for our reputation.” 
“For our reputations, yes. For our lives, walk away.” 
Thistle observes, “We need allies.” 
Orsik enumerates all the allies they could get: “Lord Zymorven, the Hand of Yartar, Urgula from Triboar, soldiers from Everlund, members of the watch of Silverymoon who are fans of the 3HPP.”
Unla Kee can’t resist, “Hsing of the Lord’s Alliance?” 
Orsik ignores him and continues, “We don’t have an ally problem. Our problem is how do convince people that we didn’t bring a demon into the city?” 
And then Thistle has a brilliant idea – “We didn’t. We go to the Lord of Silverymoon and declare that Bob Saget, our friend, is dead. A demon killed him. And we have come to deal with that foul creature.” 
“What if they try and use spells on us to see if we are lying?” 
“We are emissaries of Everlund. They won’t use magic.” 
Orsik mulls it over, “We kill Bob.” 
Vardis mutters, “Walking away is always the better option.” 
Orsik ponders whether there is a reason he had the reputation he had. 

# Chapter 65 - The Fight with the BBEP
## (Big Bad at the end of the play)

## 7th day of the 1st tenday of Eleint of 1492 
### Thistle
As Blagothkus’ castle flies towards Silverymoon, Thistle remembers something. Laela’s near Ironslag. For all Thistle knows, she might be a prisoner of the Giant. 
He looks outside at Evermoors below the clouds and mutters to himself: 
“Laela, I know you’re hoping I’ll come to save you from whatever monstrous torment Adminond Kroskas, Ilymrith, or whatever has inflicted upon you, but I have to save Silverymoon. You’d never forgive me if I let the city burn to save you.” 
### Orsik 
“We need some allies who would be discrete,” says Orsik to the 3HPP.  
“There’s Darlathara who owes for her election,” mentions Thistle. 
“Lord Zymorven owes us one for saving his son,” observes Vardis. 
“Urgala for figuring out what happened to the love of her life?” notes Unla Kee. 
“Tamalin Zoar was someone who helped us once,” remembers Orsik. 
“And then there is her brother, High Captain Horix Zoar,” observes Vardis. 
“What about the Hand of Yartar?” asks Thistle. 
“And then there is the Zhentarim,” notes Vardis. 
“They like it when we do things for them, not for us,” observes Orsik. 
“What about those rich folks in Mirabar and Xanthalar’s Keep?” asks Unla Kee. 
Orsik observes, “We could fetch some guards from Parnast or get some guards from Silverymoon…” 
Then he sighed, remembering Wallace, and said, “Let the folks in Parnast be in peace.” 
And then he perks up: " We could always go to the Harpers and make the case that I am trying to redeem my family name from the damage that my son, Orsik, with a K, did.” 
They stare at him, and he laughs, “I don’t think that Krowen Valharrow would go for that…” 
He turns to Escarlotta and shouts, “To Zymorven Hall!” 
## 10th day of the 1st tenday of Eleint of 1492
### Lord Zymorven
The castle settles down a few miles from Zymorven Hall. Lord Zymorven is not surprised. His scrying magic tells him whenever anyone approaches his homestead. 
“I am the guard of the road!” he mutters, thinking about how he would like to have his giant killer sword right now.
“No matter!” 
He stands in his full armor, ready to defend his homestead. 
“I recognize Vardis, Unla Kee, and that charming fairy, but where is the dwarf?” 
There is a dwarf among them, but he looks a little bit older. 
The dwarf pulls out Lord Zymorven’s giant slayer and says, “I am Orsik’s father, Orsic, and I have come for help.” 
“I owe your son a debt for saving my son from a terrible set of choices and death.” 
“My son produced a play starring a famous actor who created a persona, Bob Saget, that was a demon. Unfortunately, a real demon, or doppelganger with magical powers, killed and replaced the real Bob. And now this creature plans to destroy Silverymoon with a zombie plague.” 
“We must tell the leaders of the city!” shouts Zymorven.
“We can’t. Bob will release his horde and destroy the city. So, we have decided to assemble a strike team to attack quietly. The watch will know if we fail and face a weakened evil.” 
“How noble of you! So different from your son!” 
### Orsik 
He puts on a masterful performance of dwarf acting and says, “Yes.”
As he marches towards the castle, he tells Zymorven, “Don’t stay anywhere other than the tower, and don’t go out at night and don’t go into the shadows.” 
“Why?”
“We have a vampire problem.” 
## 2nd day of the 2nd tenday of Eleint 1492
### Urgala Meltimer

“Lord Zymorven!” she says, smiling. 
“Ma’am,” says the Lord. 
She then notices Orsik’s father and the other members of the 3HPP, and her smile darkens. 
“What brings you here?” she asks, worried. 
Orsik explains the situation. 
She looks at him and then at Lord Zymorven and says, “I suppose we could have one last adventure.” 
“Saving Silverymoon is a thing of ours,” he says enigmatically. 
### Darthara Shendrel
The Lord Protector is not happy to see the 3HPP. She had assured Krowen Valharrow that these were the contractors he was looking for, and that had blown up in her face. 
She remembered Krowen’s tone. 
“They betrayed the circles. We lost many agents and a powerful set of magic that will be expensive and time-consuming to replace. I took a risk on these people, and they have betrayed us all.” 
She examines the dwarf, convinced he is Orsic, but the dwarf isn’t Orsik. 
He also explains that he is here to redeem his family name. 
“Your son did a terrible thing. I lent him my credibility and gave him the magic artifacts to help the heroes of the Ordning. What did he do? He unleashed hell up and down the cost.”
She notices how Orsik’s father seems particularly excited, “He did that? Tell me more!” 
Vardis snikers, “Some father you turned out to be.” 
Unla Kee shrugs, “Kids.” 
“He betrayed the Harper’s circles! Several agents were killed!” She then stares at the elder Orsik, “I am not sure if the apple falls far from the tree.” 
She stares at the Dwarf and realizes he’s just a father who wants to fix his son's mistakes and shouldn’t be blamed. 
“He did save the town. And more importantly, the information the 3HPP provided that we used against Cindrell did prevent the town from changing its allegiance to the Lord’s Alliance.” 
Then she turns to Vardis – “I am so pleased to see you have abandoned that boat anchor of a miscreant. As a Correlon cleric, your association harmed those who believed in your god.” 
She notes that the cleric is displeased and continues, “He seemed to know everything, always could figure everything out. There was something off about his intelligence.” 
### Vardis
He stares at Darathra and contemplates expressing himself freely, but then he remembers how much funnier it will be when she finds out she helped Orsik. 
So he turns to her and asks, “Will you join us?” 
She pauses, “Yes. I owe you that much for saving the town from Cindrell. But after this, all of our debts are cleared.” 
As they enter the castle, Urgala asks, “Why are we forced to stay in this part of the castle, and why are there zombies?” 
Unla Kee snickers, “It is an interactive experience.” 
Vardis is about to laugh hard when Orsik mutters, “There’s a vampire.”
Lord Zymorven shouts, “ A vampire? You brought this thing next to my house?” 
“We didn’t bring it that close. We were like a mile away, and it was sunlight,” mutters Vardis. 
Zymorven coughs, “The castle must be positioned away from Silverymoon. We can’t allow any undead vampiric infestation.” 
At that point, they hear a roar.
“What was that?” 
“A dragon,” explains Orsik.
“A DRAGON!” shrieks Zymorven. 
“Technically speaking, it’s a Dracolich,” explains Vardis.
“A DRACOLICH?” 
Unla Kee says, “I still think we should have just gone with the plan where we unleash…” 
Orsik interrupts him before Zymorven can hear, “We agreed that was a bad plan.” 
But Unla Kee isn’t dissuaded, “but was it?”
Vardis ignores him and turns to Orsik – “Anyone else?” 
“I am not begging for help from the Harpers, and I don’t think the Zoars will want to help. And the Hand of Triboar isn’t going to mix well with this group.” 
“Then I guess this is it?” 
## 4th day of the 2nd tenday of Eleint 1492 
### Unla Kee
Having spent years studying the fates, Unla Kee is sure that Bob is not the cause of the world's end. And for that reason alone, he is bored. 
The burden of knowing that it’s all irrelevant because he knows how to stop the end of the world. And only he knows. And he also knows the world doesn’t end. 
Unless he decides otherwise. 
And he also knows that if they don’t stop Bob tonight, Silverymoon will be the victim of a zombie apocalypse.
This brings him to a conflicted decision about Kaelen. Kaelen was a playwright who attempted a summoning ritual that was partly thwarted. 
Instead of summoning a fearsome force of destruction, he summoned Bob Saget. 
Unla Kee’s friends felt that Bob was a pet that could be exploited for profit. Unla Kee knew better. He knew that Bob was evil incarnate and that, in the end, destruction would follow. But, then again, as he would say, “Money?” 
He doesn’t understand Orsik’s reluctance to unleash the Dracolich and the vampire on Silverymoon. 
“The Destruction and Chaos would be – beautiful.” 
Unla Kee noodles – “Orsik doesn’t understand the beauty of the plan; with the Dracolich creating chaos, an attack on Bob would have the perfect cover. The destruction of the royal theater house would be one more tragedy in a day of tragedies that could be blamed on the Cult of the Dragon. As for why they would attack Silverymoon? Laeral Silverhand, as First Lord of Waterdeep, who created the coalition that defeated Severin and Sandesyl, is a perfect patsy.” 
Unla Kee takes a swig of his drink and forgets. 
He opens his eyes and remembers where he is, in Silverymoon, watching Thistle examining Kaelen’s house. 
“I could fireball the place?” 
But Thistle will have none of that.
So, his mind wanders to the discussion between Zymorven and Orsik in his castle. 
“Kill the young man? I think not!” shouts Lord Zymorven. “We should interrogate him.” 
Orsik sighs – “I get your desire for impartiality, but we don’t have time.” 
And the argument would have gone for hours if Urgala had not reminded everyone of how Kaelen’s original plan was the mass murder of the entire town of Triboar, and the 3HPP thwarted that plan, and that as far she is concerned, they should have killed Kaelen on the spot. 
Thistle breaks his reverie again. 
“So Kaelen is sitting there in his house tapping his fingers. The place looks like it’s covered in dust. The door to the house hasn’t been opened in a while. Something is very wrong with him.” 
“Fireball?” 
“No, we don’t need to attract attention. And we don’t know if Kaelen is somehow connected to Bob. The demon doesn’t know we are about to attack him.” 
Unla Kee sighs. 
### Vardis
Vardis has been standing watch for Thistle, which is a good thing. If the patrol saw a fairy casing a house, there’s no telling what would happen. 

The guard sees Vardis, recognizes him as a member of the 3HPP and a goodly race, and smiles. 
Vardis waves his hand and makes a noise, and the guard smiles back and tells him to be careful. 
The guard speaks to him for a few minutes and leaves. 
Just then, Orsik appears from the shadows, “Fucking racists.” 
“Hey, I benefit from Elven privilege.”
Thistle floats into view – “I think we leave Kaelen where he is.”
Vardis nods, “He may or may not be powering the demon, but if we kill him, Bob will know, and we will lose the advantage of surprise.”
Lord Zymorven coughs, “We will tell the Lord of Silverymoon before we attack?” 
“Do we want to put them in a state of panic?” 
Orsik whines – “but how are we going to get the credit if we don’t tell them?”
“That’s the concern here; we are only doing it for the accolades, and here I thought it was to remove the stain of your son’s and my best friend’s actions,” says Vardis with a  mischievous grin.”
Unla Kee pipes up – “We are going to make a big mess. They’ll know we did it. And then, Lord Aerasume won’t be too happy to hear that he let a demon summon a horde of zombies in his theater next to the royal palace. He might want to get rid of us.” 
Vardis almost laughs when he sees Orsik say in a flat, bored, and annoyed tone – “Good Orsik wouldn’t do it just for the accolades. He would do it because it’s the right thing to do.” 
The cleric is bemused and thinks that perhaps Orsik is finally seeing the wisdom of the “let it burn” strategy he was a fan of.
To his surprise, Lord Zymorven speaks, “My dear boy, I understand having to cover up your son’s failings.” He pauses and says, “We have that in common.” 
Vardies tries hard not to laugh when Orsik mutters, “Yes, we do have that in common.” 
“I know many in Silverymoon, and I will ensure they hear of your courageous, heroic sacrifice. I will take care of everything.” 
Vardis wonders if Orsik’s gritting teeth can grind adamantium steel when his friend says, “That’s so generous of you.” 
### Darthala 
“I owe the 3HPP many things. And I should tell the Harpers about this. But I made compromises and made choices. Not all of them wise,” are the thoughts going through Darthala’s head as she waits in the darkness.
“Explain to me what we are doing here?” 
“Bob Saget has created a portal at the entrance of the Theater. Theatergoers enter the portal to see a play. Meanwhile, in the real theater, he creates zombies with the help of a Red Wizard in league with the Drow,” explains Thistle. 
The theatergoers, dressed in their finest, approach the doors that appear to open to the theater. When they have all entered, the door slams shut. 
“But they are inside?” 
“No, they’re not. We thought so, too, but the portal is inside the theater.” 
With that, she watches as Unla Kee casts a dispel magic spell to remove the wards that protect the entrance. 
“Alarm spells.” 
And then she enters and sees the horror that Thistle described. The theater is a large rectangular building with a stage at one end, a series of benches at the orchestra level, and a balcony that can be reached from the back. There appear to be patrons sitting watching an empty stage. 
The stage itself is splattered with blood and the odd body part. 
Darthala looks at the patrons and realizes they all seem to be wearing one of three repeating costumes. She stares more intently and realizes that they are dressed like the 3HPP. 

She looks at the half-orc behind the concession stands and stifles a gasp. 

The head lolls oddly from side to side, disturbingly and unnaturally; its neck is broken. 
She looks at another in an Orsik costume and gulps – a Tabaxi whose neck is partially severed, with muscles and bones visible through the gore.
“They are all zombies.”
Thistle whispers, “That is what we said.” 


# Chapter 66 Orsik experiences personal growth and the fellowship against Bob disolves. 

## Night of the 5th day of the 2nd tenday of Eleint 
### The battle with Bob. 

The battle is over. Bob is gone. 

It wasn’t that epic of a battle. Bob was ready with his zombie army. Despite the attempt at caution, the 3HPP and their allies were noticed. It looked grim. But Orsik had a sinister plan up his sleeve. He could make bags of holdings as an artificer, and he prepared two of them. So, while Bob taunted them, Unla Kee and Thuul approached the creature. As they got close enough, Unla Kee tossed the bag of holding towards the demon, and as the bag stood at the feet of the demon, Thuul rushed forward, stuffing a second bag in the first. 

Bob, the Red Wizard, and Thuul were caught in the blast. 

Lord Zymorven was momentarily outraged when he realized several citizens of Silverymoon who were still alive got caught up in the blast. 

But Bob is gone. 

With Bob gone, the zombie hordes were easy enough pickings for Vardis’ power over the undead, a few fireballs, and the sword and bow of Zymorven, Daratha, and Urgala. 

The Drow fighters who had been there to protect the Red Mage fled before being caught. Who knows what happened to them? 

Vardis finds the whole outcome very funny, the ease with which Bob was disposed of. 

“Touring the top food places of the Astral Plane,” laughs Vardis. 

The theater is full of dead zombies dressed like members of the 3HPP—a mass of decayed bodies dressed to look like Vardis, Orsik, Unla Kee, and Thistle. 

“Where are all the theatergoers?” asks Urgala.

“We don’t know. We entered the theater to see what they were all raving about and found these zombies. And since Bob was about to start the apocalypse today, we killed them. Honestly, I am disappointed I did not get to see what everyone was raving about.” 

Lord Zymorven tries to keep calm, “Does that mean we killed 200 of Silverymoon’s citizens?” 

Orsik shakes his head, “We’ll go find them.” 

Vardis adds, “And I am very motivated to go find them because I am really interested to see what this play was about. Like -why- was it so cool.” 

Darathra is furious, “We sent him to the Astral plane. We were supposed to kill him. He’ll be back.” 

“I had a foolproof idea,” protests Orsik.

“He can come back?” queries a confused and worried Vardis.

Darathra stares up at the theater's roof, pleading to whatever gods of Faerun made her ally with the 3HPP, and shouts, “Do you not understand how the multiverse works?” 

“I saved Silverymoon, right?” whines Orsik.

She pinches the bridge of her nose with her left hand, then rubs her temples with each hand, then takes a deep breath, “What you did was send 300 innocent people to a pocket dimension that.”
