Orsik interjects, “Bob did that.” 

“We have no way to bring them back.” 

Orsik looks at him,r confused, “So we’ll get them back.”

And Darathra screams in exasperation, “THE ONE PERSON WHO KNOWS WHERE THEY ARE IS LOST IN THE ASTRAL PLANE!!!” 

“Well, when he comes back, we can ask him.”

Darathra, “YOU WANT TO BRING A DEMON BACK?” 

Urgala, who has finally recovered from the shock of the battle,e asks in a worried tone, “Is this my fault, too?” 

“We’re heroes!”

“We just sent the only evidence of this crime to the Astral Plane.” 

Orsik takes the tone of a parent reassuring their child, “What about the zombies?” 

“They are going to blame us for the zombies. See how they are dressed like you? They’ll assume this is something you did. And the powers that be will need to blame someone. THIS IS A DISASTER!” 

Vardis looks at her patiently and says, “Calm down. Flying off the handle won’t do anyone any good.” 

Orsik stares at the carrion and death and says, “We could burn it all?” 

Vardis turns his head, “Burn everything?”

Orsik warms up to the idea and shouts, “Yeah, we could burn everything. And if there is nothing here, there is no evidence. It was a tragic fire.” 
Urgala nods, “There is some promise in that idea.” 

Vardis shrugs, “The absence of evidence isn’t the evidence of absence of a crime.” 

Orsik dismisses his friend's concerns by tapping his headband, “My headband is tingling. This will work.” 

Vardis, who never liked the plan to save Silverymoon, mutters, “I think your headband is malfunctioning.” 

Lord Zymorven coughs, “Orsik, I admire your ruthlessness. You focused on what needed to be done. You chose to sacrifice those innocent victims, and that was a hard choice.”

Vardis nods, “Collateral damage is unavoidable in war.” 

“Yes, and as the commander on the field, Orsik and Orsik alone made that decision. I was not a party to that decision. And I would have made different decisions. But he was in charge, and it was his decision alone.” 

Vardis begins to understand that Lord Zymorven is making it clear who will bear any responsibility for what happened. 

“And so I declare that our debts between our families are cleared. And if anyone should inquire what happened, I will say that I obey my commander who made the decisions in the heat of the battle and did not consult me in any of his plans.” 

Orsik studies the windbag, and mutters, “Good Chat.” 

Lord Zymorven is annoyed. “I am not ready to be cut off. I have several more crucial points to make.” 

As Vardis wonders if he can turn Lord Zymorven, Urgala mutters, “There is a reason I dumped his ass.”

“Why did you date him in the first place?” 

She shrugs, “Two people adventuring, strange things happen.”
Vardis sighs, “Ah yes, the experimental years.” 

Urgala sighs, “You’re in the forest in the middle of the night.”

“I am too familiar.” 

“Exactly.”

And then Vardis looks around, “Look at the company I keep.”

Urgala smirks, “I wasn’t going say anything…” 

Orsik realizes what she thinks, “We’re just friends.” 

Vardis insists, “We go way back. Legacy friends.” 

Urgala has a smirk on her face when she says, “Of course you are.” 

Lord Zymorven has been droning on, and to change the conversation, the elf and the dwarf turn to listen to him: “Furthermore, I want to be clear: There is no evidence that Bob was a demon. No demonic actions were taken, and no definitive proof was provided. I could not testify in a court of law that Bob was a demon.” 

Orsik realizes that the Lord is still trying to cover his ass, “Well, if he comes back, that might be proof.” 

Lord Zymorven turns pale, “I have no opinion on that. I just want my position vis-a-vis the events here to be clearly understood.” 

Orsik is annoyed, “Well, if you are not proud of the events and what we accomplished.”

The old fighter becomes even paler. “No, I am very proud of what we accomplished and **your **valiant leadership.” 

Vardis coughs, “We? You’ve been shirking responsibility… ” 

Orsik silences him, “No, I’ll take we. He’s supposed to help with the aristocrats and spread the good news that we saved the town.” 

Urgala’s ex is frantic: “I have been trying to make the point that a bunch of theatergoers have disappeared.”

Vardis shrugs, “Only one theater’s worth.” 

“These were not random people. These are members of society. Over 200 disappeared.” 

Orsik looks around, “There are about 50 zombie bodies. So we burn the place down, and people can’t identify their loved ones. Body parts are all over the place. Some are destroyed. They died in the fire.” 

“And who will we blame on the fire,” shouts Zymorven. 

Orsik feels annoyed that Lord Zymorven doesn’t see the brilliance of his plan, “There was a threat in the theater. We rushed to neutralize it. Unfortunately, a fire broke out. We were unable to save the theatergoers.” 

“You intend to tell folks that you went into the theater and used fire spells????” he shrieks. 

Orsik sighs, “Vardis was turning the undead like it was going out of style. And the battle got boring, so he used fireball and flamestrike.” 

“Not a particularly effective spell,” mutters Vardis. 

Lord Zymorven shouts, “No. What happened was that we walked into the theater and saw what was going on. We tried valiantly to protect the innocent, but Bob, who was a demon, used fire spells in the theater. Unfortunately, the conflagration was intense. And although we defeated Bob, we could not save anyone else.” 

Vardis raises an eyebrow, “Don’t forget we saved the town.” 

“Yes, and we saved the town. I think that’s a fine story. Can we all agree to this?” 

Orsik smiles, “We’re getting our stories straight? Cool. I am down with that version.”

Realizing everyone must agree, he turns to Darathra, who thinks momentarily, “We’re all caught in a mess. If I told anyone I helped you, I’d get into so much trouble. My take is I was never here and never saw anything.” 

Vardis snarks, “No one takes accountability anymore.” 

Orsik nods, “Sounds Harper-like.” He pauses and puts his hand over his mouth in the shape of an o. “Did I say that out loud?” 

Darathra tries to kill him with her glare as she marches out. 

Orsik shouts, “You weren’t that big a help in the battle, lady.” 

She takes her sword and sheathes it. Then she shakes her head in frustration and marches out. 

“Need a lift,” asks Orsik. Seeing that she ignores him, he shouts, “Well, there was plenty of space. You should leave before it gets hot. And establish an alibi.”

Darathra turns around at him and spits each word, “I am out of here. We are done. Don’t ever come looking for me.” 

“So, life if I say hi, you won’t say hi?” 

“Of course, I’ll say hi. But no more calling in favors, no more special requests.” She stops at the door to the theater, turns inside, and shouts, “And no more plays.” 

Orsik starts to wonder how to burn the place down. 

Unla Kee takes it as his cue. He points one finger up, and a mote of fire heads up to the balcony. Then, it explodes in fire and the flammable materials there burn. He then points the other finger into the middle of the theater, and a bright, shiny star streaks into the middle. With a sudden explosive force, the room burns brightly. But the fire isn’t robust enough to sustain a burn. 

Thistle suddenly appears with all the rotting food and places it in a pile. He turns to Orsik and asks, “Can you please smash the benches?” 

“Because I am a dwarf?” 

“No, because you are strong.” 

“I am going to let that casual racism just drop.”

Vardis looks at him, “We’re in Silverymoon, and the racism just seeps into every conversation.”

“You're right. Two hundred hundred fewer today.”

Vardis laughs, “I am sure there were good people.” 

Lord Zymorven, hearing the discussion of race, decides it's his moment to leave the room quietly.

Urgala's laughter bursts into the room. “I haven’t had this much fun in years. Zymorven caught up in a mass murder spree? Is Darathra covering up a crime? I feel bad. I felt like I was doing you a favor. You have done me a favor. Do you have any idea for how long I have had to deal with those two self-righteous jerks because I had an affair? You knocked those two arrogant bastards seven pegs. If you need anything from me, I’m here for you.” 

Unla Kee observes that Bob’s victims were not the “goodly races.”:

“We could blame them? Here’s what we say - this was a demonic cult of the ungodly races that we uncovered. When they saw us arrive, they were convinced that their plot would be thwarted, and so slaughtered the theatergoers. The people of Silverymoon are going to believe this because it fits their preconceived racist stereotypes.” 

Orsik is troubled by this and says, “Look, we already have a story with Lord Zymorven. We can’t have two stories. So thank you, Unla Kee. It's a good initiative, but we’re sticking with the original plan.” 

Thistle drops his bow from the taught position. He was planning to pincushion the mage if Unla Kee didn’t get with the program. 

Vardis tries to settle everything: “This is just a normal part of the brainstorming process. There are no bad ideas. But we have chosen a plan, and it's time to execute.” 

Orsik sighs, “Okay, so can we start the fire?” 

Thistle nods and places the crushed beams on the pile of compost that he uses to ignite a bonfire. The 3HPP realizes that this bonfire will do the trick. 

Orsik wants to stay to be praised by the city for saving them, but Urgala encourages him to run. 

“There are spells that could reveal the truth. If Zymorven tells his story and nobody can find you guys, there will be less of a desire to ask hard questions, and it gives us time to find the missing people?” 

Orsik is  initially flustered that he didn’t come up with that, “Is his band failing him?” He then has a profound insight, “You know, the thing I am learning is that smart people surround themselves with other smart people and encourage them to come up with good ideas. My job is to identify the most brilliant idea.” 

Urgala looks at him and tries to keep the laugh out of her voice but can’t keep it out of her eyes, “I am leaving because I am going to lose my game face if I stay here much longer.” 

As they leave, Unla Kee feels a bit down. Orsik thinks it’s because Vardis’ ability to turn undead made it hard for him to shine and says, “You set up the Astral gate. Without you, we’re fighting Bob.” 

Unla Kee is startled. “Oh, I know that. I was just thinking about the chaos, civil strife, and evil we could have caused if we had told my story. The city would have erupted into violent race riots. It’s not that I am evil, but it’s one hell of a way to burn this evil place to the ground.”

And with that, they leave the burning building. 

The 3HPP arrives at the eastern city gate with the theater blazing in the background. There, a guard is delighted to see the real-life heroes made famous in the play, but when he sees the flames, he becomes agitated. He apologizes profusely and runs away, muttering something about someone he cares about who attended the play. 

To avoid meeting their friendly vampire and her spawn, the 3HPP board their airship in the morning.

## 6th day of the 2nd tenday of Eleint 

As they settle into the control tower, Orsik turns to Escarlotta, “This castle isn’t safe.” 

She’s surprised, “Safe for me.”

“But not for us.” 

The control gems form a confused expression.

Orsik mutters, “I didn’t capture the castle to have a room.” 

“You captured the castle,” she says in a disapproving giantess mom's voice. 

“Yes, we own it.” 

“You own it? Really? I am the flight command system. I am integrated with the whole castle. You are here because I find you amusing, and my goals line up with your goals. Think of this as a partnership, an agreement.”

Vardis asks, “Tenancy in common?” 

Orsik ignores her and turns to his friend, “If we get rid of the dracolich and the vampire, then we can have the whole castle.”

Then he stares at the control crystals and says out loud, “I am sure I can figure out how to use these crystals on my own.”

Escarlotta whispers gravely, “What?” 

Vardis tries to brush it off, “The headband has been defective.” 

Before this can escalate, Escarlotta says, “Look, you can’t get rid of me. I am the control system. But more importantly, why can’t we be friends?” 

Surprised, Orsik whines, “Because you keep telling us what to do and what not to do.”

She grumbles back, “I want to talk to people. You come in and give me orders, have no small talk, and don’t ask me how my day was.”

Orsik whines, “You have us traipsing around Faerun when we should save the world from giants.” 

Escarlotta is startled. “So why don’t you save the world from Giants? You have the conch. Why don’t you find out what’s up with King Hekaton?” 

Orsik is surprised. “That’s what you want us to do next?” Up until now, Escarlotta seemed more interested in revenge than saving the world, more furious at not being in the Ordning sweepstakes than ending the war. 

Her tone changes to a wistful reflective “Blagothkus and I had a long chat, and I realized that I misplaced a lot of my rage and anger at dying onto him. I came to understand his choices better. And we realized that we are good to each other, happy together, and willing to share the castle.” 

Orsik mumbles, “That’s a change.” 

“Yes, we saw how Thistle was willing to leave the woman he loved for a higher cause, and that made me appreciate how Blagothkus killing our son wasn’t his fault. The gods and, in particular, Amn were manipulating him for Amn’s agenda. We were caught up in events where our choices were circumscribed. If you can tell me how your day went, I’m happy to be your navigational system.”

Vardis tries this - small-talk - thing, “We saved silverymoon.” 

“You were willing to do the right thing. You were willing to clean up your own. You were willing to give up on the woman you loved to save a city of people who think that because of their race, your allies aren’t good. That is what heroes are. “

Orsik realizes he’s a hero and says in awe, “We did save them!” 

“That touched me. And it made it possible for Blagothkus and I to repair our relationship. And it made me want to talk about my feelings and needs.” 

Orsik is touched and then decides to treat Escarlotta as part of the team, “Vampire first?”

“I worry that the realms would be destroyed if you were to die. You are a critical player in the future of our corner of the world.” 

Orsik beams, “Could we get her to agree to join us and slaughter some enemies?”

Vardis nods, “Do the ends justify the means?” 

Blagothkus speaks for the first time, “That is precisely how we ended up in this emotional conflict between me and my wife.”

Escarlotta speaks, “Yes, the vampire must be destroyed. But the greater good matters more. It matters more than my petty hatred and frustrations. We must stop this mayhem that the giants are engaged in.” 

Orsik acknowledges her input with a thoughtful scratch of his chin, “What if we find good dragons and the Vampire and the Dracolich attack them?”

The gems bunch up into a ball as if in deep thought and then expand, “You want to make a deal with a vampire?” 

Vardis asks Orsik, “What do we have to offer her besides our blood?” 

Unla Kee chimes in that as she is the last member of the cult of the drag, there must be some cult artifact she wants that the 3HPP could fetch for her. 

Vardis turns to him, perplexed, “Memorabilia?” 

“Oh no, I am thinking something like an evil tome how to create a Dracolich from a dragon. Or an artifact that can be used to control Dracoliches.” 

“So, trade one problem for another?” 

Orsik observes that they do that a lot. Vardis nods and mutters, “That is our MO.” 

There’s a moment of silence, and then Orsik mutters, “Oka,y Unla Kee, blow the conch.” 

And he does. And —the world around them shimmers. 

## 6th day of the 2nd tenday of Eleint  - 
### Into the Maelstrom.

When the world stops shimmering, the 3HPP find themselves atop a glowing glyph carved into the floor of an alcove in the northeast corner of a rough-hewn chamber. 

The air is thick with the scent of the sea. The walls are slick with moisture and adorned with bioluminescent lichen, snails, starfish, and barnacles. Tiny crabs scuttle across the floor, avoiding a glowing glyph carved into the northeast corner. The chamber is filled with the haunting melody of a pipe organ and a powerful female voice. The music emanates from a spiral staircase that leads upwards. To the right, a circular pool plunges 40 feet deep. Two hallways stretched in opposite directions, each adorned with a life-sized statue of a storm giant. 

The dimensions of the room are most definitely storm giant-sized. It is roughly circular with a 100-foot diameter, and the ceilings are over fifty feet high. 

Orsik asks, “What do we need to do?” 

Vardis responds, “Talk to Queen Serissa because she might know where Hekaton is?” 

“I want to study these caverns a bit more… They are interesting.”  Orsik, examining the cavern masonry, is impressed despite himself. He mutters, "Storm Giants did this? Huh. Not bad. For giants, that is. The stonework's a bit rough and could use a dwarf's touch, but the scale of it... impressive, even for me." He runs a hand along the wall, noting the quality of the stone and the craftsmanship, grudgingly admitting, "Alright, maybe they know a thing or two about carving. Still, wouldn't want to live in it." He looks at one particular section, “Shoddy work, the kind of thing you would never see if a dwarf was making this place.” 

He then says, “Aha, I knew it. That’s why we couldn’t find this place. It’s deep underwater. With the dimensions of this place, it should have been obvious where it was, but not if it was hidden. And what better place than under the sea?” 

As Orsik examines the masonry, Vardis examines the pool and shouts, “Aha, this pool isn’t a pool but a passageway. It’s the physical entrance into this place.” 

Vardis turns to Unla Kee, “Will the conch take us back to the Sigil? Or let us escape?” 

Orsik mutters to himself, “C’mon, let’s keep going.” 

Vardis puts his foot down: “I would like to know how it works before we desperately look for an escape.” 

Just then, Orsik pattern matches to his insight from the previous day, “I don’t have to have the best plans, I just have to recognize them.” He says out loud, “Yes, that’s a great idea, Vardis.”

Vardis tries hard not to have his eyes roll. 

The conch is silent. The conch wielder can only blow on the device once per day. 

With that, the 3HPP explores the place they just landed in. The first place they look at is the geyser to the south. Vardis urges caution, and it’s a good thing because the geyser erupts, and scalding hot water that would have hurt fills the room. 

Thistle notes, “Right-hand rule?” 
And so they head to the north passage. At the end of the hallway, a statue depicts a male storm giant blowing a pink and blue conch shell.

On either side of the statue are guest rooms with beds fit for giants. Past the guest room to the west is a store room full of giant supplies. 

Orsik examines the wine barrels to see if there is any particularly good vintage, but there is not. 

# Chapter 67 Orsik looks for love in all of the wrong places. 

Having explored the rooms to the north, the 3HPP decides to go upstairs. 

Orsik, Vardis, Thistle, and Unla Kee emerge from the 40-foot-wide hall. Their careful footsteps ensure that no sound is made as they walk on the polished stone floor. As they round the corner, the sight that greets them is breathtaking.

The great hall stretches before them, a cathedral of hewn rock bathed in the ethereal glow of a colossal crystal window. The ceiling soars high above, vaulted and adorned with intricate carvings that dance in the light. Pillars, thick as ancient trees, rose from the floor, their surfaces encrusted with barnacles and draped in vibrant coral.

Looking through the window, they realize that Orsik was right; they are deep under water. Unla Kee whispers, “How does light get to this deep a place?” Vardis rolls his eyes, “Magic.” 

Two figures stood bathed in the spotlight on a raised platform to the north. One, tall and statuesque, her voice soaring with the power of a storm, is Mirran, the eldest daughter of King Hekaton. The other, Nym, her sister, moves with practiced grace. Her whalebone mallets tapa rhythmic melody on the shell of a hulking crab, its body a living instrument that pulses with each note.

Below them, a gathering of giants mingled, their forms casting long shadows in the flickering light. A cloud giant, his beard a wispy cloud, stood in animated conversation with a fire giant, her eyes burning with an inner fire. His breath frosted the air, and a frost giant shared a laugh with a stone giant, his skin as rough as granite.

Orsik stares for a moment and boldly walks in. His footsteps echo over the music. He coughs. The music stops. Everyone stares at him. 

He announces, “I am looking for Serissa. I joined this “Dwarf and Giants that Love Each Other” matchmaking service, and when we were matched, I was told to come here. It’s been a bit of a journey, and I wondered where she was.” 

There is silence. 

And then Mirran laughs and says, “My goodness, I am sure Queen Serissa will be delighted that love came through. But she’s not ready to meet anyone right now, and indeed, not a manly dwarf like you. She wasn’t expecting you! Now, she has important activities of state to manage, and I know our sister will want to look her best for her new beau.” 

She turns to Nym, “Nym, when do you think she will be free?” 

“In a few hours?” whispers the other sister. 

Orsik looks around and realizes that they are overmatched. He turns to Unla Kee - “You got anything?” The halfling surprisingly shakes his head. 

“Well, I guess we’ll wait. Has she said anything about me?” 

Nym smiles evilly, “She was like our mother, always interested in the little people. We knew she had a fascination, but not this. Just like her mother.” 

Orsik groans, “Casual racism.” 

Vardis corrects him, “It’s fetishism.” 

At that, Nym can’t shut up, “It is not a fetish. It is racism. Giants are superior to little people.” 

Orsik, playing the role of a man who shows up to pick his girlfriend up only to discover the family disapproves, grabs Vardis and mutters, “We’ll go downstairs and wait for the queen.” 

The two storm giant guards march them downstairs to the easternmost hallway, where guestrooms for the little people exist. 

They each settled in for the night. 

Just then, Mirran arrives in the doorway.

Vardis turns to her, “Who designed this, midgets?” 

“It was my mother. She had a thing for your kind.” Mirran sounds as sweet as possible. The 3HPP suspects everything.  

“She’s waiting for you! Come, come!” 

Vardis whispers to Orsik, “How big of a trap is this?” 

“We’re going to find out.” 

They walk towards the large cavernous room with the pool and teleportation circle. With a wave of her hand, it becomes brightly lit. And there are four giants, the same giants that were upstairs. 

She turns to them as she rushes past them, “Show your loyalty to your queen, and whoever might become queen, by getting rid of these unwanted guests that would claim that a Storm Giant would want to mate with a,” and she pauses as her face clearly shows her disgust, “dwarf.” 

Unla Kee is confused, “Are they the unwanted guests?” 

The four giants laugh and advance. 

Thistle mutters, “I think we are.” 

Orsik shouts, “The door to the guest room is too big for them to march in; let’s go in there and fight from there.”

Vardis nods and starts to run back. Thistle and Unla Kee pause and then follow him. 

Unla Kee turns to Orsik, “So we doing the bag of holding trick?” 

Orsik looks at him and says, “No.” 

As Mirren rushes past the advancing giants, she shouts in Giant, “They are assassins. Kill them all. Show your loyalty.” 

Orsik turns to the rest of the crew, “We have been accused of worse.” 

They all nod. 

Thistle, who has experience in court intrigues, reflects on what is happening. “The situation reminds me of the time that Queen Titania made a fool of that formorian,” he thinks. That’s when it clicked, and just in time, Orsik was getting ready to make a cardinal sin, “Don’t kill the sister.” 

“Why?”

“Because if we kill her, then the Queen will believe the other sister that we are assassins.” 

Orsik looks at him, “Damn, that’s cold.” 

Unla Kee shouts, “So, everyone but the Storm Giant?” 

Sizzling with energy, a bolt of lightning leaps from his hand, racing towards the fire giant. But why stop at just the one? Upon contact, it splits, and three new currents of electricity ricochet off to other giants.

The giants laugh. And then the fire giant bellows, “Well played, little one. But these paltry tricks of yours are not enough to stop four lords of the giants. I am Duchess Tartha, Count Vaal, and to my right is the Jarl Hellenhild, and the quiet one is Thane Braxow. You have come to kill the Queen, and we will do our duty and kill you.” 

Unla Kee runs towards the room's safety but still hears the tail end of the exposition. 

Orsik touches the giant slayer blade Lord Zymorven granted him, whispering, “Well, this is what I saved his son for.” But first, he thinks, “Some magic.” 

He takes a moment to say a few short syllables quickly, then shudders with a chill as the magical power of Expeditious Retreat raises the hair on his neck and arms. He is unsure if the world is a little slower or he is just faster—or both—but it seems each step he takes can carry him farther than before.

With that, he rushes towards the Stone Giant Thane and swings the sword twice, striking him both times.  The giant is unaffected by the dwarf’s magic sword, other than the bleeding. Orsik growls, focusing his energy on attacking and striking twice more. This time, the Giant succumbs to the sword's power and falls prone. And with him prone, Orsik strikes one more time and kills him. 

Duchess Tartha laughs, “So this will be a challenge. Perhaps the Queen did see something in you. But I can assure you you will not see her in this life.” 

She prepares to strike him and is stunned when he moves out of her reach. 

Mirren, who was near him, took a swipe as he ran but missed. 

Vardis casts a wall of fire across the hallway entrance, separating him and the rest of the 3HPP from the giants. 

Count Vaal clicks his tongue, and his feet break free of the bonds of gravity and rocket into the air, unbound by such petty things as natural laws.

Orsik grumbles, “Flying? Can cloud giants fly? I should have known that.” 

Count Vaal mocks him, “Yes, little one, you should have.” 

The Duchess stares at the fire, marches right through unaffected, and hits Thistle with a slash of her giant sword. 

As she does, she also walks by Orsik, who strikes her. 

As arcane syllables tumble from Thistle’s mouth, jolts of electricity crackle about his weapon, warping it into an actinic new form. The air itself seems to spark with anticipation, charged with the raw energy of a storm. It is no mundane projectile, instead a manifestation of the tempest. It is chained destruction, ready to fly.

And fly it does, exploding on Tartha. 

Then, before she can recover, another of his arrows strikes her. 

And then Thuul opens his mouth, and a rush of ice like that of an ice dragon comes out of his mouth and hits the Fire Giant. 

Vardis, Orsi, K, and Unla Kee turn to the Ranger. “Thuul can do that?” 

Thistle shrugs. 

Unla Kee remembers how Thistle when fighting at the climactic battle of Parnast, chose not to use his bow as efficiently as he could. Unla Kee thought that Thistle had a tendency to create challenges that he could overcome instead of attacking with all of his abilities at once. 

But then the mage thinks some more, “No, he’s hiding his abilities so that if and when one of us decides to betray him, we are surprised by what he can do.” Thus, Unla Kee’s estimation of Thistle grows.

As a parting gift before she leaves, Merrin hurls a lightning bolt that lands on Vardis and strikes Unla Kee as well, as the electric charge spreads around Vardis. The wizard and cleric are hurt, but not too severely. 

The Jarl stares at the wall of fire. She can’t run into it and worries that Firegiant Duchess will steal the glory. But then she notices Thistle flying above the wall. And throws a rock. The boulder misses the target by a lot. 

Vaal stares at her, mouth agape, and Tartha taunts her. Hellenhild mutters, “I was trying some new powder for a better grip.” 

Unla Kee, dazed by the lightning, pokes his head out and sends a mote of fire from his hands streaking past Tartha to a point equidistant between Count Vaal and Duchess Tartha. The mote leaves harmless sparkles in its wake. But when it arrives at its final destination, it explodes with fury. 

Tartha was just in range, but the fire didn’t affect her; instead, it seemed to invigorate her as for Vaal and Hellenhild, the fire sears. Hellenhild is beginning to look worse. 

Orsik maneuvers to strike Tartha, but as he hits her, he remembers that his flaming sword is less effective against the Fire Giant. The giantess resists the sword's magic from the first strike, but the second strike leaves her prone. Orsik moves away from her. 

Vardis is about to cast a guiding bolt when Unla Kee giggles. 

“Anything you would like to share?”

“Cloud Giants can not only fly but cast misty step.” 

Vardis moans, “

# Chapter 68 - Into the Maelstorm
# Vardis
Vardis stares, his mouth ajar, his sword hanging limp in his hand, and mutters, “The fairy’s creature can now breathe out death and destruction.” 
“Do it again,” he mutters. But the unconscious Thistle needs to be alive for Thuul to breathe again. 
Down the hallway, his wall of fire burned. It cast a bright light towards him and radiated only heat on the other side where his friend Orsic was fighting.
“He’s alone with two giants,” thinks the cleric of Corellon. “Ever since Orsik became smarter, he hasn’t liked his chosen career. It’s like he was too dumb to be anything but a sellsword, and now that he can be anything, he resents those choices. It’s almost as if he has a death wish.”
But the sound of the giant roaring beyond the wall of fire focuses his mind on the here and now. “Flamestrike should do the trick.” 
And then he remembers the endless debates in Waterdeep at the Temples before his heretical views on the true nature of the responsibilities of the Clerics of Corellon made him persona non-grata about how Flamestrike used to be more potent in the past. But after he ended the significant sundering era, Ao weakened that magic’s power. 
It was why he had followed the light aspect of Corellon to use Fireballs.
“Hold on.” 
And a fireball incinerates the Frost Giant and injures the Cloud Giant. 
Vardis mutters, “Orsik is still alive.” And then he cowers inside the doorway to the guestroom. 
And then he hears the crunch of Count Vaal’s morning star on Orsik and thinks, “Is Orsik still alive?”
Then he overhears the Dwarf taunting his opponent, “That hurt, but you did swing that morning star with grace and style. So I will give you that.” 
Vardis notices that Thuul is trying to figure out what to do, and he points to the downed fairy and urges the guard, Drake, to help Thistle. 
The creature mumbles and agrees.
Vardis wonders about the situation outside in the great hall. He overhears his companion’s shout. 
Orsik  - “This monster is still alive, and we must hurt it a lot.” 
Unla Kee – “Take that.” A shimmering glow surrounds his head, and then the cloud giant screams in agony and floats in the air, wobbling.
Vardis sees two hand axes impale themselves on the giant. 
And then he thinks, “Good thing I chose to follow the light, eh Corellon?” His hands move apart in a semicircle, from which, like spokes of a wheel, jet lances of pure flame toward the Count. 
He hears Thistle’s moans as the Healing Word’s effect wakes the ranger. 
And then Vardis smiles as one of the half pints unleashes their magical arrows on Count Vaal. The cloud giant, incapacitated by the other half-pint, barely notices the pain from the arrows as he grabs his head, still reeling from the psychic lance. 
“Shit, it’s Unla Kee’s turn,” he mutters. “A simple magic missile, and we’re done.” 
But that, as Vardis knows, would be too easy. 
“Say this about Unla Kee; say he won’t do the easy thing.” 
But this time, the self-centered wizard strikes the countdown with a few blasts of power. 
Vardis sighs in relief and blinks the wall of fire out of existence. 
He notices a bloodied Orsik still standing surrounded by the corpses of the giant lords. 
“Is he happy or sad? Hard to tell.” 
## Orsik
“Still alive,” he thinks. It’s said with the grim satisfaction of being alive and the bitter regret that he’s still a fighter. 
“After a well-fought battle, the one good thing is the plunder.” On Vaal, he finds a diamond-studded platinum earring and a vest of platinum embroidery. Tied to his belt is a teleportation conch and a potion of water breathing. On the Fire Giant, Duke Tartha finds a necklace of gilded Dragon’s teeth and another conch. Hellenhild finds a cloak of white dragon scales and another conch on the Frost Giant Jarl. And finally, on the Stone Giant Thane Braxow he rips out 15 gemstones embedded in his flesh. He looks at his friends. “12,000 gold pieces and some giant toenails.” 
Vardis shouts, “We need to move them from here.”
Orsik is about to get angry but then remembers that being the smartest person in the room isn’t always about having the right idea but about recognizing the right idea from other people and amplifying it. 
“The storeroom?” 
Orsik then starts the painful process of dragging the bloody and batter bodies north past the guestrooms for Giants and into the storeroom. 
And just in time, Mirran descends the staircase to see why no noise and no giant lords are coming up to celebrate. 
Orsik, hiding, hears her step into the Portal Chamber. She mutters, “They are all gone. Well, I suppose they killed those little people and decided to go back to their homelands and spread rumors about Serissa.”
He then barely can overhear Nin screaming something about loose ends at her sister. 
And with that he joins the rest of the 3HPP for a long rest in the guest chambers. 
A few hours later, as they prepare to head out, Orsik turns to his followers. 
“So our situation is the following – Serissa is the current Queen. The current Queen is being undermined by her two sisters, Mirran and Nym. Serissa believes her father is still alive and may have information that we need. Serissa’s mom was interested in an accommodation with the little people.”
He notices that Vardis is staring at him, “My plan was to make it appear that Serissa was also interested in the little people by claiming that I was a suitor she had found through a matchmaker in Waterdeep.” 

Orsik glares at his friend, who tries hard not to laugh. 
“What we need to do is get to the Queen. I recommend a full front assault.” 
He looks at Vardis, who is sad and concerned, Thistle, who just says no, and Unla Kee, who is already running towards the stairs to confront a castle full of storm giants and pauses. 
“Why?”
“Because we don’t all have a death wish, buddy.” 
## Thistle
After the foolish plan of a full frontal assault was discarded, Thistle cast pass without trace and sneaked alone upstairs to the Great Hall. 
Unlike the day before, there is no adoring crowd of Giant Lords watching the two sisters play their living organ and sing their frightful tunes, just a pair of bored guards. 
As he wonders how he will head past the two hill giants guarding the entrance to the throne room, Nym slams her fist into the living organ and shouts, “I must go speak to the Queen,” and heads to the door.”
“An opportunity!” 
He flies close to the ceiling, too small to be noticed and impossibly stealthy, and sneaks past the guards and into Maelstorm’s throne room. He flies through an archway and enters a large amphitheater. Stone bleachers overlook an oblong chamber with a 50-foot-deep seawater pool near its center. 
Facing the pool and the doors are three enormous thrones. The middle is carved from a solid chunk of obsidian, has four blue dragon skulls affixed to its base, and floats 1 foot above the floor.
“What did Joon say about that throne? Damn it, I can’t remember.” 
He then examines the people sitting on the thrones. In the middle is the Queen.
Serissa is in the rough equivalent of a Giant’s twenties. She exudes both regal authority and youthful vigor. She stands at an imposing height of over 20 feet, a statuesque and powerful figure. Her long, flowing hair is a vibrant shade of silver blue, cascading down her back like a waterfall of moonlight. Her eyes, the color of a stormy sea, have an intensity that speaks of both wisdom and the tempestuous nature of her giant lineage.
Her skin is a pale, almost luminescent blue, with a faint shimmer that reflects the lightning she commands. She wears a magnificent gown crafted from delicate, ethereal fabrics that shift and change like the clouds in a stormy sky. The gown is adorned with intricate patterns of gold and silver, reminiscent of lightning bolts and thunderclouds.
On her head rests a crown of enchanted silver encrusted with precious gems that glow with an inner light. The crown is both a symbol of her authority and a conduit for her elemental powers. Around her neck, she wears a necklace of pearls and sapphires, each stone holding a fragment of storm magic.
Her demeanor is a blend of youthful curiosity and the commanding presence of a ruler. She moves with the grace of a dancer and the strength of a warrior, every step echoing with the faint rumble of distant thunder. 
When she speaks, her voice is like the rolling thunder, commanding attention and respect from all who hear it. Yet, her tone has a softness, a hint of the gentle rain that follows a storm, revealing her compassionate side.
Serissa is a formidable and inspiring figure, embodying the raw power of the storm and the grace of a true leader. 
Thistle realizes that there is a reason why she and not her sisters are Queen. 
To the left is whom he comes to learn is Imperator Uthor, a middle-aged Storm Giant his presence exuding an air of authority and experience. Once a vibrant silver, his long hair has faded to a more subdued steel-gray, tied back neatly to reveal a face etched with the lines of age and wisdom.
His eyes, a piercing shade of stormy gray, hold a perpetual look of condescension as he gazes at Queen Serissa from her right. His skin, a deeper shade of blue than the Queen’s, is marked with faint scars and lines, testaments to a life of service and battle.
He wears a finely crafted deep blue and silver tunic adorned with intricate patterns that mimic the chaotic beauty of a thunderstorm. The tunic is paired with a belt made of enchanted leather, fastened with a large, ornate buckle that symbolizes his rank and authority within the court.
He wields a magnificent trident in his right hand, its three prongs crackling with latent electricity. The trident symbolizes his status and a weapon of immense power, its shaft made of a rare, sturdy metal that glows faintly with an inner light. The prongs are sharp and gleaming, ready to unleash the fury of a storm at a moment’s notice.
His rigid and formal stance reflects a lifetime of discipline and duty. Despite his age, his body is still muscular and strong, a testament to his enduring prowess. His left-hand rests casually on an armrest, fingers tapping lightly as he looks at Serissa. 
His voice, when he speaks, is deep and resonant, carrying the weight of authority and experience. There is a slight sneer in his tone, a hint of the condescension he feels towards the young Queen that matches his look. 
But the person on the left startles Thistle, a giant Adminond Kroskas. Thistle is tempted to strike but decides that the futility of the action makes this the wrong time and place to do anything. 
He flies closer to overhear the conversation. 
Nym – What new way will you disgrace us? Will you follow our mother to her death with your love of little people? At least she had the decency to care about them as a people, not to mate with them. You gave a dwarf a conch to come to here to go out on a date? Who helped you? A matchmaker for the rich and stupid of Waterdeep? Was it “Ok Ogre?” or was it “The short for the Tall?” 
Uthor – \*cough\* My Queen, far be it for me to suggest. 
Serissa snars – “They are liars.” 
Merissa soothingly – “We agreed. We felt they were assassins and had them killed.” 
Adminod – “Can you describe them?” 
Adimond freezes when he realizes this isn’t some random group but the 3HPP. 
Adimond – “Did you find the bodies?” 
Nym – “The Frost and Fire giants eat little people.” 
Serissa squirms. 
Nym – “I am sure they are well digested.” 
Imperator said in a barely disguised voice of irritation, “Mirran and Nym, why don’t you confirm these events? I wouldn’t want this to be another misunderstanding.” 
Adimond – “I agree Imperator.” 
He then turns to Serissa, “My Queen, the experiment to create a new dragon-fairy hybrid is proceeding. And I believe I will have a force loyal to you that will always guarantee the Storm Giant’s position in the Ordning.” 
Thistle mutters, “I like it when the bad guys tell us their plans.” 
Thistle knows that this blue dragon hasn’t survived this long without an exit strategy. The creature survived the collapse of the oracle, it must have an escape route. It’s then that Thistle remembers what Joon said about the Maelstorm. The Maelstrom has a series of interconnected tunnels. Adimond’s exit strategy is the pool of water in front of them. 
Serissa waves her hand at Adimond – “Yes, I know about your experiments. But if we found my father, we wouldn’t need them. I need someone I could trust to go look for him.”
The Imperator sighs – “We have sent so many, your highness. The clues you have provided have gotten us nowhere.” 
Thistle realizes that the Imperator likes having this Queen in power, a Queen he can manipulate. 
Thistle looks over the scene and realizes Adimond and the two sisters are allies. The blue dragon casually directs them to speak, supporting their arguments and indicating when to act. 
Adimond - my dearest, although I know your complaints and this shocking discovery of your sister’s decision to look for love in all the wrong places.
Mirran pouts - They weren’t suitors. They were cheaters 
She stamps her foot down. 
Mirran - They were assassins. We dealt with him. See, Imperator, we should be Queen, not Sarissa.

Serissa screams and attempts to get up from her throne, but the Imperator grabs her arm and forces her to reconsider. 
Adimond says – Perhaps a change in leadership might be appropriate if the Queen can not restrain her temper, Uthor?
Imperator – “Do not confuse your privilege with the right to speak about these matters. 
Adimond – “I am just an alchemist doing the Queen’s bidding.” 
## Unla Kee
Unla Kee enjoyed swimming, especially underwater, and he most definitely enjoyed all of that when he could breathe underwater.
After Thistle had informed them that the great enemy was in the throne room, the group debated their options.
Orsik, to Unla Kee’s surprise, wanted to charge. But his best friend Vardis and Thistle, once again, being the party poopers he knew they were, pushed back.
Instead, they agreed to sneak into the throne room through the waterways that linked the Maelstorm. 
In the portal chamber, there was a pool that led to an underwater portcullis. The portcullis was intended for much larger creatures than the Half-Pints. The poor pint had to squeeze through, barely avoiding getting cut on some barnacles. 
Through the portcullis, he arrives at what gave this palace its name. 
“The magnificent vortex—a true testament to the raw, untamed power that only I could hope to wield someday. I stand at the edge of this half-mile-wide whirlpool, watching with a smirk as it drags hapless ships and their pitiful crews to a watery grave. Imagine their terror, knowing they are powerless against such a force. Yet, with my unparalleled intellect and unmatched cunning, I see through to the heart of its mystery: a 100-foot-deep, 180-foot-wide sinkhole at the ocean’s bottom. “ 
He looks down and thinks, “The sinkhole is a marvel, a mere 100 feet deep and 180 feet wide. Child’s play for someone of my extraordinary capabilities. At its nadir lies a 50-foot-diameter drain, the linchpin of this aquatic spectacle. Plugging it would dissolve the vortex, but naturally, that’s far too simple for one as brilliant as me. The drain is covered by an iron grate, crusted with barnacles and 10-foot-wide gaps—perfect for any of my numerous plans.”
And then he cackles as he thinks - In these dark, oppressive depths, I am the master of my fate. The sinkhole’s secrets, its treasures, its power—all of it will belong to me. And when I emerge, the world will be forever changed by the sheer force of my genius.
Orsik, swimming to a tunnel on the floor, disrupted his reverie. Staring at Vardis, he realizes that the Dwarf has discovered that the treasure is no longer with the ships but was stolen by some crabs. Crabs Orsik intends to kill.
“I can’t have him have all of the fun…” 

# Chapter 69 Seaworld 



Vardis, Thistle, and Unla Kee watch as Orsik advances on the crabs. They are wasting valuable time and resources for Orsik to get some gold.

Thistle looks for a mechanism to cause the portcullis to fall but realizes that the storm giants use brute strength. Raising the gates by hand is more straightforward as they can swim underwater for protracted periods and are very strong. 

Vardis notices that the Crabs have noticed Orsik. He grabs some sulfur and bat guano, points to the right between them, and a mote of magical fire rushes forward. It stops in midwater, and a burst of fire engulfs the two beasts. 

“Magic operates very differently than fire underwater.” 

Thistle signals to Thuul, and while the crabs are still fixated on Orsik and Unla Kee, they don’t see Thistle, who hits one of them with his arrows, and Thuul, who spits acid.

Vardis waves his hand, and a single chime of a dolorous bell fills the water. He points at the remaining crab. The creature writhes under the effect of Toll the Dead. 

Then, before it can react, two more arrow bolts strike him from Thistle's hand. 

Unla Kee, impressed with the power of Toll the Dead, casts the same spell, but this time, the Crustacean ignores the awful sound. 

There is one last badly injured monstrous beast, and Orsik tosses two hand axes. The first bounces off the crab’s armor harmlessly. The second kills the creature. 

As he digs through the treasure, he shouts, “Anyone who thought this was a bad idea doesn’t get any treasure. Except Vardis, who’s my friend and always has my back.” 

The haul is unimpressive at first, just some gold pieces and a goblet, until Orsik stumbles on two obvious magic items. The first he is to learn later is a Magma Warpick. 

The second item is Weaving Wasp Needles. These needles can be used to craft clothing or to restrain an enemy. 

Orsik is very pleased with himself. 

Thistle takes stock of their current situation. They are standing on the edge of a vortex that goes up 180 feet and is over 100 feet across. At the bottom of the vortex is a drain that would require a 50-foot plug. Going through the vortex would be hard but possible. Around the bottom of the vortex are shipwrecks. The crabs and the storm giants have probably picked them clean of any valuables. 

“Let’s continue counterclockwise,” he mutters. 

A bit further down is an underground tunnel that ends in a portcullis. Looking through the portcullis, it’s clear that there is air. Right in front of the portcullis is a killer whale. 

“I don’t think it is the right way,” he explains to his allies. 

But Vardis, Orsik, and Unla Kee need to be more convinced. 

Vardis points out that Thistle is the master of infiltration, and he could look. 

And so the fairy slips through the gate. Unlike the Fire Giants, the shark is more observant, notices Thistle, and swims straight at him. 

Thistle, wanting to avoid killing another beast of nature, casts “Speak with Animals.” The creature is confused, and Thistle shouts, “We are not your enemy.” 

The shark mutters, “Nym promised better tasty snacks. You are barely a mouthful. But you will do.” 

Thistle swims up, and as he does, the Shark snaps at him, wearing him down slightly. However, Thistle escapes into the air above the pool. There, he notices a stairwell going up and snacks.

He picks up a pile of snacks and throws them in the water. The shark devours the food in glee. 

Thistle coughs, “Ahem?”

“You are feeding me. You are not Nym. Why are you feeding me?” 

“Nym sent me.” 

The shark sticks its head above the water and tries to bring Thistle into focus, “You are much smaller than many of Nym’s friends. And I am so sorry for biting you. They don’t usually come that way. She comes from the shore.” 

Thistle asks, “Have you seen any dragons?”

The shark explodes - “ There's a dragon here? If there had been a dragon, I would have defended Nym. I would have killed it. Do you see any dragon bones? Of course, you don't. What are you saying about there's a dragon? Where's the dragon?”

Thistle tries to explain, “The dragon is a friend of Nym’s.” 

This confuses the shark, and it shouts, “If you have more fish, I’ll be happy to talk; if not, this conversation has become very boring.” 

Meanwhile, the rest of the 3HPP swim past the whale onto the shore. 

After devouring his snacks, the shark declares in a large, imperious voice, “I am Nym's Whale. I am her favorite pet. Nobody comes here other than her. I am the great whale. I protect Nym while she swims. “ 

At this point, the shark gets annoyed. “Are you sure you are a friend of Nym’s? I have never seen anyone but her here.” 

“I am usually invisible.” 

The shark snorts, “I don’t think you are her friend. But you fed me. I just want you to know that I must eat you if you get into the water. It won’t be personal.” 

Thistle watches as the creature submerges. 

He then looks around and declares, “The stairway going up probably goes to her quarters. I wonder what’s down the south hallway?” 

The hallway leads to an air-filled cave under 5 feet of water. A staircase in the south walls rises to another cavern. Thistle admires the cultivated coral garden. Coral growths sprout from walls and rock formations, all colorful and immense.

Thistle knows this coral is worth a fortune, but Adimond is here. Mining it would take time, resources, and risk exposure. He turns to the rest of the 3HPP and says, “This coral is worthless.” 

The ranger floats through the room past a stalagmite. A fleeting moment of horror blanks out all rational thought: the stalagmite he is passing has sprouted a single red, blinking eye above a slobbering, fanged mouth. Tendrils leap from its body, whipping out to snag the unwary for its next meal, the rocky hide proof against all but the strongest blows.

The tendrils reach out to every member of the 3HPP. Only Thistle and Unla Kee are grappled. 

It drags the ranger to its mouth and bites, but the fair dodges the teeth. 

Vardis reaches for the guano and sulfur, carefully measures the room, and arranges the fireball so that the fiery explosion reaches but does not touch Thistle. 

Orsik rushes in and strikes with his sword, but the tough hide stops his blows. 

Thistle tries to get free and fails. 

Unla Kee also prepares a fireball and uses Vardis’s previously carefully positioned fireball for his. And once again, the room explodes in fire, and not one of Thistle’s whiskers is singed. 

The creature flails at Vardis and Orsik to no effect. 

Vardis casts Toll the Dead, but the creature can see through the evil of the mournful sound of the bell and is unaffected. 

Unla Kee scoffs at Vardis and reaches for his bag of guano and sulfur. Once again, the room fills with a fiery explosion. The creature is now clearly hurt but still alive. 

Orsik then whacks the creature with his sword, and it dies. He is pleased with himself. He looks for treasure but finds nothing. He turns to Thistle and asks, " Are you sure this Coral is worthless?”

“Yes.” 

With that, the ranger heads towards the southern passageway. The passageway opens into another large room with a pool and a shark. The room also has several exits: a passageway to the southeast blocked by a portcullis, a spiral staircase going up, and a staircase going west. Around the room are cases of whale food. In the southeast corner is an anemone chest. 

Thistle throws some fish into the water. The shark starts chomping and then pokes its head up. It shouts, “Well, hello there. You are tiny.” 

“I am a friend.” 

“Yes, you are. Mirran usually feeds me. Why are you here? Is she okay?” 

“Phew. Mirran’s my pet.”

“Your pet?” 

“Yes. She needs this awkward space with no water to survive, so I bring her back whenever she goes out.” 

“Do you have any other pets?” 

“No, just the one. Having more pets is too much work. They start to squabble and demand things. I’m very much a one pet animal.” 

“Does she bring any pets?”

“Oh no. Although she spends much time hitting something outside the pool.” 

Thistle takes note of the sparring dummy down the west passageway. He asks the whale, “Where does she come from when she arrives?” 

The shark points his nose to the spiral staircase and continues, “Whenever she needs to rest and relax after a frustrating day, she comes here. And I take my pet out for a ride so it can relax.”

Thistle wonders if the shark has a mechanism to open the portcullis, and it says, “No. And if I did, I wouldn’t use it; otherwise, the pet would not feel in control. I am pretty progressive. By the way, have you met the other whale? It thinks it is the pet. A disgrace.” 

While Thistle was talking to the whale, Unla Kee explored the anemone. He recognized it as an engineered animal used to store valuables. As he looked at it some more, he noticed it was trapped. Opening it would require a delicate and careful application of touches. Unla Kee proceeded and was successful in opening the chest. 

He looks inside and finds 400 platinum pieces from various countries.

Thistle, done talking to the whale, floats down and turns to his team - “Do we wait here for Mirren to come down, or do we go up?” 

Unla Kee is already moving up the spiral staircase. 

Thistle groans, “Up it is…”

As they go up the spiral staircase, Thistle notices a secret door. 

# Chapter 70 Confronting Adimond and a lot of exposition


Thistle stands at the door and prepares to listen. 

Unla Kee tries to push past him to open the door, but Thistle blocks him. 

The fairy hears nothing but wind and water and carefully opens the door.

Inside is the armory containing weapons and armor sized for storm giants. Thistle quickly searches and finds four quarterstaffs carved from sunken ship masts, a large barrel holding six tridents, an open crate containing ten coral-encrusted rocks, and a storm giant-sized mannequin wearing a golden suit of scale mail. Hanging on the walls are three enormous shields made from dragon turtle shells and six massive greatswords with coral-inlaid hilts and sharpened whalebone blades lined with shark teeth. The shields and the weapons aren’t precious, especially given their weight.

Orsik and Thistle immediately get into a debate about whether they should steal the golden armor. Thistle argues about the sagacity of stealing from the Queen. Orsik takes the position they won’t notice. Finally, they agree that although it is a profitable heist, they should conserve resources and avoid accumulating enemies until after they deal with Adimond Kroskas. 

And with that, Thistle takes the door to the east. Three descending sets of stairs lead to submerged areas southeast, north, and northeast of the armory. 

Orsik turns left and arrives in a room full of submerged caves. Four fully submerged caves contain sharks. A passage heading northeast opens up into the vortex area. 

When Thistle refers to the Sharks as fish, Orisk corrects him, “These are mammals.” 

The fairy, a bit of a naturalist, looks at him aghast, “Sharks are fish.”

To which Orisk responds, “I have a headband of intellect, and I say that these are mammals, not fish.” 

Thistle is angry: “No, a shark, although having some attributes similar to a mammal's, is not a fish.” 

Somehow, cooler heads prevail, and they don’t come to blows. 

While this is going on, no one is paying attention to Unla Kee, who notices some giant clams and starts to poke and prod at them. 

At that point, Thistle notices Unla Kee struggling to open the clam and offers his sword. Inside the clam is a grapefruit-sized pearl worth 500 gold. 

As Unla Kee is about to reach for it, Thistle slams the clam shut, “After we deal with Adimond.” 
Orsik, seeing how Thistle is very protective of the Queen’s treasure, opens the other clam behind his back and finds a crystal ball. He stuffs it into his Bag of Holding. Later, he will act surprised that he found it. 

Thistle is not only distracted by Unla Kee but also by the passage to the Vortex. 

At this point, Unla Kee, demonstrating his uncanny ability to put things together, observes, “These pools are all connected to the Vortex. If we go through the Vortex, we can pop up in the throne room.” 

Thistle turns to the 3HPP, and they shout, “It’s your moment, man, let’s do this.” 

They swim through the Vortex, up through the pool, and arrive in the middle of the throne room. The imperator and Queen Serissa are stunned—Iymrith (or Adimond Kroskas) or whatever name she is using now glares. They were arguing.

Orsik charges and strikes Iymrith-Storm Giant. Both times, his sword strikes deep. Iymrith-Giantess is shocked. 

But not as shocked as the storm giants in the room are when she turns into a blue dragon and uses her wings to buffet everyone. 

Once again, Vardis reaches for the bat guano and sulfur, points his finger at an epicenter of the blast that will only affect the dragon, watches as a streak of lights flies out, and then basks in the warmth of the exploding blast. 

Iymrith sends out wave after wave of dragon fear. All but Orsik and Thistle succumb. 

The dragon heads to the pool, shoves Thistle aside and dives into the water. The last words are, " This is not the end of this.”

The Imperator and Queen Serissa recover from their shock and start demanding answers. Just then, Mirren and Nym charge in. 

Finally, Queen Serissa raises her voice with a regal bearing and demands silence. She then asks, “Could someone please explain to us what is going on?” 

Thistle screams - “He’s getting away!” 

Orsik shouts - “He’s afraid of us!” 

Vardis points out calmly - “You had a dragon here, did you know that?” 

Queen Serissa waves her hand again, demanding silence, “Could someone explain, very carefully and in simple language, what the hell is going on?” 

Thistle flies towards her, “That blue dragon is one of the leaders of the Cult of the Dragon. And we were tracking him down.” 

Queen Serissa turns to her sisters, “Did you know this creature was a dragon? Did you knowingly ally yourselves with a dragon?” 

Just then, more Storm Giants show up. 

Mirren and Nym vigorously shake their heads and mutter apologetics. 

Thistle shouts, “He’s getting away!!” 

Queen Serissa waves her hand dismissively, “That is of no concern to us. What is of concern is who the dragon is and who you are.” 

“Enemy of the dragon1” 

The Imperator squints at Thistle, trying to figure out how this tiny fairy could be a threat to an Ancient Blue Dragon. 

“I am its nemesis!” the fairy shouts. 

The imperator, who is over 30 feet tall, examines the 3-foot fairy and tries hard not to laugh. 

Serissa turns to Orsik, “Brave knight?” 

Orsik sighs, “The Dragon’s name is Adimond Kroskas. We’ve been tracking Adimond. He’s a blue dragon in disguise who has infiltrated your court. And we are here to save you.”

Silence.

Mirren breaks the silence, “They were trying to assassinate you.” 

The Imperator cuts her off, “I think we have heard enough from you. Guards take them to their quarters.” The queen’s sister protests but moves meekly away.

Orsik then plays a game: “Also, Serissa, I think we were going to go on a date. Did your sisters tell you about that?” 

The Queen’s eyes bulge.
“We can discuss it later.” 

She stares at him, confused, and then realizes that he could be helpful to her.

Thistle steps in, “We hear your father is missing. Can we help you find him?” 

The Imperator rolls his eyes - “Nonsense.”

Queen Serissa uses a very dismissive tone of voice: “Weren’t you the one who suggested we take Adimond Kroskas’ input and support into the coming collapse of the Order?”

“I didn’t know,” he stammers. 

She twists the knife, “Isn’t it your job to know.” 

The Imperator stumbles, “He confused us all. His magics were powerful.” 

Thistle screams - “He didn't confuse us!” 

Orsik shouts - “He didn’t confuse me.” 

Vardis calmly notes - “We weren’t confused.” 

Serissa once again calls for silence, “And this is why we must ally with the little people. “ 

Orsik is annoyed, “Little people? I’ve spent a lot of time in Silverymoon.” 

The Queen gets the innuendo and rolls her eyes, “Ah yes, those bigots. My comment is about relative size, not a critique.” She pauses, “If I’m not mistaken, you refer to us as giants?” 

“Touche.”

“I prefer the term non-giant.” 

“Me too.”

Serissa then explains the correct pronunciation of the term for giants by giants. This could have turned into an excellent linguistics discussion on giant words, but the 3HPP realizes that the term requires giant-sized lungs to be said correctly. And since they can’t pronounce it, they show little enthusiasm. Queen Serissa picks up on their disinterest and moves on. 

The queen mutters, “Unfortunately, with Adimond gone, our attempt to create a weapon against the other giants to retain power is no longer available. It was a stopgap until we found my father.” 

She pauses thoughtfully, “I disagreed with the Imperator but had no choice. Adminod Kroskas claimed his fairy/dragon hybridization experiments would create a new breed of dragons that we could use as a weapon against the other giants.” 

Thistle asks hopefully, “Did he experiment here?”

“No, and these experiments are no longer important. We must find my father.” 

Orsik interjects, “It’s also important to hear about your attempt to create super mutants.” 

“And what about my girlfriend,” asks Thistle. 

“Girlfriend?” 

“My girlfriend is Leela, and she was captured along with me and Thuul. Thuul and I escaped as the cult disintegrated. Adimond got away and took a bunch of people, including her.”

The queen takes a moment to think, “You think he was using them in experiments?” She sighs, “Unfortunately, only my father knows where Iymrith lives.” 

Orsik asks, “So Thistle, you are dating a fairy-dragon hybrid super-mutant that an ancient blue dragon bred to enable one group in a giant civil war to secure its prominence?”

“There is no evidence of that.” 

“What about the caravans?” 

“There is no evidence of her being a fairy-dragon hybrid.” 

Orsik rolls his eyes. 

The Imperator says, “I think it is time I provided some historical context. Adimond Kroskas, or Iymrith or the Doom of the Desert, has been conducting hybridization experiments since - forever. It is rumored that he created Drakes. His experiments on fairy-dragon hybridization were part of a deal he had made with Tiamat during the war of the dragons. When Tiamat’s plans were thwarted, Adimond approached us. We did not know he was a dragon.”

Vardis coughs loudly. 

“Before the shattering of the Ordning,” he looks at the 3HPP condescendingly and asks, “You are familiar with the Ordning, I presume? Yes.” Then he continues with his professorial tone, “King Hekaton was arguably the most powerful of all Storm Giants. From this citadel, deep within the trackless sea, he presided over a court that included representatives of every race of giant, from mighty Storm Giants to Hill Giants.” 

Orsik checks to see if the Hill Giant guards object, but they don’t, and mutters, “Amazing.” 

“He used the power of this throne, a gift to keep the more unruly giants in line. As long as he reigned, fear of my king's wrath and respect for the Ordning was enough to keep the lesser giants from rising against him.” 

But in recent years, King Hekaton has been convinced that the age of the Giants is past, as evidenced by the growing distance between them and their gods.

Annam, the Allfather, didn't answer prayers, and his divine offspring were out of touch, constantly waging war against one another on the outer plains. My king came to believe that the giants were no longer the rightful masters of the world. Several months ago, his fear became a reality when the ordning was shattered.

The king was profoundly shaken by the realization that we, the storm giants, might lose our epic status among giants. He did his utmost in the aftermath to hold his court together, bullying the weaker giants into submission.”

Orsik laughs at the arrogance and contempt of the comment. 

Queen Neri was particularly fond of the small folk. She visited them often before the Ordnung was sundered, rising out of the sea to meet them on the shores. Neri continually urged Hekaton to respect the small folk. Yes. And leave them alone if you cannot countenance forming alliances with them. Hekaton, inherently distrustful of the small folk, wanted nothing to do with them, but he respected his wife's desire. The queen continued to visit the small folk from time to time until the day she failed to return from one of her trips to the sword coast.  We found Neri's corpse shortly on a small island where she had been known to meet humans. It was clear she had been killed by small folk. My king wept for days before threatening to unleash his vengeance on the Coastal territories.

I could not calm his rage, so I asked Princess Sarissa for help. Sarissa, who shared her mother's affection for small folk, urged her father to uncover the truth before lashing at anyone he encountered. Thankfully, the king was swayed by his daughter's level-headedness and wisdom, and once his anger ran its course, he set out to learn how his wife met her fate. Unfortunately, King Hackathon was not as wise as before the Ordning. Many years ago, he had decided that his two eldest daughters, Mirren and Nym, were unfit to rule and that Serissa, his youngest, was his most worthy successor. 

Mirren was tempestuous and prone to emotional outbursts. Nym was the opposite, cold and unloving. The head king adored both but doubted their ability to keep the lesser giants in line. So he named Sarissa as heir apparent. He foolishly made this known to the two elder sisters years before his disappearance. 

Orisk laughs. 

“What’s so funny?” 

“There is more to ruling than subjugating other giant races.” 

Queen Serissa applauds Orsik, “And this is why we must make better contact with the small folk.” 

She pauses, “If we are to ally, what should I call you?”

“Dwarf?” 

“Very well, dwarf. And you are an elf, Vaardis?” 

“Yes.” 

She examines the fairy closely, whose height is barely the length of her eyeball, “And you are a fairy?” 

Unla Kee shouts, “Wait, I am going to put on my horn because I identify as a dragon!” 

She laughs, “You have a good sense of humor, sir. And that is why we must make common cause with the Dwarves, Elves, Fairies, and Halflings.” 

Then, more somberly, she says, “After my father disappeared and I took the throne, my sisters were furious. I thought they were willing to live with the decision. And they might have, but Adimond showed up offering magical weapons. Mirren and Nym allied with him, and who knows what they promised or offered.” 

Thistle stamps his wings, “Allying with a dragon? They are not fit to lead.” 

Serissa again asks for silence, “We have three problems. The first is we must find my father.” She pulls out a token. “This token was found on the bodies of the small people who were found dead with my mother.” 

Unla Kee takes one look at it and thinks, “The Golden Goose. The chit used on the Grand Dame. It's worth ten gold. It is from that boat in Yartar that was run by this guy named Lord Drylund. He was trying to become the new Waterbarron. A position that is elected and open after the death of the current Waterbarron. It was the kind of place only high rollers could gain admittance. But there may be a way to make some coin off this.”

He looks at it carefully, twists the fate to make Queen Serissa more gullible, and declares, “I have an idea, but I would need some money for the research.” 

The Queen is surprisingly pleased and asks, “How much?” 

“50,000.” 

The Imperator laughs, “Perposterous.” 

The Queen sighs, “Unfortunately, the resources Adimond demanded drained even our treasury.” She pauses, “There is the hideous golden armor.” 

The Imperator is speechless, “but your highness!” 

“The king will be pleased to know it was given to Unla Kee to help with his research. Just keep it between ourselves that it was melted down for research.” 

She winks at the 3HPP.

Unla Kee realizes she knows.

Then he realizes she knows, “I am still the queen of the storm giants. If you were to take this money and not produce results, there would be consequences.” 

“I wouldn’t dream of it. 

# Chapter 71 - Seeing through new eyes
And it’s just then that Adimond Kroskas’ latest manipulation of the fairy happens. 
Thistle is covered in a green, gooey substance. 
Thistle stares at the pool of water and shrieks as the gooey substance evaporates.
“This is horrible.”
The giant queen, the imperator, the storm giant guards, the other half-pints, and the pint stare at the screeching fairy. 
Thistle flies up and screams - “LOOK AT THIS!” 
They all stare at him, uncertain. 
Taking each eye in one hand, he stretches them open and shouts – “HE GAVE ME DRAGON EYES!” 
Silence. 
“THIS IS AWFUL.”
Orsic coughs, “Is that all?” 
“Oh, no, I have true seeing as well, "Thistle announces, his voice trembling with a mix of fear and excitement. True seeing, a rare and powerful ability that allows him to perceive the world as it truly is, beyond any magical illusions or disguises. 
“That’s cool.” 
“Yes, that’s cool, but dragon eyes? WHAT WILL I SAY AT THE GRAND BALL? And what will my love say.” 
The giants are trying hard to squint and see the tiny eyeballs on the 18-inch fairy. 
Queen Serissa coughs, “I am not sure I can see the issue.” 
Thistle flies into her face, opens his left eye, stretches the eyelid open, takes his left and pulls the eyelids even further apart, and then, with his right hand, points at the eyeball, which looks very dragon-like—“This is an abomination.” 
Serissa squints and stares and mutters, “It’s quite fetching!” 
“IT’S DRAGON EYES!” 
The Imperator, trying to calm everyone down, points out that it comes with a blessing: True Seeing. 
Thistle glares at him. 
And just then, Orsic steps forward - “Do I look different? I have been trying to change and want to know if True Seeing reveals anything.” 
“REALLY? THAT’S WHAT YOU ARE WORRIED ABOUT? HOW ABOUT FOCUSING ON ME FOR A MOMENT? Adimond Kroskas is messing with me again.” He starts to sob, “I am not one of his freakish experiments.” And after his sobbing stops, he mutters, “I am not a freak.” 
Orsic shrugs, “Okay, not the right time.” 
Thistle stares at him, “Orsic.” 
“Yes,” says the dwarf expectantly.
“Nothing’s changed.” 
Queen Serissa coughs, “Can we return to the matters of importance?” 
Thistle flies right into her face, opening his eyes wide, “THIS IS IMPORTANT!” 
The Queen stares at the rage in Thistle and smiles, “You are like the storm that rages. But now, I need you to focus so we can save my father and get your revenge on Adimond Kroskas.” She waves her hand at Unla Kee, “The mage claims to know where this,” she holds up a wooden coin with a golden goose on it, “comes from. He says it’s a chip used on a gambling ship in Yartar, called ‘The Golden Goose.’” 

“A booze cruiser,” corrects Unla Kee. “Didn’t let me in. Said I wasn’t good enough.” He pauses and mumbles, “Some of you thought it was because I had no money and am a diviner; I think it’s racism.” 
The Imperator coughs, “Perhaps we can return to the topic of hand.” 
“The Maelstrom has a magical device that can teleport you anywhere you want. And where would that be?” 
“Yartar, I guess?” mutters Orsik. He recalls the mess that was Yartar—the home of the Harpers that was destroyed by Adimond Kroskas, his betrayal of the Harpers, and the rescue of Lord Zymorven’s son from inside the prison where a vampire was involved in some kind of magical experimentation. Then he wonders, or was it torture? 
Unla Kee – “What about the castle? I want my castle back. We fought and clawed and worked our way to get that castle. And it’s so cool.”  
Orsic sighs, “We’ll have to deal with the Vampire.”
“I have sunbeam,” mutters Vardis.
“That will be critical.” 
With that, the group leaves the Queen and is teleported to just outside the control room of Blagothkus’ castle. As they enter, Thistle shouts, “I AM NOT A FREAK.” 
Blagothkus is taken aback. He tries to lower himself to see what Thistle is shrieking about, but he can’t get that close to the ground. So he lowers his hand and brings the 18-inch creature to a few inches from his eye. 
“Oh, you have dragon eyes! That’s so cool.” 
Thistle pouts, “My arch nemesis did this to me. It’s not cool.” 
Escarlotta asks in the soothing, silky voice of a mother to her child, “Do you have any cool powers?” 
Thistle brims with pride, “Yes.” 
“He can see me for who I am,” answers Orsik. 
“That’s so cool!” exclaims Escarlotta as if she were talking to a child trying to decide if he likes his birthday present. 
“I thought you hated dragons,” mutters Thistle. And with as much snark as Queen Titania could muster asks, “You -are- a giant, right?” 
Escarlotta sighs, “It was Blagothkus’ infatuation with power and obsession with Dragons that brought down the Ordning and killed my son.” 
Blagothkus laughs uncomfortably, the repaired relationship still fragile, “I did hate them, but not anymore. I have a relationship with them.” 
“What’s the difference,” asks Vaardis. 
“I wouldn’t invite them for dinner, whereas in the past, I would have them for dinner?”
“Live and let live?” pipes Orsik.
“As long as they live over there and we live over here. I know you think that’s segregation and it’s racist. But I don’t. I think different people want to be with their kind. And we’re all better off when they are there, and we are here.” 
Unla Kee turns to Orsik – “Racism.” Orsik nods. 
Escarlotta turns the conversation around – “You have been gone for several days. The Vampire needed to be fed. We found some places. They seem to be very happy and satiated.” 
“Little people?” asks Orsik. 
The moonstones in the control room turn bright red, almost as if Escarlotta is embarrassed. 
“Yes. There are so many of them and we had a problem. But we noticed that you didn’t like the Uthgars so we set the Vampires on a tribe of them we spotted.” 
At this point, Unla Kee says, “All of this good vs evil is not working for me. What I see is a bunch of racists and bullies who are causing harm.  Is it really about killing a giant or a drow or a human who is evil, or is it about fighting the evil in their hearts, racism?” 
“And how do we do that?” 
“Kill them all?” 
There is a pause. The control room disappears, and the sky is visible. 
“I am assuming that killing is only one option? My husband and I have reformed.” 
“I suppose,” as Unla Kee absorbs the apparent threat that Escarlotta is making, mess with her and hers, and Unla Kee might fall into the void. 
“Enough of this; tell us what happened at the Maelstrom?” 
So Thistle walks them through what happened. Both cloud giants are horrified when he gets to the part about Adimond Kroskas. 
“What did the Bane of the Desert want to do?” 
“The who?”
“The blue dragon you described is the Bane of the Desert. She is a powerful, ancient blue dragon. And she wanted to make a hybrid fey-dragon? And that fool Serissa was working with her?”
Blagothkus shudders, “We must go find Hekaton. We need his strength to defeat the dragon.” 
“But first, we need to get rid of this Vampire,” observes Unla Kee. “The Vampire is the worst kind of racist. It feeds on those it thinks are inferior. And this particular vampire, Sandesyl, wants to turn every dragon into a purer form of dragon. I mean, she’s all kind of wrong.” 
With that, the group turns to debating how to kill the Sandesyl. Eventually, they remember her offer to leave the castle if they went on a quest for her. 
Thistle argues that before attempting to kill a vampire, perhaps the quest won’t be as damning as they think and less life-threatening. 
Blagothkus notes the urgency of saving the realms and suggests that this is not the time to remove unwanted guests. 
Unla Kee will have none of that and shouts, “What do we want? It's a vampire-free zone. When do we want it? Now!” 
Thistle wins the day and approaches Sandesyl’s tower in the middle of the day. 
The vampire hides in a shroud of darkness, obscuring everything inside the tower. What Sandesyl doesn’t know is that Thistle can see in the darkness. 
“I’ve never had a fey vampire spawn. Would you like to be one?” 
Vaardis can’t resist, “A fey-vampire-dragon? A triple threat!” 
“I am not a freak!” 
The cleric is shocked, “I didn’t say you were!” 
“I am not a freak!” 
The vampire coughs, “I presume you are here to go on the quest to find something for me?” When Sandesyl sees the group be silent, she explains – “What I am looking for is a particular tome that when the apostate Severin attempted to bring Tiamat into the world, was lost.” 
When probed about its contents, she deflects – “Some arcane details of historical importance. Nothing that would interest anyone other than a member of the cult.” 
Orsik asks, “A play?” 
“A romance novel?” queries Thistle, remembering her interests. 
And with that, Sandesyl turns to one of her spawns, “It’s not the final book in the series. This is for official cult business.” 
The spawn, safely hidden in the dark, rolls his eyes at the Vampire. Thistle, who can see the expression on his face, tries hard to suppress laughter. 
The vampire continues, “The magical tome is in Xanothal’s keep. I would get it myself, but there is an interdimensional puzzle that you need to go through that is always bathed in bright sunlight. As for why it was there? Towards the ritual's climax, the Cult was split between traditionalists and apocalypse seekers. Severin needed to rally everyone to the cause and offered to hand over a Blue Dragon mask to a traditionalist who would investigate the mask. To do that investigation, he needed the book. Unfortunately for Severin, an inner circle member, Iskander betrayed the cult to the Lord’s Alliance, who sent the Heroes of the War of the Dragon to the tower. The heroes secured the mask but missed the tome. I want that time.” 
Vaardis sighs, “And let me guess; it’s one of those wizard towers with all sorts of crazy puzzles that reset after everybody escapes?” 
“Yes. “ 
“How cliché.” 
“I agree,” sighs the Vampire. “All these powerful wizards acquiring all sorts of power, and what do they do? They go and create an automatically re-stocking and re-populating wizard tower.” 
Thistle sighs, “I don’t want to help the cult of the dragon. What’s in it for us?”
