“This castle? I am a powerful and ancient vampire who has at her beck and call an ancient white dracolich. This is the only way you regain control of this castle.” 
She pauses, “Although the idea of a fey-vampire spawn is starting to look attractive.” 
After Thistle squirms, “I have a privileged position in this castle. But my problem is that I can’t control it. Escarlotta is impervious to my charms. And so I have had to make arrangements with folks who take control of the castle. Now, this opportunity to get this important historical tome has presented itself, and I think to myself, maybe a new home is just what I need. I will find myself a nice dark lair, study the lore of the cult, and re-establish some of the rituals that were interrupted due to the usurper.” 
“That’s a long-term career plan,” observes Vardis. After his unfortunate dismissal from the temples of Corellon, he has been wondering what the future holds. Her bold proposal to reanimate a cult makes him think that perhaps a personal long-term career goal of establishing an alternative believers cult of the Elf god might be an option. 
Sandesyl continues while Vardis is distracted by his musings, “After all, the true believers know that the Usurper had it wrong. Salvation is not when Tiamat enters the world, but when all dragons are turned into dracoliches and rule Faerun.” 
“Tell me about the Doom of the Desert before I answer?” 
“Ah yes, Ilymrith. She is an ancient blue dragon.” And if it was possible for a vampire to show love, Sandesyl’s eyes and body glitter with a powerful lust and passion for the creature. “She is one of the greatest of dragon kind.” And then she let slip, “With that time, I could convince her to embrace the endless life of the Dracolich and the immortality it offers.” Then she continues holding herself as if embracing the dragon in her arms, “I am told that the Doom is famous for its experiments. She has bred new kinds of dragons and new kinds of species.”
“Dragon-fey?”
At that, Sandesyl’s reverie is interrupted, and she starts to laugh, “Don’t be ridiculous; that’s impossible.” And then she turns to Thistle, “Ilymrith has been working to undermine the Giant world. Not because she cares about Giants, but she needed access to the Storm Giant’s castle to continue her experiments on hybridization of dragons,” pauses and turns to Thistle, “and fey.” Then she cackles at the idea. 
Thistle whispers, “I’ll have to talk with my colleagues.” 
“Take your time; it’s not like I am going anywhere…” 
Thistle turns to his friends – “We can just charge?” 
Unla Kee, ever the gambler, stops him. “Look, I am all for charging, but I think a clever ploy has a better chance of success.”
“Let’s go find out what exactly this Dracolich who is on the tight leash of this Vampire is really about,” mutters Vardis. 

The 3HPP heads into the giant glacier that Blagothkus’ castle is built around and descends 50 feet into a large chamber through icy tunnels of blue ice. The walls are translucent, revealing objects embedded in the ice – coins, helmets, livestock, a handful of kobolds, and an ogre or two. Frost coats the tunnel floors and crunches underfoot as they advance. 
Finally the 3HPP arrives in the main vault, a glittering cavern of ice with jagged walls and icicles the size of stalactites. The grand vault is split into two levels: an egg-shaped upper level with a sheer ledge overlooking a sunken level 30 feet below, where a massive pile of treasure rests beneath an icy glaze. Clinging to the ceiling above the hoard, wings tucked in tight along its sides with claws gripping the ice, is a vast white dracolich. 
Thistle then turns to Vardis, “I know what a Dracolich is. It’s a dragon born from an egg that a Vampiric stork brings.” 
Before Orsik and Thistle can argue, Unla Kee interrupts them – “A Dracolich, like a lich, has a phylactery that is used to store their life essence. If you control the phylactery, you control the dragon.” 
“I’ll handle this,” mutters the dwarf. 
“Hello, neighbor.” 
The dracolich opens an eye and then closes it, obviously uninterested. 
Unla Kee looks at Orsik and mutters, “Never send a fighter to do a wizard’s job.”
“Oh, great and powerful dragon!” 
At this, the Dracolich opens its eyes, spreads its wings, and lands just far enough to be dangerous. 
“We wanted to hear your story and how it is that you are here.”
The Dracolich sighs and begins, “I am Glazhael the Undying. I was once Glazhael the Cloudchaser who, with Talis, ruled over the skies of the north. Until we met the thrice-damned heroes of the war of the dragons, I made a deal with them at our first meeting: they would go their way, and I would go mine. And I did, for a time, until Talis called on me to tell me that the White Wyrmspeaker had been captured. Severin had declared that she was the new Wyrmspeaker and wanted me to bask in her glory in the coming of Tiamat. It was a short-lived partnership. We found the heroes at the Tomb of Diderius, and I was killed for the first time. The dwarf Stroud striking me down with his bow.”
“The first time?” 
“Yes, the first time. I was brought back from death as a Dracolich through an arcane ritual Jorgen used in Xonathal’s tower. The ritual required the use of a Dragon Mask. Normally, a dragon must be alive, but Jorgen had found a way to harness the Blue Mask’s power to transform a dead dragon into a Dracolich. Jorgen thought I would be grateful to him, but I was Talis the White Wyrmspeaker’s partner, and Jorgen was her enemy. I left and ran to Talis. But Severin was adamant that a Dracolich could not serve the cult. I was hurt and ready to head into the yawning abyss when Talis observed the small matter of a settlement in the north that needed someone to spy on. And so I went to the Mkoz glacier. And there – as the fates would have it – I would meet Stroud and the First Faction again. And just as I was about to kill them all, they told me that they had found the blue mask and they told me where to find it. And so – knowing how much this meant to Talis – I left to find it.” 
“They lied to you?”
“Yes, and so, to clear my dishonor, I agreed to a plot to murder the First Faction. And once again, I was defeated. When I recovered, I discovered that the First Faction and the grand alliance had destroyed the cult, Talis was no more, and the vampire Sandesyl had my phylactery.” 
Orsik smirks; he has his opening, “So, how do you feel about that?” 
“I AM A DRACOLICH,” she roars, and ice shards fall from the cavern ceiling. “I do not bow to anyone.”
“Oh, so you aren’t at her beck and call?” 
And with that, Glazhael unleashes a torrent of dragon curses that make even Orsik turn a little pale. 
Orsik asks when she stops speaking – “what if we made a deal with you? What if you helped us kill her?” 
The dragon stops fuming, “How? If she sees me, she can destroy my phylactery.” And then the dragon pauses, her skeletal jaw forms into a cruel smile as she remembers something -“During the battle when you have weakened, I can destroy her. When you have almost won, I will seal your victory. But not a moment sooner. I know how to kill the moonelf romance-book-loving vampire.” 
“How?” 
“That is my secret. Now go.” 

# Chapter 72 - Exploring the Lower Courtyard
Glazhael the Undying emphatically explained that her plan to defeat Sandesyl was a secret she wouldn’t share. 
And so the 3HPP had to rely on the word of an undying dracolich.
As they leave, Orsik turns to the creature, “Dragons can’t just say things simply.”
Vardis agrees, “Always got a be churching it up.” 
As they walk away, the words of the Lord’s Alliance ring true, “No alliance is surer than one where narrow interests align.” 
As they walk out of the Dragon’s lair, the 3HPP decide that killing any potential undead allies of Sandesyl is a good plan. And so the lower courtyard of Blagothkus’ castle is a good enough place to start. 
From within the glacier, they follow a corridor to an ice-sculpted stairwell that opens to the lower courtyard. 
Walls of solid ice enclose this courtyard, which is partially overhanging another courtyard. Standing in that courtyard is Blagothkus, who is watching. The courtyard is held aloft by sweeping arches of ice that soar to a height of over 100 feet. Doors of sculpted ice fitted with iron hinges lead to various outbuildings and the main keep on the far side of the courtyard from the gate towers.
As Orsik looks around, he notices a lot of skeletal remains. 
“Let’s get rid of them. Toss them over,” orders Orsik.
“Do we want skeletal remains to be falling onto the ground?” asks Vardis, whose appreciation for the rights of the dead as a cleric of Corellon worries him. Will the Dwarven gods seek vengeance? 
“Better there than as animation material for Sandesyl.” 

Vardis, confronted with the brutal logic, remembers that burning corpses is a Dwarven tradition. “Let’s burn them on the way down?” 
“Okay then,” and with that, Orsik opens a bag of holding, empties it of any valuable contents, and then proceeds to fit it with corpses. 
Thistle just stares at the peculiar site of skeletal remains being transported in a bag of holding. 
When the grizzly work of collecting all the remains is completed, Orsik takes the bag to the front of a covered gateway leading from the courtyard to an open portcullis, and a drawbridge.
 Thistle stares from above from the gateway. 
Orsik then proceeds to dump the remains overboard. 
Unla Kee stops drinking and shouts, “Target practice!” 
The dead bodies of the dwarves are tossed overboard. As they fall, Unla Kee and Vardis burn them with spells. 
And so the day of burning fire is noted in the Evermoors. The day the midday sky lit up with fire. 
 Blagothkus from the upper courtyard smiles and shouts, “I have been looking for someone to do this for almost a year! THANK YOU!!!!” 
Orsik acknowledges the thank you and asks, “We were told there were undead here? We only found dead?” 
The cloud giant tries hard not to sound like a condescending smart ass and says, “Undead like the dark. They are probably not out in the sunlight.” 
“That makes sense!” 
Vardis tries hard not to laugh. 
From the gateway and the drawbridge, Unla Kee leads the group to the doorway on the port side of the castle. 
He sighs as he sees that whatever was here is dead and still dead, and more importantly, whatever treasure was taken a while ago. The room is a barracks, and the place has all the signs of a group of adventurers who burned, slashed, and stolen. There is still some soot on the walls. 
“None for us?” asks Orsik.
“None,” replies a down Unla Kee.
But Thistle, whose eyes of True Seeing see all, finds some scattered coins among the linen and the cracks of the room. 
As he does that he goes, “Huh. That’s interesting.” 
“What?” asks Unla Kee. 
“Not all of the corpses were dwarves; some were dragonwings.” 
“Dragonwings?” 
“High-ranking members of the cult with the ability to fly.” 
It appears that the story of this place involved the castle belonging to the cult, and then belonging to someone else. 
After sifting through all the remains, the group heads to the starboard door to the stables. 
As Unla Kee enters, the stench of the place takes over, but what really draws his attention is the decaying and yet very animate Wyverns. 
Thistle shouts, “These are undead.” 
Unla Kee asks, “Can I use dominate?”
Vardis reminds him that the undead can not be dominated. 
“Fireball, 6th level, here we come!”  
Orsik rolls his eyes, “3rd, please. We need that sunbeam for later.” 
“FINE!” 
The red seed of light zips through the air faster than Unla Kee can blink. He sees its trace against his eyelids as he remembers to shut them against the fiery blast that roars. Opening his eyes, he still sees traces of the crimson bead’s path toward its deadly end.
One Wyvern took the full blast; the other managed to avoid some harm. 
Vardis then shouts to Orsik, “Okay, here’s the plan. I will use the divine power of Correllon to turn them; whichever one  doesn’t get turned, you attack.” 
Orsik nods. 
Vardis holds the holy symbol aloft and, with a flash of radiant light, sends a wave of divine energy hurtling outward. The light envelops the creatures who flee in the wake of the gods' celestial power. One head goes to the bow side of the stable and the other to the stern side. 
Orsik sighs as he dashes towards the stern, “Out of range again.”
Thistle’s arrow strikes the sternside Wyvern, but they are immune to his lightning damage and don’t die. 
Unla Kee decides that now is the time to use his magical rock, so he removes the artifact of wonder. 
The world shimmers, and then a pile of hay disappears.
The 3HPP just stares. 
“I believe the hay is in the ethereal plane?” 
Thistle nods. 
“Two more shots!” 
The cleric, the dwarf, and the fairy sigh. 
Vardis completes the incantation and hurls a lance of pure light toward the undead monstrosity foe. The creature is hit and begins to glow. 
The glow helps Orsik aim his glowing green blade to strike true, and soon enough, the creature is truly dead. With that, he runs towards the stern.
Thistle moves to get a better shot, but the stables provide enough cover for one of his arrows to miss. 
Unla Kee holds the stone artifact aloft and asks, “Wand of Wonder?” 
Vardis shouts, “Yes, we save spells for the real battle!” 
Orsik shouts, “No, magic missile! End the battle!” 
Unla Kee listens to their shouts and stops, ready to invoke magic. With four bass thrums, three missiles of crystalline energy appear above his head. They wobble and spin slowly before their points rigidly align with Unla Kee's gaze.  They fly toward the wyvern, swift as diving raptors.
“You made an excellent point, Orsik.” 
Vardis follows up with a sacred flame that leaves the creature just ever so charred. 
The Wyvern strikes and misses against Orsik and tries to flee towards the stern side.
Thistle strikes him with two arrows and thuul burns him with a burst of acid, but the creature still lives. 
Unla Kee reaches for the artifact of wonder, and once again, Orsik dissuades him. And then Unla Kee weaves strange, arcane gestures with his free hand as he recites alien words. The caster’s eyes become glassy and distant as the spell nears completion, then the deafening peal of a funerary bell sounds.
“They are undead! That’s necrotic damage!” screams Orsik.
The wyvern dies to Orsik and Vardis’ surprise. 
“They are resistant, not immune. I know what I am doing,” the magic smugly states. 
“No,” mutters Orsik.
“Debatable,” grouses Vardis. 
With the monsters dead, the take begins. 
Thistle finds a copper figurine of the goddess Yondala, the creator of the halflings. 
Unla Kee is delighted, “I know about this trinket. It lets you cast a ritual spell in half the time.” 
Orsik finds 120 gold pieces in saddle bags. As he empties the gold into his bag, he notes a bag of carefully wrapped sweets and a cork. 
The dwarf is about to discard the cork when Vardis thinks that this cork is special. “With our luck, this cork will be necessary to save the realms,” he declares and casts detect magic. 
Thistle examines it with the aid of his magical eye.
It is a cork, nothing more. 
Orsik declares, “We have a bottle of champagne de le stompe. If we need a cork, we can use that one.” 
As they leave the barracks and head into the open space of the lower courtyard, Unla Kee mutters, “That’s interesting.”
Seeing that the group is receptive, he says, “The key element of being a lich is the phylactery. As long as that is not destroyed, you can come back.” 
“You know a lot about being a lich?”
“Just idle intellectual curiosity.” 
“Are you saying these are liches?”
“Yes. Sandesyl is conducting broader experiments on the undead to create alternatives to Dracoliches. They will come back.” 
And so again, the 3HPP collects the remains and throws them overboard. Unla Kee and Vardis burn them with their spells as they fly overboard. 
“Where did you learn that?” 
“Magic School” 
“I love how you just say magic school. Like there’s no name, just – magic school.” 
But that pisses off Unla Kee – “ I went to Nirany School of the Arcane. That was not the most prestigious school, but the master wizards spent time mentoring their apprentices, so we had many more opportunities to learn. We also had more freedom to choose our classes than the typical magic school with its rigid adherence to specific classes and methodologies.” 
Orsik rolls his eyes. 
While this goes on, Thistle quickly examines the remaining rooms in the gatehouse and determines that they are empty and have no corpses. 
From the center of the lower courtyard, the group heads towards the stern and to a room on the port side.
It’s an abandoned kitchen. Unfortunately, Unla Kee’s brash entrance provokes undead. A chill emanates from everywhere at once. Rime creeps across surfaces and his breath plumes before him in the frigid air. A pained and distant moan rises with the wind, and in his peripheral vision, he sees several figures detach themselves from the shadows and glide toward the 3HPP—weightless, formless specters. He notes that two of the specters look very different. 
Orsik takes note of a misty raven. He remembers stories from his childhood about spectral ravens that were very dangerous, “The raven appears to be the real problem.”
The rest of the group is tempted to say, “It’s just a ghostly raven.” 
He strikes the raven before they can dissuade him but does not kill it. 
Two of the specters float toward Orsik and strike him with a wave of negative energy, draining the life force from him. 
Vardis uses a warning flare and thwarts one of the creatures with a flash of bright light. 
The raven then flies away from Orsik, incorporeally, to the other end of the room. From there, it caws, charming Orsik and Unla Kee. The effect of the charm leaves them stunned and prone. 
One of the specters attacks the stunned Unla Kee, critically wounding the mage. 
# Chapter 73 - Orsik is stunned
Orsik is stunned, which for him has been a rare occurrence ever since he became super-intelligent. Being stunned happens when you can’t anticipate the other person’s actions. Now, he realizes that you can be stunned in a completely different way. Where the stupidity of someone’s actions stuns you.  
But in this case, he reminds himself, it’s neither. Really, what’s going on is that this spectral Raven has stunned him. And all he can do is ask himself, “Where did it go wrong?” 
He reflects that his decision to become a fighter wasn’t a decision but more a natural outcome of the physical traits he was blessed with. He was strong, he was obstinate, and he wasn’t particularly clever. 
Now that he is intelligent, he sees the limitations of his chosen profession. His job is to go into the breach, smash, kill, and be targeted while other people do the clever stuff. 
“Woot! I killed the creature with my sword by standing next to it! Woot! I am so cool.” 
Orsik remembers when he derived joy from being the most challenging, brutal, and dangerous monster in any room. He couldn’t outthink you, but he could beat your face into a pulp, and when it was beaten, you couldn’t think straight. 
He now understands the meaning of the phrase, “Drag them down to your level and beat them with experience.” 
While Orsik reflects on the nature of his existence, Thistle flits about and kills a specter with his arrows. 
Poor Unla Kee, who is also stunned, is unconcerned, even when a figure detaches itself from the shadows and glides toward him—weightless, formless, undead. Even when he feels the negative energy of the creature sap his life force as the undead creature flows through him. From the corner of his eye, Unla Kee sees that Orsik is also being targeted by this variation of the specter. And then watches as the creature flows through more of the 3HPP. 
And then the Raven emits a soul-chilling cry inducing despair. Thistle, although ordinarily resistant to such evil, isn’t. The cry reminds him of his lost love. Of the despair he has felt over the years looking for her. 
Although terrified and with trembling hands, he manages to strike down the Raven with his bow. 
And the fear, that intense feeling of despair, is gone. At the same time, Thistle redoubles his efforts to find his lost love. 
Orsik notices the flash of a fireball, and the searing hot flames jolt his consciousness free. And he stands around, ready to do what fighters do.
Except they are all dead. 
And the rest of the group is just staring at him smugly as if they are trying to tell him that he’s not really needed. 

Orsik knows better.
With the undead gone, the 3HPP looks around. 
“This was the kitchen,” remarks Unla Kee. 
Thistle notices a note. 
“A recipe?” asks Vaardis.
“No, it’s a bit of a rant. Apparently, Sandesyl was accused of betrayal and treachery. I don’t think that Vampires can create specters…” 
That font of all things arcane, Unla Kee, inserts casually, “A specter is created when the soul of a creature is ripped from it while they still live.” 
“That sounds like what Sandesyl would do.” 
Thistle turns pale as he realizes that Sandesyl isn’t a dilettante vampire obsessed with Romance Novels – “She’s a disciple of Sammaster.” 
Everyone turns to him, “Sammaster was chosen by Mystara and the person who originally figured out how to make Dracoliches. Uncle Joon told me about how some members of the Cult of the Dragon believed that Dragons were just the perfect form of undead but that there had to be other magical processes for creating undead and that there were members of the cult who pursued that magical research.” 
Unla Kee mutters, “Not part of the end game, so not interesting.” 
“What end-game?” 
“THE END GAME.” 
Meanwhile, Vardis finds a magical gem that he hands to the wizard. 
“A gem of elemental summoning,” says Unla Kee as he pockets the item. 
“So we going to take this Sandesyl character down?” asks Vaardis. 
“Not now,” announces Unla Kee.
“What?” 
“Although she isn’t part of the end game prophecy, I have an intuition about bad plans, and this one is screaming bright red – bad idea. So no.” 
Orsik is pleased because he was stunned when Vardis asked Unla Kee. “That would be the kind of stunning thing that happens when you hear something stupid,” he says to himself. 
And just then, Unla Kee rushes from the Kitchen to the unlit barracks. 
And again, Orsik is stunned. 
“From the unlit Kitchen to the Unlit Barracks…” 
“Look, we have money, and we can always get a raise dead spell,” observes Vardis. And then thinks, “Unless you happen to be a cleric banned from his temples. But that’s what money is for.” 
Unla Kee returns sheepishly to the Kitchen, realizing he had already explored the barracks earlier. Overhearing the conversation, he says, “And it’s not like we must go to the ‘good’ clerics.” 
“But will they raise or animate you?” asks Orsik. 
“Great question,” asks Thistle, “I guess you find out after the fact?” 
To the north of the barracks are a series of rooms. One contains some dead stone giants that were obviously the cooks for the giants. 
Another contains food that has since rotten, but Orsik finds a bottle of Blackstaff wine. He can’t resist sharing his recently acquired fine wine expertise, “Blackstaff is a type of wine from Waterdeep. It has a big, bright, and unbalanced flavor. It was said to perfectly capture the spirit of the City of Splendors. The wine was exported to the Western Heartlands, the lands along the River Chionthar, and the city of Baldur’s Gate as of the late 15th century, Dale Reckoning. Explains why we would have found this bottle here.” 
Across the hall from the pantry is a room containing half of the dragon’s remains, wearing the distinctive purple garb of a Wyrmspeaker, the inner circle of the Cult of the Dragon. From the creature’s garb, it’s clear that this was the Black Dragon Wyrmspeaker. 
Thistle remembers stories about this Wyrmspeaker. “She’s Rezmir. Rumor has it that she was in charge of this castle and responsible for moving the hoard that was part of the ritual. The rumors have it that she was killed by the heroes of the War of the Dragons. Others speculated she ran afoul of internal politics.” 
Just then, Orsik finds a piece of paper that bolsters the theory of internal disputes, “Look at this …” 

The note he finds writes – “The idea that I need to be audited beggars’ belief. My operations are sound, and my commitment to the cause cannot be doubted. The auditors who infiltrated the Black Dragon Hatchery were spies. And that our troops would think I needed to be investigated reflects some of our mercenaries’ lack of faith in the cause. The five heads of Tiamat do not attack each other and do not distrust each other. They are one and five at once. Let us not forget that. Any auditing of any operation going forward must involve all of us or be considered a trick by our enemies to sow dissension or disrupt our plans.” 
Orsik scours the room for treasure but finds nothing. 
“So now what?” asks Vardis. 
“I’ve been thinking. This glacier has a lot of ice that is not part of the caves that the Dracolich uses. Sandesyl must have carved her lair into the ice. It explains how she could take control of the castle and shows up wherever she wants.”
He then turns to Unla Kee, “Meanwhile, the mage is right. We need to rest up. Sandesyl isn’t going to let us raid her lair and retreat. We will have to get rid of the Ogre Zombies. But once we do that, then she’s really going to be pissed off.” 
With that, the group arrives back in the castle’s control tower. 
The crystals and runes spin and twist and fly into the shape of a female cloud giant’s head. The mouth of the head, formed from runes, gems, and lights, moves and forms words, although the sounds seem to come from everywhere. 
“I’ve been working on my avatar. Not quite what I want,” declares Escarlotta. She then turns to the 3HPP – “Did you remove the undead infestation?” 
And before they can answer, she remarks, “You do realize that the undead were the products of Sandesyl’s necromantic experiments?” 
At this point, Blagothkus jumps in – “I just want to make it clear that this isn’t my fault.” 
Escarlotta sighs, “Not your fault? We discussed This precisely about taking accountability for your actions and decisions.” 
“After the War of the Dragons, my husband became obsessed with the role of giants. I was crashed into a glacier in Mkoz. My worthless husband could not get the help of the Frost Giant Jarl to free me. The heroes of the War of the Dragons were able to free me. For a while, I was under the control of the Frost Giants, who used me to defeat the dragons at the final climactic battle in the well of the worlds above the summoned temple of Tiamat. The poor fools thought Sandesyl had fled the castle and it was theirs to command. She had not. Instead, once the cult was destroyed, she took time to transform the new occupants into undead. You met the Jarl, I believe. He was a skeletal giant you destroyed?”
She pauses, “Sandesyl discovered she could not control the castle or me. And so she agreed to find my husband, who was stuck on the Mkoz glacier. I assumed that the fool would get over his ludicrous megalomania. Still, instead… Seeing the failures of the Dragons and convinced that he was part of some kind of Giant renaissance, he visited the Oracle. There, the All-Father explained that he was a failure and that our son was the leader the giants needed, and all our son had to do was kill his Father. Our son failed, and in my grief, we ended up stranded where you found us. Blagothkus implored that we move, and I refused. My castle was infested with undead, and my son was dead.” Then dramatically, the runes reform into a floating fist with a finger pointing at the Cloud Giant, “and my husband was a fool.” 
At this point, Thistle is tempted to ask why Escarlotta is obsessed with destroying the Cloud Giant Countess.

“And to top it all off, Countess Sansuri was making a play for the Ordning! Imagine that classless poseur of a magic-wielding cloud giant as the QUEEN of all giants! THIS IS ALL HIS FAULT!”
“He made a deal with the devil,” observes Unla Kee.
“No, a Vampire. Tiamat was the Devil. Severin was the one who made the deal with the Devil.” 
Thistle feels awkward: “It’s not like you want a devil running a play.” 
And Orsik adds, “Sometimes folks make deals with devils and don’t even realize they are making deals with devils. They don’t understand every clause in the contract.” 
Thistle continues, “They audition for the part, and you don’t know.”
“People have to be forgiven for these kinds of things,” says Orsik soothingly. 
The runes stop moving and return to the configuration of the control panel. 
Escarlotta mutters, “Forgiveness is hard. But you are right.” And again, the runes form a fist with a finger, “Are you going to do anything other than sit on your fat ass?” 
Blagothkus stares, “Stand?” 
The couple appears to be in a standoff again when Orsik intervenes and explains to Escarlotta that he’s learned that sometimes, direct action is necessary to deal with the mistakes of the past. Just watching and praying is not enough. 
She agrees and informs the 3HPP that Blagothkus will help them defeat Sandesyl. 

# Chapter 74 - The Wand of Ill Fortune
In the morning, the members of the 3HPP find a letter sealed with Orsik's logo. 

Each of them, in turn, looks up at Orsik and sees his smile. 
So they open the message.
*The dracolich has agreed to help us kill the vampire in exchange for us locating and returning his phylactery. This is a Bad Idea. A freed dracolich could wreck untold horrors upon Faerun. I, Orsic, am a Hero (tm), and heroes do not free dracoliches. We do need the help in battle, though. So what should we do?*
*
We have a potential ally—an old friend, you might say—who can help us out of this predicament: Bob Saget.
*
*Now I know what you're going to say. Didn't we fight Bob? Isn't Bob an evil demon? Bob isn't our friend, and he's certainly not an ally. Yes, yes. That's all very well and good. But listen...
We had to fight Bob. He had a zombie army and was going to Waylay Silverymoon. A hero like me, Orsic, doesn't let a town be ransacked and destroyed, not even Silverymoon. I had to act. We had to act. We couldn't let Bob go through with this plan. But the truth is, I kind of liked Bob. He was funny. He made us a LOT of money. I didn't want to fight him and I didn't want to kill him. So I talked Unlakee into sending him to the astral plane instead. I knew Bob would be safe there and we could get rid of the zombies and be Heroes (tm). And we did it. The plan worked perfectly. Bob was safe, and we even sent one of his drow allys with him and four stooges. The astral plane is a dangerous place. I didn't want Bob to go alone. And we let his other drow get away safely. Everything went just like I drew it up.
*
*So now's the time to reunite with Bob. Vardis has a spell: Planar Ally. He can bring Bob to us. I'll explain the whole story to him just like I did to you. And then we ask for his help: fight the vampire alongside us, help us retrieve the phylactery, and the dracolich is yours to keep. Sure we had to destroy your zombie army, but a dracolich is way better than a zombie army anyway.
*
*Do you understand? Do you see the sheer brilliance of this plan? Can you comprehend its awesome, intricate, unvarnished intellectual fortitude? We get the dracolich for the battle. We get Bob for the battle. We don't have a pesky dracolich to deal with at the end of the battle. We remove Bob as an adversary (he's been plotting his revenge against us). Maybe we can even ally with Bob again someday. We would be fools not to follow this plan, my plan. We will all be Heroes (tm) again.*
*As we should be. As we shall be.*
*I await your acquiescence.*
Thistle is impressed and immediately pens a reply and hands it over to the grinning Dwarf. 
*Why wouldn't (1) bob betray us just to see us fail. (2) Bob betray us in hopes of getting the dracolich for free (3) Bob using the dracolich for something even worse (e.g. how is Bob in control of a dracolich not worse than a free dracolich? They are both problems, but being problems together is harder to clean up)*
Orsik tut-tuts the sloppy grammar, poor capitalization, and generally less than excellent prose. He then responds with another missive of his own. 

*1. Bob is plotting against us right now anyway. We can try to get him on our side. Worst case, he continues to plot against us.
2. Get the dracolich for free how? The vampire has to be dead for us or Bob to get the phylactery.
3. A free dracolich is Our Fault. And we will not be Heores if we let it happen. Unless you want to try to kill the dracolich immediately after we've spent all of our spell slots killing a vampire. Bob in control of a dracolich is... unfortunate? But Bob's actions are His Fault.*
Orsik glares. 
Thistle stares. 
At this point, Unla Kee asks, “Perhaps we should examine exactly what the rules for Planar Ally are.” 
- You beseech an otherworldly entity for aid. The being must be known to you: a god, a primordial, a demon prince, or some other being of cosmic power. That entity sends a celestial, an elemental, or a fiend loyal to it to aid you, making the creature appear in an unoccupied space within range. If you know a specific creature’s name, you can speak that name when you cast this spell to request that creature, though you might get a different creature anyway.
- When the creature appears, it is under no compulsion to behave in any particular way. You can ask the creature to perform a service in exchange for payment, but it isn’t obliged. The requested task could range from simple (fly us across the chasm, or help us fight a battle) to complex (spy on our enemies, or protect us during our foray into the dungeon). You must be able to communicate with the creature to bargain for its services.
- Payment can take a variety of forms. A celestial might require a sizable donation of gold or magic items to an allied temple, while a fiend might demand a living sacrifice or a gift of treasure. Some creatures might exchange their service for a quest undertaken by you.
As a rule of thumb, a task that can be measured in minutes requires a payment worth 100 gp per minute. A task measured in hours requires 1,000 gp per hour. And a task measured in days (up to 10 days) requires 10,000 gp per day.
-  If the task is aligned with the creature’s ethos, the payment might be halved or even waived. Nonhazardous tasks typically require only half the suggested payment, while especially dangerous tasks might require a greater gift. Creatures rarely accept tasks that seem suicidal.
- After the creature completes the task, or when the agreed-upon duration of service expires, the creature returns to its home plane after reporting back to you, if appropriate to the task and if possible. If you are unable to agree on a price for the creature’s service, the creature immediately returns to its home plane.

Vardis examines the rules and says, “What prevents him from sending the Dracolich to attack us?” 
Orsik shrugs, “Maybe we can give him something else of value, like a wand of wonder?” 
Unla Kee shakes his head, “No.” 
“And if I am not mistaken,” mumbles Vardis, “Bob goes back to the Abyss, and now he has an emissary on the prime material plane. And that emissary is invulnerable because the phylactery is somewhere in the abyss. I’m not sure given a demon a dracolich is -heroic-.” 
Seeing his friend point out the flaws in his plan would have made the old Orsik angry, but the new Orsik understands that plans get better when people contribute their perspectives and that he, as the most brilliant person in the room, has to identify those good points and incorporate them into the plan.
“Alright, maybe we double cross Bob? We give him the phylactery and then send the Dracolich to get it?” 
“A backstab?” asks Thistle. 
“Not really, because he was never on our side,” observes Vardis. 
“So, a stab?”
Orsik grouses, “The Bob plan was good, but the problem of the Dracolich remains. Let’s table it for now.” 
Orsik looks at the 3HPP, pleased. He knows that sometimes you have to table a plan when the team doesn’t see how right it is and just bring it back when they are ready to accept it. 
Unla Kee is disappointed. The potential for mayhem and chaos was high, and since he knew it wasn’t the end of the world, it was not dangerous. 
Later, when the sun has risen, the heroes climb to the top of the control tower, some 70 feet above the upper courtyard. 
“So we do this with no spells, right?” 
The rest of the 3HPP nods at Orsik’s admonition. 
Thistle fires at the Ogre Zombies, as do Vardis and Orisk. Realizing what is happening, the zombies head to the tower and climb. 
Vardis stares at the zombies – “I wonder why they are doing that?” 
At this point, Orsik asks, “Can we use mold earth on the stone?” 
Unla Kee sighs, “I think I explained this to you. Yes, the arcane lore about the mists of Ravenloft does talk about a dark lord who the Dark Powers seduced with the ability to mold stone as if it were earth. But the cantrip doesn’t work that way, normally.” 
Everyone looks at Unla Kee – “Look, the story of Slerdax is quite interesting. He’s a dark lord trapped in the mists of Ravenloft. He runs a restaurant that periodically opens its doors in some cities and traps the unwary. Slerdax wants to be praised for his brilliant cooking and excellent service but is cursed to doubt. And so whenever someone says nice things, he thinks they are lying and tries to kill them. The killing is done subtly. But if subtlety doesn’t work, the seething rage of an undying dark lord works. To escape, you must either kill the dark lord, and most people don’t show up in full battle dress at a dinner party, or convince him that you are telling the truth.” 
At this point, everyone is staring at Unla Kee – “Look, I told you I went to a fantastic school of magic, where the forbidden lore teacher was interested in teaching us the dangers of Ravenloft. I took the advanced course, which covered his particular area of interest: minor lords with small domains.” 
And with that, Unla Kee uses toll the dead on one of the Zombies. 
Vardis mutters, “Not exactly the best cantrip to use.” 
And then proceeds to use it on the same monster, “but when the monster is injured, it is the best cantrip to use.” 
Unla Kee is irritated. 
And the zombies continue to climb up the tower. 

Blagothkus pauses, looking at the zombies. 
Orsik asks with an affectation of irritation, “Do you have another boulder?” 
“Of course he does,” snarks Vardis. “He only has the best boulders.” 
The cloud giant smiles and nods, “Why, yes. Only the very best.” 
With that, the giant drops it on one of the oncoming Zombies, whose ranks are thinning. 
Thistle fires two arrows at two zombies to make the wizard and cleric’s toll on the dead more effective by giving them more wounded targets. He admires his handiwork until he sees Unla Kee pull out the small boulder they found at the one stone. Thistle groans, the boulder of wonder. 
A puff of green, noxious smoke quickly expands into a cloud. 
“Stinking cloud.” 
The cleric shrugs, “That was almost useful. But they are immune to poison.” 
Orsik is about to start screaming and sighs, “I can’t.” 
“Are you surprised at this point?” asks Vardis. 
Blagothkus, new to the group, says, “I have met better magic users. I know better magic users. I have a whole binder full of better magic users.” 
Orsik looks at the cloud below and thinks, " Great. Not only are they immune, but we can’t target them.” 
Blagothkus mutters, “Binders.” 
“You will dismiss this spell,” he growls at Unla Kee. 
The wizard stares and agrees. 
And then Unla Kee mutters, “Every moment is not about optimizing your actions; it’s about living. And living is about taking risks, going outside the box.”
“Which is why I wanted to bring Bob. The demon of dad jokes.” 
“And give him a real dracolich,” sighs Vardis. 
Meanwhile, somewhere in the Astral Plane, Bob wonders why he hears the faint sounds of his name. 
Vardis stares at the oncoming Zombies and kills one. “I’ll leave the weakened one for Unla Kee.” 
Orsik then turns to Blagothkus, “You’re up. You know you are the second giant friend we’ve had. Our first was Harshnag, but he died when the Oracle fell on his head. We learned about this place from your son, who is a ghost. Your son wanted to kill us. He also told us you weren’t good enough for the All-Father.” 
Blagothkus clenches his fists, thinks about tossing the dwarf down, then remembers that he’s all about personal growth, takes some deep breaths, and tosses a boulder on one of the onrushing zombies. 
While the other members of the 3HPP are enjoying good-natured ribbing and laughing, Thistle is flying off to the side and looking for any oncoming monsters. 
And he notices Glazhael the Undying sneaking towards the tower. 
“The dracolich is coming!” he shouts. 

Orsik has noticed that Thistle’s ability to see things hasn’t been up to par recently, and he is distrustful. 
So, the dwarf decides to end one of the zombies. 
Unla Kee then summons the arcane powers of his artifact, and darkness envelops the zombies!
Orsik cheers, “SEE! I knew this was going to be useful!” 
Vardis mutters, “Except now we can’t use toll the dead!” 

Glazhael advances on the group and uses her dragon fear on the 3HPP from a safe distance. Thistle notices that if she had a heart instead of an empty husk of a chest wall, it wouldn’t be in it. 
Vardis notices how Glazhael is moving and mutters, “Shadowboxing us.” 
Thistle and Thuul are affected, but the rest of the 3HPP are not. Blagothkus, for his part, shouts, “I am not going to wait here. I plan to go downstairs.” 
Orsik shouts – “I thought your wife said you had to participate in this battle?” 
“And I will, once the dragon does its thing. I will come back up and attack. I am not standing here to get killed. You might think of doing the same.” 
Vardis hears what he says, shoots an arrow into the darkness, and kills a zombie. 
Blagothkus turns to Orsik, “Alright, I am going down to get some more rocks.” He pauses and says, “Not to question your plan, but I finally figured out what the zombies were there for. Their job was to keep anyone fighting long enough to give her pet dracolich a chance to kill them.” 
Orsik turns to the other members of the 3HPP, who are now just Vardis, as the dragon's fear has overcome Unla Kee. “I told you we needed Bob.” 
Then he takes his decanter of endless liquids and dumps two gallons of mayonnaise on an approaching Zombie. The zombie covered in mayonnaise loses its footing and falls but is not killed. 
And with that, Orsik heads for cover. 
Thistle, heading Blagothkus’ warning, moves away from the tower and shoots two more Zombies. 

Unla Kee, ever the gambler, picks up the giant artifact and conjures up its wild magic. The tower shimmers green and then is covered in green grass. The wizard thinks as he heads down into the building, “Hmm… while in the darkness, they go slower, but once outside, they will go faster. Not a bad tradeoff.” 
Glazhael advances, and her annoyance is visible as she breathes on the tower, which is covered in ice. As she flies away, she screams, “THEY ARE ALL DEAD!” 
As she flies away, Orsik shouts, “Bob’s still an option!” 
# Chapter 75 - The Vampire's Lair
## Orsik
The Undead Ogres were still climbing up the tower, although they were slowed down for a minute because of the darkness Unla Kee had cast. 
“The wand of wonder accomplishing something other than amusing the wizard, surprising.” 
Blagothkus shouts, " The zombies can’t see us, and Glazahael has left. We can use this opportunity to run to Sandesyl’s tower.” 
The dwarf is not impressed with the giant's tactical and strategic awareness. The whole purpose of the maneuver was to kill off the ogres here, where they were primarily defenseless. 
Blagothkus, however, won’t shut up, “Orsik, I know we thought that the Zombie Ogres were intended to act as a reserve, but now we know that they are just a way to keep Sandesyl’s enemies trapped so that the dragon can kill them from above. We need to run.” 
The stout fighter shouts, “Then run. I have a mission, and I intend to complete it.” 
## Thistle 
The dragon fear is, well, quite something else. 
Of course, with his new dragon eyes, I should be terrifying the dragon, not the other way around. But that’s not how the world works if you are a fairy experimented on by a mad dragon. 
Wait, that’s odd. Glazhael has moved out of the eyeshot, almost like she wants me to approach Sandesyl’s tower. 
As Thistle flies to the tower, he sees Unla Kee fly with his giant Pegasus wings. Vaardis is running, and even Orsik seems to have abandoned the quest to kill all of the ogres. 
## Vardis. 
“Not that again,” thinks Vaardis.
“Last chance to summon Bob,” intones Orsik. 
“One enemy at a time,” whispers Thistle.
Vaardis sighs, “I liked the idea until I realized we were planning to give him a near-invulnerable emissary in our plane of existence.” 
“There were flaws, but we could work around them.” 
Vaardis pleads with his eyes to anyone who will help him. Surprisingly, Unla Kee does when he explains that Bob is currently trapped in the Astral Plane. 
“Trapped?” shouts an excited Orsik. 
“Yes, and as long as he is trapped in the Astral Plane, he doesn’t have access to all his power.” 
“So he’s impotent.” 
“I wouldn’t use that term, but yes.” 
“Let’s leave Bob where he is and go kill a vampire.” 
## Orsik
“The tower is empty,” he groans. 
Blagothkus sighs, “Yes, she’s carved out her own lair in the ice caves below.” 
“Why would she be in her lair?” 
“Because that’s where she is normally.” 
“But last time she was here.” 
“Yes, but last time you hadn’t killed her wyvernliches, gotten rid of her material for zombies, and started an attack on her Ogre Zombies. And most importantly, she invited you.” 
“But Glazhael killed us.” 
“Sandesyl isn’t the kind of person who is trusting of her minions. She’ll send several of her spawn to find your bodies, and when parts of them are brought to her, she might believe you are dead. At which point she may leave her lair.” 
Orsik is pleased. An enemy worthy of his tactical brilliance. 
Blagothkus
“So, are we going down?” 
The dwarf stares at him glumly, “how are we going to sneak with this guy?” 
Blagothkus is delighted, “Look, Escarlotta said I had to help you guys if you felt I was a help. How about I just stay here and guard the entrance?” 
Vaardis pats Blagothkus on his kneecap in a mixture of contempt and reassurance and says, “Oh, don’t be like that!” 
Before he can respond, the fairy squeals, “Staying here would be a hindrance.” 
And with that, Blagothkus sighs and squeezes himself into the tunnel below the tower. 
The tunnel under the tower opens up into a large cavernous area. 
 Vaardis
“Right hand rule,” shouts Thistle. 
Vaardis looks at him and remembers something the old guard of the cult of Correllon would say. If you keep doing something the same way, he will ensure the other way is easy. It’s why Vaardis always did things the other way. Although it wasn’t clear, things were simpler that way. 
Thistle ignores him, follows the ice tunnel north, and emerges in a large cavern with a statue of an enormous red dracolich with a terrifying blend of draconic might and infernal, demonic features. Its skeletal frame still crackles with arcane energy, and burning embers light its empty eye sockets. Blackened, jagged horns curve back over its skull, and leathery, tattered, and bat-like wings spread wide. Its claws are sharp and imbued with hellish fire, capable of searing through the toughest armor. Demonic runes are etched into their bone structure, pulsating with a malevolent glow. 
The cleric stares at the statue momentarily and then says, “Yep, I know this.” 
Everyone stares at him, and he answers, “Seriously? You don’t know who this is? Fine, I’ll explain. It’s Inferno, the Hellbound Dracolich, a once powerful red dragon who made a forbidden pact with a demon lord in its quest for immortality. The legends say that this was the first of the Dracoliches.” 
Orsik nods, “makes sense.” 
Meanwhile, Unla Kee notices something about the statues, “well, isn’t that interesting.”
Vaardis glares, “Will you share with the class?” 
“This is Sammaster, the original leader of the cult of the dragon holding the Tome of the Dragon - the definitive book on Dracoliches. How odd. It almost looks like Sammaster is learning the secrets of undeath from Inferno.” 
## Thistle 
Thistle - Can we please go?
Orsik - No, we need to get the treasure. 
Thistle - We can get it afterward. 
Orsik - But what if we need the treasure to defeat Sandesyl? 
The fairy sighs and scans with his new eyes, determining that other than some valuable gems, “There is nothing of value.”  
But it’s too late; Unla Kee is already flying up to the statue of Inferno and prying the gems loose from the demon’s eyes. 
The four statues surrounding the dracolich animate and advance on Unla Kee. 
Thistle flits up and down as if he is throwing a tantrum – “I TOLD YOU THIS WOULD HAPPEN!” 
The fairy stops and then turns to Blagothkus – “Go clean up his mess, the rest of us will move forward.” 
Orsik turns to him, “I don’t understand why you’re upset. We agreed not to waste resources, and we aren’t wasting any other than a giant we didn’t count upon.” 
“A giant we could have used to grapple the vampire?” 
Thistle’s frustration mounts as Orsik shrugs. 
Continuing to the right, Thistle sees a large cavern to the northwest with two vampire spawns. The same vampire spawns he met earlier in Sandesyl’s lair. Both are in their coffins gossiping.
Thistle sighs and looks south, “perhaps we can try that way.” 
Violating the right-hand rule, he goes towards the south along an ice-hewn passageway that opens into two caverns. To the east are orc zombies busy cleaning. To the west, he hears the sound of a Frost giant wheezing. 
He stops, sniffs, and concludes that Sandesyl’s lair is to the west – through two vampire spawns or a frost giant. 
“I thought we were going to fight a vampire, not an army of minions,” he mutters to Orsik.
Who is curiously smiling – “Good news, we have a minion. And there is Blagothkus, slightly worn down from defeating the armored horrors but still in fighting shape. 
“Did you find the Frost giant, Jarl?” the cloud giant asks. 
“The who?” 
Blagothkus rubs his temple, “Look, I lost the castle when it crashed and landed on the Miklos Glacier on the world's spine. Long story, but it's not important. Anyways, the Heroes of the War of the Dragon showed up, and they convinced the local frost giant jarl, Brunvild, that he should help them repair and steal the castle. When the castle left the Miklos glacier, Brunvild thought he was rid of Sandesyl. But he wasn’t. She was a stowaway. And when she discovered he planned to destroy the Cult of the Dragon, she was happy to let him try. Once he did, however, she turned on him. That’s why there were so many Frost Giant skeletons. Since she couldn’t run the castle without me, she came to me. Brunvild is kept alive, so she has something to snack on. I tried to starve her out, but she knew how to survive for a very long time. Esclarlota has a different version of the facts.” 
Orsik chirps, “Another ally?” 
“He’s too weak.” 
Thistle thinks, “A distraction?” 
And with that, Thistle flies off, carefully hiding in the shadows, past the orc zombies and the ghost that gives them particular instructions on keeping the caves clean. 
He stares at the exhausted and emaciated Frost Giant, sighs, and flies into his face – “Hello.” 
The giant opens his eyes and sees Thistle just a few feet away. 
“I’m here to free you, Harshnag sent me.” 
“Harshnag’s here?” 
“Well, no, but he sent us to free you.” 
The giant squints, “Aren’t you a little bit small to be allied to a giant?”  
Thistle’s eyes roll. “Yes, I am a fairy. I also have a mighty bow, access to some powerful magics, and am possessed by these Dragon Eyes.” 
“Is Blagothkus alive?” 
“Yes, but he’s seen the error of his ways. He’s here to help.”
“Help? He’s why I am trapped here. He’s why my men are dead. He’s why the dwarves trusted me are dead.” He pauses, “Have you seen the dwarves?” 
Thistle remembers the cadavers he tossed over the Evermoors and says, “They aren’t here. Maybe they escaped?” 
Brunvild sighs contentedly. “What about the Orcs? Sandesyl has transformed them into skeletons and put over them a ghost. It’s part of her sick experiments to see if she can create a cleaning service made of undead.”
“No overhead. Smart.” 
“Yes, but the ghost is very obsessed about the particular cave being clean and has not let the orcs move out of it for two years.” 
The Giant stares at him through tired eyes, “Sandesyl lives. If she lives, then I can’t escape.” 
“That’s the plan: you escape, draw some of her allies from her lair, and then we can kill her. We need your help to distract them.” 
Brunvild stares at the fairy, and Thistle recognizes that look. Is the look of a prisoner convinced that trying one more time to escape is better than staying here and living like a blood bank. Brunvild sees that same expression in Thistle, and it’s that moment that convinces Brunvild to try one more time. " Very well.” As Brunvild gets up, he turns to Thistle. “Flying creatures have had a weird impact on my life. First, there was a Kenku, and now you.” 
Thistle is momentarily stunned. The story of the flying Kenku who defeated the Cult of the Dragon, which terrorized the cultists, was a story the prisoners told themselves. He didn’t exist. Kenkus don’t fly. But here was Brunvild saying he knew him. 
The fairy wants to stop and ask him questions, but the sound of guard drakes moving towards the giant’s nest, following Brunvild, who is lumbering out through the southern tunnel, stops him. 
He flies back frantically to the rest of the group, “We need to attack now.” 
The vampire spawn is not surprised. However, the 3HPP are surprised when Orsik waits for Unla Kee to cast haste. Thistle turns to Orsik, “huh?” 
“Yeah, apparently, he has a haste spell.”
“Why hasn’t he been using it?”
Unla Kee shrugs, “it would be too easy that way.” 
# Chapter 78 The first defeat of Sandesyl

The Vampire Spawn sees what’s coming and decides to warn the others. It’s not fleeing, it’s warning. “Perhaps,” the vampire spawn thinks, “Sandesyl will see it that way too.” 

Vardis sees it run, and from his outstretched hands, a raw blast of coruscating divine energy flies toward the spawn, which then burns when with the concentrated power of Correlon’s wrath. 

Thistle floats like a fairy and then, from his position, strikes at one of the other spawns. 

Seeing the battle, Blagothkus declares - “I will stand back and protect the magic user.” 

Orsik growls, “No, you will not defend the magic user. You will charge into battle.” 

The giant looks miffed. 

Orsik, the motivator, shouts, “Look, you defeated all those armored horrors by yourself. You are the key to this victory.” 

The giant sniffles, “Really?” And then charges into the fray, attacking and destroying one of the spawns of Sandesyl. 

Vaardis smiles, sensing the battle is almost over. But then, to the south, he sees another one of the Vampire’s minions with several guard drakes advancing on them. And to the east from another chamber is a giant skeletal dragon. Unla Kee matters - “A failed attempt at a dracolich, that’s why needed the book.” 

Thistle mutters, “I could have scouted, and we could have come up with a better plan, but Orsik wanted to charge.” 

At first, the dwarf is upset, but then he realizes that sometimes, when you are working with great people, they will come up with better plans and that, as a leader, it’s crucial to listen. And that he did what he was working so hard to do, not let other people provide input into the planning process. He sighs, “You’re right. My bad.” 

Vardis raises an eyebrow. 

“Do I attack the advancing hordes, or do I attack the Vampire? And where is the vampire?” He looks around and notices a coffin in a chamber whose entrance is in the western wall of the cavernous chamber. “I can see a coffin, but there is no vampire.” 

With Sandesyl nowhere to be found, he aims with his bow and strikes one of the drakes. Two more arrows strike the drake from the bow of Thistle. 

Orsik rushes in, kills the drake, and then looks around for the spawn directing these creatures. He strikes savagely at the undead creature. 
Then, seemingly out of nowhere, a fireball explodes, its heat and flames engulfing Vardis, Blagothkus, and Unla Kee. 

Blagothkus cannot avoid the heat, but Unla Kee does and, more importantly, doesn’t look his concentration. 

Blagothkus sees the Skeletal Dragon's jaw snap at his arms as the fire recedes. He howls in pain. 

The creature strikes at Thuul with its claws. Thuul avoids the left claw but lands on the right claw. The drake’s tough armor protects him, and he shakes his head, ready to fight. 

Meanwhile, Orsik is surrounded by guard drakes. His armor and his skill in battle leave him confident that, barring a lucky strike, these are not dangerous. And, of course, he gets cut by the jaws of a drake that got lucky. 

Unla Kee opens his eyes and sees Orsik surrounded. He does some quick math and realizes that a fireball will hurt five times more enemies than allies. A moment later, Orsik and the drakes are engulfed in a ball of fire. 

The drakes are angry and snarl and bite, but Orsik dodges every one of their attacks. 

Vardis shouts, “Another fireball?” 

Orsik screams, “I can handle the burn.” 

The fire bursts out lightning the entire cavern and then disappears. Vardis closes his eyes, hoping to see his friend standing, and opens them when he hears Orsik’s roar of approval. Whereas there were five drakes before, now there are two badly wounded creatures and one vampire spawn that is badly burned. 

Thistle aims at one and kills it. 

Orsik screams at Blagothkus, “Kill the dragon!” 

Blagothkus mutters, “I am injured. Can someone heal me?” 

The enraged dwarf shouts - “You don’t want to fight; I will kill you myself.” 

The cloud giant is taken aback and sputters, “What would escarlotta say?” 

“I don’t care.” 

Realizing that Orsik is not joking, the giant picks up a rock from the cavern floor and tosses it at the skeletal dragon. 

Orsik rushes past the last drake, which dies moments later from Thistle’s bow, and strikes the dragon first with a slice across its chest, then strike to its claw, followed by a crushing blow to one of its legs. 

The creature is in pain but still capable of fighting. If it weren’t undead, the beast would flee. 

Sandesyl, from her perch in her cave, is raging. Her minions have failed her. She is not worried about being killed. She has a secret place to escape, and nobody knows its location. A place where she can safely regenerate. “But first, I need to kill that wizard.” 

She invisibly moves through the crowd and grabs him with one of her claws, but the wizard avoids the attack. She then uses her other claw and has him in her grasp. Unla Kee smiles; he is ready for that and doesn’t lose his concentration. 

But Orsik’s haste ends when the Dragon strikes the Wizard. But the wizard doesn’t die. 

When she moves out of the way and hides behind the dragon, Unla Kee smiles. With words that mix prayer and an ancient magic language, his spell reaches out to the celestial sphere and opens a pathway to a foundational force of the universe, without which—quite literally—all the world would lie in darkness. He looks at the glow coming from between his fisted fingers and then throws out his open palm, directing the brilliant power of the Sun in a searing line of radiance.

The vampire tries to avoid the worst of the blast, but she can feel fate's twist against her. Unla Kee is smiling, and she screams, “A diviner!” 

Before she can recover, Vardis summons the power of the blazing sun of Olympus from Correlon’s domain and lets loose the tiniest fraction of its celestial brightness. The cavern is brightly lit, the skeletal dragon collapses, its’ magic force is spent, and Sandesyl is burned. 

Blagothkus and Thistle strike the last of the cult of the dragon, the last of Samaster’s disciples, with their ranged attacks. Sandesyl screams in rage but can’t get out of the range of the bright light that permeates the caverns from the spells of Thistle and Unla Kee. Her rage echoes throughout the cavern, and she turns into mist. 
	
Meanwhile, deep in the caverns, Thuul and the last drake snarl at each other. The drake strikes Thuul. But Thuul shrugs off the bite and then kills it with a vicious bite of his own. 

Orsik is burned, bloodied, and standing and annoyed, “WHERE IS THE DRACOLICH?” 

Thistle observes, “Well, we did decide to attack in her lair, and there is no way for the dracolich to enter.” 

“Glazhael said she would show up if we got the vampire deeply injured.” 

“Yes, and there was no way for her to come.” 

He stomps his foot down, like a petulant child, but realizes that Thistle is right, “Fine, we need to kill that vampire.”

He rushes to the coffin and tosses it open. It is empty. 

“Where is she?” 

Thistle stares at the ice and mutters, “Endless cracks go deep into the caves. I have no idea where she is.” 

“Okay, fine, we open the chests and then find this Dracolich.” 

Thistle is about to scream, “Wait!” but is too late. Orsik opens the chest, and a lightning bolt strikes him, Vardis, and Unla Kee. 

# Chapter 79 Dealing with Blagothkus and Yartar
**
Adventure Overview:**
The adventurers are caught between the mortal world and the machinations of powerful beings such as Dracoliches, vampires, and giants. Their current mission is to secure control of a legendary flying castle, a powerful stronghold that can change the course of their fate and the balance of power in the realm. Along the way, they must navigate treacherous alliances, uncover dark secrets, and face off against dangerous foes, all while seeking to restore the storm giant king, Hekaton, to his throne.
**Setting:**
- *Flying Castle:* A massive, enchanted fortress floating among the clouds, with various levels, dungeons, and hidden chambers. The castle's control systems are tied to Escarlotta, a ghost who can either aid or hinder the party depending on their actions.
- *Dracolich's Ice Cavern:* Deep within the castle lies a frozen lair where a Dracolich guards its phylactery. The cavern is filled with ancient magic, treasure, and hidden dangers, but it's also the key to Dracolich's power.
- *The Grand Dame (Gambling Boat):* Moored in Yartar, this luxurious floating casino is a den of vice and secrets. Here, the party must infiltrate to learn about the storm giant king’s fate, facing wealthy nobles, hired guards, and criminal elements while deciding whether to play by the rules or cause chaos.
- *Yartar and Thieves’ Guild Hideouts:* Yartar’s underworld provides crucial information and contacts, but navigating this criminal network may force the adventurers to make shady deals, face double-crosses, or earn valuable allies.
**Key NPCs:**
- **Blagothkus:** A cloud giant who once allied with the Cult of the Dragon but now stands as a pivotal figure in the castle’s control. His fate is tied to his son, who is trapped as a ghost until Blagothus is slain.
- **Escarlotta (Ghost):** Blagothkus’ spectral wife and the central controller of the flying castle’s systems. She is a complex entity—sometimes helpful, sometimes obstructive—who holds her own desires for the fate of the castle and her family.
- **The Dracolich Glazeal:** A cunning undead dragon who struck a deal with the party to retrieve its phylactery. Though powerful, it is not entirely trustworthy and may turn on the party if the situation benefits it.
- **Lord Khasperi Dryland:** The charismatic and secretive owner of the Grand Dame gambling boat. He is connected to the plot involving the storm giant king, Hekaton, but extracting information from him will not be easy.
- **Queen Serissa (Storm Giant):** Acting head of the storm giant court, Queen Serissa has tasked the party with finding her father, Hekaton. She provides guidance but is desperate, knowing that the disappearance of the storm king threatens the balance of power in the realm.
**Expanded Key Plot Points:**
- **Castle in Turmoil:** The adventurers must establish control over the flying castle. Blagothkis, driven by guilt and the desire to restore his family’s honor, may either help the party or become a hindrance. Escarlotta, controlling the castle’s systems, could side with the party if convinced they would save her son. This power struggle plays out as the adventurers decide the fate of the giant family and the flying fortress.
- **The Dracolich's Phylactery:** Hidden in a cavern of ice within the castle, the phylactery of the Dracolich Glazeal holds great power. To solidify their alliance with the Dracolich or to destroy it and free the castle from its influence, the party must battle through undead and icy dangers to reach the artifact.
- **Infiltration or Sabotage at The Grand Dame:** In Yartar, the adventurers must board Lord Dryland’s luxury gambling boat, "The Grand Dame," to uncover information about Hekaton's capture. The adventurers could take a subtle approach, using disguises, gambling, and gathering intelligence. Alternatively, they may choose direct action—disrupting the boat’s operations, defeating its guards, and potentially sinking it to the riverbed if they choose open conflict.
- **Gambling with Fate:** While on The Grand Dame, the adventurers can engage in high-stakes gambling that could result in tremendous riches or devastating losses. Through this, they may curry favor with or antagonize the boat’s elite patrons. They may unlock access to Dryland’s private quarters and darkest secrets if they win big.
- **The Hidden Network of Yartar:** Beneath the surface, Yartar is run by a secretive Thieves' Guild that holds the key to finding Hekaton’s kidnappers. The adventurers may need to negotiate with these criminals, trade favors, or make enemies of the city’s underworld to pursue the truth.
- **Storm Giants’ Desperation:** As the adventurers dig deeper into the plot surrounding Hekaton, they uncover a conspiracy involving human factions and ancient powers, all vying for control of the giants’ kingdom. Betrayals from unexpected sources, such as one of Hekaton’s closest advisors, add further twists to the tale.

# Chapter 80 The end of Sandesyl and Blagothkus

The cavernous room is lit brilliantly; the ice shakes with a roaring sound, and then silence. 

Orsik shrugs and looks inside the chest. There are thousands of gold coins and hundreds of platinum pieces. “Sandesyl chose to live in squalor,” he thinks. 

As he dumps the coins into his bag of holding, he finds a ring. 

He looks at it for a bit and hands it to Vardis. Vardis looks at it and puts it into his pocket. 

After emptying the last of her treasure, Orsik stretches his back and says, “We should see the Dracolich.” 

“Excuse me,” shouts the cloud giant. “I need some healing.” 

Thistle observes that he doesn’t think he turns to mist when brought to death’s door and, therefore, could use some healing himself. 

Vardis sighs and mutters, “Fine, let’s camp and find this undead dragon.” 

Thistle mutters, “We have two hours before the vampire reforms.” 

An hour later, they stand in the glacier's center, inside Glazhael the Undying's lair. She is surprised to see them living. 

“Sandesyl is dead?” 

“Mostly. We need your help to finish the job. Do you know where her lair is?” 

The dracolich smiles, and its eyes glow, “Yes. Do any of you have the magic to break through this ice?”

Unla Kee casts a firebolt. Glazhael rolls her eyes and sighs. She walks up to the same location and flicks her tail at the wall. The wall shatters, and behind it is a plain-looking coffin.

“Get me my phylactery.” 

Thistle is uncertain, “How do we know this is the right coffin?” 

“She’ll be inside the coffin. Open it, and you will find my phylactery. Once I have my phylactery, I will leave and you can do whatever you want.” 

Orsik and Vardis agree, “It was the deal.” 

Unla Kee can’t wait anymore. He opens the coffin, and there is the perfectly preserved corpse of Sandesyl, along with a small box inlaid with gold and locked shut. 

As he hands the box to Glazhael, Orsik asks her, “How do we kill a vampire?” 

Glazhael laughs, “I don’t know.” 

As he watches her leave, Unla Kee mutters - “I know.” 

Orsik turns to him, “Wooden stake through the heart? Holy wafers? Sunlight?” 

Unla Kee shakes his head. “Obviously, you went to a second-rate institution for your studies in necromancy, Orsik. At my alma mater, we learned that that was all rumors and superstitions. A vampire simply needs to be killed in its coffin. The how is irrelevant.”

Orsik shrugs.

Unla Kee examines Sandesyl and considers the power he could have if he were a vampire. He thinks he could manipulate her using telekinesis. 

But before he can, Orsik notices what the halfling is up to, pushes him asid, and stabs the vampire. 

The 3HPP and Blagothkus are in the control room a little bit later. There is Brunvild and a furious Escarlotta. 

“This is all your fault. You made a deal with dragons!” 

Brunvil adds, “And a vampire!” 

“And a vampire!” 

Escarlotta has heard from Brunvild the real version of how Blagothkus reacquired his castle and is unhappy. 

Orsik wants to kill Blagothkus because he doesn’t trust him. Thistle agrees but wants to make sure that Escarlotta doesn’t shut down the castle. He then remembers the ghost in the Eye of the All-Father. 


He begins, “Blagothkus, when we were,” and pauses for emphasis as if he can’t remember, “Where was it? Oh, yes, the oracle. We did run across this ghost that said he was trapped for all eternity.” 

Escarlotta’s gems form two narrow slits, and two red gems act as pupils that pulsate and stare at Blagothkus. 

Thistle continues, “I believe his name was Eigeron.”

“Our son!!! Is a ghost? What does he need to find peace?!” 

“He sent us here. He wanted revenge on the person who turned him into a ghost.” 

Escarlotta shouts, “Who?” 

Thistle let’s the question hang. 

Blagothkus whimpers, “That’s not what happened. It was an accident.” 

Escarlotta screams in rage, “Kill him! FREE MY SON!!!!” 
Orsik shrugs, “Happy to oblige.” 

Before Blagothkus can react, Vardis strikes him with a guiding bolt, Orsik smites him with the sword, Thistle pierces his critical arteries, Unla Kee burns him with electricity, and Brunvild finishes the job with one of Blagothkus’ old hammers. 

Escarlotta sobs. 

At this point, Brunvild tries to make a case for the castle being his because of salvage rights. Orsik, having killed one annoying giant, is itching to kill another. Brunvild can see the danger and mutters, “I salvaged the castle. It doesn’t belong to me, I just want … ” 

As the two of them get closer to blows, Escarlotta shrieks, “THIS IS MY CASTLE!” 

The room becomes silent. 

“You are passengers on my castle. You saved my son. So you have earned safe passage on my  castle.” 

Thistle mutters, “Delighted to be your guests!” 

Brunvild backs out of the room and mutters, “I’ll just find a nice place to sleep. Big castle, after all!” 

Before Orsik can offer any other helpful suggestions, Escarlotta’s gem forms two hands that are clasped together and, in the town of a disapproving mother, says - “Have you forgotten your mission?” 

“Put on a play?Frame Silverymoon for looting the Uthgardtian artifacts? 
“No, find Hekaton, defeat the blue dragon, save Thistle’s beloved?” 

Orsik can’t resist, “Uncover the plot of the Margasters?”  

She growls, “HEKATON is the priority. We have a clue. It’s in Yartar, and we are going there.” 

Later that day Orsik is looking down at the clouds and below that the Evermoors. He muttes to Thistle who is next to him, “It’s not that fast.” 

Escarlotta overhears him, “Are you saying something about my speed?” 

Thistle, who can sense that she is getting annoyed, mutters, “ No, we are not saying anything about the speed of your castle. It’s a fantastic castle!” 

The sun has set, and below are the lights of Yartar shimmering in the dark. The Grand Dame, is still at dock, getting ready to set sail for it’s nightly voyage. Escarlotta shouts, “So what’s the plan? Are we going to land and sink the boat? Are we going to sneak around? Queen Serissa’s clue isn’t -that- helpful. I mean, a wooden coin from a gambling casino? It could be a coincidence.” 

Thistle shouts, “We shall sneak. Take a position up here, and I will fly down.” 

Unla Kee watches him fly down and remembers that there are vampires in Yartar. “Perhaps that’s a way for me to achieve immortality. To what end? I have seen the end of the world, and I can save the world. Would being a vampire change anything?” He shakes his head. “No, it would not.” 

A few hours later, Thistle arrives back on the castle deck. 

“It’s a regular boat.” 

It’s nighttime, and they have time before the morning. 

“The problem is that we have this tenuous clue, a chip from a casino. We need to find a way to get someone to talk, ideally the ship’s owner,, Lord Khaspere Drylund.”

Vardis agrees with his friend, “We need to get him to talk.” 

Unla Kee asks, “What if we sank the boat and stole all the money from the bottom of the sea?” 

Thistle remarks, “Yes, but that would get in the way of us saving my girlfriend and defeating Adimond Kroskas. So first we get the information, then we can discuss sinking boats.” 

Orsik pats Unla Kee on the back, “We have 75,000 gold pieces in our treasure room. Let’s get down there and gamble some of it away. Lord Drylund wouldn’t let us in the last time; now he will beg to let us gamble on his Grand Dame!”

Thistle thinks, “One roll of the dice, and it will be my Grand Dame.” 

# Chapter 81 - On the Grand Dame

After a few days of an uneventful voyage in the clouds, Estrella shouts out 
“We are approaching Yartar.”
The 3HPP’s journey has led them to the unexpected location of Yartar and, more intriguingly, to a unique gambling boat known as the Grand Dame. Imber, a merchant on the docks, informed them that the ship was owned by Lord Khaspere Drylund, who was manipulating votes to become the next Waterbaron. The connection of a minor power's petty politician to the disappearance of the Storm Giant King remained unclear. 
“There is more to this boat than we think. Someone on the boat must know who could have been involved in a scheme to disappear a storm giant king,” Orsik declares, his determination palpable. 
Orsik then remembers how Waterbarons get elected. The job is a lifetime appointment. When the current Waterbaron dies, an election occurs. Thus, the current Waterbaron works to keep his enemies from uniting, and his friends close to evade a premature end to their tenure. This creates a very peculiar and lethal bit of politics in the city. 
Orsik then turns to the 3HPP, “Unla Kee is the gambling den expert, he should be on point here,” he says, showcasing their resourcefulness. 
“Right. Okay, how much money do we have?”
“A lot.” T
“We gamble it all.” 
Vardis glares at Orsik, who then turns to the crazed Wizard, “By on point, I mean you can give us your thoughts and ideas rather than make all the decisions. Our biggest problem is getting an invite onto the boat.” 
“Where do you want me to land the castle?” interrupts Escarlotta.
Thistle, who has added more formal gatherings than he remembers, sighs and says, “We could sink the boat and steal all the gold?”
Orsik gives him a dismissive glare, “We need information, and dead people can’t give us information.” 
And then the dwarf remembers getting invited to the Grand Dame was a problem.
Thistle, irritated and a master of court etiquette in the realm of the fey, mutters, “If we land the castle near the boat, it screams. 
“And what about the gambling?” mutters the halfling diviner. 
The crafty dwarf mulls over his options, “We could give him the only known bottle of Champagne de Stomp.”
Thistle sighs, “Look, if we show up with a cloud giant castle, a bottle of Champagne de Stomp, and announce that we are here to gamble, the Grand Dame will gladly let us in. There is nothing like arriving at a Casino in fancy clothes and an expensive transport that screams – take my money.” 
At this point, they all look at their clothes, “and we don’t have fancy clothes.” 
Fortunately, ever since Vardis was kicked out of the Cult of Correlon, he had to learn how to make his vestments. Using the fabric of the Cloud Giantess, he creates some epically showy numbers for the 3HPP. 
Orsik’s attempts are not as successful. Escarlota tsk his attempts, but he retorts, “I don’t have the practice and skill to create what is in my head.” 
As the group gets dressed, they stand on the tower walls, watching their castle land near the docks of Yartar.
“We have landed,” shouts Escarlotta. 
Thistle flies off the tower and heads to the ground; as he does that, a series of arrows lands nearby. 
He looks around and notices that the docks are abandoned. Several hundred feet away are the city walls, where everyone is very well-armed, apprehensive, and confused that a fairy has appeared. 
A few seconds later, Unla Kee lands and shouts, “WE HAVE 1,000,000 GOLD, AND IT’S BURNING A HOLE IN MY POCKET! I just ransacked a dragon hoard and stole a giant cloud castle. I WANT TO GAMBLE!!!” 
As the tower residents stare at the sight of a halfling with a 12-foot wingspan, he pulls out two bags of holding and shows them. 
The captain of the guard is about to shout when a voice from the gates shouts, “Captain, if they wanted us harm, they would have harmed. Let me speak to them.” 
A few moments later, Lord Khaspere Drylund and his retinue appear and introduce themselves. 
Orsik, who took a stroll, steps in front of Unla Kee and presents a bottle. “Lord Drylund, we have heard there is no better place to gamble on the Grand Dame. And I hope this bottle of Champagne de Stomp will get us in?” 
Confused, Lord Drylund stares at the bottle and says, “The Grand Dame is exactly where you should be. And I promise the food and alcohol will be comped.” 
He takes the bottle and hands it to one of his courtiers. 
Orsik is disappointed that the Lord doesn’t recognize the bottle's rarity but resists the urge to lecture him. 
Realizing that the cloud giant castle isn’t the herald of an invasion, the city guard re-opens the gates to the docks, and merchants and on-lookers quickly arrive. 
The 3HPP are gently whisked towards the Grand Dame, the owner wanting to ensure they get most of the money these foolish adventurers wish to gamble. 
As they board the boat from the docks, there is a minor bit of drama when the 3HPP is asked to give up its weapons, but Orsik remembers that he has other options now. 
From the dock, they enter the main cabin, which has been transformed into a gambling hall. The centerpiece is a gold-plated wooden sculpture of a goose resting on a table at the aft. Glasses of fine wine, free for the taking, are arrayed around it. An unflattering portrait of a fat, foppish, grinning man (Lord Drylund) hangs on the wall across a wooden spiral staircase leading up to the dance hall. 
Most of the floor space is taken up with tables of card games. From dusk until dawn, attractive dealers sit behind the tables, distributing cards to the players while small crowds look on. No actual currency passes into the hands of the hired help. Instead, bets are made with coin-sized wooden chips called golden geese, so named because each bears Lord Drylund’s golden goose emblem.
