Before the 3HPP can start to mingle, Pow Ming steps in front of them and asks, “How many chips will you be buying?” 
Orsik stares at him and asks, “What’s your job?” 
“I am in charge of everything so Lord Drylund can do his job.” 
The Shou human pauses, politely, and forcefully asks, “How many chips do you want?” 
“How many are required for the high-rollers table?”
He declares, “You want the Sava tables.” 
Thistle turns to Orsik - “Sava is a drow variant of the game of chess. Two players play against each other using ebony pieces resembling drow warriors, mages, and priestesses. The goal is to destroy the other player’s noble house. It is a game of skill, deception, and insight.” 
The fairy then muses, “In a city with this many lethal conspiracies, it’s no surprise that the rich and powerful play this game.” 
“So, how do we find a player?” 
“You get invited to play a game.” 
Thistle starts scanning the room to see who could be an easy mark. He spots a beautiful woman clad in a gorgeous purple dress. She has black hair that is cropped short on the sides. She’s wearing a stunning necklace set with black stones around her neck. 
Before Thistle can determine her skill, her white-haired noble opponent pushes a stack of golden geese across the table. He then rises from the plush chair and declares, “I don’t know how you do, Lady Atalia; you seemed to know my every move before I knew it myself. Thank you for the game,” and then bends down and kisses the woman’s extended hand. “Now I must find another table lest I squander all the coin I’ve spent a lifetime acquiring.” 
She softly says, “You do that, Lord Hubbard. Until next time.” 
Unla Kee quickly sits down to face her. 
Lady Atalia smiles, says, “Are you rich?” and then laughs. “Of course you are; you announced your fortunes the minute you arrived.” 
“What are the stakes?” 
“5000?” 
“Only?” 
“I thought we would play for 5000 and then see where it takes us. You are the great adventurers who stole a castle from cloud giants. I am a poor lady playing these games of skill, chance, and treachery.” 
Orsik realizes she’s trying to play them. Unla Kee, on the other hand, is convinced she is a poor lady who is out of her depth. 
Meanwhile, Thistle finally finds a mark, Unla Kee. 
As Orsik stares at the money he’s about to lose, he notices that Lady Atalia is probing his brain with telepathy. 
Orsik turns to Thistle and Vardis and says, “I can’t watch.” He pulls them away from what he hopes is her mind-reading range and explains the situation. Vardis observes that they have no magic they can use to counter her power. Thistle notes that she probably has a lot of information that she might be willing to trade. 
“Why?” 
“Because we could threaten her with that expose?” 
Vardis declares grimly - “But not in a dark alley. We do not threaten women in dark alleys.” 
Orsik turns to Vardis, “Okay.” 
The cleric then suggests that they lose the game and tries to convince her to talk and share information. 
With that, they approach the table. Unla Kee is excited and is about to pull off the golden geese, which are worth some 10,000 gold on the table. 

Orsik realizes that even if he told Unla Kee how she was cheating, the diviner would be confident that his victory was even more certain. So the dwarf first asks Unla Kee to let him play, but Unla Kee refuses. Then he tells him to get a drink, and Unla Kee calls a server. Finally, the fighter has had enough, pushing him out of the chair, “I am playing first because I’m smart. I’ve got the headband. I’m only going to come in for 1000. I can win this game because I am smart!” 
Unla Kee is momentarily upset, but the drinks arrive, and he forgets. 
Lady Atalia appears to be losing, and Orsik is a bit surprised and is about to go for the kill when she – obviously aware of his plans – counters and then wins. 
She puts the golden geese in a silk pouch and says, “Another game?” 
Orsik stares at her and says in a quiet but dangerous voice, “Oh, my fair lady, it’s not a fair game when you read my mind.” 
Lady Atalia denies it in an equally threatening and dangerous voice. 
Orsik thinks Lady Atalia doesn’t even want the whiff of scandal, so he mutters, “Perhaps we could have a private conversation about some other topics? You like gold, and we have a lot of it.” 
She stares and then speaks directly to his mind, “We need to talk. Not here. Follow me when the boat returns to the docks. Nod if you understand me.” 
With that, Orsik stands up and leaves. Unla Kee sees the open slot and rushes to sit down, but Orsik once again stops him. 
As the evening progresses, Vardis mutters about wanting to meet Lord Drylund. Pow overhears that and says Lord Khaspere Drylund would like to meet them for a private drink in his office. 
The private offices are decorated in the nouveau riche style, where expensive, tasteless things are displayed. The windows have purple velvet curtains and scented candles in gaudy candelabras. In the middle of the room is a table with a large aquarium tank resting atop it. The aquarium contains a coral reef that hosts Lord Drylund’s pet octopus. Other furnishings include a silk hammock, a writing desk (covered with quills and loose sheets of blank parchment), a small table holding a silver wine decanter and matching goblet, and an iron stove. 
“I see you already gambled with Lady Atalia?” 
Orsik smiles. 
“She’s perfect.” 
But Orsik isn’t very talkative. This annoys Drylund, who picks up the last remaining bottle of Champagne du Stomp outside of Ravenloft – “Shall we drink this?” 
Orsik turns pale, “My lord, you should hold onto that. Perhaps a lesser vintage?” 
“You’re right. I should save this for a better occasion when I become the Waterbaron?” The Lord laughs, “That’s exactly what I should do.” 
Thistle says, “Happy to share it with you then.” 
“Of course you would.” 
“And in that vein,” asks Orsik, “how can we help?” 
Lord Khaspere Drylund smiles, “Your arrival here has been a godsend and a positive endorsement for my candidacy.” 
“But unfortunately, when the election happens is unclear.” 
“People die every day,” observes Orsik. 
“I know such a tragedy. You know there’s been a rash of nobles disappearing.” 
“Thrown out of windows?” 
“Oh no! And how much could it hurt to fall from 10 feet up?” He pauses, “A group of adventurers freed one and magically reappeared next to this father.” He thinks, “Lord Zymorven’s son!” 
“Lord Zymorven’s son?” asks Thistle. 
“It was in all the papers?” asks Vardis. 
“Yes. And it was a terrible blow for the Waterbaron.” 
Orsik cuts to the chase. “Who are your key rivals? We like your establishment. We think you are the right choice for Yartar and want to ensure you end up where you deserve to be.” 
Thistle asks, “Who would be foolish to oppose you?” 
He stares at the group, “are you offering to help?” 
“We could kill all your opponents?” observes Orsik.
The Lord splutters and then composes himself. “No, that won’t work. Too many guilds and assassins would come looking for their killers. There are three ways we win elections. The first is convincing people we are the best person for the job. The second is by bribing folks with promises after you are made Waterbaron. The third is by holding a loved one’s ransom.” 
Thistle turns to Orsik, “Are you making him an offer to kidnap?”
Orisk brushes the fairy aside, “We’ve had some entanglements with various groups, and it would be good to have an entire city on our side. Certain cities are not on our side. You seem like the guy we can get behind and help each other.” 
Lord Drylund stares at them and tries to size them up. After a few moments, he says, “When I become the Waterbaron, I promise that Yartar will be friends with the 3HPP. And because of that friendship, the 3HPP has committed to protecting our cities from cloud giants!” 
The owner of the Grand Dame swallows the last remaining alcohol, “I think it’s best if we continue this conversation in a different private location. I need you to meet some associates.” 
Orsik smiles, “Very well then.” 
With that, Lord Drylund ushers the group out. As Orsik gets up, he decides that now is a good time to ask about the coin and why it might have been found where King Hekaton disappeared.

# Chapter 82 - Hekaton's location revealed at last

Orsik looks at the presumptive Waterbaron and smiles, “Getting information from him will be easy.” 

He shows Lord Drylund the golden goose that Queen Sansuri had given the 3HPP in the Maelstorm, a coin found on the body of one of the assailants who had murdered her mother, Queen Neri. A murder that would then lead to the unraveling of the Ordning and the disappearance of King Hekaton. 

Lord Drylund acts unimpressed, “Yes, that’s one of ours. I know it’s worth ten gold pieces, other than that …” 

Thistle is unimpressed with Orsik’s questioning. “Why is one coin different from another?” he muses. 

But Orsik was trying to throw Lord Drylund off his game, and he succeeded. The fool volunteers information - “On this ship, the coin is worth ten gold; off, it’s worthless. But you knew that. Why do you ask about it?” 

Orsik explains where the coin was found and why they are asking. 

Lord Drylund walks towards the aquarium, picks up his Octopus, and pets it. 

Thistle can’t figure it out Lord Drylund’s game. Unla Kee does and signals that Lord Drylund is lying and is worried. 

Orsik speaks reassuringly, “Why are you worried? We’re all friends.” 

Thistle flies up to him and says, “We are all friends.” 

Drylund mutters, “I am not nervous. I am not okay. But stories of murder and death of Queens and Kings of Storm Giants worry me. Imagine if they thought I was responsible.” 

Even Thistle can figure out he’s lying. 

Orsik reaches in with the verbal blade, “Look, we’re here to support you in this upcoming election. And we’re supposed to be meeting with your associates later today? And it would be great if we knew more by then.” 

Drylund splutters, “I will have nothing because I know nothing. We have many customers, and one must have left with a coin in their pocket.” 

“Then you won’t mind if we ask around?” 

“Ask!”
As they leave the room, Orsik asks, “So, where do we meet tomorrow?” 

“Ah yes, near the Waterbaron’s palace in the central market.” 

Thistle whispers to Orsik, “Isn’t that where they tried to kill us last time?” 

Orsik smiles and says, “Very well.” 

The night flies by. Unla Kee wanted to gamble, but the free booze got in the way. 

As they leave the boat in the early dawn hours at the Yartar Docks, they notice that Lady Atalia seems to be waiting for them. When they try to follow, she moves away discretely. But not so discretely that she cannot be followed. 

She stops and sits down at a tavern. The 3HPP sits down at her table. 
## 
Orsik.

“Mr. Orsik, please sit down,” she whispers to the dwarf. 

As he sits, she says, “This place has a drink that is perfect for getting rid of the effects of alcohol.” She stares at Unla Kee, “He will need five.” Then she turns to Orsik, “You wanted information, what kind?” 

He smiles, “You know.” 

“Yes, I do. You wish to know if your fake identity as your father is working out. You want to know if I have noticed how intelligent you are.”

Orsik is infuriated at the invasion of privacy and gruffly tells the group, “Someone else has to do the talking.” 

## Thistle 

“We want to know the connection between the Grand Dame and King Hexaton.”

Lady Atalia laughs. “That’s what your concern was? I completely misread you. I thought you were looking for Lord Artan’s kidnappers.” 

Thistle is confused, “Lord, who?” 

“A young lord of Yartar who was kidnapped. I thought you were looking for his kidnappers.” 

Orsik can’t be quiet and interrupts - “Look, that’s a very parochial concern. Yes, we know about Lord Drylund and his kidnappings and his assassinations. And we don’t care. We are interested in the connection between the Grand Dame and the disappearance of King Hexaton.” 

Thistle notices that she looks pretty anxious. As he probes deeper, he picks up on the fact that she, like Drylund, is trying hard to deflect the conversation away from King Hexaton. It’s as if they are afraid of talking bout it.

“I can’t help, but I know someone who could. An evil spell caster named Oosith.” 

## Vardis

“Evil is very binary. And it’s pretty judgemental of you. I mean, what if there is some underlying childhood trauma?”

Lady Atalia is taken aback. She didn’t expect this from a cleric of Corellon. 

“You are so right. And I am so glad you said that. Because now I feel I can unburden myself to you. Oosith has been running the operation to kidnap lordlings. I have been blackmailed to help them. How? Why was my ability at Suave noticed, and then Lord Drylund figured it out? He insisted that I help. I had no choice.”

Vardis looks at her and says, “Yes, it’s amazing how when our lives are on the line, we have no choice.” 

“Exactly.” 

Orsik then nods, “It’s like killing a Vampire by making a deal with Lich.” 

“Could we Stay on the Acolich Dray?” 

“It’s just an example. She doesn’t know what we are talking about, do you?” 

Sensing that there is more than she wants to know, Lady Atalia says, “No, I have no idea.” 

Vardis turns to her again, “Do you know anything about this person?”

“Not very much, other than he is in the sewers to avoid being caught by the Hand or the Shield of Yartar. Fish People protect him.”

Vardis turns to Thistle, who mutters, “Kuo-Toans.” 

Lady Atalia reaches out to Vardis and says, “Look, I know where he is. And you would do me a great favor in killing him. I am not an evil person. I was blackmailed. And I do so desperately want to go back to the light!” 

Vardis groans as he thinks of all those foolish people who believe in the “light” and “dark” motif of morality and goodness. He’s tempted to give her a full-throated sermon on the perversion of that motif in a world where people can see in the dark, but then he decides not to.

Instead, he turns to Orsik and says, “I think we learned everything we needed to. We have another meeting to go to.” 

Lady Atalia stares at him, “You won’t help?” 

And the 3HPP walked away. 

## Unla Kee

The wizard is sober. And he was able to get enough rest to memorize his spells. He’s pretty pleased with the drink the tavern produced and is determined to find out the recipe. 

But now, he’s in a market full of goods. 

Just then, Lord Drylund appears. 

“Let me introduce you to Tapouach, who sells the best apples. And Tapuz, whose oranges are impressive for their sweetness. And here’s Mitbach, where anyone buys goods for their kitchen.” 

Unla Kee notices Tapouach’s purse, which is close to his hand. He lifts it while Drylund talks to the poor merchant. 

When Unla Kee opens the bag, he finds 23 coppers. “This guy is so poor. That was a waste of my time.” So he decides to steal an apple as well. 

As Drylund speaks, he eats it. 

Tapouach notices, but realizing that Unla Kee is a friend of the Lord, he smiles. 

Orsik 

“So we are here to meet with someone, a Sorcer named Oosith perhaps, who specializes in kidnappings?” 

Lord Drylund, who speaks with the characteristic h’s and gargled r’s of the Calamanishan, is shocked. 

“We do not discuss this here. We use code. Oosith is my campaign manager in charge of special persuasion operations.” 

Orsik says, “And he’s in the sewers?” 

Drylund looks angered, “How did you know this?” 

Before Orsik can dazzle with his intelligence, Vardis answers, “Because we are equally connected.” 

Lord Drylund mutters, “This is an unacceptable security risk. I must know.” 

Orsik is still puzzled by the choice of language, “Campaign Manager?” 

Lord Drylund laughs, “It’s essential to appeal to what the voters value. They value their children, and I have them. If they wish to receive them, they will vote for me.” 

“You need a better campaign manager, and I think we can do a better job than this guy.” 

“Perhaps? Or you could work together? I will take you to him.” 

“You will.” 

And he does, leading them deep into the sewers of Yartar. The sewer tunnels are roughly 8 feet high, and there is a trench in the middle where the sewage flows. On either side of the trench is a pathway. 

At last, the sewers open into a large area, and a Kuo-toan appears. 

Thistle 

“Spend enough time with the Cult of the Dragon, and you will learn undercommon,” Thistle mutters. 

Thistle translates for the 3HPP. 

“The KuoToan is unhappy with Drylund because only the emissary is supposed to come here. Drylund then explains that they are friends. The KuoToan then mutters that they are not members of the society. Drylund then assures - him/her/them - that they will soon become members.”  

Just then, Thistle interrupts, “We are friends and have come to help.” 

The KuoToan glares, “This is a private society business.” 

The fish man turns to Drylund - “You should have never brought them here.” 

Drylund responds, “But they wanted to meet the boss.” 

At that, the Kuo Toan says, “The boss, yes, I think they should.” 

Drylund then turns to the 3HPP: " Unfortunately, I cannot join you. For operational security reasons and plausible deniability, I cannot see Oosith.” 

The Kuo Toan laughs, “The boss doesn’t want to see your ugly face!” 

Thistle latches onto that - “Lord Drylund, will you let them speak to you like that?” 

Drylund is demure, “They are doing their job.” 

Orsik then tries to use a deep, scary voice to intimidate Lord Drylund, but he slips, and his voice becomes squeaky. 

But Drylund caves. 

“I insist we go,” he says to the Kuo Toans. 

Thistle is happy and exclaims, “We are here to ensure that we clear up any misunderstandings between you and Mr Oosith.” 

And with that, three more Kuo Toans emerge from the darkness and march Drylund and the 3HPP through a great ladder and down a ladder to a much larger circular room. The room is filled with water except for a walkway heading east to a small room at the other end. Along the edge of the circular room is a walkway that heads north and south from the entrance that leads to a pipe and a valve. The room is 15 feet high. The water appears to reach a depth of 15 feet. 

Thistle hears a voice in their head as they step on the ground: “I apologize for the unpleasant local. Perhaps you could come to my office?” 

The rest of the 3HPP acknowledge that they also hear it. 

“Finally, a friendly voice,” acknowledged the ranger. 

Orsik nods and is pleased when he says, “At last, someone treating us with the respect and dignity we deserve!” 

Thistle piles on, “It so good to meet friends and allies.” 

“Lord Drylund tells me that you wish to help him get elected? Such a conversation will work best in my office at the end of the walkway. And Mr Kee, there is alcohol.” 

Thistle laughs when he hears Orsik say, “A siren song.” 
## 
Orsik

“I only trust Vardis. And I don’t trust you,” Orsik thinks. 

He grabs Drylund and shoves him forward. 

The voice says gently, “There is no need for hostages, Mr. Orsik.”

Orsik ignores the voice and thinks, “And that’s why I have one.” 

Drylund is shoved first into the room. Orsik expects a trap, but instead, he finds a room that looks like the offices of a very important person. 

The KuoToans follow the 3HPP to the door.  

Just then, the trap is sprung. The Kuo Toans slam the door shut and try to lock it. 

Unla Kee counters by casting telekinesis and shoving the door open. 

Drylund pleads with Orsik, “You have to save me. They’ll kill me.” 

Orsik pauses and stares at the man, “You have to tell me what you know about Hekaton.” 

“Is that your only price?” 

“Yes.” 

Drylund looks terrified, takes a deep breath, and whispers, “I’m a member of the Kraken Society. We’ve been keeping Hekaton prisoner on the Morkoth. It’s a special ship that can’t be detected. The Morkoth sails around the northern islands of the Trackless Sea.” 

“So how do we find it?” Orsiks asks. 

“You have a flying castle; fly there and use your eyes. Now, can you please…” 

Orsik sees his eyes bulge out in terror, and then they roll back into his eyesockets, and blood flows from his nose as he dies in Orsik’s hands. 
And then the mysterious voice calls out, “Well, that was annoying. Finding a new Waterbaron will be tricky. And we will have to kill you.” 

The creature tries to charm Orsik and fails. 
## 
Thistle 

“There's no point in letting this go to waste,” says the fairy as he quickly moves the treasure into Orsik’s bags of holding. 

Two items grab his attention. The first is a pendant with a capital R wrapped in robes that belonged to nobles. 

Vardis looks at it and mutters, “Another fetch quest.” 

The other is a peculiar device that causes some scattered golden goose coins to glow. Thistle picks one up and notices a number. 

Unla Kee burps, “Well, that’s how casinos prevent forgeries.” 

# Chapter 83 -  Kee and Wing Casino

Lord Khaspere Drylund was dead. And the darkest secret of the entire War of the Giants was revealed: King Hekaton was alive. More importantly, Orsik knew precisely where he was. 

Now, all he had to do was get out of the sewers. 

But to get out of the sewers, he had to get out of this circular chamber. 

And to get out of that room, he had to get through four Kuo-Toans. 

Easy-peasy. 

The Kuo-Toan, in the water, tosses a net to capture him. It falls to the side harmlessly. 

Still, in the room at the end of the walkway, Thule rushes out the door past the Kuo-Toan, who was trying to hold the door shut. Seizing the opportunity, they strike and miss. 

Thule returns the favor with a blast of his breath weapon. 

Thistle sees two Kuo-Toans further down the ramp, casts a lightning arrow, and then hits them both. 

With the two Kuo-Toans that tried to strike Thistle but could not hit Vardis, the cleric rushes through and uses his mace of terror. A field of powerful energy fills the room. Only one of the Kuo-Toans is unaffected. The affected three jump into the water and submerge themselves as far from Vardis as possible. 

To the north and south of the room are two valves. Next to each valve is a Merrow. They simultaneously turn them, and water rushes in from two pipes to the east. The room starts to fill with water. 

The one remaining Kuo-Toan Whip tries to capture Thistle with his net, but he deftly avoids him. 

Unla Kee uses his telekinesis to open one of the drainage valves, slowing the water flow. 

Orsik stares at the water and remembers his ring of swimming: " I never thought I would need this thing.” He jumps into the water, swims south, reduces the Merrow to 5 HP, and then closes the valve using his superior strength. 

The mysterious Oosith finally reveals himself. He is an aboleth, and his first action is to dominate Unla Kee. His attack on Thistle fails when Vardis uses his powers as a light cleric to blind the creature. 

The three Kuo-Toans, who were frightened, now flee through the drainage opened drainage hole.

Thistle strikes the Merrow to the North. Again, the Merrow is not killed, but the damage caused the domination of the Aboleth to be broken. The creature sees what’s going on and decides to flee. 

Vardis opens another drainage valve, and now the water level drops. 

Seeing the water level drop, the only Kuo-Toan whip in combat closes the drainage valve Unla Kee had turned on. 

The aboleth commands Unla Kee - “Destroy them!” 

Unla Kee ponders the options for a moment. On the one hand, this is the perfect time to use a dominant person, but of course, he didn’t prepare that spell. “Fireball,” he thinks and then discards it. “I know,” he says, pulling out his rock, the wand of wonder. 

And he becomes invisible. 

The Aboleth shrieks in everyone’s mind, “What the hell did you just do?!” 

Orsik and Vardis mutter, “Yeah, that’s Unla Kee for you.” 

Orsik swims from the southern valve to where the last Kuo-Toan stands and kills him. 

The Aboleth shrieks, “The Kraken will have its revenge!”

Orsik stares at the dead body of Lord Drylund, turns to his friend, and asks, “Hey, Vardis, can we raise him?” 

“I am a light cleric, not a necromancer.” 

Orsik groans, “You can cast raise dead?” 

“Oh, right.” Vardis steps close to the body and announces, “Like all Politicians, that’s impossible. His brain is gone.” 

Thistle observes that True Resurrection would work, but that would cost 50,000 gold pieces, and that is that. 

Thistle then quickly scours the body for any treasure and finds a ring with keys, 120 gold pieces, and three platinum pieces. 

Orsik mutters, “I want my Champagne Du Stomp, back.” 

Thistle looks at the body, wondering what to do about it, and Orsik shouts, “Take the gold, leave the body.”

Thistle agrees. Thuul picks it up in his jaws and drops it in the room. Thistle covers it up and locks the door to the best of his ability. 

A few minutes later, the 3HPP emerged, covered in sewage, into the sunshine. 

Thistle mutters, “Lady Thalia? There’s a power vacuum, and someone needs to fill it.” 

Orsik retorts, “She wanted us to kill the Aboleth, and the Aboleth is very much not dead.” 

Vardis looks at him oddly, confused that his friend is so unwilling to run a trivial scam. He sighs and says, “We’ll lie?” 

“What about Pow?” asks Thistle. 

“The COO. That sounds like an excellent idea,” acknowledges Orsik. Orsik is delighted that his newfound intelligence allows him to entertain other ideas. “What would we offer him?” 

“Not to die,” say Vardis and Thistle in unison.

Orsik is disappointed and realizes that his role is to help the team improve their ideas. “That’s not a good plan. You can threaten him once, but once he leaves, he will have no loyalty to us. We are not going to stay here forever.” 

Thistle thinks for a moment, “We can offer him more?” 

“He can easily say I want a hundred percent as this is my casino.” 

Thistle jokingly says, “What if we put the Casino in the Castle?” 

Orsik smiles, “Now that is a good idea.” 

“And we could have shows? We gotta have shows!” 

“Of course, it’s a casino.” 

Orsik rubs his chin, “This is a big upgrade from a boat docked in Yartar. A flying castle going around the world with live theater is a really good idea. Yeah, that’s a great idea.” 

Thistle red teams the proposal, “What about the customers? Will they want to go on a Casino for an indefinite period of time?” 

Vardis turns to him, “It’s like a cruise ship. When we land, they can come on and off. We can even make it so they can visit any place before we go and pillage and mess it up.” 

“I am not sure about the business model.” 

Orsik pooh-pooh’s him, “It’s a casino, the strongest model. Who could bankrupt a casino?”

Thistle and Vardis stare at Unla Kee. 

Thistle insists they talk to Lady Atalia first after deciding on their plan. 

Sitting at another fashionable cafe, Lady Atalia smiles when she sees the 3HPP. Her smile evaporates when she discovers that they didn’t kill the Aboleth. 

“You let that thing escape? Damn you.” She stands up, straightens her clothes, and marches out of the cafe, furious. 

As the 3HPP tries to catch up to her, a small group of female rogues stops them. 

“Lady Atalia is under our protection.” 

“We did just that. We got rid of someone who threatened her. A sorcerer named Oosith.” 

The rogues look at the very well-armed 3HPP and shrug, “In that case, we’ll just follow you.” 

When they approach her house, Lady Atalia is standing outside looking very pissed off. Thistle tries to speak to her, and she starts snarling at him with a very plebian dock accent, “You were supposed to kill him. I don’t want anything to do with you. We’re done.” 

Thistle is taken aback, “You are not going to become the new Waterbarron with an attitude like that.” 

She stares at him, “The Kraken doesn’t like it when anyone disrupts its plans. Drylund’s going to come back, and he’s going to be …” 

Orsik mutters, “I don’t think he’s coming back. But you knew that?” 

Lady Atalia sighs, “I would if I had drunk my potions. It’s amazing how easy it is for people to read if they think you can read their minds.” 

And with that, the door is closed. 

Just then, one of the Hands turns to Orsik, “We would be delighted to take an equitable share of the Grand Dame, as well as an operating fee and a security fee.” 
Thistle asks, “And that would be how much?” 

“95% of the turnover and an annual security fee.”

The 3HPP nods politely and heads out to the docks. Their stench allows them to quickly go through the crowds that work to avoid being near them. 

Thistle again cautions against the Casino plan as they head to the Grand Dame - “A flying casino will have a repeat business problem.” 

Vardis brushes him off - “It’s like a cruise ship.” 

Once at the ship, they meet Pow. 

Orsik explains the plan, and Pow is intrigued. He speaks with the accent of a citizen of Shou - “There is a tremendous opportunity here that I had not considered. We land in a city, open up the rooms for gambling, and then fly to a different location for more gambling. The nature of our flying ship makes it possible for us to avoid any taxation and protection fees. We also have a lot more space and rooms.” 

Thistle notes, “And there are kitchens and no monsters. Like there is no undead, and this whole area in the ice caves could be a winter wonderland.” 

Unla Kee mutters, “I am only going along with this if I get naming rights. I want this place to be called *Kee and Wing Casino*.” 

Pow shakes hands with the halfling. 

Thistle then flies off and heads to Lord Drylund’s room. There, he finds no incriminating paperwork or notes, and there appears to be no correspondence. He is surprised. Any organization needs some way to communicate. 

He does find some treasure, including an ornately decorated weapon with an octopus pommel. 

Before he heads down, he suddenly remembers the crazy woman in Everlund who warned about the Kraken. She was a member of a group called “Fighters of the Light, " a group that the 3HPP had helped and, as a result of helping, had become members. 

# 
Chapter 84 - Thistle Decodes the Conspiracy

Thistle remembers Mary. Mary was an old lady in Everlund who warned passers-by of the Kraken. She had sent them on a goofball quest to get what she called an artifact but was a trinket. Her group was known as the “Fighters of the Light.” He remembers that part of the reason they went on the quest was that they didn’t have enough money to pay the admission fee, which was, at the time, *five* gold pieces.  

The artifact had been in the dark, brooding peaks of the Nether Mountains. Orsik and the team had been intrigued by the many legends of lost Netherese magic. Uncle Joon had noted that the mountains were also full of orcs, while the eastern peaks were home to several blue dragons chased out of the northern Anauroch desert by the Doom of the Desert.

A detail that Thistle had ignored at the time. Now he knew who the Doom was: Adimond Kroskas. 

“So what did you find,” asks Unla Kee.

“Nothing of value,” answers Thistle. 

Orsik is suspicious and asks about treasure, and Thistle quickly shares that. 

“That’s valuable.” remarks the mage. 

“No evidence of any written communications,” shrugs the fairy. “There’s always some written communication.”

“Were you expecting printed stationary?” 

Unla Kee wonders for a moment about the Kraken Society and whether they are using the name Kraken or whether there is some kind of Kraken manipulating events. Krakens were covered in the Nirany School of Magic, his alma mater, but more as legendary creatures, not as things that exist. 

As for Thistle, Uncle Joon never mentioned anything about society, but Krakens were famous in the Feywild. 

Unla Kee explains, “According to the sage of legendary creatures, they are Leviathans of Legend. At the beginning of time, Krakens served as fierce warriors of the gods. When the gods’ wars ended, the Krakens shrugged off their servitude, never again bound by other beings. According to Prof BigEyes, the Kraken is a primeval force that obliterates civilization's greatest achievements as if they were castles in the sand. I remember Prof BigEyes observing that when such creatures interact with the rest of the world, societies, and cults spring up to worship the creature. Such a thing may have happened with this Kraken.” 
Thistle then mutters, “But whether the Kraken Society is affiliated with a Kraken or just plays dress-up is unclear. Any evidence of telepathy?” 

Unla Kee scratches his head, trying to remember, “Oh, yeah. I  did ask how they talk and what languages they speak, and we were told telepathy over a limited range, like 100 feet.” 

Thistle thinks aloud - “Lord Khaspere Drylund and Lady Atalia are not the kind of people with the resources to steal a giant and hide him in a boat that can’t be tracked. What’s more likely that some mythical powerful creature would be involved in the mayoral elections of some non-descript town or dragons.” 

Everyone turns to him, confused. 

“Ilymrith, or Adminond Kroskas, wanted to continue his experiments on fairies.” He pauses for dramatic effect, “LOOK AT MY EYES!!!!” 

Pow is about to offer a compliment when he picks up the body language of the rest of the 3HPP and has the appropriate look of horror. 

“He needed a patron, and who better than the desperate Queen of the Storm Giants. Ilymrith is probably behind the dismantling of the Ordning itself. With Hekaton out of the way, he could work into Queen Serissa’s good graces and continue his work. The Kraken society is just a rebranded Cult of the Dragon.” 

Unla Kee looks at Thistle and tries hard not to point out the logical flaws but can’t help but wonder how a dragon could manipulate a lost oracle of giants. He then thinks that, more importantly, if such a dragon existed with such power, how would Unla Kee get that power? Given the opportunity for personal power, Unla Kee tries to poo-poo the idea, “A dragon with such power? I’m sure the dragon is a patsy. We shouldn’t try and kill him but talk.” 

“Dragons are evil. Did you see what he did to my eyes? DRAGON EYES!” 

Even the oblivious mage can pick up on the tone of anger from Unla Kee’s voice and is silent. 

Thistle then mutters, “I am not a freak.” 

As Thistle sits there stewing, Unla Kee wonders if there is another approach they could take to redirect Thistle from his murderous rage. 

“Could I join the Kraken society?”

“Do you want to work for a cult that has a remote brain-exploding device?” 

Unla Kee shudders. 
“What about the SEER? We could ask her?” 

Vardis splutters and Orsik shuts that idea down. 

At this point, Thistle has recovered from his rage, “Whenever there is trouble, it’s a dragon.” 

The 3HPP stares at him. 

He then rants, “The War of the Dragons, that thing that cost me my eyes.” He pauses, closes his eyes, and says, “I am not a freak.” After taking a deep breath, he continues, “The War of the Dragons was brought upon by Tiamat. She wanted to come into the world. So she tears up the North. And that’s how Iymrith gets the money to start capturing Fairies. Then, the First Faction defeats Tiamat, and Iymrith needs a new sponsor. The Dragon needs someone with resources and is desperate. Iymrith comes up with the plan of perverting the Oracle of Amman and starting a Giant war. She had hoped to use Eigeron, but his idiot father, Blagothkus, killed him. She’s about to give up on the plan and then comes up with a new plan. The war can still start. She just needs to find a new ally. She uses the Oracle to tell every giant lord that the ordning has been sundered. And the war starts. When the war started, she figured a giant would want her help. But they are all caught up in their master plan. Some are worried that if they work with a dragon, they will lose favor with their gods. None of them suspect the scam.  With the war raging, Iymrith is desperate, and she hatches a new plan involving the Storm Giants. She approaches Hekaton with an offer of help, but Hekaton says no. What to do? The Doom of the Desert decides to kill Nerissa. Iymrith assumes the King would go ballistic over the Queen’s death and wants her fairy-dragon hybrid to wage war on the little people. But Serissa gets in the way and convinces Hekaton to calm down, research, and figure it out. So, Iymrith now decides to get rid of the King. But this time, she wants him unreachable. So the dragon kidnaps Hekaton and hides him on a ship that no one can scry. Without Hekaton, Queen Serissa doesn’t have the credibility to hold the Giant world in order. Iymrith gets an introduction from the two sisters with a promise to deliver the throne to them. Queen Serissa is desperate for help and agrees to help the Dragon with his insane experiments. Now, no one must suspect that a Dragon is behind all this. I, of course, have a particular insight into their evil and saw through her plans. Lymrith needs a patsy and some muscle to kill Nerissa and kidnap Hekaton. What a better patsy than a bunch of dead-end cult of the dragon losers who are told that there is a new super powerful big bad that is a KRAKEN!” He pauses, sweat on his folder dripping, his eyes half-crazed, “It all fits.” 

Pow and the 3HPP stare at each other. Unla Kee breaks the silence, “Yep, that sounds about right.” 

“I’m guessing this means we are not going to Everlund?” asks Vardis

“Straight to the northern isles of the Trackless Sea.” 

Pow then turns to his new partners, “Will we have time to hire a crew to start the cleanup?” 

They look at him. 

“I need to hire a crew for basic cleanup and demolition work. I am thinking of ten people. It will cost about three sp per laborer, and we will have to double that since we are asking them to fly on a castle. And then there is the cost of supplies, so 1000 gold?” 

“If you stay here for a few days, I know some guys.” 

Thistle and Unla Kee want to purchase magic with their newfound wealth and agree. 

The next day, Pow has got his crew and says they will need one more day for supplies. The Shouan then starts to probe about the budget. 

He quickly explains that it will cost about 500,000 gold to turn the entire place into a grand casino. But for it to be a serviceable Casino, they will need 50,000 gold. As the place makes more money, they can reinvest and make it the jewel of the realms over time. 

Somehow, the conversation turns to theater. Pow asks, “You had this amazing theatre group headlined by that actor Bob, who was always in his demon costume! I went to several of the shows in Yartar. I heard he went to Silverymoon. The story I heard from a customer is that the place burned down, and he disappeared.” 

Orsik explains that the theater burned down, but Bob hasn’t disappeared. He has decided to take a leave of absence. 

Thistle explains that they still have the script of the play. 

Vardis reminds Thistle quietly that the script is a summoning ritual for a demon that they barely averted. 

Pow is pleased to hear that the script was saved, “Excellent! With Bob and his crew gone, we can use that script to bring in the crowds.” He then turns to the crew he has hired, “These are trusted members of the town. I have worked with them for many years, and they are all good people.” 

Thistle notes that they all appear to be from Shou and wonders to whom they are personally loyal. 

As they wait for the materials to be loaded, Thistle decides to talk to Escarlotta and see if they can do anything for her. 

She blushes when she hears that Thistle wants to do something for her. All control gems start to pulsate red, and Thistle assumes it’s blushing. 

“The grass on the tower and the tower itself is very dirty. It would be nice to have it clean!” 

“Done!” He shouts to Pow, “Our priority is to clean this tower.” 

“And some gold trim on the door with a nameplate …” 

“We will get to that!” And shouts to Pow - “put that on your list.” 

Finally, the day to leave arrives. As the ship takes off, Unla Kee asks Pow, “So if I gamble, can we set it up so I win?” 

Pow looks at Unla Kee as if to a small child to whom he must explain the simplest things, “Yes, but then we would lose money.” 

Unla Kee is unconvinced. 

Escarlotta sets the course for the Northern Isles of the Trackless Sea.  



# Chapter 83  - King Hekaton is free. The Ordning can be restored. Promises are made. 

Orsik sees the Wizard and throws two hand axes. The first ax hits, and the second embeds itself in the mast behind the wizard.

“What the hell happened?” 

Unla Kee sniggers, “He had a contingency spell and probably teleported to somewhere safe.” 

Orsik picks up the hand ax that didn’t disappear with the wizard and kills one of the cultists climbing up the mast. 

Vardis looks down into the sleet storm. Orsik shouts, “Light them up.” 

A few moments later, the mote disappears into the sleet storm, and a bright spherical light forms. Correlon's cleric is unsure how many died, but he heard several screams. 

And one of the cultists, drenched and shivering, appears on the top aft deck of the ship. He is outside the storm and sees Vardis. Using the armed ballista, he shoots, and Vardis is wounded. 

Unla Kee, flying with his pegasi wings, looks at Thistle, who nods and then unleashes a fireball on the few remaining cultists, burning Thule. 

Thistle looks into the storm, but his eyes of True Seeing don’t reveal an invisible Wizard. 

Orsik shouts, “Free the Giant, then let’s use the Conch to get out of here.” 

Thistle nods but wants to kill the Wizard, so he scouts around the fore part of the ship. But sees nothing. 

Orsik and Vardis climb into the sleet storm and arrive at the chains binding Hekaton.

Thule tries to burn the chains with his acid and fails. 

Orsik swings his blade, and one of the chains breaks. “This will work!” 

Vardis is about to swing his sword when Orisk realizes that magical weapons must break the chains. Vardis acknowledges his brilliant friend and casts a spiritual weapon. The weapon strikes the chains to no effect. 

The archmage appears inside the aft cabin and casts prismatic spray. And once the spell is cast, close the door. 

Orsik feels a blue light envelopes him and freezes him. His body aches from the cold, but he lives. More importantly, the pain does not distract him from keeping the spell working. 

As for Vardis, an indigo light covers him but does nothing. 

Unla Kee shrieks, “This sleet storm is a nuisance. I can’t burn anything I can’t see!” Then he remembers that he can cast haste. He flies into the storm but cannot fly and lands prone on the deck. 

Just then, the first mate opens the door to the same cabin with the wizard and strikes down Unla Kee with a poisoned weapon. 

Orsik screams, “Just get some healing to me, and I will break these chains!!!” 

Thistle acknowledges and flies towards Orsik, but a chunk of sleet knocks him onto the ship's deck. Thistle mutters, “This storm is getting in the way.” 

Orsik then shrugs his shoulders, tries to move through the storm, slips, gets up, and breaks one chain. “Two chains left, and it’s all on Vardis.” He shouts, “YOU CAN DO THIS!!!!” He then thinks, “Damnit, my DNRRR has no Do not Reanimate clause. And with my luck, this wizard is a necromancer.” 

The wizard opens the door and glares at the storm. He thinks for a moment and then casts a cone of cold. However, Unla Kee saw him and countered the magic. The wizard glares and re-enters his room. 

The first mate shouts, “You will die, halfling.” And indeed, Unla Kee is brought to death’s door. 

But Thistle was ready and struck him with an arrow. The first mate staggers inside. Thuul uses that opportunity to rush in.

Orsik staggers toward a chain. He stands and swings his sword. The blow misses the chain. Then he raises his blade again and hits the metal, but it is a glancing blow. He glares. 

Vardis brings his spiritual weapon down on one chain and damages it, but the other slash is as ineffective as the first. 

The Wizard steps around Thuul, twirling his hand, and a whirlwind erupts from it to roar outward. Flakes of snow and shards of ice materialize in the freezing air, whipping in ever-widening circles as the blast fans out from his fingertips. The skin of his hand grows pale and tingles with the cold, but it’s nothing compared to what the spell inflicts on the remaining members of the 3HPP. 

And this time, the sleet storm is gone. Orsik has survived one murderous attack after another and kept focused, but this last one was too much. And as the spell evaporates, he can look around at the carnage of the battle. Burned corpses were soggy from the storm, blood dripping from the bodies that fell from the fast onto the deck, and Unla Kee was barely hanging on. Vardis, he can tell, is also scarcely alive. 

Thistle can see the first mate, aims an arrow, and hits the broken door the first mate used for cover. But Thistle has a second shot, and his aim is true this time. 

But Vardis’s spiritual weapon slashes through one chain and then moves again and slashes through another. Vardis swings his magical mace and destroys the damaged chain. 

The Wizard stares down at his opponents, and for a moment, he wonders if he should turn into a Tyrannosaurus Rex and rip their guts from end to end. But then he thinks, “Perhaps one of them might have some other trick up their sleeve? No, I need to kill them all at once.” 

And Vardis sees the familiar twist of his hand and the snow and shards of ice whipping in ever-widening circles from the wizard’s fingertips. He tries to avoid the worst damage but is still barely alive. 

As for Orsik, he’s out. 

Seeing Orsik out and thinking that Cleric can be finished shortly, the first mate attempts to kill Thuul. And the summoned beast disappears. He smiles; he has enough time to kill Vardis, but Vardis sees him and summons Correlon's light, and the first mate misses. 

Thistle, barely alive, shouts, “I have a plan!” 

Vardis stares at him and thinks, “This is going to be another one of those hare-brained schemes of his.” He shouts, “JUST HEAL ORSIK SO WE CAN TELEPORT OUT OF HERE!” 

Thistle shouts, “I plan to pop a goodberry into Orsik’s mouth.” 

And he does.

Orsik recovers from his near-death experience, pulls out the conch, and blows. 

Moments later, they find themselves in the grotto in the basement of the storm giant stronghold, the Maelstrom, sitting on an arcane gate. 

Hekaton stands up. 

The Storm Giant King fills the space, his head nearly scraping the vaulted ceiling carved from a single, colossal geode that pulsed with a faint, internal light. He is a mountain of a being, sculpted not from granite but from the very essence of a tempest.
His skin holds a thunderhead's deep, bruised purple, laced with a network of fine lines like lightning scars across a vast plain. Hair, the color of winter moonlight, cascaded down his broad shoulders, mingling with a beard as thick and tangled as a storm-wracked forest. 
His eyes, though. Those were the proper windows to his soul. They were the color of a storm-churned sea, shot through with flickering veins of amethyst light, currently swirling with a maelstrom of grief and suppressed rage. Those eyes had witnessed the rise and fall of empires, the slow crawl of glaciers, and the birth of stars. Now, they held the crushing weight of recent loss, the raw wound of betrayal festering beneath his regal composure.
He is clad in armor crafted from the scales of a beast known only in legends. Each scale is larger than a shield and overlaps like the plates of a fortress. The armor shifts and shimmers with each subtle movement, reflecting the geode's light in a dazzling, chaotic dance. Around his waist is a belt of woven lightning, from which hang talismans of bone and carved gemstones, humming with barely contained power. 
The air crackles around him, heavy with the scent of ozone and the distant rumble of thunder. He is a storm-given form, a king stripped bare by grief yet still radiating an aura of power that could topple mountains and silence the heavens. He stands there, a colossus silhouetted against the shimmering light, a king teetering on the precipice of despair, his every breath a promise of the storm to come. He is Hekaton, and the very stones trembled before him. Heavy with the weight of a thousand storms, his gaze sweeps across the 3HPP. He opens his mouth, and when he finally speaks, his voice is the rumble of distant thunder, laced with the chilling whisper of the wind. "Where am I? Who are you?" he begins. 
Thistle flies up to him, saying, “We rescued you from a boat you were chained into. The Kraken society had captured you.” 
Orsik adds, “We were sent by your daughter to rescue you.”
“Yes, the one not trying to betray you,” adds Thistle helpfully. 
“And not trying to steal the throne,” finishes Orsik. 
“My daughters?” he roars. “I must go to the throne room at once.” 
Orsik sits down, “I suppose we should follow.” 
“You need healing, buddy,” mutters Vardis. 
And he is healed. And Orsik is feeling much better and runs after the King. 
As the dwarf enters the throne room, he sees Queen Serissa delighted at seeing her father again. And he tries to look as dashing as possible; after all, she will someday be the Queen. The Queen sees him and smiles. 
Meanwhile, the Imperator shouts, “So wonderful to see you alive!” 
“Daddy, we’re so happy to see you!” shout Nym and Mirran. 
Unla Kee, who has caught u,p mutters, “No, you aren’t.” 
After a few more moments of joyous family reunion, King Hekaton turns to the 3HPP and says, “This is a moment of great joy, but we have problems.” 
Thistle nods, “Yes, we have to rescue my girlfriend.” 
King Hekaton smiles, and his stern face softens, “The affairs of the heart are paramount, but the realms are in danger.”

“Yes, from the same blue dragon, Iymrith, who tried to steal your kingdom.” 
“The doom of the desert?” 
“Precisely!” 
King Hekaton turns to his daughter, “Serissa, explain.” 
And she does. And as part of this tale, Mirran and Nym’s failures are laid bare. 
The King is startled and angry, but he is careful. He turns to the Imperator and asks, “I presume you were the one who left no stone unturned to find me?” 
The Imperator attempts to deceive, but the 3HPP clarifies that Serissa remained hopeful while the Imperator offered little support. 
The King marches up to the Wyrmskull Throne and sits. He turns to Serissa and says, “You are now the Imperator.” He then turns to the old Imperator and says, “Brother, Uthor, you are dismissed.” 

Uthor stares at his brother, and the storm rages in his body, and he smiles, “Yes, my brother, it is time I took care of other business.” He hands his insignia of office to Serissa and marches out of the throne room. 
There is a stunned silence. 
“As for you two, Nym and Mirran, you betrayed me, you betrayed Serissa, who was your Queen, and you allied yourselves with a,” the intensity of his rage and anger and fury explodes from his voice, “dragon.” He pauses, “Go to your chambers. I will decide your fates later.” 
Mirran and Nym try to protest, but the new Imperator orders the guards to take them to their chambers. The new Imperator follows the guards, enjoying this moment too much.
The King puts on his obsidian crown and turns to the 3HPP, “My friends, you have done much for me. And I wish to do more for you. But my kingdom is in ruins. The All-Father had visions of grandeur that were foolish. And he broke the Ordning, and now, thanks to you, I can put it back as it was. And yet, it galls me that Iymrith will live while I bring Chief Guh of the Hill Giants, Thane Kayalithca of the Stone Giants, Jarl Storvald of the Frost Giants, Countess Sansuri of the Cloud Giants, and Duke Zalto of the Fire Giants to kneel before me. If you were to kill them all or convince them of their ways' errors, I could join you in one final battle against the Doom of the Desert.”
Orsik turns to his friends and says, “Thanks for the offer, but we’re good. We can do this on our own.” 
The rest of the 3HPP are not as sure. Thistle knows that the love of his life is somewhere near Duke Zalto’s kingdom.
Vardis knows his friend may not care about the map to the treasure, but he does, and the map is near Zalto’s abode. 
And Thistle knows that Escarlotta will not be happy to discover that Countess Sansuri will not be defeated. 
As for Unla Kee, he’s already running past the King to the teleportation chamber to get back to his cloud castle and kill an ancient blue dragon. 

# Chapter 84 - Choices are made.

King Hekaton rushes after Unla Kee, who enters the teleportation chamber behind the throne room. 

The halfling wizard can’t wait to return to his cloud castle and head off to kill the ancient blue dragon. 

“There is treasure to be had and excitement to be found,” he thinks. 

The rest of the 3HPP arrive a few moments later with Imperator Serissa. 

She looks at them, “I can’t convince you to take care of the problem that affects both our people?”

Orsik looks at her, realizing he could do the heroic thing. He thinks that he could save the Giant King, score the Giant Princess, and perhaps someday become the Giant King himself, but then, would that make him a hero?  

“No.” 

His personal honor does not permit it. He must fight for something that others may think is meaningless. 

He must kill Iymrith. He must either kill her or die in the attempt because heroes kill dragons and save girlfriends. That is heroic. 

The Imperator sighs and then attempts to teleport the heroes back to their cloud castle. 

Nothing happens. 

She looks at her King. 

He thinks. 

“Unfortunately, something is blocking the teleport spell,” he mutters. “I fear someone has cast forbiddance.” 

Unla Kee is unconvinced and demands to take control of the teleportation chamber. And for a moment, he ponders whether he should teleport into Waterdeep's treasury chambers rather than get his castle. 

He decides against it. 

After a moment, he says, “Yup, most of the castle is blocked. Someone or something has made it impossible for us to get to the castle except for some parts of it.” 
Thistle looks at Unla Kee - “That's impossible. They would need a very powerful cleric and to have the cleric on hand. We haven’t been that far away for that long.” 

Hekaton looks at Thistle and says, “Thistle, the Cult of the Kraken has access to mysterious powers and magic. Did they know about your castle?” 

Unla Kee thinks for a moment about how they brought their castle to Yartar and attacked a high-ranking member of the Kraken Society, who escaped. Every member of that society probably knew about the castle. 

“But how could they have sent a message from so far away?” he wonders. And then he remembers how Lord Drylund died as if something had triggered that. And he thinks that maybe the members of the Kraken are interlinked in some kind of hive mind. He then thinks this is information the King doesn’t need to know because the decision to show the castle to the world wasn’t his idea. And the King might think it’s his fault for not stopping Orsik, whose idea it was. 

He turns to the King, “No.” 

“Strange indeed how they knew to bring such a powerful spell caster.”

Serissas declares, “You were kept on a ship we could not find, despite the best of divination magic I could pay for. The Kraken Society knows how to hide things.” 

Hekaton nods his head.

“We will rest and head out in the morning,” declares Orsik. 

And indeed, they do. 

King Hekaton teleports them just outside the great doors of Ironslag. 

Orsik isn’t tempted to find his lost treasure because that’s the obvious thing to do. Nor is he interested in saving the King’s kingdom because that’s the thing anyone would do. 

From Ironslag, the 3HPP heads through the Silver Marches and directly to Fork Road. Their final destination is the town of Ascore at the end of Fork Road. During the Netherese Empire, Ascore was a port city of the dwarven nation of Delzoun on the northern shores of the Narrow Sea. However, by the 14th-century DR, it was a lonely, windswept ruin on the edge of the Anauroch, near the Frozen Sea, reputed to be haunted by ancient evil.

As Thistle explained, the desert Anauroch is the side effect of the mad king Krasus’ actions that brought down the Kingdom of Netheril. 

Vardis thinks it’s interesting that the 3HPP’s story began near Anauroch in Parnast and will now end one way or another in Anauroch. 

From Ascore, Iymrith’s lair is close by. 

Along the way, they encounter merchants from Citadel Adbar who tell them of rumors that the Uthgardtian Barbarians are raiding and that the Giants are on the prowl. 

The 3HPP expresses confusion and doubt about the veracity of such rumors. 

Thistle is horrified when the merchants say they are going to Silverymoon - “That’s a very bad place. They have cults to Asmodeus! They had a horde of Zombies that we saved them from! There is a demon running, or was running a play! Don’t go there.” 

The caravan driver looks at Thistle and then turns to Orsik, “He’s not a goodly race, eh?” 

The guard next to the driver looks at Orsik, sees the young, brash, arrogant, obnoxious dwarf, and asks, “Are you Orsic?” 

Orsik looks at him, “No it was my son.” \\

A few days later, the 3HPP are preparing to sleep the night when the SEER appears with her gold pseudo dragon, Hsing. 

She wants to make a deal.

Orsik growls at her, “How did you find us?” 

She explained that Hekaton was a friend of the Lord’s Alliance and willing to share the information she needed. When Orsik observed that it was impossible for her to just show up in the exact place she needed to, she sighs, “It’s called Divination.” 

The dwarf sits down, “What do you want?” 

“I have a deal to make. It seems that Hekaton trusts you. He sees you as - heroic. And he said he would be willing to consider a permanent alliance between the King of the Giants and the Lord’s Alliance if you dealt with his problem.” 

“Not interested,” mutters the dwarf. 

“I could make all of your problems go away? Instead of being known as the annoying prat, I could make you a hero!” 

He laughs, “A mass hypnosis?” 

“No, I am the SEER. I will claim that this was all part of an elaborate ruse I set up to get you infiltrated and close to King Hekaton. You will have statues and monuments all across Faerun.”

He stares at her, “Think about it!” 

She smiles, confident he will agree. 

“But I have other gifts for your group. Unla Kee, I could get your cloud castle back. And I could get it back without Escarlotta.” 

Before he can answer, she turns to Vardis, “And you, Cleric of Lorellon, I heard how much you worry about losing your friend. With my help, he’ll become a hero and live. All he has to do is say yes and get rid of these giant lords.”

She pauses, “I could also solve your Lorellon problem and get you into the temples again. I might even be able to engineer an apology.” 

Vardis is about to answer and turns to Thistle, “And for you, Thistle, we have arrows of dragon slaying and a powerful magical bow. If you only turn from Iymrith and deal with the Giants, these magical artifacts could be yours. Once you have dealt with the Giants, go deal with Iymrith. I don’t care.” 

She waits for them to say yes, but they don’t.

The SEER is confused. Her information was impeccable. Her agents had told her their weak points, and she had exploited all of them, but they weren’t saying yes. 

She is flustered, but she has been in more challenging negotiations.

Unla Kee mutters, “See, the castle is mine already. So you aren’t giving me anything I don’t already have.” 

The SEER wants to scream, “WHAT?” but catches herself. She knew this was possible: “I could guarantee a tax-free license for your casino and a permit for all cities that belonged to the Lord’s Alliance.” 

Unla Kee shrugs, “No.” 

Thistle is pained but agrees, “No.” 

Vardis looks at Orsik and says, “No.” 

The SEER is furious, “You don’t understand what is at stake here. We have an opportunity for the first time in 40,000 years to have peace between the Giants and the rest of the kingdoms of the North. Hekaton will agree to peace. But he will only do it if you four handle his problems. For some reason, he thinks this is a sign from the All-father.”

She then stops and composes herself, but the venom drips from her words, “I tried to offer him anyone else, but he said - no. He insisted that it had to be you. Why he thinks you four are so important is beyond me. But I need you to stop with your bizarre quest to go get yourselves killed and create a better world.” 

Orsik looks at her, “See, killing a dragon is heroic.” 

“No, no, it’s not. There are so many dragons in the realms. Each of them pretends that they are some kind of all-powerful force. They aren’t. They are self-absorbed, narcissistic, arrogant agents of chaos. Anyone can kill a dragon. Not anyone can save the realms.”

Orsik looks at her and says, “No, killing the dragon and saving his girl is heroic.” 

She stares at him in disbelief, “If you do this, I will guarantee that your name is cursed for a thousand generations.” 

Orsik screams, “Bad mouth me across the realms? No, I don’t think so. I am going to be a hero. Killing a dragon is heroic. Saving my friend’s girl is heroic. Having you create a fake story about my life while I do the bidding of your organization doesn’t make me a hero. It makes me a fake.” 

He attempts to strike her, but she is in a sanctuary, and he finds he can’t. 

Vardis casts a fireball, but she counters it. 

Unla Kee has heard enough, and he envisions a prison, crafting its floor, sides, and ceiling from the hardest thing he can think of. A diamond box, a kind of jeweled jail, takes form before his eyes, though only he can see it. With the last gesture, he pinches ruby dust in his pocket and turns it like you would a key. 

She is trapped in a forcecage. 

She smiles. 

And a few moments later, she escapes. She does leave a note behind. It says,  “Orsic, I won’t bad mouth you. I disagree with your choices. But if you defeat Iymrith, at least consider the possibility that you could do the realms a favor.” 

He wonders what happened and realizes she must have cast a time stop and teleported herself out after she wrote the note. 

There is a moment of silence. 

Thistle looks around, “Does anyone want a goodberry for dinner?” 

A few days later, the 3HPP is finally in Ascore. According to Uncle Joon - A millennia-old road ran from the west and into a great door set in a hill, flanked by two massive stone statues of crouching griffons with grim aspects. The door opened into a black yawning passage that descended into the earth. Beyond, the road exited the hill and came to the ruins about the base of the cliff above Ascore.

Two famous monuments are located within the city: the stone ships and the red pyramids. The stone ships were mighty vessels, designed for trade and warfare, and nigh indestructible, and they plied the Narrow Sea. Up to twenty stone ships could be docked in Ascore any time, with thirty more out at sea. In the ruined city, these were still visible, half-buried in the sand, with the stone docks standing proudly against the encroaching sea of sand and the empty hulks of weird stone ships emerging from the dunes to the east. 

As for the red pyramids, they stood in a circle in the very center of Ascore, carved out of a strange red stone. Uncle Joon told Thistle to avoid them. 

So the group navigates around the eroded city and its strange monuments until they come to the toppled stones of an ancient ruin that stood resilient against the arid wind and oppressive sun, the stone bleached bone-white after eons. Little remains of the original structure, though they imagine a reconstruction of the soaring pillars and central monolithic structure. 

And as they walk through it, the ground trembles. Two blue dragons emerge from the sand, one in front and one behind them. 

Orsik prepares to fight. Thistle notices something strange: the dragon’s bottom half resembles a purple worm. 

# Chapter 85 - A deal is made

The blue dragon—purple worm hybrid roars, and Orsik feels his blood boil. He is alive, and soon, he will be a hero, dying fighting these abominations. Using all of his skill and sophistication, he savagely hurts the creature. It bleeds. It howls. 

He smiles. 

The creature strikes him with his tail. 

“Wait, that’s unfair. I am not near his tail,” he screams. 

It turns out that the hybrid's tail was far more flexible than Orsik had guessed. And it is much longer. It swings out from under the creature. 

The sting misses. 

Thuul rushes to the dragon coming from the south and attacks. Thistle spreads his arrows between the two dragons. For a moment, he thinks, “Those arrows of dragon slaying would have been useful.” 

Then, lightning strikes crossed from opposite sides of the courtyard, striking Unla Kee, Thistle, and Vardis. 

Unla Kee tries to use a counterspell, but Orsik shouts, “Lair actions. They are not spells.” 

And then the dragon Orsik was fighting strikes again, and this time doesn’t miss. Orsik uses the gift Thornacious gave him so long ago and avoids being poisoned. 

Vardis summons a djinni and tells him to attack the Dragon that Orsik is fighting. 

The hybrid howls and strikes Orsik again with his purple worm stinger. Orsik is bloodied and injured and mutters, “Today is good to die!” 

Meanwhile, the other hybrid kills Thuul with his stinger. 

The Djinni attacks the Hybrid Dragon, threatening Orsik with his blade and causing thunderous damage to the dragon, but it lives. 

The first dragon prepares to breathe, but it uses its frightening aura before that. Only Orsik is affected. “I can’t be afraid. I am Orsik.” And, indeed, he is not afraid of the dragon; the magic works on his mind, and the fear his mind has is that somehow he won’t die. That he won’t die and he won’t be the hero. 

The dragon opens its jaw and a bright light forms from it. The air crackles with electric charge and a blast of lightning stretches out from his mouth to a point 90 feet away. Orsik feels the charge hit him and thinks, “This is it. I die now, and I am a hero.” For a moment, he hears a voice telling him to try and live, and he thinks, “No, this is it. I am going to be a hero!” 

Unla Kee is also hit by the lightning bolt and is struck dead. 

Vardis is practically at death’s door from the other dragon. The cleric desperately tries to heal his friend but is struck dead before he has a chance. 

Thistle flies out and fires two arrows. The 3HPP is down to him, and he’s not powerful enough to kill two adult dragons. 

“Maybe I can call the SEER now?” 

This is it. He came this close to saving his love, but his unwillingness to be patient, gather allies, and rush in means he will fail. 

The bitter taste of failure stings. 

For a moment, he’s tempted to blame the damned circlet of intelligence. Perhaps that thing was a curse?  

The dragons turn to him, and he prepares to fight. If this is how it ends, he won’t go down without some kind of fight. 

But before anyone can take action, a portal appears, and a gargantuan blue dragon steps through it. 

Thistle recognizes this creature from the Maelstrom. It’s Iymrith. 

He realizes that it’s over. He closes his eyes. Aims his arrows at her in one final valiant and pointless gesture of rebellion. 

And she starts clapping. 

He notices cultists who jump off her back and rush to heal Orsik, Unla Kee, and Vardis. 

He relaxes his bow. 

Orsik realizes he is alive. Never has a dwarf been so angry to be alive. “I WAS GOING TO BE A HERO! I WAS GOING TO DIE AND BE A HERO, AND YOU SAVED ME! I WAS THREE SECONDS AWAY FROM BEING A HERO!!!!!” 

The cultist who healed him walks away as quickly as he can. 

Iymrith stands on her two hind legs and paces around the area, her giant frame wrecking the remaining walls. Her body causes the sand to shake and redistribute, uncovering old floors and covering others. 

Her size is immense. The two hybrids are nothing compared to her. Iymrith is not just a dragon. She is a legendary force. 

Orsik mutters, “I had such a clever plan. Such a clever plan.” 
But Iymrith is ignoring him. She is talking to Thistle, “My dear boy, Thistle, it’s so good to see you.” Thistle glares at her, and she ignores him. “For centuries, I have been working on a new hybrid dragon. These creatures that you fought are deadly, but they are mindless. I have to keep them in the desert to keep out unwanted guests. I wanted something better. I wanted something that had the innocence of a fairy and the destructive power of draconic kind.” 

“I want her back.” 

The dragon smiles. “Of course you do, and I could not think of anything more amazing than you having her back. And you will.” 

She stretches her neck, and her massive head approaches Orsik - “My dear dwarf, you are everything I was promised you would be.” 

“By the SEER?” asks a dubious Thistle.

“I don’t work with her. She’s the real power of the Lord’s Alliance. She’s committed to a long-term plan to end the freedom of creatures like me to do as we please. She wants to impose an order. Orsik is right to distrust her. She is evil.” 

Orsik looks at her and says, “I WAS THREE SECONDS AWAY FROM DYING AND BEING A HERO.” 

But she ignores him in a soft voice, or at least what passes as a soft voice for an ancient power such as herself, “Every so often, I call a meeting of the five clans. The heads of the various chromatic dragons and I show them my latest creations. It would be awesome if you four fought Laela at this festival.” 

There is a gasp.

“No, I don’t want you to kill her. I want you to prove she is a Fey Dragon hybrid and as lethal as I claim she is.” 

“And I can leave afterward with her?” 

Iymrith seems taken aback and says, “I am a scientist. Of course, you can leave. I am not a slave keeper. I am an artist. She’s a creation of mine. I just need a demonstration to show my critics and patrons that their support in my experiments and investigations was worthwhile.” 

She pauses and, with almost casual indifference, says, “Or I could kill you all right now.” 

Thistle thinks and shouts, “This meets my goals.” 

When Orsik hears that a dragon might kill him, he thinks, “I CAN STILL BE A HERO!” He decides he must stop the fairy. 

“Your girlfriend is some kind of freak fairy-dragon hybrid, and you still want to be with her?” 

Thistle will not hear this, “My goal is to rescue her from Iymrith.” 

“But it’s not her; she’s like some dragon hybrid,” he says, pointing to the monstrous hybrids. 

Thistle begins to waver - “I have to see her.” 

Iymrith sighs, “The whole point of the experiment was to create a creature that could exist simultaneously in the feywild and the prime material plane. The creature could swap its physical form by forming an interplanar link and swapping out its body. Laela is still very much a fairy. She has a squeaky voice and always complains about her wingtips needing wingmuffs. The dragon would be the ultimate infiltration force. The shapeshift would not be magical. It would be intrinsic to their nature. Furthermore, as a fairy, no magical detection would work.” 

Orsik can feel his chance slipping, “She’s an abomination.” 

Thistle is upset, “Why are you calling her an abomination? We haven’t seen her. Like, how do you know she’s an abomination.” 

“She’s been transformed into an evil dragon.” 

Vardis, sitting in the sand, looks at the two arguing and shouts to Orsik, “It’s been a long dry spell. He’ll take anything.” 

“It’s been three years,” pleads Orisk. He wants Iymrith to kill him. He wants to be a HERO. 

Iymrith can see Thistle wavering - “I just need your help to show how effective she is as a dragon.” 

Orsik says, “No. I am not helping you. This is not about being a hero. Helping a dragon is not heroic.” 

Thistle pauses, “I am a good person, and although this meets my goals, it is questionable.” 

The dragon stares, “I believe there has been a misunderstanding about my goals. I infiltrated the dragons to get investors.”

“You started a draconic civil war that has caused the realms to go into chaos.” 

“I needed investors, and when you need investors, you do what you must do.” 
Thistle chimes in, “We had investors. Some of them weren’t good people. I can relate.” 

Orsik thinks momentarily and asks, “Who are the investors?” 

“Important people.” 

She realizes that’s not enough, “A variety of important people.” 

Orisk stares at her and shrugs, “Look, we are going to do this demonstration in front of them, so it’s not like we won’t see them.” 

Iymrith exhales, “Of course, of course. Well, there are the various giant lords whom I have been playing off. They thought I was a giant, but now - thanks to you - know I am a dragon.” She pauses and whines, “It’s why I need this. Things will get ugly if they don’t see results, and you and your girlfriend will not live.” 

Orsik can sense she wants to say more, “And a delegation of Red Wizards.” 

“That can’t go bad.” 

“And a representative of the Unseelie court.” Iymrith looks at Thistle, hoping he won’t hear. But when she speaks, the mountains tremble.

“Now you have gone too far,” shouts Thistle, who cocks his bow. 

“And the Seelie court.” 

Thistle stares, “Did you say the Seelie court?” 

Iymrith looks genuinely surprised, “You didn’t know that? You thought I was experimenting with her subjects in Queen Titania’s realm, and she didn’t know or approve?” She laughs. A tear falls from her face and splashes on the ground. “There’s a reason you were kicked out of the Seelie court.”

Thistle puts his arrow into his quiver and mutters, “It checks out.” 

Iymrith is relieved. “Look, I need this. And if you do this, the experiment will be declared a success, and we will all get to go home. The contracts will be satisfied, and I will get to leave.” 

Orsik says, “It seems to me that you being in breach of contract is far better for us.” 

The dragon pauses, puts a claw to its mouth, and then sits down. “I will still escape. You’ll be dead. Dragons will be fighting. Laela will be imprisoned and used in some horrible way. She will probably use it to make more hybrids. Or if you do this, you rescue the girl, make Thistle happy, stick it to the Seelie and Unseelie courts.” 

“Not heroic,” says Orsik. 

“But everyone gets what they want?” 

“You misread me.” 

“You’re right. You want to be dead.” 

“I would have been a hero! All you had to do was delay arrival for three seconds.” 

Iymrith is frustrated, “Can someone please tell him to be quiet?” 

The cleric who healed Orsik is worried that Iymrith may not be happy with their performance on the battlefield. 

Orsik shouts to Thistle, “Let’s talk.” 

Iymrith smiles and walks away. 

“Let’s say yes and then use the time to rescue your girlfriend.” 

“Works for me.” 

Thistle shouts, “We agree, but I must see proof of life.” 

Iymrith pulls out a sending stone and hands it to him. It’s a tiny thing in her gargantuan claws. 

And then he hears Laela’s voice - “That’s you?” You’re alive?” 

“I’ve come to get you!” 

“You’ll get yourself killed. And where the hell have you been? I’ve been looking all over for you.” 

“I’ve been looking for you!” 

Vardis notes, “If she’s been looking for you, Thistle, that’s not consistent with Iymrith’s version of the facts.” 

Iymrith notes, “One last question.” 

“Where are you?”

“In a cage in Iymrith’s lair.” 

Vardis, annoyed, blurts out, “That you have been looking all over.” 

Laela is outraged, “Who’s that annoying person? Why is there an elf? Are you working with elves now? I thought we said we don’t like elves!” 

“I needed helpers,” whimpers Thistle. 

The voice is cut off. 

Iymrith picks up the stone and says, “Fine, she’s not working for me. I found her. She torched a caravan as a dragon. She was in her dragon form. I captured her. But now she refuses to switch into her fey form. She practices this powerful form of fey meditation, fey-fullness.” 

Thistle smiles, “She has to be one with nature.” 

Orsik laughs, “I get it. The secret is that she’s always angry!” 

Iymrith and Thistle glare at him. The dragon looks glum, “I admit it. I can’t get her to turn into a dragon. Everybody believes I have lost the mojo. They think I am not the great Iymrith. I -need- to show them that I still got it.” 

Orsik looks at her, “For an all-powerful dragon, you sure have a self-confidence issue.” 

Vardis asks, “Iymrith is trying to get her groove back. Can’t you just breathe lightning on everyone?” 

The dwarf adds, “Like I am more confident than you.” 

Iymrith suddenly takes on the posture of a terrifying ancient blue dragon and looks at him, “I am convinced of my powers as a dragon. I’m good in my skin. I just want to be known as something more than just another powerful dragon.” She pauses and cocks her head up, “I want to be known as an artiste, a creator. I need this. It will validate my work and my credentials. I won’t just be the Dragon that got lucky with drakes. ”

Orsik turns to Vardis, “Lots of parallels with Sandesyl’s experiments.” 

The group confers momentarily, and Orsik says, “Okay, so where does this happen?” 

“At the coliseum.” 

“The what?” 

She draws a map on the ground, a map of stunning detail and precision. 



“As I said, Laela emerges from the hole in the ground. You guys go for a few rounds, and then you go.” 

“Does she have to appear as a dragon from the hole?” 

“No, she just needs to turn into a dragon.” 

Unla Kee, who has been drinking, shouts, “This seems like an incredibly bad idea.” He pauses and then shouts, “I’m in.” 

Orsik thinks, “We’ll have to talk to her before the event, probably for a few hours. And figure out why she’s not turning into a dragon. We don’t want her to get nervous.” 

Vardis coughs, “And have some performance anxiety.” 

Orisk continues, “You don’t want it to go badly. Can we make it go well by having a short conversation?” 
Iymrith is exhausted and frustrated. She plops on her belly, puts her claws over her head, and then stands up on her hind legs. She stretches her neck to its full extent, roars, and then lets loose a bolt of lightning that overwhelms the sun. 

She starts screaming, “I SHOULD NEVER HAVE DONE THIS EXPERIMENT!!!!” She then pleads to Thistle, “How was I supposed to know that combining Dragons and Fairies would create this much drama?” 

“Perhaps a play?” asks Thistle. 

Orsik realizes his plan: “Great, then we are agreed. We’ll come a few days early, have a script, and work with Laela. We will have some dialogue. We want a top performance.” 

Iymrith is confused. 

Thistle says, “And we need two skin-tight unitards.” 

Orsik says, “You, of course, see the need, Iymrith?” 

The dragon looks confused. 

“Of course you do. I was being presumptuous. You understand what we are doing and have thought of it before. We’re just repeating the details for our benefit.”

The dragon agrees, “Of course I had, but keep repeating the details so I can make sure you missed nothing.” 

Orsik turns to Thistle - “How many rehearsals? A few hours might not be good enough.” 

Vardis notes, “The last time we rehearsed that little, we got Bob.” 

“Right, we need more time. We should come days in advance.” 

The dragon tries to object, but Orsik continues, “We’ll need time to focus on our craft. We can’t be distracted. There might be other things in your lair, and we need focus.” 

Thistle adds, “We believe in method acting. You have to be in the location. And if you are not, you can’t sense the experience.” 

Iymrith, desperate and thinking this idea of a play might just work, says excitedly, “YES!” 

Orsik whispers to Thistle, “Anything else?” 

Thistle turns to Orsik, “I don’t have the headband of intellect.” 

Thistle shouts, “We’re good.”
