The cleric who healed Orisk is relieved. There was a possibility that Orsik’s death would be a mercy killing to what the cleric would have had to suffer. 

“Well, it’s a three-day hike to my lair.” 

Orsik asks, “Can’t you fly us? Or teleport us?

Iymrith looks at him contemptuously, “You can’t teleport because the coliseum has been magically protected from Scrying and Teleport. And as for flying on me, only those who worship my greatness can fly on me. But these abominations can carry you.” 

“You have many worshippers?” 

“Enough. I have people who see me as the pinnacle of my type. They recognize my creative, artistic, and scientific brilliance.” 

“Think how many more you will have after the performance!” 

Thistle stares at her briefly to see what he can learn. And what he realizes is that Iymrith is indeed different from other dragons. She has tools she appears to be using. And equipment she seems to be wearing. But even for all her power, and the wonder of her tools, she is in a jam. Even a creature as powerful as her can make one too many deals. 

Thistle mutters, “Why not just eat the Red Wizards?”

“Because they are the minions! I made a deal with Szaas Tham.” She knew it was a mistake and pleaded, “I needed investors.” 

Orsik can’t keep his mouth shut and asks, “Don’t you have a hoard? Why did you self-fund this initiative?” 

Iymrith suddenly looks angry, horrified, and confused and roars, “SPEND. MY. HOARD!” 

# Chapter 86 - A deal with bob

With the deal with Iymrith concluded, the 3HPP stand alone in the empty desert. Orsik pulls out the conch, and they are again in the Maelstrom. 

Standing in the throne room, Thistle notes who is there: Imperator Serissa, King Hekaton, and the hill giant guards Tug and Cog. 

The King looks at the nearly dead heroes who left just a few days ago and refused his request for help. He thinks these heroes have been coming and going too freely into his castle, and perhaps they were reminded of their place. 

Before he can begin, Thistle flies up and shouts, “The lair of the dragon and they…”

Hekaton has had enough. “You have returned.” 

“Yes, and we found the lair,” shouts Thistle, but Hekaton’s voice booms, and no one hears the fairy's words. 

“I am the King. And I get to speak first. I am pleased to see you here.” He looks them over, “and in one piece.” 

Orsik mutters, barely audibly, “More pleased than me.” 

He stares at Orsik and continues, “I know you crave the heroic death at the hand of a dragon, but I am glad to see you alive!” 

Thistle is getting frustrated. He has important news, and instead of sharing it, Hekaton is dawdling. He wants to say something when he notices out of the corner of his eye that Serissa is annoyed. 

Vardis, who notices the same thing, turns to Orsik, “She’s pissed.” 

Orsik and Vardis stare at the Giantess, and Orsik mutters, “It’s like she’s happy to have him hear but disappointed, like some kind of daddy issues?” Vardis asks Orsik, “When did you become so aware of relationships?” Orsik taps the band on his head. Vardis nods. 

The king, however, has not noticed his daughter’s discomfort. 

“Thistle, why have you come here?” 

The fairy speaks as fast and loudly as he can, “Traitors among giants! Giants are working with dragons! WE KNOW WHERE AND WHEN THEY WILL BE!” 

Hekaton sighs, “I have been told of such horrors, terrible news. But my hands are tied.” 

Orsik has had enough, “You’re the king. Untie them!” 

Upon hearing that, Imperator Serissa stands up and unloads all her pent-up frustrations from last year. She is angry at the King, who declared that his two sisters weren’t qualified to be Queen, thus encouraging them to find other ways to become Queen. She’s furious at Hekaton for disappearing on a rage-filled quest instead of staying at his post as King when the Ordning was shattered. She’s angry at everyone who didn’t listen to her that her father was alive. And most of all, she’s furious that her father is not the Giant she remembers. 

“Thistle was willing to set aside his quest to find the love of his life to save you. She put the needs of the world over his beloved! You abandoned me to get revenge! You abandoned your post and left me in a nest of vipers!” 

The 3HPP stares at Serissa in shock. And then, they all reach for a snack, sit back, and watch what happens next. 

Hekaton is furious. He wasn’t expecting this. “I am King, and I make the decisions!” 

And then she says, with all the venom she can muster, “I was the king when you disappeared, and I could have left you to rot.” 

Thistle coughs. 

The two giants glare at him. Thistle was coughing because the goodberry got stuck in his throat. Not because he wanted to interrupt the two giants. 

“My girlfriend,” he gasps. 

Serissa then continues to scream at her father, “EXACTLY! HIS GIRLFRIEND WAS LESS IMPORTANT THAN SAVING THE WORLD!” 

Thistle coughs again, “I think we can find a win-win here.” 

Hekaton stares at Serissa and Thistle. At that moment, Thistle realizes that Hekaton is not some giant but almost a quasi-deity. His size, power, and strength are beyond measure. And that storm giant is angry. And Thistle realizes Hekaton is not angry at Thistle or Serissa but at his bad choices. 

Somehow, the Giant sees this fairy, whom he could squish without effort, trying to convince him to do the right thing. He stops and says, “What win-win?” 

“Your traitors are gathering in one location. It’s a perfect opportunity.” 

“Why didn’t you begin that way?” shouts the giant. 
Orsik says laconically, “He tried, but you interrupted him. And then you got into an argument with your daughter.” 

With that, the daughter and father start screaming at each other again.

Hekaton sits on his throne and huffs when they are both spent and exhausted. 

Thistle turns to Serissa, “There’s something for you, too; Iymrith will be there.” 

At that, Serissa shouts, “I will take the palace guard and defend our honor, even if our king will sit her like a coward.”

And that starts another round of screaming. 

At this point, Orsik tries to soothe their hurt feelings. Hekaton feels guilty for his quest to get vengeance for his wife, which leads to so much chaos in the world. Serissa feels betrayed by her father. He’s unsure of what to do, mumbles about the future fates, and needs to do the right thing. 

Hekaton hears Orsik, and yet nothing seems to affect him. His rage, his guilt, and his pain are too much. 

As things spiral out of control, the King, trying to find a way out without losing face, remembers that Unla Kee is a diviner. 

“Are you a diviner?” 

The mage tries to stand up and then decides that the better approach is to lie down, acting as if talking to a King isn’t a big deal because it isn’t. Unla Kee nods. 

“I am asking you to divine what the better plan is. To strike at Iymrith’s lair when all of my enemies are present, or my plan to hire agents of chaos and kill them one by one?” 
Orsik is shocked, “You can figure out the right path?” 

The king looks at Orsik, confused, “He’s a diviner. That’s what they do.” 

“But you never did that for us!” 

“One time I steered you wrong? Name it!” 

“You steered us wrong every time.” 

“No, I steered you to the riskiest path. And that is always the right thing.” 
Unla Kee stares at the King. The safe play is to do what the king wants. The risky option is to take all the troops from the castle and attack the king’s enemies in Iymrith’s lair. He doesn’t hesitate, “Take everyone and attack Iymrith.” 

Hekaton agrees - “Then we attack now?”

Orsik smiles, “We have some more preparations. In 5 days, we will meet at Iymrith’s lair. For now, we have some business to attend to.” 

With that, they use his teleportation chamber and appear in Ascore. 

The 3HPP plans to summon Bob and pay him to fight against Iymrith. As a bonus, they hope to secure the release of the 200 citizens of Silverymoon currently trapped in a pocket dimension. 

With that, Vardis casts Planar Ally, the light shimmers, a portal opens, and Bob appears. 

His demonic, raspy voice is softer, his arrogance a little bit gone, and it seems he is afraid. 

“Do you fools know what you have done?” 

Unla Kee mutters, “We brought you here?” 

“Yes, thank evil, you did. Do you have any idea what you have done to your planet?” 

“Saved the world from a Giant Civil War?” 

“Oh, yes, that. I was referring to the artificer’s decision to use ISDs. This violated the 1489 Treaty on Export Regulation of Interplanar Storage Devices.” 

Unla Kee asks, “What is an ISD?” 

“As defined in the treaty, an ISD is all non-transient portable magical devices that establish connections across planar boundaries are classified as Interplanar Storage Devices (ISDs). Bags of holding and portable holes are classified as ISDs. Non-portable, and typically permanent, portals (such as the permanent portals established by the IPCC) are not ISDs.” 

There are more confused looks. 

“The Treaty was signed in Sigil between the representatives of the Trading House, Chromax, and the Githyanki, Githizeri, and Mindflayers to regulate the use ofISDss as weapons.”

More confusion.

“An ISD, when placed in another ISD, will instantly teleport any entity nearby into the Astral Plane. It has been observed that the typical entity is a massively powerful entity, MPE, that the three empires would want to secure for their armies.” 

“So you’re a massively powerful entity?” 

“Yes. But that’s beside the point. Planets are sources of MPEs that can disrupt the balance of power. When a planet is found in violation of the treaty, any empire can lay claim to that planet and any MPEs found nearby.”

“So?” 

“The three empires have just discovered that Toril violates the treaty. They are planning to come here.” 

Unla Kee asks, “How many planets have you been to?” 

“Many. The planes of the abyss harvest the souls of countless worlds.” 

Unla Kee needles him, “You are a bit worried for a massively powerful entity.” 

“I’m not worried. I am like - are you that stupid? And I am annoyed I got snookered by the oldest trick in the book. And do you know why? Because I didn’t check to see if Toril was a signatory. And I didn’t check to see if this planet had secret societies and religious prescriptions against using ISDs. Your planet is doomed. And I am stuck on it, thanks to your stupid spell. And worse, I was trapped near your planet in the Astral Plane. I would be captured and turned into a vassal of one of the empires. Now I am going to have to serve the likes of you.” 

At this point, Unla Kee smiles. He smiles because he has always known about the infinity of planets. And because he knew about the end of the world. And nobody ever asked him if it was ‘this’ world. If this world ends, he knows he will survive it. 

“All of your demonic powers haven’t given you the power of divination to know what I know.” 

“Fine, it was boring. Being trapped in the Astral Plane for eons is boring.” 

Unla Kee smiles at him, “It was only an hour.” 

And for some reason, Bob believes him. “It was the longest, most boring hour of my existence.” 

Orsik sees an opening, “Didn’t you send a few hundred people to the Astral Plane?” 

Bob thinks, then says, “No, it was a pocket dimension.” 

At this point, Unla Kee looks at Bob and asks, “Didn’t we try to kill him?” 

Orsik uses a very patronizing tone to say, “ Unlucky, that was a misunderstanding. We had to end the zombie army, but didn't want to hurt Bob. So, you know, we had to come to a compromise where we sent him away for a while.  Make him think about what he had done. That's in the past. We're moving on.” 

Bob is furious and puffs, “I am a powerful monstrous demon.”

“Of jokes,” says the dwarf.

“That was a portfolio change, not a demotion.” He then looks wistfully at the 3HPP, “ The demonic powers, I thought it would be a more lighthearted way of torturing people. They thought it was more appropriate for the kind of people I was given to punish.   I would get souls that needed to be punished and powerful people who had jesters they killed or treated poorly. I  would, for all eternity, crack bad jokes.” 

“That’s not too bad,” says Orsik.

“Although if that didn’t work, I would engage in more traditional punishments, of flailing the skin, burning them in pots of fire, etc.” 

“That’s bad,” winces Orsik.  He then pauses and shifts the conversation, “There’s going to be a big battle coming up with a bunch of dragons and giants. And we need your help to slaughter them.” 

“Slaughter evil creatures?”

Vardis interjects, “You could enslave them?” 

Unla Kee pokes Bob again, “ Consider how weak the zombies you had before were? Don’t you want some strong zombies? Unless you're a weak demon. Do you need inferior zombies to feel good about yourself?  If you can't handle it. We could find another demon.” 

Vardis twists the knife further, “Another MPE from the astral plane. And maybe this time one that is a massively powerful entity.” 

“ARE YOU QUESTIONING BOB SAGET!!!!” 

“We all get afraid,” says Unla Kee.
“Happens to the best of us,” adds Vardis. 

Seeing how he is trapped, Bob says, “I need something of value.” 

At this point, the 3HPP realized they hadn’t planned what to offer. At first, they provided the cloud castle, but they didn’t have it. Then Orsik suggests they offer the Dracolich but remembers they don’t have that either.  

As they start throwing out outrageous proposals, Unla Kee says, “ Bob would enjoy having two zombie blue chromatic dragons hybridized with purple worms. They're unique. No other planet has them.” 

Bob suddenly is excited, “You have two of Iymrith’s creations?” 

“Yeah.”

“You have an invitation?” Bob says excitedly. He pauses, “Could you tell me exactly what you want to do? Where is that supposed to happen?”

“Her lair,” shouts Thistle.

“Oh, that’s boring. But if it was at her grand reveal where she shows her latest creations,” 

And Thistle interrupts, “Which is in front of her patrons.” 

Vardis adds, “Where can you give her the biggest public humiliation?” 

Bob stops, takes a deep breath, puts his hand over his mouth, and then squeals. He looks at the 3HPP and says, “You want me to perform at that event?”

“Yes?” 

“I get to perform my act in front of the most powerful, deadly creatures.” 

“Yes.”

“Do I get many speaking lines? How many lines do I get?”

Unla Kee shouts, “Depends on how you do in the audition.”

Vardis concurs, “We need to see if there is a role. Bring that energy.” 

“I AM OUTRAGED THAT AFTER ALL OF THE ACTING and MONEY, I HAVE MADE YOU with MY STELLAR ACTING?” 

Orsik turns to the others, “I’m with Bob.”

“Can I give a bad guy speech before my attack?”  

At that point, the 3HPP realizes they have a new plan. They will polymorph Bob into a dragon. 

Except, Unla Kee doesn’t have the polymorph spell.  

And that means they have to go back to Silverymoon. 

Bob, however, hasn’t noticed. “I can go up in front of everyone and anyone, a who’s who of bad guys, and unleash hell and chaos, and those fools will applaud me because they think I am some dragon that Iymrith created?” 

“That’s possible,’ says Vardis.

“And that would disgrace Iymrith forever? When they discover her fraud? THIS IS PERFECT!” 

Bob says, “I will do it!” He insists it’s not because it’s the opportunity to pull off a spectacular joke but to prove to everyone he’s not a joke of a demon. Ever since he got his new portfolio, the rest of the powers of the Abyss have thought he is a bit of a joke. 

At this point, Orsik asks again about the prisoners. 

“Yes, I can free them. But that will cost you.” 

“How much?”

“20,000 gold.”

“Done.”

Bob is flustered, “That was supposed to be a lot of money.”

“Yeah, your play was much more successful than you think.” 

The demon then stands up and roars,  “If I don’t get to put on this performance for the Eons, I will hunt you down and burn you.” He looks at Orsik and says, “You signed one of those DNRRRs?”

Orsik thinks this Demon has a plan but is unsure what it is. 

At that point, Bob asks about the dragons he was promised. 

“They’re right there,” says Unla Kee. 

“I have to get them?” 

“They are right there.” 

“They are currently living.” 

“Are you not strong enough?” 

“ARE YOU QUESTIONING MY POWER?” 

And with that, he summons several more demons. 

“They will be fighting for some time.” 

Orsik is delighted at Bob's new power. He asks, “Bob, will you summon other demons for the big battle?” 

“No, this is my show. I share my stage light with no one.”

“But if you were in danger, you would? Or crack more dad jokes.” 

He bellows, “I would impose my will on the enemy. I would use all the powers of the abyss to win.” 

“Okay, then.” 

“Now, how about we free those people?” 

And Bob is about to free them when Orsik asks, “Where will they appear?” 

“In Silverymoon at the door of the theater.”

Orsik sees an opportunity to be a hero. He turns to Bob, “How about we time this so they appear in Silverymoon tomorrow at 9 am?” 

Bob looks at him and smiles. 
The next day, the conch sends everyone to the Maelstorm, where the 3HPP uses the teleportation chamber to arrive outside Silverymoon. 

Entering the city of Silverymoon, the 3HPP listens to rumors about themselves and what happened. Things have become more desperate over the last few months. The disappearance of notables in the fire has caused chaos. Although the ruse of using the dead zombie bodies worked initially, it was quickly discovered that they weren’t the nobles. What happened and who is to blame has become a political hot potato. The current ruler, Lord Methrammar Aerasumé, son of Lady Alustriel Silverhand, is under attack because he was the patron of the play. 

As for the play, they learn that everyone’s interpretation of the play is different. It’s almost as if they have each seen a different play. That has further caused distrust in the ruling elite.  

Because of the fire's impact on Silverymoon's politics, the 3HPP is being heavily discussed. Folks are wondering who they are and where they came from. The recent arrival of the 3HPP in Yartar in a giant cloud castle in the middle of a giant civil war has caused Silverymoon to fracture into groups that blame Lord Aerasume for trusting the 3HPP and those that blame the 3HPP for everything. 

As one passerby says, “First, they create a play that results in the disappearance of the leaders of Silverymoon, and then they show up in a giant’s castle in the middle of a civil war?” Some see the 3HPP as heroes fighting against the giants, others as agents, and others as leaders of some convoluted plot. 

If that wasn’t enough, the economic situation has gotten worse. 

At a local market, they hear rumors about fire giants still causing mayhem, looking for pieces of adamantium with these massive divining rods. From travelers in the south, there are tales of Stone giants who have destroyed the settlements near Deliymbar Road, which heads into Anauroch. 

And from Sword Coast merchants, they hear tales of Frost Giants who have attacked ships on the Coastal Cities. One rumor says that they destroyed Bryn Shander, the largest of the towns in the Icewind Dales. 

Finally, the increase in raiding by the Uthgardtians, who seem to target trade caravans heading to Silverymoon particularly, has made the current leader of Silverymoon even more unpopular. The 3HPP hears that if the current leadership had not abandoned the alliance of the Silvery Marches, maybe the merchant caravans would not be so exposed. 

# Chapte 87 Rescuing Bob's Prisoners

Once inside the city of Silverymoon, Orsik had an idea. 

“We can’t just be heroes, we need allies.” 

Everyone looks at him. 

Thistle stares at him, “We don’t have time for this.”

“Yeah, and we need allies. We should blackmail the mayor to get some allies.” 

Unla Kee perks up, “No, we want money.” 

“We need allies.” 

Vardis sighs, “Bob’s going to free the prisoners at 9 am.” 

Thistle pauses, “Let’s bluff him.” 

And with that, they march up the steps to the Lord Methrammar Aerasumé palace. Showing their papers from Everlund, they are presented to the Chamberlain. The Chamberlain is at first reluctant to disrupt his lordship, but Vardis stares him down, and he lets them through. 

The 3HPP are led to a large room with a rotunda, stained glass windows, a large table, and a haggard-looking Lord eating breakfast. 

“You?” he shouts. “You have caused nothing but disasters!” 

Thistle smiles, “We come with solutions!” 

Vardis slides in, “We are here to do something other than cause disaster!”

His Lordship sits down, “yes?” 

“We have heard you have some missing folk!” 

“ I have 200 notables missing. They disappeared during the destruction of the Royal Theatre after a play by Bob Saget. On top of that, no one can seem to remember the play, so there is a suspicion of magical infernal powers. Do you know what that does to my reputation as protecting Silverymoon as a bastion of the goodly races and people?” 

Thistle then says slyly, “What if you were able to get them back?” 

“I have sent all of my wizards! Nobody has figured this out!” 

“We discovered who is behind it,” declares Thistle triumphantly. “And they have been riling up the giants.” 

“This is connected to the Giants,” gasps Lord Aerasumé. “Do you have any proof?” 

Thistle presents a feather and says, “We did find this relic in the lair of a dragon who has been behind all of the chaos in the North. And he has other prisoners in his lair as well.” 

“Which dragon?” 

“Iymrith.” 

The head of the Royal Guard stares at the feather and mutters, “This trinket is the secret to bringing back our people?” 

“There is no risk; just let us get to the Theater to perform the ritual.” 

At this, High Mage Taern Hornblade, hero of the War of the Dragons, coughs, “This is very suspicious.” He coughs, “Very. Having the Lord present and this failing would be a disaster.” 

Another courtier says, “But if it works and the Lord is not present, it would be an even bigger disaster. No one would believe he had anything to do with it.”

Orsik is pleased, “Lord Aerasumé, you must be there.” 

Taern mutters, “I will also be there to ensure this is not some kind of trap.” He turns to the Royal Guard, “Cordon off the area! The mourners have to be kept away. They have left statues, paintings, and ribbons, turning it into a memorial.” 

The Chamberlain interrupts, “When was this going to happen?  His Lordship has a hectic schedule. Can we wait until Tomorrow?”

Vardis shrugs, “Busy, busy, busy!” 

Thistle adds, “We must return to Iymrith’s lair to rescue other hostages.” 

Rebecca Deakin, the sage from Wyrmworks, who Thistle had asked to research Iymrith and now is an advisor to the Lords of Silverymoon tut-tuts, “Iymrith is a legendary dragon of unsurpassed power? How did the four of you figure anything out? It is almost absurd.” 

Unla Kee is annoyed. " Have you ever heard of the Wizard of the Crag? Us of the Flying Castle?” 

Rebecca is undaunted, “Perhaps -you- are behind the events?” 

Orsik smirks, “We are diplomats from Everlund. Are you accusing Everlund?” 

And then the Captain of the Royal Guard shouts, “I told you, your Lordship, that that city would be the bane of us. We should have crushed them in the last war.” 

Lord Aerasumé watches the free-for-all of shouting courtiers and says, “I see.” The room becomes silent. “Did you say in a few minutes this event would happen?” 

Thistle nods, “Yes. We must go down there to ensure you are seen behind getting these people back.” 

“And the real power behind all of this was Iymrith? Was the Doom of the Desert also behind the Giant Civil War? And you have come to do this for ?” 

Looking at the time, Thistle speaks before Orsik can begin his negotiations, “That’s what we would like to discuss after we have shown we are serious.” 

His Lordship stares at Thistle, confused. 

Orsik realizes that that would cause his Lordship not to trust the deal and says with an air of sleaze, “Thistle is very noble and wants to do the right thing. But we want to end Iymrith.” 

“If Iymrith is behind the events that have disrupted my kingdom, I am sure I would be willing to consider some aid. Especially if the involvement of Silverymoon could be kept secret.” 

Orsik decides now to get his revenge, “Could you deal with house Margaster?”

His Lordship is confused, “They are an irrelevant house.” 

Thistle won’t let it slide, “They allied themselves with Iymrith.” 

But maybe it’s the big wink he does to Orsik, or maybe it’s the tell where he scratches his jaw, or perhaps it’s the groan Orsik does when he sees what a lousy liar Thistle is that causes his Lordship not to believe the fairy. 

“The Margasters have never been a goodly house here, and there have been rumors about their involvement in unsavory businesses. But why do you not like them?” 

Thistle blurts out, “Cambions.” 

His Lordship stands up outraged, “Are you accusing me of having Devils in my city?” 

There’s an awkward silence as everyone tries not to look at his Lordship. After all, some demonic or diabolical presence stole 200 notables from the city, and the creature was an ally of his Lordship. 

He realizes what he has said and says, “Yes. Perhaps we should investigate them. The recent failures of our protection of our citizens have perhaps made it less righteous of us to dismiss warnings from others. Perhaps this is one of those times where we shouldn't assume all is good.” 

Taern gruffly says, “I never liked the House. They always struck me as corrupt. Look into them, I say.” 

A few moments later, Thistle stands outside the Theater where the 3HPP burned. There are ribbons and flowers from people who come to pay their respects. He notices how touching the place has become a memorial to those lost. He tries not to remember that the ribbons are not for the possibly living victims of Bob but the dead ones. 

The fairy turns to Unla Kee - “Can you craft me a pedestal?” 

Unla Kee casts mold earth, and Thistle now stands 10 feet off the ground. 

He holds the feather and hopes Bob keeps his end of the bargain.  

“Ictum. Antium.” And then he pauses, “What was the last word?” 

There is stunned silence. 

He smiles, “Right, Merthium.” 

Just like that, a magical door appears where the door used to be, and the 200 guests walk out very happy, talking about how great the play was. They are very confused when they turn around and see the theater burned down. 

The Chamberlain shouts, “Huzzah for his Lordship!” And the crowd shouts, “Huzzah.”

Orsik shouts, “Huzzah for Everlund!” 

There is an awkward silence. 

Thistle shouts, “You made a good decision to put your faith in the goodly races and the fairies,” 

Orsik interrupts, “And the good people of Everlund!” 

Thistle glares at him, “And his Lordship is not done here! He plans to help me and my allies, the adventuring group known as the 3HPP, rescue the remaining prisoners that Iymrith has and help us end her tyranny!” 

The crowd cheers. 

His Lordship smiles tightly. 

Taern Hornblade laughs and whispers, “Well played fairy.” 

Thistle looks at him and remembers that Taern Hornblade was at the final battle when Tiamat’s temple was destroyed. He remembers the stories about how the wizard was a pompous blowhard. 

His Lordship retires with the 3HPP to the rotunda. 

Orsik senses that he might get nothing if he doesn’t ask for what he wants. So he asks for all of the leading wizards of Silverymoon. 

His Lordship raises an eyebrow and then laughs. 

“They have more important things to do.”

“What could be more important than protecting Silverymoon?” 

Vardis coughs and says, “They are protecting cambions?” 

Taern looks at the 3HPP icily and says, “Perhaps you have not heard a saying we have in Silverymoon? Do not meddle in the affairs of wizards, for they are subtle and quick to anger.” 

His Lordship, sensing Taern’s irritation and, at the same time, feeling that he should do something, says, “Although our leading Wizards are far too important to leave the city, I think we could spare five of our battle mages?” 

Taern mutters, “Four.” 

“Five,” says his Lordship. Orsik and Vardis cannot determine if it was out of pique or something else. 

As Taern writes the orders, Lord Aerasumé turns to the 3HPP, “I must admit that the diplomats of Everlund would help me, and Silverymoon was something I never expected. Our most recent conversations suggested they would rather see us rot in hell.” 

Everyone in the rotunda laughs. 

Lord Aerasumé then speaks, “I would like to speak to Thistle alone.” 

Everyone is startled, and there is some concern from his allies, but Thistle is unconcerned. 

His Lordship turns to the fairy, “You have done me a good turn, and I will do your kind a good turn. It is time the fairies became a goodly race of Everlund.” He pauses, “In a few months, assuming you have dealt with Iymrith.”

He pauses again, “I wish I had thought to blame a dragon. After all, Silverymoon had fought against the Cult of the Dragon, and the theater was burned down—and Dragons breathe fire. I am not fool enough to think it was the Dragon and wise enough not to care. And as Lord of Silverymoon, the death of Iymrith will be a good thing.” 

Thistle is about to say something, and his Lordship says, “Go.” 

As they leave the building, Orsik says, “We have a lot of gold, and this city has a lot of magic. Let’s go spend it.” 

They walk to the various schools of magic and magical stores, purchasing scrolls and spells that might help them in their final fight. 

As they walk around, Vardis turns to Orsik and says, “You going to try and kill Iymrith?” 

“It’s how my story ends. Heroically defeating a dragon or dying.”

Vardis chokes up for a bit, “I guess I am happy to have been your friend all these years.” 

Orsik is about to have an emotional moment, coughs, and says, “Remember NRRRR”
